title: Significance of serum AMH and antral follicle count discrepancy for the prediction of ovarian stimulation response in Poseidon criteria patients
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: This study evaluates the predictive value of discordant anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) and antral follicle count (AFC) measurements for ovarian response outcomes in patients with poor ovarian response characteristics according to POSEIDON criteria. Among women with discordant markers—defined as low AMH (<1.2 ng/mL) with adequate AFC (≥5) or adequate AMH with low AFC (<5)—AFC emerged as the superior predictor of oocyte yield and non-poor ovarian response during stimulation. Specifically, an AFC of 6 demonstrated 77% sensitivity for predicting non-poor response, whereas AMH thresholds showed lower predictive utility with only 31% sensitivity at 1.19 ng/mL. When serum AMH falls below 1.19 ng/mL but AFC remains at least 6, patients have moderate likelihood of achieving adequate oocyte retrieval, indicating that AFC may better reflect functional ovarian reserve in these discordant cases. The use of recombinant gonadotropins was identified as an additional positive predictor of non-poor ovarian response. These findings suggest that in POSEIDON criteria patients with discordant ovarian reserve markers, AFC should be weighted more heavily than AMH when counseling patients and predicting stimulation outcomes.

title: High estradiol levels in fresh embryo transfer cycles are not associated with detrimental impact on birth outcomes
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: This study examined the relationship between serum estradiol levels during fresh embryo transfer cycles and birth outcomes in assisted reproduction. Contrary to previous concerns, elevated estradiol levels were associated with improved birth outcomes, with a dose-dependent increase in good birth outcomes observed across estradiol concentrations from 1000 pg/mL to 4000 pg/mL or higher. Cycles with estradiol ≥4000 pg/mL demonstrated significantly greater likelihood of good birth outcomes compared to cycles with estradiol <1000 pg/mL. In contrast, elevated progesterone on the day of trigger was identified as a negative prognostic factor, reducing the likelihood of good birth outcomes. These findings suggest that high estradiol levels in fresh transfer cycles do not compromise reproductive success and may even be beneficial, while progesterone elevation warrants clinical attention as a potential marker of cycle complications.

title: TP53 Gene Polymorphism at Codon 72 as a Response Predictor for Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates how TP53 gene polymorphisms at codon 72 predict treatment response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy in breast cancer patients. The TP53 gene plays a central role in inducing chemotherapy response, and variants at codon 72 were examined for their association with pathologic complete response (pCR) and stable disease outcomes. While TP53 polymorphism itself did not independently predict pCR, both Arg72Pro and Pro72Pro variants were significantly associated with increased odds of stable disease rather than complete response. Immunohistochemistry emerged as the only independent predictor of pCR in multivariate analysis, suggesting that protein-level expression is more informative than genetic polymorphism alone for predicting complete response. These findings indicate that TP53 codon 72 polymorphisms may serve as useful markers for identifying patients likely to experience stable disease during chemotherapy, while other factors determine the likelihood of achieving pathologic complete response.

title: Human metaphase II oocytes with narrow perivitelline space have poor fertilization, developmental, and pregnancy potentials
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary:
# Summary

Human metaphase II oocytes with narrow perivitelline space (NPVS) demonstrate significantly compromised reproductive potential across multiple developmental stages. These oocytes show higher rates of degeneration and abnormal fertilization, including increased odds of zero pronuclei (0PN) formation and reduced two pronuclei (2PN) fertilization rates. Even oocytes that achieve successful fertilization display substantially lower cleavage and blastocyst development rates compared to those with normal perivitelline space. Importantly, blastocysts derived from NPVS oocytes have markedly reduced clinical pregnancy potential, suggesting that the morphological abnormality of narrow perivitelline space is predictive of poor reproductive outcomes throughout embryonic development and implantation. These findings indicate that perivitelline space morphology is a critical oocyte quality indicator that affects both early fertilization events and subsequent developmental competence in assisted reproduction.

title: Infertility and Risk of Postmenopausal Breast Cancer in the Women’s Health Initiative
summary: This study examined the relationship between infertility history and postmenopausal breast cancer risk in the Women's Health Initiative, finding a modest but statistically significant association (HR=1.07). The infertility-breast cancer link was largely mediated by age at first term pregnancy, with approximately 46% of the association explained by delayed childbearing beyond age 30. Among specific infertility diagnoses, male factor infertility showed the strongest association with postmenopausal breast cancer risk (HR=1.21), particularly in women with first births after age 30, while ovulatory, tubal factor, and endometriosis-related infertility showed no significant associations. Women with infertility histories had characteristic reproductive patterns including lower parity, fewer pregnancies, shorter breastfeeding duration, and higher rates of bilateral oophorectomy, all established breast cancer risk factors. The observed associations were driven primarily by reproductive mediators including parity, breastfeeding, and menstrual cycle irregularity rather than by infertility diagnoses themselves, suggesting that the reproductive consequences of infertility rather than its underlying causes drive breast cancer risk.

title: Association between diet quality and ovarian cancer risk and survival
journal: JNCI Journal of the National Cancer Institute
summary: This study examined the relationship between diet quality and ovarian cancer outcomes in a large prospective cohort. The researchers found no significant association between overall diet quality and ovarian cancer risk, regardless of the dietary assessment method used. However, better prediagnosis diet quality was associated with improved survival outcomes, with both the Healthy Eating Index-2015 and the alternate Mediterranean diet score showing reduced all-cause mortality in patients with the highest diet quality scores compared to the lowest. The Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension dietary score showed no association with mortality outcomes. These findings suggest that while diet quality may not influence the initial development of ovarian cancer, maintaining a high-quality diet before diagnosis is associated with better long-term survival in ovarian cancer patients.

title: Evaluating the developmental potential of 2.1PN-derived embryos and associated chromosomal analysis
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: This study evaluated the developmental competence of 2.1 pronuclear (2.1PN) zygotes, which contain an extra pronucleus and are typically considered unsuitable for embryo transfer. The research demonstrated that 2.1PN zygotes successfully develop into blastocysts at rates comparable to normal 2PN zygotes in advanced maternal age patients (≥38 years), with early cleavage patterns and Day 3 embryo quality serving as independent predictors of blastocyst formation. Notably, 2.1PN-derived blastocysts exhibited euploidy rates similar to those of 2PN-derived blastocysts, suggesting that the additional pronucleus does not substantially compromise chromosomal integrity. The study recommends that euploid 2.1PN-derived embryos, when identified through preimplantation genetic testing, could be considered for clinical transfer in selected patient populations. While the mechanism by which the additional pronucleus might cause embryonic chromosomal abnormalities remains unclear, these findings challenge the conventional exclusion of 2.1PN embryos from assisted reproductive practice and suggest potential clinical utility for advanced maternal age cases.

title: Is a day 7 blastocyst predictive of the reproductive potential of sibling day 5 and day 6 blastocysts?
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary:
# Summary

Day 5 blastocysts demonstrate significantly higher ongoing pregnancy rates compared to day 6 and day 7 blastocysts, with the timing of embryo cryopreservation representing a critical predictor of reproductive potential. When controlling for maternal age, oocyte retrieval outcomes, and embryo ploidy status, both day 6 and day 7 blastocysts show substantially reduced pregnancy outcomes relative to day 5 embryos. Notably, the presence of a slower-developing day 7 blastocyst sibling does not meaningfully predict or impair the reproductive potential of day 5 or day 6 embryos within the same cohort, suggesting that embryo development timing is an embryo-specific characteristic rather than a cohort-dependent phenomenon. These findings indicate that day 7 blastocyst formation, reflecting delayed embryo development, should not be used as a prognostic indicator for sibling embryos and does not necessitate reconsideration of day 5 or day 6 embryo viability. The results support embryo selection strategies prioritizing earlier developmental stages for cryopreservation and transfer in assisted reproductive technology cycles.

title: Serum anti-mullerian hormone, sex hormone, and nutrient levels in reproductive age women with celiac disease
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: Celiac disease in reproductive-age women is associated with significantly reduced ovarian reserve, as evidenced by lower anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) levels that correlate negatively with disease severity markers including tissue transglutaminase antibodies, intestinal histologic grading, and disease duration. The condition also disrupts hormonal balance, characterized by reduced testosterone and elevated prolactin levels, while causing widespread nutritional deficiencies in iron, folate, zinc, and selenium. The impaired ovarian function observed in celiac disease patients appears multifactorial, driven by both disease severity and associated nutrient malabsorption, suggesting that intestinal inflammation and nutritional status are key mediators of reproductive dysfunction. These findings indicate that celiac disease represents an important but potentially underappreciated contributor to infertility risk in women of reproductive age, with implications for screening and management strategies in this population.

title: Three possible diagnostic biomarkers for gastric cancer: miR-362-3p, miR-362-5p and miR-363-5p
summary: This study identifies three microRNAs—miR-362-3p, miR-362-5p, and miR-363-5p—as potential diagnostic biomarkers for gastric cancer. All three microRNAs were significantly overexpressed in gastric cancer cells compared to normal tissue, suggesting they accumulate in the malignant microenvironment. The elevated levels of these miRNAs showed strong correlations with clinicopathological features of gastric cancer, indicating their potential utility in characterizing disease progression and severity. These findings support the use of microRNA profiling as an early diagnostic tool for gastric cancer detection, as these specific miRNAs may be detectable in patient samples before conventional clinical manifestations appear. The consistent overexpression pattern across all three miRNAs suggests they may work through related biological pathways in gastric carcinogenesis. These biomarkers could improve early cancer diagnosis and potentially inform treatment decisions based on individual tumor microRNA signatures.

title: Trophectoderm grade as a predictor of beta human-chorionic gonadotropin rise in early pregnancy
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: This study examined whether trophectoderm (TE) grade—a morphological assessment of blastocyst quality—predicts the rate of β-human-chorionic gonadotropin (β-HCG) rise in early pregnancy. The researchers found that trophectoderm grade was significantly associated with early pregnancy serum β-HCG levels following fresh day 5 embryo transfer, with grade C embryos showing higher β-HCG rises compared to grade B embryos in fresh transfers. Notably, this association was specific to fresh embryo transfers, as frozen embryo transfers showed no significant differences in β-HCG rise based on trophectoderm grade. These findings suggest that trophectoderm morphology may serve as a useful predictor of initial pregnancy hormone dynamics in fresh transfer cycles, though the mechanism underlying this differential response between fresh and frozen transfers warrants further investigation.

title: Anti-Müllerian hormone does not predict cumulative pregnancy rate in non-infertile women following four IUI cycles with donor sperm
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: This study evaluated whether anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) and antral follicle count (AFC) could predict pregnancy outcomes in non-infertile women undergoing four cycles of intrauterine insemination with donor sperm (ds-IUI). Although AMH and AFC showed a strong positive correlation (r = 0.67, p < 0.001) and AMH levels ≥1.1 ng/mL were initially associated with ds-IUI success, receiver operating characteristic curve analyses revealed that neither ovarian reserve marker reliably predicted cumulative pregnancy rates. Notably, women with low AMH levels still achieved successful pregnancies, challenging the conventional assumption that diminished ovarian reserve necessarily indicates poor reproductive outcomes. These findings suggest that AMH and AFC should not be used as standalone predictive markers for pregnancy success in non-infertile populations undergoing donor sperm insemination, as other physiological factors beyond ovarian reserve appear to drive fertility outcomes in this population.

title: History of infertility and anti-Müllerian hormone levels among participants in the Nurses’ Health Study II
summary: This study examined anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) levels, a marker of ovarian reserve and menopause timing, in women with a history of infertility from the Nurses' Health Study II. While overall infertility history showed no significant association with AMH levels, the relationship varied substantially by the underlying cause of infertility and age at infertility onset, with women experiencing infertility after age 30 demonstrating significantly lower AMH levels. Notably, endometriosis and ovulatory disorders showed opposite associations with AMH—endometriosis was associated with lower AMH levels consistent with reduced ovarian reserve, while ovulatory disorders (particularly PCOS) were associated with elevated AMH levels. The findings underscore that the specific etiology of infertility, rather than infertility status alone, is the primary determinant of AMH levels, and given AMH's predictive value for menopause timing and associations with chronic disease risk including cardiovascular disease and breast cancer, these reproductive history factors may have important implications for long-term health outcomes beyond fertility concerns.

title: Constructing a predictive model for live birth following fresh embryo transfer in antagonist protocol for polycystic ovary syndrome
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: This study developed a predictive nomogram for live birth outcomes following fresh embryo transfer using antagonist protocols in patients with polycystic ovary syndrome. Multivariable logistic regression analysis identified multiple independent factors significantly associated with live birth success, including female age, body mass index, duration of infertility, serum testosterone and progesterone levels, the number of high-quality cleavage-stage embryos, embryo type, and total number of embryos transferred. The resulting predictive nomogram achieved substantial discriminatory accuracy with an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.804, demonstrating robust performance in outcome prediction. This nomogram provides clinicians with a quantitative tool to assess individual patient prognosis and guide personalized treatment planning in assisted reproduction for PCOS patients. By integrating both patient demographic factors and laboratory parameters with embryological characteristics, the model offers clinical utility in counseling patients about expected live birth probabilities and optimizing embryo transfer strategies.

title: Objective Performance Indicators Differ in Obese and Non-obese Patients During Robotic Proctectomy
summary:
# Summary

Obesity significantly impairs objective performance indicators during robotic proctectomy across multiple surgical steps, with obese patients demonstrating increased camera kinematics (more movements, longer path lengths, higher velocities), more frequent arm swaps, and greater instrument articulation compared to non-obese patients. These differences are most pronounced during left colon mobilization, pelvic dissection, and rectal transection phases. The technical challenges associated with obesity—including bulky mesentery, heavier organs, and impaired vascular visualization—necessitate prolonged energy activation times, extended step completion times, and more frequent master clutch use to maintain adequate exposure in confined spaces. Arm swap frequencies and camera control behaviors serve as reliable indicators of surgical complexity and procedural difficulty, with instrument kinematics correlating to clinical outcomes. The increased wrist articulation observed in obese patients may represent a compensatory strategy to achieve better leverage in narrowed workspaces, though the overall pattern suggests that obesity substantially increases the technical demands and cognitive burden of robotic rectal surgery.

title: The association between the use of fertility indicators and fecundability in a Danish preconception cohort
summary:
# Summary

This Danish preconception cohort study examined how different fertility awareness methods influence the likelihood of conception. Overall use of fertility indicators was associated with significantly improved fecundability, with an adjusted fecundability ratio of 1.14 compared to non-users. Among specific methods, cervical fluid monitoring demonstrated the strongest association with conception (adjusted FR 1.46), followed by urinary ovulation testing (adjusted FR 1.35) and counting days since the last menstrual period (adjusted FR 1.18). Feeling ovulation and fertility apps showed more modest associations with fecundability, while basal body temperature measurement showed no significant benefit. These findings suggest that certain fertility awareness methods, particularly those that identify the fertile window through biological markers, may enhance conception rates by enabling better-timed intercourse in preconception planning.

title: Predictive models of miscarriage based on data from a preconception cohort study
summary: This preconception cohort study developed predictive models for miscarriage risk by analyzing multiple demographic, lifestyle, and medical factors. The strongest predictors of miscarriage were advancing female age at conception, advancing male partner age, and a prior history of miscarriage, with female age showing the highest relative risk. Modifiable risk factors including higher body mass index, smoking, and lower diet quality (as measured by Healthy Eating Index-2010 score) were associated with increased miscarriage risk, while being married and prior oral contraceptive use were associated with reduced risk. Several lifestyle factors including caffeine consumption and alcohol intake showed medium-level associations with miscarriage, though some unexpected findings emerged such as omega-3 supplementation being slightly positively associated with miscarriage. The findings confirm that maternal and paternal age are the most critical factors determining miscarriage risk and highlight the potential value of dietary interventions and smoking cessation in preconception counseling. Overall, the study demonstrates that miscarriage risk involves both immutable factors related to parental age and modifiable lifestyle variables that could be targeted in prevention strategies.

title: Inflammation and Ovarian Function in Reproductive-Aged Women
journal: American journal of human biology : the official journal of the Human Biology Council
summary: This study demonstrates that systemic inflammation, measured by C-reactive protein (CRP), significantly impairs ovarian function in reproductive-aged women through multiple mechanisms. Elevated CRP levels are associated with reduced biomarkers of ovarian reserve, specifically decreasing follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) and inhibin B while showing minimal association with anti-müllerian hormone (AMH). The research reveals that excessive inflammation disrupts the follicular microenvironment by promoting premature follicle death, altering follicular wave dynamics, and potentially triggering apoptosis of ovarian cells. Inflammatory conditions including endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, and Crohn's disease are consistently associated with lower AMH levels and reduced ovarian reserve. These findings suggest that chronic inflammation plays a critical pathophysiological role in ovarian aging and reproductive dysfunction, with implications for understanding fertility impairment in women with inflammatory diseases.

title: Target trial emulation of preconception serum vitamin D status on fertility outcomes: a couples-based approach
summary:
# Summary

This couples-based target trial emulation examined how preconception vitamin D status affects fertility outcomes in individuals undergoing fertility treatment. Vitamin D plays critical roles in reproduction through its involvement in spermatogenesis, folliculogenesis, and implantation, with vitamin D receptors distributed across the hypothalamus and both male and female reproductive tracts. The study found that females with sufficient preconception 25-hydroxyvitamin D (25(OH)D) had 28% higher likelihood of live birth compared to deficient females, and this association was particularly pronounced among couples with normal BMI where both partners maintained adequate vitamin D status. While vitamin D biomarkers including 25(OH)D, vitamin D binding protein (VDBP), and bioavailable vitamin D were positively associated with live birth, vitamin D status showed no association with pregnancy loss or semen quality parameters, suggesting that vitamin D's fertility effects operate through mechanisms beyond male gamete quality, potentially including hormonal regulation, immune modulation, and epigenetic modifications. The findings indicate that obesity and associated chronic inflammation may attenuate vitamin D's protective effects, highlighting the importance of considering metabolic status when evaluating vitamin D's role in reproduction.

title: A prospective study of male physical activity and fecundability
summary: This prospective study examined how male physical activity patterns influence fecundability (the probability of conception per cycle) in couples attempting pregnancy. The researchers found that both vigorous and moderate physical activity were generally inversely associated with fecundability, suggesting that higher exercise intensity may reduce conception likelihood. Notably, bicycling emerged as a particularly problematic activity, with soft comfort seats showing a consistent reduction in fecundability (fertility rate 0.75 for ≥3 hours/week), while hard racing-style seats showed no significant effect, suggesting that prolonged perineal pressure from padded seats may impair male reproductive function. The negative association between soft-seat bicycling and fecundability was similar or stronger among overweight and obese men (BMI ≥25 kg/m²), indicating that higher body weight may compound the reproductive risks of this activity. These findings suggest that male physical activity, particularly vigorous exercise and certain types of cycling, may represent a modifiable factor affecting couple fertility, with implications for men counseled on lifestyle optimization during conception attempts.

title: The association of triglyceride-glucose and triglyceride-glucose related indices with the risk of heart disease in a national cohort study
summary: This national cohort study demonstrates that triglyceride-glucose (TyG) indices combined with anthropometric measures—particularly TyG-BMI, TyG-WC (waist circumference), and TyG-WHtR (waist-to-height ratio)—are significantly associated with increased heart disease risk, with TyG-BMI showing the strongest association (HR 1.73 in the highest quartile). The TyG index alone was not significantly associated with heart disease, suggesting that incorporating body composition measurements enhances the predictive value of the glucose-triglyceride marker. These composite indices reflect insulin resistance, a well-established independent cardiovascular risk factor, and participants with elevated TyG indices exhibited higher levels of inflammatory markers (WBC and CRP), hypertension, and diabetes prevalence. The associations remained consistent in non-diabetic participants but were attenuated in diabetic patients, except for TyG-BMI which retained significance in type 2 diabetes patients. These findings suggest that TyG-related indices incorporating adiposity measures may serve as practical, economical screening tools for identifying individuals at elevated cardiovascular risk, particularly in non-diabetic populations.

title: Using Patient Experience Surveys to Identify Potential Diagnostic Safety Breakdowns: A Mixed Methods Study
summary: This mixed methods study demonstrates that patient experience surveys serve as a valuable tool for identifying diagnostic safety breakdowns in clinical practice. Patients who provided low scores on provider-related questions were nearly seven times more likely to perceive diagnostic breakdowns, while low scores on practice-related questions doubled this likelihood. Patient-perceived diagnostic breakdowns encompassed three primary categories: accuracy issues, timeliness problems, and communication failures, with communication breakdown themes centered on providers not listening, dismissing patient concerns, or failing to recognize symptom significance. Low scores on access questions were particularly associated with perceived timeliness delays, while provider-related scores correlated most strongly with both accuracy and communication breakdowns. The structured survey data identified 815 distinct breakdowns across 619 encounters, suggesting that routine patient feedback mechanisms provide an easily monitored screening method for detecting diagnostic safety vulnerabilities in clinical care. These findings highlight the importance of provider attentiveness, timely diagnosis, and effective patient-provider communication in preventing diagnostic errors.

title: Delays in the final stages of fertilization are strongly associated with trichotomous cytokinesis and cleavage arrest
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: Delays in the interval between pronuclear breakdown and first cytokinesis are strongly associated with abnormal early embryonic development, including trichotomous cleavage and developmental arrest. Extended pronuclear fading to first cleavage intervals—particularly exceeding 2.78 hours—correlate with direct unequal cleavage at the first mitotic division and subsequent chromosomal segregation errors. Similarly, intervals exceeding 2.50 hours predict unequal cleavage at the second mitotic cycle and reduced blastulation rates. These timing delays appear to disrupt the normal symmetry of early cell division, leading to aneuploid embryos with compromised developmental potential. The identification of specific temporal thresholds suggests that precise synchronization of pronuclear events with mitotic progression is critical for normal embryo development. These findings may be relevant for assessing embryo viability in assisted reproduction and understanding how developmental timing disruptions could impact fertility outcomes.

title: Evaluation of the association of some circulating miRNA molecules in the metabolic syndrome
summary: This study identifies miRNA-143 and miRNA-371 as circulating biomarkers significantly elevated in metabolic syndrome compared to pre-metabolic syndrome and metabolically healthy obese individuals. Both miRNAs show strong correlations with key metabolic parameters including fasting glucose, triglycerides, body mass index, and lipid profiles, suggesting their involvement in metabolic dysregulation. At the mechanistic level, these miRNAs interfere with insulin signaling pathways and regulate adipogenesis and adipose tissue function, contributing to the development of insulin resistance and metabolic dysfunction. The findings establish miRNA-143 and miRNA-371 as potential therapeutic targets for metabolic syndrome, which itself encompasses obesity, type 2 diabetes, dyslipidemia, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. The distinct expression patterns of these miRNAs across metabolic states support their utility as diagnostic or prognostic markers for metabolic disease progression.

title: The profile of oxidative stress markers (arachidonic and linoleic acid derivatives) in patients with benign prostatic hyperplasia in relation to metabolic syndrome
summary: Benign prostatic hyperplasia is strongly associated with chronic inflammation, infectious agents, and autoimmune dysregulation, with metabolic syndrome and age-related factors serving as significant risk drivers. The study reveals a distinct inflammatory lipid mediator profile in BPH patients characterized by elevated pro-inflammatory oxylipin derivatives (5-HETE, 12-HETE, 15-HETE, 5-oxoETE, and 9-HODE) and pro-resolving lipid mediators (RvE1, LXA4, MaR1, and 10S,17R-DiDHA), while classical mediators like PGE2, LTB4, and 18RS-HEPE are significantly reduced compared to controls. These oxylipins are metabolically derived from arachidonic acid and linoleic acid through cyclooxygenase, lipoxygenase, and cytochrome P450 pathways, where they regulate inflammatory cell recruitment and immune responses. The elevated pro-resolving mediators suggest a compensatory anti-inflammatory response attempting to counteract the dominant pro-inflammatory state, though this appears insufficient to prevent disease progression and symptomatology including lower urinary tract symptoms and prostate enlargement. The association between metabolic syndrome and altered lipid mediator profiles, particularly elevated LXA4, indicates that metabolic dysfunction directly influences the inflammatory lipid signaling landscape in BPH pathogenesis.

title: Assessing the health and economic burden of obesity-related complications in East-Asian populations: implementation of risk equations in the Core Obesity Model for Japan and model validation
summary: This study implements risk equations in the Core Obesity Model to assess obesity-related complications in Japanese populations, revealing significant ethnic and metabolic differences compared to Western cohorts. Obesity drives multiple serious comorbidities including type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, several cancers (breast, colorectal, endometrial), non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, chronic kidney disease, and musculoskeletal disorders. Notably, Japanese individuals demonstrate a threefold higher diabetes incidence and twofold higher rates of obstructive sleep apnea and gout at equal BMI levels compared to Western populations, attributed to differential body fat distribution with greater abdominal and visceral adiposity in Asian individuals. Type 2 diabetes further elevates cardiovascular risk including ischemic and hemorrhagic stroke, while postmenopausal women show increased susceptibility to obesity-related breast and endometrial cancers. The model reveals that Japanese populations paradoxically show lower coronary heart disease incidence but higher stroke rates, highlighting important ethnic variations in obesity-related disease patterns that must inform public health interventions and economic burden assessments in East Asian regions.

title: Multiomic profiling reveals timing of menopause predicts prefrontal cortex aging and cognitive function
summary:
# Summary

This multiomic study demonstrates that age at menopause (AAM) is a strong predictor of prefrontal cortex aging and cognitive function, with earlier menopause associated with accelerated brain aging, lower cognitive scores, frailty, cardiovascular disease, and increased all-cause mortality. The study identifies bile acid metabolism as a key pathway linking AAM to cognitive outcomes, revealing that later AAM is associated with activation of the alternate cholesterol metabolism pathway through increased CDCA:CA ratios and gut microbiota-derived bile acids. Molecular profiling shows that genes associated with better cognition are enriched among AAM-associated loci and have increased expression with later menopause, while inflammatory and mTORC1 signaling pathways are downregulated with later AAM. In surgical menopause, altered circulating microRNAs disrupt NAD+ activity and IGF signaling, suggesting distinct molecular mechanisms between natural and surgical menopause. These findings establish AAM as a heritable trait involving DNA-damage response pathways that has systemic effects on aging, with implications for using AAM as a biomarker to predict cognitive decline and healthspan across the lifespan.

title: Association between factor I fibrinogen (rs6050) and factor XI plasma thromboplastin (rs4253417) genetic polymorphisms and recurrent spontaneous miscarriage in Saudi women
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated the association between two coagulation-related genetic polymorphisms and recurrent spontaneous miscarriage (RSM) in Saudi women. The fibrinogen rs6050 C/T polymorphism showed significant correlation with RSM susceptibility, with the CT genotype conferring substantially elevated risk (odds ratio 4.18, p=0.001), while the TT genotype demonstrated protective effects. In contrast, the factor XI rs4253417 C/T polymorphism showed no significant association with RSM in this population. The mechanism linking fibrinogen to pregnancy loss appears to involve alterations in fibrinogen and thromboplastin levels that promote thrombotic events, which subsequently lead to pregnancy complications. These findings suggest that genetic variations affecting coagulation proteins, particularly fibrinogen, represent important thrombophilic risk factors for recurrent pregnancy loss in Saudi women, contributing to the understanding of how hypercoagulability may underlie reproductive failure.

title: Association of Parity with Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus in Japan
summary: This study demonstrates that parity is significantly associated with increased type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) risk in postmenopausal but not premenopausal Japanese women, with this relationship substantially mediated by postpartum weight gain. The association between parity and T2DM strengthens with metabolic factors, as higher parity correlates with increased obesity, elevated body mass index, and greater waist circumference, particularly in postmenopausal women. A history of gestational diabetes mellitus emerges as a strong independent risk factor for T2DM development in both pre- and postmenopausal women, highlighting pregnancy-related metabolic dysfunction as a critical determinant. Beyond T2DM itself, the study underscores the substantial cardiovascular consequences of diabetes in women, revealing that women with T2DM face significantly elevated risks of coronary heart disease, acute myocardial infarction, and stroke compared to men. Interestingly, while higher parity associates with greater hypertension prevalence, it inversely correlates with family history of T2DM, suggesting that environmental and metabolic factors rather than genetic predisposition may drive the parity-diabetes relationship.

title: Association between atherogenic index of plasma and infertility: a cross-sectional study based on U.S. women
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study of U.S. women establishes a significant positive association between the Atherogenic Index of Plasma (AIP) and female infertility, with infertility risk increasing progressively across AIP quartiles. The mechanistic basis involves dyslipidemia triggering endothelial dysfunction, oxidative stress, and inflammation—each of which impairs reproductive function and promotes infertility. Notably, the AIP-infertility relationship exhibits a nonlinear pattern, with particularly elevated odds ratios at both very low (AIP < -0.21) and very high (AIP > 0.22) values, while intermediate AIP levels show relative stability. The association was strongest among younger women (ages 21-30), those with lower socioeconomic status, non-smokers, non-drinkers, and individuals with elevated BMI, suggesting that metabolic lipid profiles represent an important modifiable risk factor for infertility across specific demographic subgroups.

title: Higher oxidative balance score is associated with a decreased risk of infertility: an analysis of NHANES 2013–2020
summary:
# Summary

Oxidative stress plays a critical role in female reproductive dysfunction by impairing oocyte maturation, embryonic development, and implantation, while elevated reactive oxygen species contribute to infertility-related pathologies including endometriosis and tubal dysfunction. This NHANES-based analysis demonstrates that a higher oxidative balance score (OBS)—encompassing both dietary and lifestyle factors—is significantly associated with reduced infertility risk, with women in the highest OBS quartile showing a 43% decrease in odds of infertility compared to the lowest quartile. Both dietary OBS (11% risk reduction per unit increase) and lifestyle OBS (showing greater protective effects) independently reduce infertility rates, with lifestyle factors including antioxidant-rich food consumption, physical activity, and avoidance of smoking, alcohol, and obesity proving particularly influential in managing oxidative stress. The study further identified that infertile women have elevated BMI and higher prevalence of conditions associated with oxidative stress—including endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, and pelvic inflammatory disease—as well as reduced micronutrient levels (zinc, magnesium) known to support antioxidant defense. Collectively, these findings suggest that maintaining a favorable oxidative balance through dietary antioxidants and lifestyle modifications represents a modifiable therapeutic approach to reducing female infertility risk across multiple etiological pathways.

title: Ovarian endometrioma recurrence after laparoscopic surgery: First assessment of ACSAP predicting score
summary: Ovarian endometrioma, the most common form of endometriosis affecting 2–10% of women of reproductive age, is strongly associated with infertility and can lead to premature ovarian failure after recurrent surgical interventions. This study developed and validated the ACSAP score, a predictive tool incorporating five clinical variables—age, cyst size, previous endometrioma surgery, revised American Society for Reproductive Medicine stage, and post-operative pregnancy—to stratify recurrence risk following laparoscopic excision. Multivariate analysis identified younger age, larger tumor size, previous surgery for ovarian endometrioma, and advanced disease stage as independent risk factors for recurrence, while post-operative pregnancy emerged as a protective factor, likely through pregnancy-induced anovulation and amenorrhea promoting endometrial lesion necrosis. The ACSAP score demonstrated strong predictive performance, with 5-year cumulative recurrence rates of 5.3%, 20.2%, and 48.0% in the training set for low-, intermediate-, and high-risk groups, respectively, and 0%, 16.5%, and 36.5% in the testing set. Extended post-operative medical treatment (≥15 months with gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists, oral contraceptive pills, or levonorgestrel intrauterine systems) significantly reduced recurrence rates in intermediate- and high-risk groups, providing clinicians with a validated strategy for patient stratification and individualized management planning.

title: 代谢综合征组成部分与子宫内膜癌的因果关系研究
summary: This study employed Mendelian randomization analysis to investigate causal relationships between metabolic syndrome components and endometrial cancer risk. The research identified obesity and its constituent measures—body mass index, overweight status, and body fat percentage—as significant causal risk factors for endometrial cancer, with p-values <0.001 and odds ratios >1. Conversely, several lipid and glycemic parameters showed unexpected inverse associations with endometrial cancer, including elevated cholesterol, triglycerides, and phospholipids, as well as type 1 diabetes and certain glycemic markers. The mechanistic pathways linking obesity to endometrial cancer involve adipose tissue functioning as an endocrine organ, promoting hormone production (particularly estrogen through aromatase activity), chronic inflammation, and cellular proliferation, with additional contribution from adipose-derived mesenchymal stem cells supporting tumor growth. Type 2 diabetes and elevated fasting insulin levels also demonstrated causal associations with increased endometrial cancer risk, highlighting insulin resistance and metabolic dysfunction as critical mechanistic pathways. These findings underscore obesity and type 2 diabetes as independent causal risk factors for endometrial cancer and suggest that more than half of endometrial cancer cases may be attributable to obesity.

title: Obesity and overweight and associated factors among women with infertility undergoing assisted reproductive technology treatment in a low income setting
summary:
# Summary

This study examined 2,812 infertile women undergoing assisted reproductive technology in Ghana and found that elevated body mass index (BMI) is highly prevalent among infertile populations, with 76.83% of women presenting with elevated BMI. Elevated BMI significantly impairs reproductive outcomes by reducing oocyte and embryo quality through morphological abnormalities, resulting in lower pregnancy rates and increased miscarriage risk. Women with elevated BMI required higher doses of gonadotropins for ovarian stimulation, indicating reduced ovarian responsiveness to hormonal treatment. Secondary infertility was more common in overweight and obese women, with occupational sedentary lifestyle, physical inactivity, and compromised dietary habits emerging as key modifiable risk factors, particularly among traders and health workers. Age (35-44 years) and higher educational attainment were statistically significant predictors of elevated BMI, suggesting that socioeconomic factors and occupational patterns drive metabolic dysfunction in this population. These findings demonstrate that obesity-related metabolic dysfunction substantially compromises fertility treatment efficacy in low-income settings and identifies lifestyle interventions as potential avenues for improving reproductive health outcomes.

title: Human genomic regions of systemic interindividual epigenetic variation are implicated in neurodevelopmental and metabolic disorders
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies correlated regions of systemic interindividual variation (CoRSIVs) as epigenetic loci that show substantial and stable DNA methylation differences across individuals and are enriched in disease-associated genomic regions. The authors demonstrate that current Illumina methylation arrays predominantly target CpG sites with negligible interindividual variation, limiting their ability to detect disease associations and explain poor reproducibility in epigenome-wide association studies. In contrast, CoRSIV probes exhibit high interindividual variation (median IRR 0.22) and remarkable stability (ICC 0.82), and their enrichment is particularly striking in endocrine disorders (53.5-fold), neurological disorders (25.7-fold), and metabolic disorders, with even stronger signals in neurodevelopmental conditions including autism spectrum disorder (21.1-fold), ADHD (54.7-fold), and intellectual disability (22.5-fold). Using CoRSIVs in future epigenetic studies could provide 153- to 345-fold greater statistical power for detecting disease associations compared to standard approaches. These findings suggest that systematic epigenetic variation at promoter regions with functional methylation-based transcriptional regulation contributes substantially to disease susceptibility across metabolic, endocrine, and neurodevelopmental disorders.

title: Scalable Deep Learning of Histology Images Reveals Genetic and Phenotypic Determinants of Adipocyte Hypertrophy
summary: Using deep learning analysis of histology images, this study identified adipocyte hypertrophy as a key driver of metabolic dysfunction, demonstrating strong associations with elevated fasting glucose, glycated hemoglobin, leptin, and triglycerides, while inversely correlating with protective adiponectin and HDL cholesterol levels. Adipocyte enlargement promotes a pathogenic cascade including low-grade chronic inflammation, insufficient angiogenesis, excessive collagen deposition, and dysfunctional adipokine release, collectively contributing to insulin resistance and type 2 diabetes risk. The research identified four novel genetic variants (rs73184721 in NAALADL2, rs200047724 in NRXN3, and rs140503338 and rs11656704 in ULK2) that associate with increased adipocyte size and adverse metabolic traits, with notable sex-specific effects in females. Mean adipocyte size correlates positively with BMI, age, and glycemic markers in visceral adipose tissue, while showing inverse relationships with protective adipokine levels, establishing adipocyte hypertrophy as an independent predictor of cardiometabolic disease beyond body mass index. These findings suggest that measuring adipocyte size histologically, combined with genetic risk variants, could improve stratification of individuals at high risk for obesity-related metabolic complications.

title: Proximity extension assay revealed novel inflammatory biomarkers for follicular development and ovarian function: a prospective controlled study combining serum and follicular fluid
summary: Using proximity extension assay technology, this study identified novel inflammatory biomarkers associated with ovarian reserve and follicular development by analyzing both serum and follicular fluid samples. Oncostatin M (OSM) emerged as a key biomarker, with significantly higher serum levels in women with normal ovarian reserve and correlations with antral follicle count, retrieved oocytes, and matured oocytes, establishing OSM as a potential indicator of ovarian function. Three signaling pathways—OSM signaling, IL-10 anti-inflammatory signaling, and PI3K-Akt signaling—were identified as central mechanisms regulating ovarian reserve capacity. The study revealed distinct inflammatory profiles: mature oocytes displayed elevated angiogenic and chemotactic factors (VEGF-A, CCL11, CCL4, FGF-19, MMP-1) but reduced apoptotic markers (CASP-8, SIRT2), while a panel of 22 proteins including LAP TGF-β1, IL-7, IL-10, and CSF-1 were consistently elevated in normal ovarian reserve. The findings highlight the importance of balancing pro-inflammatory and anti-inflammatory pathways for optimal follicular development, with potential implications for understanding infertility-related metabolic dysfunction including endometriosis-associated reproductive decline.

title: Fetal birthweight and maternal urinary incontinence in Chinese primiparas: a population-based study
summary:
# Summary

This population-based study of Chinese primiparas reveals a significant association between fetal birthweight and postpartum urinary incontinence (UI), with each 1.0 kg increase in birthweight correlating with a 32% increased risk of UI across multiple subtypes including stress, urgency, and mixed incontinence. The relationship between birthweight and UI risk is non-linear, with a critical inflection point at approximately 3.9 kg; birthweights below this threshold show substantially increased risk per kilogram gained, while birthweights above 3.9 kg show reduced risk with increasing weight. Women delivering infants in the highest birthweight tertile (3.4–6.0 kg) face notably elevated UI risk compared to lower tertiles. Pregnancy-induced anatomical and metabolic changes, combined with delivery-related pelvic floor damage—particularly with vaginal delivery—constitute the primary mechanisms linking birthweight to UI, with vaginal delivery associated with greater stress and urgency incontinence compared to cesarean delivery. Multiple comorbidities including hypertension, diabetes, chronic constipation, and gynecological diseases interact with birthweight in modifying UI risk. These findings establish fetal birthweight as an independent predictor of maternal UI in Chinese primiparas, suggesting its inclusion in risk stratification models for postpartum incontinence prevention and management.

title: Correlation of MicroRNA-31 with Endometrial Receptivity in Patients with Repeated Implantation Failure of In Vitro Fertilization and Embryo Transfer
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies microRNA-31 (miR-31) as a key biomarker associated with impaired endometrial receptivity in patients experiencing repeated implantation failure (RIF) during in vitro fertilization. The researchers found that miR-31 expression is significantly elevated in the endometrium of RIF patients and shows strong positive correlations with increased pulsatility index and resistance index—markers of poor endometrial blood flow—while inversely correlating with reduced endometrial thickness. MicroRNA-31 represents an optimal predictor of RIF status with an area under the curve of 0.899, functioning as a sensitive diagnostic tool in assisted reproduction programs. The elevated miR-31 appears to regulate endometrial receptivity through multiple mechanisms, including effects on steroid hormone metabolism and regulation of the implantation window timing. Overall, endometrial miR-31 expression alongside ultrasound-based vascular resistance parameters provides integrated biomarkers for identifying and potentially addressing the endometrial deficiencies underlying implantation failure in reproductive medicine.

title: The association between age at menarche and infertility: a systematic review and meta-analysis of observational studies
summary:
# Summary

This meta-analysis demonstrates that both early and late menarche are associated with increased infertility risk through distinct biological pathways. Late menarche is particularly linked to primary infertility and reduced fertility rates, primarily through diminished ovarian reserve as evidenced by lower anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) levels and a smaller pool of ovarian follicles. Early menarche, conversely, associates with decreased ovarian reserve and premature menopause, while also increasing the incidence of pelvic inflammatory disease—a condition that directly leads to infertility and adverse pregnancy outcomes. The timing of menarche reflects underlying variations in the ovarian follicular pool and rate of follicular atresia, which fundamentally determine reproductive capacity. Beyond reproductive effects, the analysis reveals that early menarche carries broader health implications including increased risks of breast cancer, cardiovascular disease, and mental health disorders, while late menarche is associated with osteoporosis and bone fractures. These findings underscore that menarche timing serves as an important marker of lifetime reproductive potential and overall health outcomes.

title: Evaluation of Risk Factors and a Gene Panel as a Tool for Unexplained Infertility Diagnosis by Next-Generation Sequencing
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluates next-generation sequencing (NGS) as a diagnostic tool for unexplained infertility, which affects 10–25% of infertility cases despite comprehensive clinical examination. Approximately 50% of infertility cases are attributed to genetic defects, and the research identified pathogenic missense variants in genes including UGT2B7 and CATSPER2 in male patients, and CAPN10, MLH3, HABP2, IRS1, GDF9, and SLC19A1 in female patients (50% of female cohort). However, the study emphasizes that unexplained infertility demonstrates multifactorial etiology involving complex polygenic and environmental factors rather than single-gene causation, with modifiable risk factors including age, BMI, smoking, alcohol consumption, stress, dietary habits, and chemical exposure all contributing to reproductive dysfunction. The findings highlight that while NGS expands diagnostic capabilities, understanding unexplained infertility requires integration of transcriptomic and epigenetic analyses alongside conventional hormonal evaluation (FSH, LH, AMH, progesterone, prolactin) and consideration of immunological and endocrinological mechanisms. The results suggest that unidentified complex genetic interactions and environmental influences play critical roles that warrant future investigation beyond current molecular screening approaches.

title: Association Between Triglyceride–Glucose Related Index and Endometriosis Varies According to Educational Level
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that triglyceride-glucose (TyG) indices and their obesity-related derivatives are associated with endometriosis risk, but this relationship varies significantly by educational level. In the overall population and particularly among individuals with higher educational attainment, elevated TyG, TyG-waist-to-height ratio, TyG-waist circumference, and TyG-body mass index showed positive associations with endometriosis; however, no significant correlation was observed in the low educational level group. The mechanistic basis involves metabolic dysfunction leading to insulin resistance, which reduces insulin sensitivity in endometrial cells and promotes chronic inflammation through increased pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-6, IL-8, and IL-17α, ultimately contributing to endometriosis development. Additionally, glucose and lipid metabolism disorders trigger oxidative stress and endothelial dysfunction, further facilitating the inflammatory cascade. The associations demonstrated both linear relationships (TyG and TyG-waist circumference) and nonlinear relationships (TyG-waist-to-height ratio and TyG-body mass index) with endometriosis risk. These findings highlight the importance of considering socioeconomic factors when evaluating metabolic biomarkers in endometriosis risk assessment and suggest that metabolic management may be particularly relevant for endometriosis prevention in higher educational groups.

title: Multilayer analysis of ethnically diverse blood and urine biomarkers for breast cancer risk and prognosis
summary:
# Summary

This multilayer analysis identified multiple blood and urinary biomarkers associated with breast cancer risk through Mendelian randomization and machine learning approaches in ethnically diverse populations. In European populations, alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and triglycerides (TG) demonstrated protective effects against overall breast cancer risk, while elevated insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1) and high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL-C) increased risk. The associations differed markedly between estrogen receptor-positive (ER+) and estrogen receptor-negative (ER−) tumors: ER+ breast cancer was associated with HDL-C, TG, sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG), and C-reactive protein (CRP), while ER− breast cancer showed novel associations with elevated glucose and urinary markers including creatinine and microalbuminuria. Notably, HDL-C demonstrated contrasting effects across ethnic groups, acting protectively in East Asian populations but as a risk factor in Europeans, highlighting important population-specific variations in biomarker associations. Integration of HDL-C with conventional tumor markers (CEA, CA125, CA153) into machine learning models enhanced diagnostic accuracy for breast cancer detection. These findings support the utility of liquid biopsy techniques using blood and urine biomarkers as complementary diagnostic and prognostic tools alongside conventional imaging and histopathological approaches.

title: Association between composite dietary antioxidant index and rheumatoid arthritis: results from NHANES 2003-2018
summary:
# Summary

Oxidative stress, caused by excessive reactive oxygen species, is a central pathogenic mechanism in rheumatoid arthritis, driving inflammation, cartilage damage, and bone loss through effects on osteocytes, osteoblasts, and chondrocytes. The composite dietary antioxidant index (CDAI), which measures intake of vitamins A, C, and E, zinc, selenium, and carotenoids, shows a strong protective association against RA risk, with higher CDAI levels correlated with lower RA incidence and reduced pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α and IL-1β. RA patients exhibit significantly lower intakes of all measured antioxidants compared to healthy individuals, and increasing dietary antioxidant consumption through natural sources or supplements may help ameliorate RA development and symptoms. Beyond RA, high CDAI is negatively associated with multiple chronic metabolic diseases including hypertension, diabetes, chronic kidney disease, and osteoporosis, while also supporting bone mineral density and reducing cardiovascular mortality risk. These findings suggest that dietary modulation of the redox state through antioxidant-rich intake represents a feasible preventive and therapeutic strategy for rheumatoid arthritis and related metabolic disorders, particularly in older adults and women who bear the greatest disease burden.

title: Gender differences in the relationship between cardiometabolic index and all-cause and specific mortality in the United States adults: a national study
summary:
# Summary

The cardiometabolic index (CMI), a composite measure of visceral obesity and metabolic dysfunction, demonstrates significant gender-specific associations with mortality in United States adults. Elevated CMI is strongly associated with increased all-cause, cardiovascular, and diabetes mortality in the overall population, with particularly pronounced effects in females who show linear associations with all-cause and cardiovascular mortality. In contrast, male participants display weaker or non-significant associations between CMI and mortality outcomes when analyzed categorically, despite showing nonlinear relationships in continuous analyses. CMI emerges as a predictive marker for multiple cardiometabolic and metabolic conditions including insulin resistance, type 2 diabetes, and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, with inflammation partially mediating the CMI-mortality relationship. These findings highlight sex-specific differences in how visceral obesity and cardiometabolic risk translate to mortality risk, with females exhibiting greater susceptibility to CMI-related mortality outcomes than males.

title: Causal relationship between uterine fibroids and cardiovascular disease: A two-sample Mendelian randomization study
summary:
# Summary

This two-sample Mendelian randomization study establishes a causal relationship between uterine fibroids and cardioembolic stroke, with genetic susceptibility to fibroids significantly increasing stroke risk. Uterine fibroids are associated with multiple cardiovascular risk factors including hypertension, obesity, and dyslipidemia, while the primary symptom of heavy menstrual bleeding leads to anemia and contributes to overall disease burden. The metabolic and reproductive complications of fibroids, including impaired fertility and preterm labor, are driven by estrogen and progesterone signaling that promotes leiomyoma cell proliferation. Notably, the association between fibroids and cardioembolic stroke appears mechanistically distinct from other cardiovascular outcomes like coronary heart disease and atrial fibrillation, which show no genetic correlation with fibroids, suggesting a hypercoagulable state related to increased thrombotic markers such as elevated D-dimer and CA-125. Reverse Mendelian randomization analysis confirmed the directionality of the association, ruling out reverse causality from cardiovascular disease to fibroids. These findings highlight that uterine fibroids should be considered a potential cardiovascular risk factor in women, particularly regarding thromboembolic complications.

title: GGN repeat length of the androgen receptor gene is associated with antral follicle count in Chinese women undergoing controlled ovarian stimulation
summary: This study investigated how GGN repeat length polymorphisms in the androgen receptor (AR) gene influence ovarian reserve markers in Chinese women undergoing controlled ovarian stimulation. The researchers found that AR gene GGN repeat length is significantly associated with antral follicle count (AFC), a key marker of ovarian reserve, with the SS genotype showing substantially lower AFC numbers compared to the SL genotype. Notably, while GGN repeat polymorphisms correlated with AFC, they were not associated with ovarian response to stimulation, suggesting distinct genetic mechanisms govern ovarian reserve versus responsiveness to hormone treatment. These findings indicate that AR gene polymorphisms may serve as predictive markers for baseline ovarian reserve in women with infertility or those planning assisted reproduction, though they do not predict stimulation outcomes. The results highlight the importance of androgen receptor genetics in determining follicle pool size, contributing to understanding of how genetic variations affect reproductive capacity in Chinese populations.

title: Self-reported diagnoses of dietary allergens and fecundability in a North American cohort
summary: This study examined whether self-reported food allergies are associated with fecundability (the probability of conception per cycle) in a North American cohort of women attempting pregnancy. While food allergies are atopic diseases involving inappropriate immune responses to harmless dietary antigens, and some evidence suggests atopic disorders may relate to infertility, the researchers found that overall food allergy diagnoses and the total number of allergies were not meaningfully associated with fecundability. However, specific allergens showed opposing directional effects, with egg allergy inversely associated with fecundability while soy allergen showed positive associations, suggesting allergen-specific rather than general immune mechanisms may influence fertility. Additionally, non-monotonic relationships emerged between fecundability and factors including age at first allergy diagnosis and time since last allergic reaction, indicating complex temporal patterns rather than simple linear associations. The findings indicate that self-reported food allergies are not substantially linked to subfertility in this population, though allergen-specific effects warrant further investigation to clarify whether particular immune responses influence reproductive capacity.

title: Preservation of Vascular Endothelial Function in Late-Onset Postmenopausal Women
summary:
# Summary

Women who experience late-onset menopause (age 55+) demonstrate significantly preserved vascular endothelial function compared to those with normal-onset menopause, manifesting as >50% higher flow-mediated brachial artery dilation (FMDBA). This preservation is mechanistically explained by lower circulating mitochondrial reactive oxygen species (mitoROS), which normally suppresses endothelial function through oxidative stress. The favorable metabolic milieu in late-onset women, particularly reduced triglyceride-derived lipid metabolites (especially TG(16:0)), directly contributes to lower mitoROS production and improved endothelial health, independent of traditional cardiovascular risk factors or sex hormone levels. Supporting this mechanism, the mitochondria-targeted antioxidant MitoQ effectively improves FMDBA by suppressing mitoROS, and exposure to serum from normal-onset postmenopausal women induces elevated mitoROS in cultured endothelial cells—an effect prevented by normalizing TG(16:0) concentrations. These findings establish age at menopause as an independent predictor of endothelial function and vascular aging, with clinical significance given that each percentage-unit increase in FMDBA associates with approximately 15% lower future cardiovascular disease risk.

title: Association between female cardiometabolic index and infertility: a population-based study
summary:
# Summary

This population-based study demonstrates a strong association between elevated cardiometabolic index (CMI) in women and couple infertility, with each one-unit increase in CMI conferring a 34% increased risk of infertility. The pathophysiological mechanism involves visceral fat accumulation and dyslipidemia triggering a cascade of metabolic and inflammatory dysfunction: visceral adipose tissue secretes inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6) that promote chronic inflammation, while excess glucocorticoids drive insulin resistance and subsequent ovarian hyperandrogenism that disrupts the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis. Additionally, abnormal lipid metabolism impairs hormonal homeostasis and directly compromises ovarian and uterine function by affecting membrane fluidity and cellular signaling critical for ovulation and embryo development. The study further identifies pelvic infection history as a modifying factor, as the CMI-infertility association was stronger in women without prior pelvic inflammatory disease. These findings establish CMI as a comprehensive metabolic biomarker that integrates central obesity and lipid dysfunction to predict infertility risk in women.

title: Generation of Risk Score for Serious Non-Steroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drug (NSAID) Induced Cardiovascular Events (NAÏVE) Among Active-Duty Service Members and Veterans
summary:
# Summary

This study developed a novel risk stratification tool (NAÏVE score) to identify active-duty service members and veterans at high risk for serious cardiovascular events associated with NSAID use, including myocardial infarction, stroke, and heart failure. The analysis identified multiple independent risk factors strongly associated with NSAID-induced cardiovascular events, with advanced age (particularly 75+ years), previous cardiovascular disease history, and concurrent anticoagulant use showing the strongest associations. Medication-specific factors included NSAID exposure duration and type, with COX-2 selective inhibitors, non-selective NSAIDs, and specific agents like ibuprofen and naproxen demonstrating elevated cardiovascular risk. Additional risk modifiers encompassed demographic factors (male sex, certain racial groups), comorbidities (hypertension, diabetes, chronic kidney disease, cardiomyopathy, cerebrovascular disease), and concomitant medications (aspirin, anticoagulants). Although NSAIDs remain essential first-line analgesic and anti-inflammatory agents due to their established efficacy, this comprehensive risk model enables clinicians to better balance therapeutic benefits against cardiovascular hazards when prescribing NSAIDs in vulnerable populations.

title: Identifying Factors Associated With Breastfeeding Length Among Filipino Migrant Women in South Korea
journal: Journal of Human Nutrition and Dietetics
summary:
# Summary

This study examined breastfeeding duration among Filipino migrant women in South Korea and identified dietary quality and socioeconomic factors as key determinants. Women consuming higher amounts of fruits, vegetables, nuts, and legumes—particularly vegetables—demonstrated significantly longer breastfeeding duration and approximately twofold increased likelihood of breastfeeding for at least 12 months. Mid-range annual income (20-40 million Korean won) was similarly associated with extended breastfeeding, likely through mechanisms including improved maternal well-being, enhanced milk production, and stronger mother-infant bonding. Maternal diet quality directly influences breastmilk composition, with higher fruit and vegetable consumption increasing vitamin A, carotenoids, and human milk oligosaccharides that promote infant digestive, immune, and nervous system development. Importantly, longer breastfeeding duration confers multiple health benefits for both children—including improved cognitive development and educational achievement—and mothers, including reduced risks of diabetes, hypertension, breast cancer, and endometriosis. These findings underscore that dietary quality and income are modifiable factors that could support extended breastfeeding practices among vulnerable migrant populations.

title: Pretreatment triglycerides‐to‐high density lipoprotein cholesterol ratio in postmenopausal women with endometrial cancer
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that the triglycerides-to-high density lipoprotein cholesterol (TG/HDL-c) ratio is significantly elevated in postmenopausal women with endometrial cancer and correlates with advanced tumor stage and type I endometrial cancer. Both elevated triglycerides and reduced HDL-c are components of metabolic syndrome, a known risk factor for endometrial cancer alongside obesity, diabetes, and excessive estrogen exposure. The proposed mechanistic pathway involves increased triglycerides promoting adipose tissue accumulation, which produces aromatase and converts androstenedione to estrogen—a process particularly relevant in postmenopausal women where this adipose-derived estrogen directly stimulates estrogen-dependent endometrial cancer development. Additionally, elevated triglycerides may downregulate PPAR-γ while promoting PPAR-α-mediated upregulation of cell cycle regulators, and low HDL-c reduces protective inhibition of oxidation and inflammation, which further drive cancer development. A TG/HDL-c ratio ≥1.52 emerged as an independent risk factor for endometrial cancer, suggesting this lipid ratio may serve as a clinically useful biomarker for disease progression in postmenopausal endometrial cancer patients.

title: Association Between Mediterranean Diet and Other Healthy Habits and Sociodemographic Variables with the Values of Vascular and Heart Age in Spanish Workers
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how lifestyle factors and sociodemographic variables influence vascular and heart age in Spanish workers, identifying multiple modifiable and non-modifiable risk factors for premature vascular and cardiac aging. Chronological age, male sex, low socioeconomic status, smoking, and regular alcohol consumption were strongly associated with elevated vascular and heart age values, while Mediterranean diet adherence and regular physical activity were protective. The research demonstrates that postmenopausal women experience accelerated vascular aging due to loss of estrogen's endothelial-protective effects—including enhanced nitric oxide bioavailability and reduced oxidative stress—whereas premenopausal women maintain lower vascular age. Mechanistically, exercise and Mediterranean diet components like polyphenols and omega-3 fatty acids reduce arterial stiffness and myocardial fibrosis by promoting nitric oxide production, decreasing inflammation, and mitigating oxidative stress. These findings highlight that individuals at greatest risk for premature vascular and cardiac aging are older males with low socioeconomic status, and that lifestyle modifications—particularly increased physical activity and Mediterranean diet adherence—can significantly attenuate age-related vascular and cardiac dysfunction regardless of demographic background.

title: Polygenic Scores for Dizygotic Twinning: Insights into the Genetic Architecture of Female Fertility
summary:
# Summary

This study develops a polygenic score (PGS) for dizygotic twinning (DZT) to investigate the genetic architecture of female fertility. Natural DZT results from hyper-ovulation and is driven by a relatively small number of genetic variants with larger effect sizes, particularly in genes controlling ovulation rate such as FSHB, GNRH1, and FSHR. The DZT PGS successfully distinguishes mothers who naturally conceived twins from those requiring fertility treatments, with higher scores associated with shorter time to pregnancy and greater lifetime parity. Notably, DZT shows opposing genetic correlations with major fertility disorders: strong negative correlation with anovulatory infertility and PCOS (indicating a genetic spectrum from infertility to hyper-ovulation), but positive correlation with endometriosis. The DZT PGS and associated genetic variants could aid in predicting treatment responses and improving outcomes of medically assisted reproduction while providing insight into the shared genetic mechanisms underlying female fertility phenotypes.

title: The correlation of serum asymmetric dimethylarginine and anti‐Müllerian hormone in primary dysmenorrhea
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals a significant positive correlation between serum asymmetric dimethylarginine (ADMA) and anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels in patients with primary dysmenorrhea, with both biomarkers associated with increased pain severity. Elevated ADMA in primary dysmenorrhea is hypothesized to trigger excess AMH synthesis, potentially through disruption of vascular endothelial function and activation of bone morphogenetic protein signaling pathways. AMH, produced by antral follicles and regulated through Smad-dependent signaling, serves as a marker of ovarian reserve and is inversely affected by estrogen-mediated reduction of ADMA via endothelial nitric oxide synthase activation. The findings suggest that ADMA-related vascular dysfunction and aberrant AMH signaling may contribute to the pathophysiology of dysmenorrhea-associated pain and ovarian reserve changes, with potential implications for understanding the overlap between primary dysmenorrhea and endometriosis-related infertility.

title: Remission or Persistence? A Prediction Tool to Identify Women at Risk for Long-Term Depressive Symptoms Postpartum
summary:
# Summary

Peripartum depression affects approximately 17% of women globally, with 30–50% experiencing prolonged depressive symptoms at 6–12 months postpartum, carrying significant long-term risks for chronic mental health issues and adverse effects on infant development. Multiple risk factors predict persistence of depressive symptoms, including lower education level, multiparity, history of depression, neuropsychiatric diagnoses (particularly ADHD/ADD with lower dopamine levels), pregnancy complications, sleep deprivation, and antenatal anxiety or depressive symptoms. Protective factors against long-term postpartum depressive symptoms include university education and adequate sleep (>6 hours nightly) during pregnancy weeks 17 and 32. The study developed a prediction tool incorporating parity, education, depression severity during pregnancy, and history of depression to identify women at risk for chronic postpartum depression, enabling early intervention for vulnerable populations. These findings underscore the importance of prenatal screening for depressive and anxiety symptoms, sleep quality assessment, and targeted support for multiparous women with lower socioeconomic status or psychiatric comorbidities.

title: Optimizing infertility treatment for ovarian endometrioma: is surgical intervention preferable before or after in-vitro fertilization programs?
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, particularly ovarian endometriomas, affects 30–50% of women seeking fertility treatment and impairs reproduction through multiple mechanisms including pelvic anatomical distortion, inflammation, and generation of reactive oxygen species that damage ovarian tissue and oocyte quality. This study compared two surgical timing strategies for women with endometriomas undergoing in-vitro fertilization: surgery before IVF (OPS-IVF) versus IVF before surgery (IVF-OPS). The IVF-OPS approach yielded superior outcomes, including higher clinical pregnancy rates (50% versus lower in OPS-IVF), greater numbers of injected and fertilized oocytes, and more top-quality embryos at both cleavage and blastocyst stages. These differences stem from the protective effect of postponing surgery: endometrioma resection reduces anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels—a critical marker of ovarian reserve—through removal of healthy ovarian tissue and surgical damage to remaining vasculature, which subsequently impairs the expression of oocyte maturation factors GDF-9 and BMP-15. The findings demonstrate that performing IVF prior to endometrioma surgery preserves ovarian reserve and embryo quality, making it the preferable treatment sequence for infertile women with ovarian endometriosis.

title: Association of serum AMH levels with the number of oocytes retrieved in adolescent and young adult women undergoing controlled ovarian stimulation for fertility preservation
summary: In adolescent and young adult women undergoing controlled ovarian stimulation for fertility preservation, serum anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) emerged as the strongest independent predictor of oocyte retrieval outcomes, including total oocytes retrieved, mature oocytes, and oocytes cryopreserved. AMH, produced by granulosa cells of developing follicles, demonstrated superior predictive value compared to conventional markers such as age and antral follicle count, which showed no significant association with oocyte yield in this population. The study revealed that serum AMH levels remain relatively constant across menstrual cycles in younger women and do not decline with age as they do in older populations, reflecting distinct reproductive physiology in adolescents and young adults. Additionally, the analysis identified gonadotrophin starting dose as significantly correlated with oocyte retrieval outcomes, while conditions impairing ovarian reserve—such as cancer, chemotherapy, pelvic radiation, endometriosis, and Turner syndrome—substantially reduced responsiveness to controlled ovarian stimulation. These findings establish serum AMH as the gold-standard marker for predicting both optimal oocyte yield and poor ovarian response in fertility preservation protocols for younger women, with important implications for individualized treatment planning and risk stratification.
