title: Low morphology does not lower success after intrauterine insemination unless inseminating motile sperm count is low
summary:
# Endometriosis and Male Factor Infertility: The Primacy of Sperm Count Over Morphology

This study examined whether strict sperm morphology alone predicts success in intrauterine insemination (IUI) cycles, finding that morphology is not an independent predictor of pregnancy rates when sufficient total motile sperm counts are present. The key finding is that low morphology (≤4% normal forms) does not reduce pregnancy outcomes when inseminating total motile count (TMC) is 5-20 million or greater, whereas combined low morphology with TMC below 5 million reduces pregnancy rates by approximately 63%. Male factor infertility, affecting up to 40% of couples seeking fertility treatment, is traditionally assessed via semen analysis parameters including strict morphology, yet this analysis reveals that inseminating TMC is the critical confounding variable that explains previously reported associations between morphology and IUI success. The study's significance extends to couples with endometriosis and other diagnostic categories, where conclusions remained unchanged, suggesting that TMC rather than morphology should guide clinical decision-making for IUI candidacy. These findings challenge the historical reliance on strict morphology thresholds (ranging from 4-14%) as standalone predictors and suggest that adequate sperm quantity, not form alone, determines fertilization success in IUI treatment.

title: Plasma proteomic evidence for increased β-amyloid pathology after SARS-CoV-2 infection
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that SARS-CoV-2 infection is associated with increased β-amyloid pathology, a hallmark of Alzheimer's disease, as evidenced by reduced plasma Aβ42:Aβ40 ratios and elevated phosphorylated tau-181 (pTau-181) levels in infected individuals. The magnitude of these changes is substantial, with SARS-CoV-2 infection producing an effect on the Aβ42:Aβ40 ratio equivalent to approximately four years of normal aging. Vulnerability to these proteomic alterations is amplified in older adults and those with pre-existing conditions such as hypertension, while genetic risk factors including APOE-ε4 variant status and lifestyle factors like smoking further increase susceptibility to reduced amyloid ratios. These findings suggest that SARS-CoV-2 may increase future risk of Alzheimer's disease through sustained systemic inflammation and aberrant amyloid metabolism, consistent with earlier observations linking other viral infections to dementia risk. The proteomic evidence indicates that even non-hospitalized COVID-19 cases show measurable brain pathology signatures, though hospitalized patients demonstrate more pronounced alterations, highlighting the dose-dependent nature of viral-induced neurodegeneration risk.

title: Emerging imaging and circulating biomarkers in relation to underlying mechanisms in Bicuspid Aortic Valve aortopathy
journal: International Journal of Cardiology. Heart & Vasculature
summary:
# Summary

Bicuspid aortic valve (BAV) is strongly associated with progressive aortopathy and carries an eight-fold increased risk of aortic dissection and sudden death, conditions that frequently require surgical intervention. Hemodynamic abnormalities play a central role in BAV aortopathy development, including elevated wall shear stress (WSS), reverse flow in the ascending aorta, and increased aortic tortuosity, all of which correlate with aortic growth rates and dilatation. Circulating biomarkers reveal significant pathological changes in BAV patients, including elevated MMP-2 and reduced TIMP-1 (resulting in an increased MMP-2/TIMP-1 ratio), elevated desmosine levels reflecting elastin degradation, and specific microRNAs (miRNA-145, miRNA-17, miRNA-106a) associated with aortic disease progression. Advanced imaging with 4D-flow cardiac magnetic resonance and measurement of WSS angles emerges as a superior predictive tool, with WSS angle demonstrating unique association with aortic growth. These integrated hemodynamic and biomarker findings suggest that BAV aortopathy results from complex interplay between structural valve morphology-induced aberrant flow patterns and downstream activation of matrix-degrading proteases, providing potential targets for monitoring disease progression and therapeutic intervention.

title: Contemporary Diagnostic Work-Up for Male Infertility: Emphasizing Comprehensive Baseline Assessment
journal: The World Journal of Men's Health
summary: This comprehensive review emphasizes the multifactorial nature of male factor infertility, which accounts for approximately 20% of infertility cases and is closely linked to systemic health conditions and environmental exposures. The article identifies metabolic syndrome as significantly associated with abnormal semen parameters, while multiple modifiable risk factors—including obesity, diabetes, hypertension, gonadotoxic medications, illicit drug use, and environmental pollution—contribute to impaired male fertility. Key structural and functional abnormalities such as varicocele (present in 37.3% of infertile men), reduced testicular volume, cryptorchidism, and testicular trauma are strongly associated with male factor infertility, with testicular volume showing positive correlation with testosterone and negative correlation with follicle-stimulating hormone. The analysis reveals that men with male infertility exhibit increased susceptibility to serious comorbidities including type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease, urogenital infections, autoimmune diseases, and elevated cancer risk, establishing infertility as a potential marker for broader systemic dysfunction. Importantly, urogenital tract infections occurring in 15–20% of infertile men lead to defects in sperm function and impaired spermatogenesis, while comprehensive baseline assessment including semen analysis, testicular examination, and hormonal profiling is essential for identifying men at risk of declining semen quality despite initially normal parameters.

title: Associations of Dietary Index for Gut Microbiota and Flavonoid Intake With Female Infertility in the United States
summary: This study demonstrates that dietary patterns supporting healthy gut microbiota composition and adequate flavonoid intake significantly reduce female infertility risk in the United States population. Higher Dietary Index for Gut Microbiota (DI-GM) scores and beneficial gut microbiota profiles were associated with substantially lower infertility odds, with particularly strong protective effects in women under 35 years old, while moderate flavonoid intake showed protective benefits especially in women 35 years and older. The protective mechanisms operate through multiple pathways: flavonoids and dietary components like fiber, fermented dairy, and plant foods (avocado, broccoli, chickpeas, green tea) reduce oxidative stress and reactive oxygen species that compromise ovarian function and oocyte quality, while healthy gut microbiota regulate estrogen metabolism, immune function, and steroid hormone levels. Women with infertility had significantly lower DI-GM scores, total flavonoid intake, and specific flavonoid subclasses (flavan-3-ols and flavanones) compared to fertile controls. These findings suggest that dietary interventions promoting gut microbiota-friendly foods and flavonoid-rich sources may serve as preventive strategies against female infertility and associated reproductive disorders including endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome.

title: The Potential Protective Role of Mitochondrial Haplogroup R in Ovarian Response: An Exploratory Study
summary:
# Summary

This exploratory study reveals that mitochondrial haplogroup R confers protective effects against diminished ovarian response (DOR) by maintaining stable mitochondrial biomarkers across reproductive aging. While reactive oxygen species (ROS), telomere shortening, and declining mitochondrial DNA copy numbers typically increase with age and compromise oocyte quality and developmental competence, women carrying haplogroup R show remarkable resistance to these age-related declines. Specifically, haplogroup R carriers maintain stable ROS levels, telomere length, and mtDNA copy numbers regardless of age, whereas other haplogroups (D, F, M) exhibit progressive deterioration in these parameters. The study establishes that mtDNA polymorphisms serve as biomarkers for both reproductive aging and gynecological pathologies including endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome, with haplogroup R absent entirely in the DOR patient group compared to 10.8% prevalence in normal responders. These findings suggest that mitochondrial genetic variation fundamentally influences the trajectory of ovarian aging through regulation of oxidative stress and telomere maintenance, positioning haplogroup classification as a potential predictor of ovarian reserve preservation over time.

title: Technical and Performance Characteristics Between Different Anti-Mullerian Hormone (AMH) Assay Methods and Antral Follicle Count (AFC) in Malaysian Women with Infertility: A University-Based Centre Cohort
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated the technical performance and clinical utility of two anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) assay methods (AMH Plus and AFIAS-AMH) compared to antral follicle count (AFC) in Malaysian women with infertility. AMH, a transforming growth factor beta family member secreted by granulosa cells, regulates follicle formation and serves as a key marker of ovarian reserve, with levels correlating strongly with AFC. Both assay methods demonstrated excellent concordance with each other and with AFC across all ovarian reserve categories (low, normal, and high), with AFIAS-AMH showing competent repeatability and acceptable linearity for clinical use. The study found that while approximately one in five infertile women show discordance between AMH and AFC levels—a phenomenon influenced by factors including body mass index, vitamin D status, age, and polycystic ovary syndrome—serum AMH remains a vital clinical parameter for predicting ovarian response and guiding IVF medication dosage. Notably, endometriosis was associated with reduced ovarian reserve despite younger age, highlighting the condition's impact on reproductive outcomes independent of age-related ovarian deterioration. These findings support the clinical application of both AMH assay methods as reliable measures for assessing ovarian reserve and predicting IVF cycle outcomes.

title: Hematological Correlations as Predictors of Disease Manifestations in Psychiatric Inpatients
summary:
# Summary

Psychiatric inpatients demonstrate significant hematological abnormalities and nutritional deficiencies compared to healthy controls, with vitamin B12 and vitamin D levels substantially reduced (p = 0.026 and p < 0.001, respectively). These patients exhibit multiple blood parameter alterations including reduced hemoglobin, hematocrit, platelet counts, and mean platelet volume alongside elevated mean corpuscular volume and abnormal leukocyte subtypes, suggesting both anemia risk and immune dysregulation. Nutritional deficiencies, particularly in B vitamins and vitamin D, appear mechanistically linked to psychiatric manifestations through multiple pathways including homocysteine metabolism and platelet dysfunction. Elevated glycosylated hemoglobin and hyperhomocysteinemia show strong associations with depression and cognitive decline, indicating that metabolic dysfunction and nutritional status critically influence psychiatric symptom severity. Western dietary patterns characterized by processed food consumption perpetuate these nutrient deficiencies, which when combined with physical inactivity and stimulant use, increase risk for severe health complications. Supplementation with vitamins B12 and D represents a potentially modifiable intervention to improve psychiatric treatment outcomes and reduce symptom severity in this population.

title: Nomograms based on clinical factors to predict abnormal metabolism of psychotropic drugs
summary:
# Summary

This study developed nomograms to predict abnormal metabolism of psychotropic drugs based on eight key clinical indicators: body mass index (BMI), hypertension, alkaline phosphatase, aspartate aminotransferase, cholinesterase, albumin-to-globulin ratio, urea, and uric acid. Both genetic factors and non-genetic factors including age, sex, weight, smoking status, comorbid disease, and hepatorenal function significantly influence antipsychotic and antidepressant metabolism. Drug-specific analyses revealed that BMI is the most influential predictor for haloperidol and olanzapine metabolism, while different agents showed distinct metabolic associations—for example, sertraline correlated with fasting blood glucose, and mirtazapine/venlafaxine exhibited the broadest metabolic associations including inflammatory markers and lipid profiles. Interindividual differences in drug metabolism account for approximately half of adverse drug reactions, with age-related DNA hypermethylation in drug-metabolizing gene promoters contributing to reduced metabolic capacity in elderly patients. These nomograms and identified risk factors provide practical tools for clinicians to predict abnormal psychotropic drug metabolism and potentially improve therapeutic efficacy and safety through personalized treatment approaches.

title: Systemic inflammation response index mediates the association between relative fat mass and psoriasis risk: a population-based study
summary:
# Summary

This population-based study demonstrates that relative fat mass (RFM) significantly increases psoriasis risk, with each unit increase in RFM associated with a 5% elevation in psoriasis development odds. The systemic inflammation response index (SIRI)—a composite biomarker integrating neutrophil, monocyte, and lymphocyte counts—mediates approximately 5% of this association between RFM and psoriasis risk. The mechanistic pathway involves adipose tissue dysfunction, where elevated RFM promotes secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6) and adipokines (leptin and resistin) while reducing protective adiponectin, collectively amplifying the IL-23/IL-17 immune axis that drives chronic psoriatic inflammation. Psoriasis patients exhibit characteristic metabolic abnormalities including increased RFM, elevated inflammatory markers, and heightened cardiovascular and metabolic comorbidity risk. These findings support systemic inflammation as a shared pathogenic mechanism linking body composition to psoriasis pathogenesis and suggest SIRI as a clinically relevant inflammatory biomarker for stratifying psoriasis risk in individuals with elevated relative fat mass.

title: Association of spontaneous abortion and lifestyle with diabetes mellitus in women: a cross-sectional study in UK Biobank
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study in UK Biobank demonstrates that women with a history of spontaneous abortion face significantly elevated risk of type 2 diabetes mellitus (OR 1.17) and gestational diabetes mellitus (OR 1.38), with risk amplifying substantially for recurrent losses—three or more abortions conferred an OR of 1.33 for type 2 diabetes and 2.01 for gestational diabetes. Endometriosis and other reproductive complications including ectopic pregnancy and early pregnancy hemorrhage show strong associations with recurrent spontaneous abortion, suggesting shared pathophysiological pathways linking reproductive dysfunction to metabolic disease. Lifestyle factors including physical activity, balanced diet, and smoking status significantly modify diabetes risk in both spontaneous abortion groups, with less healthy behaviors amplifying diabetes odds. The study identifies body mass index, younger age, and socioeconomic deprivation (Townsend index) as additional risk factors associated with recurrent pregnancy loss, indicating that metabolic and sociodemographic factors interplay in determining both abortion risk and subsequent diabetes development.

title: Repeated implantation failures and infertility in patients are strongly associated with elevated integrin B3 caused by endometrial copy number variation
summary:
# Summary

Endometrial copy number variations (CNVs) are significantly associated with repeated implantation failure through dysregulation of integrin αvβ3 expression, a critical adhesion molecule required for embryo attachment and endometrial receptivity. CNVs affecting chromosomes 2, 5, 6, 7, 10, 11, 15, 19, and X cause abnormal elevation of integrin β3 protein, which paradoxically reflects a post-receptive endometrial state where the window of optimal receptivity has been exceeded. This elevated integrin β3 is further associated with disturbed hemostasis, including lower platelet counts and increased intrauterine thrombosis risk, alongside histological abnormalities such as irregular glandular morphology and tissue edema. Age emerged as a significant risk factor for CNV development, with older women showing higher CNV occurrence rates due to genomic instability and decreased DNA repair capacity. Combined assessment of CNV detection with integrin β3 expression analysis significantly improves diagnostic accuracy for identifying patients at risk of repeated implantation failure, offering a more comprehensive molecular approach to evaluating endometrial receptivity.

title: B cell translocation gene 2 expression levels in human granulosa cells is negatively associated with in vitro fertilization/intracytoplasmic sperm injection outcomes: a pilot study
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary:
# Summary

This pilot study examined B cell translocation gene 2 (BTG2) expression in granulosa cells and its relationship to assisted reproductive technology outcomes. High BTG2 expression in granulosa cells was negatively associated with multiple markers of fertility success, including oocyte retrieval, fertilization rates, blastocyst formation, and high-quality blastocyst development. Patients stratified into the low BTG2 tertile demonstrated significantly better outcomes across these reproductive parameters, while those with high BTG2 expression showed reduced implantation and clinical pregnancy rates. The detrimental effects of elevated BTG2 were particularly pronounced in women with elevated anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) levels above 4 ng/mL, suggesting that BTG2 expression may serve as a predictive biomarker for poor in vitro fertilization/intracytoplasmic sperm injection outcomes in this subpopulation. These findings indicate that BTG2 expression levels in granulosa cells warrant investigation as a potential indicator of oocyte competence and embryo development potential in fertility treatment.

title: Oocyte maturation defect in women undergoing IVF: contributing factors and effects on mature sibling oocyte outcomes
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates oocyte maturation defects in women undergoing IVF, identifying advanced age, low anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels, and increased follicle-stimulating hormone requirements as key risk factors associated with null maturation rates. Women with poor ovarian response show higher rates of immature oocyte retrieval and require increased gonadotropin stimulation and longer stimulation cycles, though notably the presence of immature oocytes does not compromise the developmental potential of mature sibling oocytes. Importantly, mature oocytes demonstrate comparable fertilization rates, blastocyst formation, aneuploidy profiles, and live birth rates regardless of the proportion of immature oocytes in the same cohort. Encouragingly, patients experiencing null or poor maturation in their initial cycle frequently demonstrate substantially improved maturation rates in subsequent cycles, suggesting biological compensation rather than permanent impairment in oocyte developmental competence.

title: Correlation between cardiometabolic index and psoriasis: a cross-sectional analysis using NHANES data
summary:
# Article Summary

This cross-sectional study of 7,327 American adults demonstrates a significant linear association between the Cardiometabolic Index (CMI)—a composite metric integrating abdominal obesity and serum lipid levels—and psoriasis prevalence. Individuals in the highest CMI quartile exhibited 71% greater psoriasis prevalence compared to those in the lowest quartile, with each unit increase in CMI corresponding to a 4% increase in psoriasis odds. The pathophysiological link involves abdominal visceral adiposity triggering infiltration of activated macrophages and T lymphocytes, which stimulate adipokine and proinflammatory mediator release, leading to chronic low-grade inflammation and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease. These hepatic and systemic inflammatory mediators subsequently activate keratinocyte proliferation and vascular adhesion molecules, exacerbating psoriatic skin inflammation. Subgroup analyses revealed that males, individuals with prior myocardial infarction, and those with lower socioeconomic status demonstrated stronger CMI-psoriasis associations. The findings establish CMI as a valuable metabolic marker for identifying high-risk psoriasis populations and suggest that metabolic dysfunction, particularly dyslipidemia and obesity-related inflammation, represents a critical mechanistic link between cardiometabolic health and psoriasis pathogenesis.

title: School-age outcomes among IVF and ICSI-conceived children: a causal inference analysis using linked population-wide data
summary: This population-wide causal inference study examined school-age developmental outcomes in children conceived through intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI) versus standard IVF using the Australian Early Development Census, which assesses physical health, social competence, emotional maturity, language, cognitive skills, and general knowledge at school entry (ages 4–6). ICSI, which involves direct injection of a spermatozoon into an oocyte, effectively treats severe male factor infertility including oligoasthenoteratozoospermia but is increasingly used in cases of mild-moderate male subfertility and non-male factor causes such as tubal factor, endometriosis, and polycystic ovary syndrome. The study found no causal effect of ICSI on developmental vulnerability or early childhood developmental outcomes compared with standard oocyte co-incubation, despite theoretical concerns about potential epigenetic abnormalities associated with the injection technique itself. Multiple factors influenced childhood developmental outcomes including maternal age, socioeconomic status, parity, paternal education, and child sex, while gestational age, delivery mode, and birthweight were identified as mediators on the causal pathway. These findings suggest that ICSI use in IVF cycles without severe male factor infertility does not increase the risk of developmental vulnerability at school entry, providing reassurance regarding the safety of expanded ICSI indications in assisted reproduction.

title: Elevated log uric acid-to-high-density lipoprotein cholesterol ratio (UHR) as a predictor of increased female infertility risk: insights from the NHANES 2013–2020
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study using NHANES data (2013–2020) identifies the uric acid-to-HDL cholesterol ratio (UHR) as a robust biomarker for female infertility risk, with women having infertility showing significantly elevated log UHR values compared to fertile controls. Elevated uric acid promotes infertility through mechanisms including oxidative stress, endothelial dysfunction, and poor ovarian response, while low HDL-C impairs reproductive hormonal balance, ovarian function, and endometrial receptivity. The association between elevated log UHR and infertility risk is particularly pronounced in younger women under 35 years of age, suggesting this population represents a vulnerable subgroup despite lower chronological age. The UHR reflects underlying metabolic and inflammatory imbalances linked to metabolic syndrome that disrupt energy metabolism required for female fertility, and lowering the UHR may represent a therapeutic target. Age significantly modifies the UHR-infertility relationship, whereas demographic factors such as race, marital status, education, smoking, and BMI do not, indicating the association is independent of these confounders. Early identification and management of elevated UHR in women undergoing fertility treatment could enable timely, targeted interventions to reduce infertility risk.

title: Artificial Intelligence in Assisted Reproductive Technology: A New Era in Fertility Treatment
summary:
# Summary

Artificial intelligence is transforming assisted reproductive technology by enhancing treatment success rates and enabling personalized fertility care across multiple clinical applications. AI systems address the major causes of infertility—including male factor (30% of cases involving sperm quality problems) and female factor (reduced ovarian reserve at 27%, ovulation problems at 14%, and tubal factors)—through diverse interventions including embryo selection, DNA analysis, and personalized ovarian stimulation protocols. Predictive algorithms and machine learning models utilize clinical parameters such as age, BMI, anti-mullerian hormone (AMH), antral follicle count (AFC), and estradiol concentrations to optimize gonadotropin dosing, predict follicle maturation timing, and determine optimal ovulation trigger days, while deep learning networks like CR-Unet standardize follicle diameter measurements and reduce clinician variability. AI-powered approaches enhance sperm assessment through convolutional neural networks that classify morphology and evaluate motility, directly improving intracytoplasmic sperm injection success, and evaluate endometrial thickness and receptivity to predict successful implantation. Machine learning models predict pregnancy achievement and multiple pregnancy probability while identifying euploid embryos and reducing miscarriage risk through preimplantation genetic testing, though careful monitoring remains essential to prevent complications such as ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome from gonadotropin stimulation.

title: Cardiometabolic index as a predictor of gallstone risk: evidence from NHANES 2017–2020
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study from NHANES 2017–2020 demonstrates that the Cardiometabolic Index (CMI), which integrates abdominal obesity and dyslipidemia, is a strong predictor of gallstone risk. Each unit increase in CMI was associated with a 28–90% increased odds of gallstone development even after adjusting for confounders, with a notable threshold effect at CMI = 0.85. Gallstone cases were characterized by elevated triglycerides, fasting glucose, waist circumference, and higher prevalence of diabetes and hypertension compared to controls, with the CMI-gallstone association being particularly pronounced in women. The mechanistic pathway involves abdominal obesity and dyslipidemia triggering chronic inflammation (elevated IL-6 and TNF-α) that impairs gallbladder epithelial function and promotes bile cholesterol supersaturation through both high serum triglyceride levels and reduced HDL-cholesterol-mediated inhibition of cholesterol crystal nucleation. CMI demonstrated superior predictive ability for gallstones compared to the triglyceride-glucose index, positioning it as a clinically useful integrative metabolic marker for identifying individuals at elevated gallstone risk.

title: Novel sonographic reference charts for early pregnancy based on known gestational age
summary: This study developed novel sonographic reference charts for early pregnancy dating using precisely dated assisted reproductive technology (ART) pregnancies, addressing significant limitations of existing curves. The research demonstrated that traditional last menstrual period (LMP) dating and established curves (Robinson/Fleming and Hadlock) systematically overestimate gestational age, with LMP-based estimates up to 4.08 days older than actual age for equivalent crown-rump length measurements. Spontaneous pregnancies exhibited substantially greater variability in growth parameters compared to ART pregnancies, likely reflecting unknown conception dates and biological variation. The new reference charts comprehensively include crown-rump length, gestational sac diameter, yolk sac diameter, and amniotic cavity diameter, all of which are useful for accurate early pregnancy monitoring and detection of complications. These improved charts have important clinical implications for reducing misclassification of post-term deliveries and enhancing pregnancy surveillance accuracy across diverse patient populations, including those with endometriosis and other causes of infertility managed through ART.

title: Preconception cardiometabolic health in patients seeking fertility services: protocol for a single-site, prospective cohort study
summary: This prospective cohort study investigates how preconception cardiometabolic health influences fertility and reproductive outcomes in patients seeking fertility services. Obesity significantly impairs fertility in both women—who are three times more likely to experience infertility—and men, though weight loss serves as an effective non-pharmacological intervention. The study examines how cardiorespiratory fitness and metabolic markers including fasting glucose, lipid profiles, and predicted VO₂ max associate with reproductive success and live birth rates, while also assessing the Metabolic Syndrome Severity Score as a predictor of fertility outcomes. Pregnancy itself imposes substantial cardiovascular demands, increasing resting heart rate by approximately 20% and cardiac output by 30%, making preconception fitness critical for reducing risks of gestational hypertension, pre-eclampsia, and gestational diabetes. Additionally, the research acknowledges psychological factors, as anxiety and stress during the fertility evaluation period negatively impact reproductive outcomes. Overall, the protocol establishes a comprehensive framework for understanding how integrated cardiometabolic and psychosocial health in the preconception period determines fertility success and pregnancy complications.

title: The Role of microRNA in the Prognosis and Diagnosis of Ovarian Cancer
summary:
# Summary

MicroRNAs emerge as promising clinical biomarkers for both the diagnosis and prognosis of ovarian cancer, with distinct expression patterns serving as predictive indicators across diverse patient populations. The miRNA-200 family members (miR-200a, miR-200b, and miR-200c) demonstrate diagnostic utility in tissue and serum samples, though their prognostic significance is complex, with high expression generally associated with shorter overall and disease-free survival. Conversely, several miRNAs including miR-145, miR-23b, and a subset of serous ovarian cancer-specific miRNAs (miR-99b-5p, miR-505-5p, miR-424-3p, and miR-324-5p) correlate with improved survival outcomes. MicroRNA-target gene pairs, particularly those involving hsa-miR-223-3p, hsa-miR-27a-5p, and hsa-miR-486-5p in combination with downstream targets, identify high-risk patient subgroups with significantly worse prognosis. Additionally, specific miRNA signatures comprising multiple markers—such as panels combining miR-205-5p, miR-145-5p, and miR-346—enhance diagnostic discrimination between ovarian cancer patients and healthy controls, positioning miRNAs as valuable non-invasive biomarkers for improved clinical management.

title: Predictive potential of combined secretomics and image-based morphometry as a non-invasive method for selecting implanting embryos
summary: This study demonstrates that combining secretomics and image-based morphometry provides a non-invasive approach for selecting embryos with higher implantation potential. Implanting blastocysts were characterized by elevated levels of Matrilin-2 (MATN2) and legumain in spent culture media, while thymosin beta-10 (TMSB10) levels were reduced, with these protein signatures measured using proximity extension assays. Morphometric parameters including internal circularity, roundness, axis ratio, and trophoblast width were significantly associated with successful implantation. Predictive models integrating MATN2 and TMSB10 protein expression with morphometric shape descriptors effectively discriminated implanting from non-implanting embryos. This combined secretomic and morphometric approach offers a promising non-invasive method for embryo selection in assisted reproductive medicine, potentially improving clinical outcomes in infertility treatment.

title: Survey of Structural Autosomal Abnormalities and Autosomal Variants in Infertile Patients Treated at Some IVF Centers in Vietnam
summary:
# Summary

This Vietnamese infertility survey reveals that chromosomal abnormalities and structural autosomal variations are significant contributors to reproductive failure, accounting for nearly 30% of infertility cases. Males exhibit higher prevalence of chromosomal anomalies (1.932%) compared to females, with inversions, reciprocal translocations, deletions, and duplications being particularly common, and these structural variations directly disrupt spermatogenesis leading to azoospermia and oligospermia. Specific variants, including the inv(9)(p11q13) pericentric inversion on chromosome 9 and quantitative variants like 9qh+ and Yqh+, are associated with decreased sperm quality, lower fertilization rates, and recurrent miscarriages. Balanced translocations and chromosomal inversions can generate unbalanced gametes, which adversely affect pregnancy outcomes and are linked to pregnancy loss in both male and female carriers. The study emphasizes that karyotyping and preimplantation genetic testing are essential diagnostic tools for screening chromosomal abnormalities in infertile couples undergoing assisted reproductive technologies, enabling tailored clinical management strategies.

title: Associations between dietary inflammatory index with menopause and hysterectomy: findings from a national health and nutrition examination survey
summary:
# Summary

This national health and nutrition examination survey study found that a higher energy-adjusted dietary inflammatory index (E-DII)—a measure of diet's inflammatory potential based on 45 food parameters and six inflammatory biomarkers including interleukins and TNF-α—is significantly associated with earlier natural menopause and increased hysterectomy rates. Participants with elevated E-DII scores, indicating pro-inflammatory dietary patterns, experienced menopause and hysterectomy approximately 0.12 years earlier per unit increase in score. These associations are notable because early menopause and hysterectomy are themselves linked to increased cardiovascular mortality, all-cause mortality, and frailty, as estrogen decline triggers bone loss, abdominal fat accumulation, and adverse cardiovascular changes. Conversely, anti-inflammatory dietary patterns characterized by higher intake of green and yellow vegetables and dairy products were associated with later menopause onset, while greater physical activity correlated with delayed menopause and hysterectomy. The authors suggest that maintaining a lower E-DII through anti-inflammatory dietary choices may be a modifiable strategy to prevent serious health outcomes including coronary heart disease, stroke, and premature mortality in women.

title: Patient-specific predictors of successful frozen embryo transfer using the freeze-all protocol: a retrospective observational study
summary: This retrospective study identifies key predictors of success in frozen embryo transfer using the freeze-all protocol for infertile couples. Younger maternal and paternal age significantly improved clinical pregnancy outcomes, with advanced male age particularly detrimental through multiple mechanisms including declined sperm quality, increased DNA damage, and epigenomic alterations that compromise embryo competency. Female ovarian reserve markers—specifically anti-Müllerian hormone levels and antral follicle counts—strongly predicted success, as did higher numbers of retrieved oocytes and metaphase II oocytes, emphasizing the importance of adequate oocyte quantity and quality. The freeze-all approach itself confers substantial benefits by mitigating ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome and enabling optimized programmed endometrial preparation for improved implantation rates. Body mass index and embryo storage duration showed minimal association with outcomes. These findings underscore that successful frozen embryo transfer outcomes depend predominantly on female age and ovarian reserve combined with paternal age-related effects on sperm quality and embryo competency.

title: Association between relative fat mass and female infertility among reproductive-aged women from NHANES 2013–2020
summary: This cross-sectional study demonstrates that relative fat mass (RFM), a novel body composition metric, shows a stronger association with female infertility than traditional measures like BMI or waist circumference in reproductive-aged women from NHANES 2013–2020. Female infertility involves multiple pathways including ovulatory dysfunction and endometriosis, mechanisms through which obesity and elevated body fat impair reproductive health. Excess adipose tissue disrupts the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis, increases peripheral aromatization of androgens to estrogens, elevates leptin levels, and reduces growth hormone and sex hormone-binding globulin, collectively impairing reproductive endocrine function. Additionally, increased body fat drives insulin resistance, chronic inflammation, and abnormal adipokine secretion—all recognized independent risk factors for infertility. The analysis reveals a dose-dependent relationship, with RFM quartile increases associated with progressively elevated infertility risk, and notably stronger associations observed in younger women and those with higher educational attainment. Weight loss during the periconceptional period emerges as a therapeutic intervention that increases conception rates and reduces adverse pregnancy outcomes such as gestational hypertension and preterm birth.

title: UKB-MDRMF: a multi-disease risk and multimorbidity framework based on UK biobank data
summary:
# Summary

UKB-MDRMF is a comprehensive multi-disease prediction framework developed using UK Biobank's multimodal data to predict risk for 1560 diseases with notable success in reproductive health conditions including endometriosis. The framework integrates basic information, lifestyle factors, clinical measurements, imaging, genetic, and environmental variables, with deep learning models (particularly FCNN for disease prediction and DeepSurv for survival analysis) substantially outperforming traditional machine learning approaches. Reproductive and genital diseases demonstrated exceptionally high predictive accuracy with AUC values exceeding 0.95, driven primarily by basic demographic information supplemented by measurement variables and imaging data. Key risk factors promoting disease occurrence include increased waist circumference, body mass index, cholesterol, and urinary microalbumin, while the absence of bipolar disorder or depression reduced disease incidence across all conditions. Joint multi-disease prediction models consistently achieved superior performance compared to single-disease models, and the framework demonstrated superior predictive accuracy compared to existing disease-specific prediction methods across cardiovascular, metabolic, and reproductive diseases. The multimodal integration approach represents a significant advancement in personalized risk stratification for complex disease patterns and multimorbidity.

title: Exploring the Inflammatory Basis of Endometrial Polyps: Clinical Implications of Hematological Biomarkers in a Retrospective Study
summary: Endometrial polyps are common benign uterine lesions strongly associated with abnormal uterine bleeding, infertility, and potential malignant transformation. Beyond traditional hormonal mechanisms involving estrogen and progesterone, this study identifies systemic inflammation as a significant contributor to polyp development, with mean platelet volume (MPV) and platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio (PLR) emerging as independent predictors. Both hematological markers were significantly elevated in endometrial polyp patients compared to controls, suggesting that platelet activation and chronic low-grade inflammatory processes may underlie polyp formation. The findings indicate that chronic endometritis frequently coexists with endometrial polyps and is characterized by CD-138-positive plasma cell infiltration, highlighting a local inflammatory component alongside systemic inflammatory dysregulation. MPV and PLR may serve as non-invasive screening biomarkers to complement hysteroscopy for risk stratification and clinical management. These results suggest that anti-inflammatory therapeutic approaches targeting systemic and local immune dysregulation warrant investigation as adjunctive treatments for endometrial polyps.

title: Methylation Risk Score Modelling in Endometriosis: Evidence for Non-Genetic DNA Methylation Effects in a Case–Control Study
summary:
# Summary

This case-control study develops a methylation risk score (MRS) to predict endometriosis by analyzing DNA methylation patterns in endometrial tissue from 881 women, revealing that non-genetic, environmental factors substantially contribute to disease-associated methylation signatures. The research identified aberrant methylation at multiple loci including HOXA10 and progesterone receptor-B promoters, which are associated with progesterone resistance characteristic of endometriosis, as well as decreased expression of steroidogenic factor-1 and estrogen receptor-beta in endometriotic cells. DNA methylation explained 12.35% of endometriosis variance, while common genetic variants explained 22.38%, with combined models capturing 34.73%—demonstrating that environmental exposures including socioeconomic status, early life environment, pollutants, nutrition, and physical activity significantly influence methylation patterns independent of genetic inheritance. The best-performing MRS, constructed using 746 DNA methylation probes via machine learning approaches (MOA, MOMENT, BLUP), achieved an AUC of 0.68 for case-control classification, comparable to other complex diseases, and when combined with polygenic risk scores showed improved but modest predictive performance (AUC up to 0.73). These findings emphasize that endometriosis-associated DNA methylation signals are substantially shaped by non-genetic factors rather than genetic variants alone, suggesting that environmental and lifestyle interventions targeting epigenetic modifications warrant investigation as potential therapeutic avenues.

title: Potential Association Between Atherogenic Coefficient, Prognostic Nutritional Index, and Various Obesity Indices in Diabetic Nephropathy
summary:
# Summary

Type 2 diabetes mellitus is characterized by multiple metabolic disturbances including insulin resistance, impaired glucose metabolism, and dyslipidemia (elevated total cholesterol, LDL cholesterol, and triglycerides), with central obesity and physical inactivity serving as major risk factors. The atherogenic coefficient, a marker reflecting pro-atherogenic lipid profiles, is significantly elevated in T2DM patients and correlates with systemic inflammation, endothelial dysfunction, and glomerular injury in diabetic nephropathy. Obesity-related indices, particularly the visceral adiposity index, are elevated in T2DM and linked to both cardiovascular and renal complications. T2DM patients exhibit significantly lower prognostic nutritional index (PNI) values, indicating protein malnutrition and compromised immune function, with PNI calculated from lymphocyte count and serum albumin serving as a mortality predictor in chronic kidney disease. Diabetic nephropathy emerges as a major microvascular complication associated with protein malnutrition, low-grade chronic inflammation, and immune modulation, while comorbidities including hypertension, dyslipidemia, and hepatosteatosis are prevalent in over 68% of diabetic patients.

title: Increased risk of young-onset ovarian cancer in women with non-alcoholic fatty liver disease: A nationwide cohort study of 2.3 million women aged 20–39 years
summary:
# Summary

This nationwide cohort study of 2.3 million women aged 20–39 years demonstrates that non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) is associated with significantly increased risk of young-onset ovarian cancer, with risk escalating as NAFLD severity increases from moderate to severe disease. The mechanistic link appears multifactorial: NAFLD promotes chronic low-grade inflammation and oxidative stress, which directly stimulate ovarian carcinogenesis through proinflammatory cytokines and pro-oxidative mediators that drive cell proliferation, angiogenesis, and anti-apoptosis. Additionally, women with NAFLD have elevated systemic estrone and estradiol levels due to increased androgen-to-estrogen conversion, and estrogen exposure is a known ovarian cancer risk factor. The study identifies metabolic dysfunction as a key pathway, as women with young-onset ovarian cancer had higher BMI, triglycerides, fasting glucose, and greater prevalence of diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidemia, obesity, and endometriosis compared to controls. Interventions targeting modifiable risk factors—including weight loss, increased physical activity, and reduced fructose and cholesterol intake—are recommended to mitigate NAFLD progression and potentially reduce ovarian cancer incidence in young women.

title: The Earlier Apoptosis in Human Sperm: Its Correlation with Semen Parameters and Assisted Reproduction Outcome
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that early-stage apoptosis in human spermatozoa significantly impairs fertility outcomes through multiple mechanisms affecting sperm quality and reproductive success. Early apoptosis shows strong negative correlations with critical semen parameters including sperm concentration, membrane integrity, and was strongly associated with elevated sperm mortality rates. Notably, when early apoptotic rates exceeded 15%, patients exhibited substantially reduced fertilization rates, normal fertilization rates, and cumulative clinical pregnancy rates compared to those below this threshold. The Syto16/7-AAD staining method effectively detects apoptosis at earlier stages than conventional annexin V methods, revealing that sperm undergoing early apoptosis display decreased nucleic acid fluorescence due to DNA/RNA structural disorder, which reduces fertilization capacity. While early apoptosis in unprocessed sperm samples showed limited predictive value, testing spermatozoa after density gradient centrifugation significantly improved the ability to predict assisted reproduction outcomes. These findings establish early spermatozoal apoptosis as a key biomarker for male infertility and treatment success in IVF/ICSI procedures.

title: Association between cardiometabolic index and infertility among American women aged 20–45 years: a cross-sectional analysis from 2013–2020 NHANES data
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional analysis of 2013–2020 NHANES data demonstrates a significant association between cardiometabolic index (CMI)—a composite measure incorporating waist circumference, triglycerides, and HDL cholesterol—and infertility in American women aged 20–45 years. Women with elevated CMI exhibited substantially higher infertility risk, with the association remaining robust after adjustment for demographic factors and showing effect modification by age, ethnicity, and income. The mechanistic pathways linking CMI to infertility involve both direct metabolic dysfunction—where obesity drives insulin resistance, hyperinsulinemia, and hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis disruption leading to anovulation—and inflammatory processes wherein dyslipidemia triggers systemic inflammation and endothelial dysfunction that impair ovarian function. CMI's superior discriminatory power for metabolic syndrome, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and depression, combined with its documented association with endometriosis (itself linked to chronic inflammation and oxidative stress), positions it as a holistic biomarker for metabolic health relevant to reproductive outcomes. These findings suggest that CMI may serve as a valuable screening tool for identifying women at elevated infertility risk through the interconnected pathways of metabolic dysfunction and systemic inflammation.

title: Phenome-wide comorbidity network analysis reveals clinical risk patterns in enthesopathy and enthesitis
summary:
# Summary

This phenome-wide association study mapped the complex comorbidity landscape of enthesopathy and enthesitis using network analysis to identify distinct disease endotypes. The analysis revealed that enthesopathy is not a single disease entity but rather encompasses four major clinical trajectories: a metabolic-psychosomatic endotype linking obesity, diabetes, and psychiatric disorders; an inflammatory-multisystem endotype integrating autoimmune musculoskeletal diseases with reproductive tract and gastrointestinal inflammation; a mechanical-injury-driven endotype centered on trauma-related phenotypes including fractures and dislocations; and an aging-intervention-related endotype characterized by bone fragility and physiological frailty. Underlying pathogenic mechanisms include mechanical overload, systemic inflammation, metabolic dysregulation, age-related degeneration, stem cell exhaustion, and impaired healing. The study identified specific disease trajectories, such as metabolic-infectious-musculoskeletal and inflammatory skin-to-joint progressions, suggesting that enthesopathy arises through multiple distinct pathways rather than a single mechanism. Current clinical interventions including corticosteroid injections, physical therapy, and surgery often fail to address underlying disease mechanisms, highlighting the need for endotype-directed therapeutic approaches tailored to individual comorbidity patterns.

title: Association between cardiometabolic index and infertility risk: a cross-sectional analysis of NHANES data (2013–2018)
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional analysis of 1,142 women from NHANES data demonstrates a strong association between elevated cardiometabolic index (CMI) and infertility risk, with infertile participants showing significantly higher CMI values (median: 0.42). The study identifies multiple interconnected metabolic pathways linking obesity to reproductive dysfunction: central obesity triggers insulin resistance and hyperinsulinemia, which impairs ovarian function, while also promoting chronic inflammation and oxidative stress that disrupt menstrual cyclicity and oocyte maturation. Dysregulated lipid metabolism—characterized by elevated LDL-cholesterol, triglycerides, and abnormal lipoprotein ratios—further compromises fertility by reducing conception likelihood by 19–36% per cycle and impairing endometrial receptivity. Critical adipokines (leptin, adiponectin, and resistin) disrupt the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis, which coordinates ovulation, while obesity-induced impairment of stromal decidualization undermines placental development and increases pregnancy complications including miscarriage and preeclampsia. The CMI threshold of 0.341 emerged as a critical cutoff for infertility risk, with this association further modified by lifestyle factors including smoking, alcohol consumption, and hypertension. Encouragingly, weight loss interventions restore menstrual regularity and ovulatory function, offering a modifiable therapeutic target for metabolically-driven infertility.

title: Prediction of grading of ovarian endometrioid carcinoma using conventional MRI features
summary:
# Summary

This study establishes conventional MRI features as reliable predictors of ovarian endometrioid carcinoma (OEC) grading, with significant implications for clinical management and prognosis. Endometriosis is identified as a major risk factor for OEC development, with the malignancy typically originating from endometrial cysts. Grade 1 OEC displays distinctive imaging characteristics including higher T1-weighted signal intensity in cystic components, elevated apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) values in solid components, and notably absent or minimal necrosis, whereas grades 2–3 OEC are characterized by lower cystic signal intensity, reduced ADC values, and prominent necrosis within solid components. These MRI features effectively discriminate grade 1 tumors from higher-grade disease, with grade 1 OEC associated with substantially better clinical outcomes compared to the more aggressive grades 2–3, which share pathological similarities with high-grade serous carcinoma. The findings suggest that MRI-based grading could guide surgical management decisions, particularly regarding staging lymphadenectomy in grade 2 OEC patients, where the procedure correlates with improved disease-free and overall survival.

title: Predictors of Nephrostomy Catheter Dislodgement: Insights from a Retrospective Analysis
summary:
# Article Summary

This retrospective analysis identifies anatomical predictors of nephrostomy catheter dislodgement (NCD), a complication affecting 52–79% of catheter malfunction cases. The study demonstrates that cortex-to-skin distance is the primary anatomical risk factor for NCD, with shorter distances significantly associated with dislodgement (p = 0.001); an optimal threshold of 46.65 mm identified by receiver operating characteristic analysis achieved 92.1% sensitivity and specificity. Shorter cortex-to-skin distances reduce stabilizing forces that maintain catheter positioning, whereas other anatomical parameters including subcutaneous fat thickness, renal parenchymal thickness, and various muscle measurements showed no significant association with dislodgement. NCD complications include increased risks of infection and bleeding, necessitating additional clinical interventions. The authors recommend preventive strategies for high-risk patients with shorter cortex-to-skin distances, including regular catheter checks, self-locking catheters, securement devices, hub sutures, and timely replacements to reduce infection and bleeding complications associated with dislodgement.

title: Serum Progesterone Levels on the Day of Oocyte Retrieval as a Predictor of Pregnancy Outcomes in Fresh Embryo Transfer Cycles
summary: This study demonstrates that serum progesterone levels on the day of oocyte retrieval serve as an important predictor of pregnancy outcomes in fresh embryo transfer cycles, with critical thresholds for both chemical and clinical pregnancy success. Progesterone is essential for endometrial receptivity and implantation, and elevated levels on retrieval day—rather than trigger day—correlate significantly with improved pregnancy rates, with cutoff values of 7.1 ng/ml predicting chemical pregnancy (76.2% sensitivity) and 7.55 ng/ml predicting clinical pregnancy. Beyond absolute progesterone concentrations, the magnitude of progesterone increase between trigger day and retrieval day emerges as an equally important predictor, with slower increases associated with unsuccessful outcomes despite absence of declining levels. Interestingly, the timing of progesterone evaluation proves crucial, as progesterone levels on the trigger day showed no significant association with pregnancy outcomes, whereas retrieval day levels demonstrated strong predictive value. Additional factors including lower BMI and higher serum LH levels on cycle day 2 were associated with positive β-hCG outcomes, highlighting the multifactorial nature of implantation success. These findings suggest that maintaining progesterone levels above an optimal threshold during fresh cycles, coupled with appropriate progesterone dynamics, is essential for supporting endometrial synchronization and maximizing pregnancy potential in assisted reproductive technology.

title: Association between the dietary index for gut microbiota and female infertility: a cross-sectional study of NHANES 2013–2018
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study of NHANES data (2013–2018) demonstrates a significant protective association between the Dietary Index for Gut Microbiota (DI-GM) and female infertility risk. Participants with higher DI-GM scores exhibited substantially lower infertility risk, with each unit increase below a threshold of eight reducing risk by 14%, while improved dietary patterns were associated with favorable metabolic profiles including lower triglycerides, lower fasting glucose, and higher HDL cholesterol. The findings underscore the mechanistic link between diet-mediated gut microbiota composition and reproductive health, as dysbiotic microbiota impairs nutrient absorption and promotes immune dysregulation through reduced interleukin-22 secretion—a pathway implicated in polycystic ovary syndrome and endometriosis pathogenesis. Specifically, pathogenic bacteria such as Bacteroides vulgatus and fungi like Aspergillus tubingensis contribute to infertility by compromising the AhR-IL-22 signaling axis and increasing insulin resistance. These results suggest that dietary interventions promoting gut microbiota diversity and healthy composition may represent a modifiable strategy to reduce female infertility risk and improve metabolic health outcomes.

title: The importance of multimodal diagnostic methods for therapeutic decision making for overactive bladder in women
summary:
# Summary

Overactive bladder (OAB) in women requires comprehensive multimodal diagnostic evaluation to differentiate it from related urological and systemic conditions and guide appropriate therapeutic decisions. The article identifies OAB as strongly associated with diverse underlying pathologies including neurogenic bladder, pelvic organ dysfunction, neurological disorders (stroke, multiple sclerosis, spinal cord injury, Parkinson's disease), and metabolic conditions (obesity, metabolic syndrome, diabetes mellitus). Urgency and daytime frequency serve as the primary driving symptoms of OAB, though clinicians must distinguish OAB from conditions presenting with pelvic pain or dyspareunia—such as interstitial cystitis, pelvic floor tension myalgia, and endometriosis—which point toward alternative diagnoses. Diagnostic assessment requires layered investigation including urinalysis to detect urinary tract infection, post-void residual volume measurement to exclude overflow incontinence, urodynamic testing to confirm detrusor overactivity, and cystourethroscopy when red flag symptoms such as hematuria, recurrent infections, or painful bladder syndrome are present. The article emphasizes that this systematic multimodal approach is essential for accurate diagnosis and effective therapeutic decision-making in women presenting with suspected OAB.

title: Association between lipid accumulation product and endometriosis: A cross-sectional study from NHANES 1999–2006
summary: This cross-sectional study of NHANES data (1999–2006) demonstrates a significant positive association between lipid accumulation product (LAP), a composite metabolic marker derived from triglycerides and waist circumference, and endometriosis prevalence. Women in the highest quartile of LAP had a 93% increased risk of endometriosis compared to those in the lowest quartile, an association that persisted after adjustment for confounding variables. The relationship between LAP and endometriosis was particularly pronounced in women over 35 years old and was accompanied by elevated glycohemoglobin levels and dysregulated glucose and lipid metabolism in those with endometriosis. These findings underscore the role of metabolic dysfunction—particularly insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, and glucose metabolism disorders—in endometriosis pathogenesis and suggest LAP may serve as a useful metabolic marker for identifying women at risk. Since endometriosis contributes to infertility, severe pain, and irregular menstrual cycles, understanding its metabolic underpinnings could inform both diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.

title: Risk factors for breast cancer: an umbrella review of observational cohort studies and causal relationship analysis
summary:
# Summary

This umbrella review identified multiple modifiable and non-modifiable risk factors for breast cancer through comprehensive analysis of observational cohort studies and Mendelian randomization. Established imaging and genetic factors such as increased breast density and BRCA mutations strongly elevate breast cancer incidence, as do metabolic and inflammatory conditions including atrial fibrillation, elevated insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1) and serum triglycerides, and higher weight gain. Conversely, protective factors include protective dietary patterns emphasizing vegetables, fruits, and soy, increased fruit and cheese intake, higher parity, and elevated sex hormone-binding globulin levels. The mechanistic pathways identified involve obesity-related adipokine dysregulation—specifically reduced adiponectin and elevated leptin and IGF-1—which enhance insulin signaling and promote tumor growth, while cardiac glycosides (particularly digoxin) may increase breast cancer risk through estrogen receptor binding. Causal relationship analyses confirmed that BMI, fruit intake, calcium channel blockers, cheese intake, and circulating IGF-1 levels have direct causal associations with breast cancer risk, highlighting opportunities for lifestyle interventions and pharmacological considerations in breast cancer prevention.

title: Risk factors for persistent infection of high-risk HPV in patients with cervical intraepithelial neoplasia
summary: This study identified five independent risk factors for persistent high-risk HPV (HR-HPV) infection and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia (CIN) in a cohort of affected patients. Advanced age (≥50 years), multiple concurrent HPV infections, elevated viral load (≥100), reproductive tract inflammation, and thyroid dysfunction were all strongly associated with persistent HR-HPV infection as determined by multivariate logistic regression analysis. These same factors showed similarly high associations with the development and progression of cervical intraepithelial neoplasia. The findings suggest that systemic factors including thyroid dysfunction and local reproductive tract inflammation, in addition to viral characteristics such as multiplicity and load, play important roles in determining whether HPV infection persists and progresses to precancerous lesions. The identification of these modifiable and measurable risk factors may help stratify patients at higher risk for persistent infection and CIN progression, potentially guiding more intensive surveillance and treatment strategies in vulnerable populations.

title: Study on the relationship between the non-HDL/HDL cholesterol ratio (NHHR) and endometriosis: a cross-sectional analysis utilizing the NHANES dataset
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional analysis using NHANES data reveals a significant association between elevated non-HDL/HDL cholesterol ratio (NHHR) and endometriosis risk in the U.S. population, with each one-point increase in NHHR corresponding to a 17% increased risk of developing endometriosis. The underlying pathophysiology involves multiple interconnected mechanisms, including inflammatory responses, oxidative stress, immune dysfunction, and altered lipid metabolism, which are also implicated in atherosclerosis pathogenesis. Endometriosis manifests clinically through menstrual cramps, persistent pelvic discomfort, and impaired fertility, with affected women exhibiting significantly higher total cholesterol and lower high-density lipoprotein cholesterol compared to unaffected controls. The association between NHHR and endometriosis was robust across demographic subgroups and remained largely independent of race, education, and marital status, though it was particularly pronounced in individuals with alcohol consumption. These findings suggest that NHHR could serve as a non-invasive biomarker for early prediction and diagnosis of endometriosis, offering an alternative to current diagnostic approaches such as ultrasound and laparoscopic-guided biopsy that are limited by sensitivity constraints or diagnostic delays.

title: Prevalence of urinary incontinence among nulliparous women and its association with underweight body mass index: a secondary analysis of a nationwide cross-sectional study in China
summary: This cross-sectional study of Chinese nulliparous women identifies urinary incontinence (UI) as a prevalent condition with multiple metabolic and behavioral risk factors, notably including underweight body mass index alongside traditional risk factors. Chronic constipation demonstrates the strongest association with overall UI occurrence, while specific UI subtypes show distinct risk profiles: stress incontinence associates with chronic coughing and overweight/obesity status, whereas mixed incontinence predominantly associates with postmenopausal status. The mechanistic pathway linking underweight BMI to UI appears to involve sarcopenia and reduced muscle mass, particularly decreased hand grip strength, which compromises pelvic floor support. Protective factors include unmarried and employed status, suggesting socioeconomic and lifestyle influences on disease expression. The authors propose that obesity increases abdominal pressure chronically, while poor habits such as straining during defecation and reduced physical capability contribute to pelvic floor weakness, collectively explaining UI's significant negative impact on mental health, recreation, and social participation among nulliparous women.

title: Ultrasound evaluation of changes in endometrial receptivity from the day of ovulation to the day of transfer in patients with a history of intrauterine adhesions: a prospective cohort study
journal: Quantitative Imaging in Medicine and Surgery
summary:
# Summary

Intrauterine adhesions (IUAs) cause infertility and pregnancy complications through endometrial fibrosis and impaired receptivity, which can persist even after standard hysteroscopic adhesiolysis treatment. This prospective ultrasound study tracked changes in endometrial receptivity markers from ovulation to embryo transfer day in patients with prior IUAs, finding that successful pregnancy was associated with distinctive ultrasound patterns. Pregnant patients showed higher endometrial and subendometrial volume with increased blood flow indicators at ovulation, plus greater endometrial peristalsis frequency and type B morphology (secretory appearance) at transfer, suggesting optimal endometrial preparation. Conversely, reduced endometrial peristalsis and elevated blood flow indices in nonpregnant patients indicated impaired receptivity. The severity of IUAs influenced these patterns, with moderate-to-severe adhesions showing compensatory increases in thickness and vascularization, while subendometrial flow indices emerged as key predictive factors for pregnancy outcome. These findings suggest that endometrial compaction, morphologic changes, and peristaltic activity are critical determinants of implantation success in adhesion patients.

title: Global, regional, and national burden of female infertility and trends from 1990 to 2021 with projections to 2050 based on the GBD 2021 analysis
summary:
# Summary

This global burden of disease study demonstrates that female infertility affects millions worldwide, with prevalence rising substantially from 1990 to 2021, particularly among women aged 25-39 years. The primary causes of infertility include fallopian tube obstruction, ovulatory disorders from diminished ovarian reserve, and endocrine disruptions, with genetic factors (chromosomal anomalies, gene mutations, and polymorphisms) contributing to 5-10% of cases. Environmental influences, endocrine dysfunctions, and hormonal imbalances collectively explain a substantial portion of infertility burden, while associated conditions including endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, and delayed childbearing significantly impact disease prevalence. Geographic and sociodemographic disparities were pronounced, with the Middle SDI region and East Asia experiencing the highest burden, while endometriosis-related complications are linked to elevated risks of melanoma, autoimmune disorders, asthma, allergic diseases, cardiovascular disease, and ovarian cancer. The study projects continued growth in infertility burden through 2050, underscoring the need for expanded access to assisted reproductive technologies and preventive interventions across diverse healthcare systems.

title: Effect of aging on semen and embryonic developmental scores in assisted reproductive technology
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how maternal and paternal aging affect semen quality, embryonic development, and assisted reproductive technology outcomes. Maternal aging substantially impairs oocyte quality and embryonic development, reducing Gardner criteria scores and iDAScore evaluations while extending time to blastocyst stage and decreasing gestation rates, particularly in women over 40 years old. The mechanism underlying maternal aging's negative effects involves increased chromosomal abnormalities, spindle dysplasia, and reduced mitochondrial quality in oocytes, which collectively lead to slower embryonic development and lower clinical pregnancy rates. In contrast, paternal aging significantly degrades semen parameters—including volume, sperm count, motility, and morphology—yet has minimal impact on embryonic developmental kinetics, embryo quality scores, or gestation rates, suggesting that sperm quality declines do not directly translate to embryonic defects. However, paternal aging does reduce pregnancy rates and increase miscarriage risk, and molecular evidence from aged male mice demonstrates impaired mitochondrial DNA copy number and gene expression in resulting blastocysts. Overall, this research demonstrates asymmetric effects of parental aging in assisted reproduction, with maternal age profoundly compromising embryonic developmental capacity while paternal age primarily affects gamete quality without direct effects on embryonic morphokinetics.

title: An assessment of total antioxidant and oxidant parameters and their correlation with embryo quality in in-vitro fertilization patients
journal: Revista Brasileira de Ginecologia e Obstetrícia
summary: This study demonstrates that oxidative stress markers significantly influence embryo quality and IVF success rates in infertile patients. Higher Total Antioxidant Status (TAS) levels in both serum and follicular fluid were positively correlated with improved embryo grade and successful pregnancy achievement, indicating that robust antioxidant defense mechanisms protect against oxidative damage during oocyte maturation and early embryonic development. Conversely, elevated Total Oxidant Status (TOS) and Oxidative Stress Index (OSI) values in follicular fluid were associated with poor embryo quality and unexplained infertility, suggesting that excessive reactive oxygen species production impairs cellular function through DNA damage, lipid peroxidation, and membrane dysfunction. The study confirms that follicular fluid composition—produced by granulosa cells surrounding developing oocytes—serves as a critical biomarker for predicting oocyte and embryo quality non-invasively. These findings underscore the importance of oxidative-antioxidative balance in female reproductive physiology and suggest that measuring oxidative stress parameters may help identify infertile patients at higher risk of poor treatment outcomes, potentially informing more personalized assisted reproductive technology strategies.

title: Correlation of the sperm DNA fragmentation index with semen parameters and its impact on fresh embryo transfer outcomes—a retrospective study
summary: This retrospective study examined the relationship between sperm DNA fragmentation index (DFI) and semen parameters in couples undergoing fresh embryo transfer. The researchers found that elevated sperm DFI shows strong negative correlations with key semen quality metrics, including sperm motility, forward movement, and normal morphology, though DFI is not associated with male age, BMI, semen volume, or sperm concentration. Despite these semen parameter differences, the study revealed no significant differences in clinical outcomes between patients with normal versus elevated DFI, including fertilization rates, embryo quality, pregnancy rates, delivery rates, miscarriage rates, or infant birth weight. The lack of impact on fresh IVF outcomes likely reflects the protective effects of selecting high-quality embryos for transfer and sperm optimization procedures that preferentially eliminate DNA-damaged sperm. These findings suggest that while sperm DFI correlates with reduced sperm quality parameters, it does not compromise fresh embryo transfer outcomes in tubal-factor infertility cases, possibly because early embryonic development is primarily controlled by maternal genes with only partial reliance on paternal genetic integrity.

title: Potential of seven insulin resistance indicators as biomarkers to predict infertility risk in U.S. women of reproductive age: a cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study evaluated seven insulin resistance indicators as biomarkers for predicting infertility risk in reproductive-age U.S. women. Insulin resistance is a key metabolic mechanism underlying infertility, obesity, metabolic syndrome, and polycystic ovary syndrome, with approximately 40-60% of women with PCOS exhibiting insulin resistance that compromises fertility and in vitro fertilization outcomes. Among the tested indicators, METS-IR, TyG-BMI, TyG-WC, and TyG-WHtR showed significant positive associations with infertility risk, with METS-IR demonstrating the strongest predictive performance (AUC: 0.619), followed by TyG-BMI (AUC: 0.617), TyG-WC (AUC: 0.611), and TyG-WHtR (AUC: 0.610), while TyG, HOMA-IR, and TG/HDL showed minimal predictive utility. The associations exhibited distinct patterns: METS-IR and TyG-BMI demonstrated nonlinear relationships with infertility risk with critical thresholds (METS-IR at 49.92 and TyG-BMI at 290.18), whereas TyG-WC and TyG-WHtR showed linear positive correlations. These metabolic indicators offer practical tools for identifying women at elevated infertility risk and may guide targeted metabolic interventions to improve reproductive outcomes.

title: Non-targeted metabolomic analysis of follicular fluid in infertile individuals with poor ovarian response
summary: Poor ovarian response (POR), characterized by inadequate ovarian response to gonadotropin stimulation, represents a primary cause of failure in assisted reproductive technology treatments, resulting in fewer developing follicles, lower estrogen peaks, high cycle cancellation rates, and diminished oocyte retrieval. Non-targeted metabolomic analysis of follicular fluid revealed distinct metabolic signatures in POR patients, with upregulated metabolites including l-lactic acid, l-tyrosine, perillyl aldehyde, and phosphatidylcholine, alongside downregulated metabolites such as estrone-3-sulfate, serine, lysolecithin, and phosphatidylinositol. These differential metabolites predominantly participate in glycerophospholipid metabolism, choline metabolism, and autophagy pathways. Multiple biomarkers demonstrated significant correlations with markers of ovarian reserve and oocyte quality: upregulated compounds (methylisoeugenol, yangonin, tryptamine, and perillyl aldehyde) correlated positively with AMH levels and oocyte retrieval numbers, while downregulated metabolites (estrone-3-sulfate and 4-pyridoxic acid) showed positive correlations with these same reproductive parameters. POR patients displayed characteristic hormonal and cellular abnormalities including elevated basal follicle-stimulating hormone, reduced AMH levels and antral follicle count, and decreased metaphase II oocyte numbers, providing a comprehensive metabolomic signature that could serve as a diagnostic and prognostic tool for identifying patients at risk of poor ovarian response.

title: Association between cardiometabolic index and cardiovascular disease: evidence From the NHANES 2007–2018
summary:
# Summary

The cardiometabolic index (CMI), which combines waist-to-height ratio and triglyceride-to-HDL cholesterol ratio, emerged as a robust predictor of cardiovascular disease risk in this large cross-sectional study of NHANES data from 2007–2018. Each unit increase in log-transformed CMI was associated with a 15% higher odds of CVD prevalence, with individuals in the highest CMI quartile demonstrating substantially elevated CVD risk compared to the lowest quartile. Higher CMI values correlated strongly with adverse metabolic profiles including elevated body mass index, waist circumference, triglycerides, serum uric acid, and HbA1c, alongside reduced HDL cholesterol and vitamin D levels. The mechanism linking elevated CMI to CVD involves visceral adipose tissue accumulation, which releases pro-inflammatory cytokines (MCP-1, IL-6, TNF-α) that establish chronic low-grade inflammation and promote atherosclerotic progression through increased leptin signaling, monocyte recruitment, and reactive oxygen species generation that impairs nitric oxide-mediated vasodilation. CMI demonstrated associations with multiple cardiometabolic conditions including hypertension, diabetes, obesity, ischemic stroke, and notably endometriosis, suggesting its utility as a comprehensive metabolic health evaluation tool superior to traditional obesity indices alone.

title: Epidemiology and occupational risk factors of male infertility based on 3,025 patients in Eastern Morocco during 2021-2023: a cohort study
summary: This cohort study of 3,025 infertile males in Eastern Morocco identified multiple risk factors and semen abnormalities contributing to male infertility. Abnormal semen characteristics were present in 78.71% of cases, with oligozoospermia (29.07%), asthenozoospermia (24.68%), and azoospermia (17.56%) being the most common findings, while oligo-astheno-necrospermia represented the most frequent combined abnormality. Occupational exposures—particularly high temperatures and prolonged sitting causing elevated scrotal temperature—emerged as leading risk factors, alongside modifiable lifestyle factors including smoking, alcohol consumption, obesity, and increased body weight, all of which deteriorate sperm quality through mechanisms involving oxidative stress, hormone disruption, and DNA fragmentation. Medical conditions such as varicocele (which causes oligospermia), diabetes, and hypertension showed significant associations with male infertility, with varicocele ligation demonstrating improvement in sperm characteristics and pregnancy rates.
