summary:
# Summary

This study investigated how two common FSHR gene polymorphisms (rs6165 and rs6166) affect ovarian response to stimulation in infertile women with diminished ovarian reserve. Both polymorphisms, located in the coding region of FSHR on chromosome 2, were strongly associated with reduced oocyte retrieval and ovarian responsiveness. Women carrying G alleles in either rs6165 or rs6166 had significantly fewer retrieved oocytes, lower follicular output rates (FORT), and lower follicular oocyte indices (FOI) compared to those with AA genotypes, with the AA genotype consistently showing superior ovarian stimulation outcomes. The molecular basis involves amino acid substitutions that potentially disrupt FSH binding affinity to its receptor on granulosa cells, thereby impairing the downstream signaling cascade essential for follicle maturation and steroidogenesis. Notably, the GG genotypes in both polymorphisms were strongly linked, and their effects on ovarian response were independent of baseline ovarian reserve markers such as anti-müllerian hormone and antral follicle count. These findings suggest that FSHR polymorphisms represent independent genetic factors predicting poor ovarian response to controlled stimulation, with potential clinical implications for personalizing IVF treatment protocols in women with diminished ovarian reserve.

title: Association between serum carotenoid levels and gallstones in adults: a cross-sectional study using the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study of U.S. adults demonstrates a strong inverse association between serum carotenoid levels and gallstone prevalence, with individuals in the highest carotenoid quartiles experiencing 30-54% lower gallstone risk compared to the lowest quartile. All major carotenoid types—including lycopene, β-carotene, α-carotene, lutein/zeaxanthin, and cryptoxanthins—independently reduced gallstone risk, with lycopene contributing the largest protective effect (37.86% of the total association). The protective mechanism involves carotenoids' antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which counteract gallstone pathogenesis through oxidative stress and macrophage-driven inflammation that otherwise promotes bile cholesterol supersaturation. Body mass index mediated approximately 17% of the carotenoid-gallstone association, while inflammatory markers (neutrophil count and C-reactive protein) accounted for additional indirect protective effects. The association was particularly pronounced in women and participants over 40 years of age, suggesting carotenoid intake as a modifiable dietary factor with potential preventive implications for gallstone disease.

title: Small RNA in sperm–Paternal contributions to human embryo development
summary: This study demonstrates that sperm deliver diverse small RNAs (sRNAs) beyond the paternal genome that serve as critical biomarkers and regulators of embryo development and fertility outcomes. Sperm-borne microRNAs, particularly hsa-let-7g, hsa-miR-30d, and hsa-miR-320b/a, positively correlate with high-quality embryo production and are predicted to regulate genes essential for embryogenesis, while ribosomal sRNAs inversely correlate with embryo quality. Mitochondrial-derived sRNAs (mitosRNA), including MT-TS1-Ser1, strongly associate with sperm concentration and quality, and can program embryonic metabolism, with their levels modifiable through dietary intervention. Conversely, piRNAs and tRNA-derived fragments associate with reduced fertilization rates, while specific ribosomal RNAs (28S, 5S, 5.8S, 12S) predict lower embryo quality. These findings establish sperm-borne sRNAs as biomarkers for male reproductive health and fertility success in IVF, highlighting the paternal contribution to embryonic development beyond genetic material alone and opening new avenues for reproductive medicine.

title: Association between serum Vitamin D levels and sperm DNA fragmentation index in varicocele patients: a retrospective cohort study
summary: This retrospective cohort study demonstrates a significant inverse association between serum vitamin D levels and sperm DNA fragmentation index (DFI) in varicocele patients, with a 10-unit increase in vitamin D corresponding to a 2.4% reduction in DFI. Varicocele, present in approximately 35% of men undergoing fertility assessment, induces oxidative stress through elevated testicular temperature and impaired venous drainage, conditions that vitamin D may mitigate through antioxidant protection via vitamin D receptor-mediated signaling in the reproductive system. A critical threshold was identified at 25.9 nmol/L, below which the protective effect of vitamin D on sperm DNA integrity becomes markedly pronounced, suggesting vitamin D deficiency substantially compromises male reproductive function. While conventional semen parameters, partner pregnancy rates, embryo arrest rates, and congenital abnormality incidence showed no significant associations with vitamin D status, the specific benefit to sperm DNA integrity independent of age, BMI, smoking, and alcohol consumption highlights a targeted mechanism of action. These findings suggest that vitamin D may play an important role in maintaining sperm DNA integrity in varicocele patients and could represent a modifiable factor to improve male reproductive outcomes in this population.

title: Logistic regression analysis of pregnancy outcome and EFI score of infertile patients with ovarian endometriosis cyst after staged oral administration of traditional Chinese medicine
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian endometriosis represents a significant cause of infertility in reproductive-aged women, affecting 30-50% of endometriosis patients. While laparoscopic surgery effectively removes ovarian endometriotic cysts, it carries the risk of impairing ovarian function, necessitating complementary treatments to optimize fertility outcomes. This study demonstrates that staged oral administration of traditional Chinese medicine improves pregnancy outcomes by enhancing endometrial receptivity and ovarian function. The Endometriosis Fertility Index (EFI) score emerged as a valuable predictive tool, with a score threshold of 7 effectively stratifying pregnancy risk; multiple factors including advanced age, secondary infertility, prolonged infertility duration (≥3 years), advanced disease stage, and larger cyst diameter (≥3 cm) were independently associated with reduced pregnancy success. The therapeutic mechanisms of key herbal components—including tanshinone inhibition of VEGF and MMP-2 to reduce angiogenesis, ligustrazine suppression of platelet aggregation and PDGF expression to limit fibrosis, diosgenin modulation of estrogen receptors to suppress endometrial growth, and flavonoid inhibition of TGF-β/Smad signaling—collectively target the pathophysiological mechanisms underlying endometriosis-associated infertility.

title: Using Life’s Essential 8 and heavy metal exposure to determine infertility risk in American women: a machine learning prediction model based on the SHAP method
summary: This machine learning study developed a predictive model for female infertility risk in American women by analyzing Life's Essential 8 (LE8)—a comprehensive cardiovascular health metric—alongside heavy metal exposure. The research identified multiple high-impact contributors to infertility risk, including specific heavy metals (cadmium, cesium, molybdenum, antimony, and tin), elevated BMI, poor diet quality, and lifestyle factors such as smoking and sedentary behavior. Notably, the study found that heavy metals and LE8 interact synergistically to influence infertility risk, with heavy metal exposure driving oxidative stress that impairs reproductive function, while poor LE8 scores and elevated BMI negatively predict fertility outcomes. The model highlights that metabolic dysfunction—manifested through obesity, polycystic ovary syndrome, abnormal lipid metabolism, and poor glycemic control—represents a major pathway linking environmental and lifestyle factors to infertility. Protective interventions including physical activity, modest weight reduction (2.5–5%), low-glycemic and Mediterranean diets, and improved sleep quality demonstrated significant improvements in reproductive health markers and fertility outcomes.

title: Assessment and prediction models for the quantitative and qualitative reserve of the ovary using machine learning
summary:
# Summary

Age-related decline in fertility results from progressive reduction of ovarian reserve, characterized by decreased number and quality of oocytes, which directly impacts fertilization competence and embryo development. Anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) serves as the strongest predictor of ovarian reserve quantity, while oocyte quality assessment requires multiple biomarkers including morphology, follicular fluid content, and granulosa cell gene expression, with female age being the primary clinical predictor available. Machine learning models, particularly Random Forest Classifiers incorporating AMH, FSH, age, and reproductive history, achieved high predictive accuracy for both quantitative reserve (AUC 0.91) and qualitative reserve (AUC 0.80), outperforming conventional assessment methods. Additional biomarkers including growth differentiation factor-9 (GDF9), bone morphogenetic protein-15 (BMP15), oxidative stress markers, and zinc were identified as significant contributors to oocyte quality prediction. The study population included patients with endometriosis (20.6%) and polycystic ovary syndrome (7.7%), conditions known to affect reproductive outcomes. Implementation of these machine learning-based prediction models could enable more accurate and accessible ovarian reserve assessment, facilitating personalized preconception counseling and treatment planning for women of reproductive age.

title: Diagnostic workup for a couple with unexplained infertility – VVOG guidance
journal: European Journal of Obstetrics & Gynecology and Reproductive Biology: X
summary:
# Summary

This VVOG guidance article provides evidence-based diagnostic recommendations for evaluating unexplained infertility in couples. Anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) and antral follicle count (AFC) serve as markers of ovarian reserve, with AMH produced by granulosa cells and correlating with primordial follicle quantity, though neither reliably predicts spontaneous pregnancy rates. Ovulation assessment is best confirmed through ultrasound monitoring of follicular rupture, urinary LH measurement, or mid-luteal progesterone levels (>3 ng/mL), with the latter indicating adequate luteal function critical for fecundity. Tubal patency evaluation is essential given that endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, and other factors cause tubal occlusion; HyCoSy and its three-dimensional variants demonstrate superior diagnostic accuracy compared to traditional laparoscopy, which is no longer recommended routinely for unexplained infertility diagnosis. The guidance emphasizes that routine invasive testing including endometrial biopsy and anti-sperm antibody testing are unnecessary when standard semen analysis is normal, advocating instead for focused assessment of confirmed anatomical and ovulatory abnormalities.

title: Association between dietary fiber intake and obesity in US adults: from NHANES 1999–2018
summary:
# Summary

This analysis of NHANES data from 1999–2018 demonstrates that increased dietary fiber intake substantially reduces obesity incidence and associated comorbidities in US adults. Individuals consuming ≥20.8 g of dietary fiber daily exhibited 26% lower obesity rates and 21% decreased all-cause mortality compared to those with minimal fiber intake, with particularly striking 38% reductions in cardiac mortality. Dietary fiber exerts these protective effects through multiple mechanisms including regulation of intestinal health, blood glucose control, lipid metabolism, and intestinal motility, while also reducing risk of diabetes, hypertension, metabolic syndrome, and cardiovascular disease. The protective effects of adequate fiber intake were notably attenuated by alcohol consumption and smoking, suggesting lifestyle factors modulate the metabolic benefits of dietary interventions. These findings emphasize that dietary fiber intake represents a critical nutritional intervention for preventing obesity and its cascade of obesity-related complications including cancer and cardiovascular disease.

title: Illuminating diabetes via multi-omics: Unraveling disease mechanisms and advancing personalized therapy
summary:
# Summary

This review demonstrates that multi-omics integration—combining genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics, and microbiomics—has revolutionized our understanding of diabetes mellitus's heterogeneous pathophysiology. Type 1 diabetes results from autoimmune destruction of pancreatic β-cells, while type 2 diabetes is characterized by insulin resistance and β-cell dysfunction stemming from metabolic overload, and gestational diabetes arises from pregnancy-related hormonal imbalances, all culminating in sustained hyperglycemia that drives microvascular and macrovascular complications including diabetic kidney disease and retinopathy. Multi-omics approaches have revealed critical interconnections, such as how inflammatory pathways correlate with lipid metabolic shifts and how metabolic intermediates interact with gut dysbiosis, while proteogenomic analyses identify therapeutic targets in gluconeogenesis and glucose uptake pathways. By enabling biomarker discovery, patient stratification, and risk assessment through single-cell resolution and machine learning-enhanced pipelines, multi-omics integration facilitates early detection of diabetes and its complications, paving the way for personalized therapeutic strategies tailored to individual disease mechanisms.

title: Severe hypertriglyceridaemia with length-related small fibre sensory neuropathy as a complication of previous gestational diabetes mellitus
summary: This case report documents severe hypertriglyceridaemia with small fibre sensory neuropathy as a late complication of previous gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) in a 38-year-old woman. Gestational diabetes mellitus predisposes women to dyslipidaemia and metabolic syndrome, with approximately 40–50% progressing to type 2 diabetes, conditions that significantly elevate triglyceride levels and neuropathy risk. The pathophysiology of hypertriglyceridaemia-induced neuropathy involves multiple mechanisms: impaired blood flow causes microvascular damage and nerve ischaemia, while elevated triglycerides simultaneously trigger oxidative stress and inflammatory responses that directly damage peripheral nerve tissue. The patient presented with progressive paraesthesias, numbness, and hyperalgesia that were causally linked to her elevated triglyceride levels and associated metabolic dysfunction. Treatment involved a multimodal approach using fenofibrate (a fibrate that reduces triglycerides 30–50% via PPAR-α activation), high-dose fish oil (omega-3 fatty acids reducing triglycerides 20–30% and oxidative stress), and dietary modification, alongside insulin-dextrose infusion during acute hospitalization. This case highlights how metabolic complications from GDM, particularly hypertriglyceridaemia, can manifest years later with severe neurological consequences if left untreated, emphasizing the need for long-term metabolic surveillance in women with prior gestational diabetes.

title: Examining the Relationship Between Dietary Glycemic Load (GL), Glycemic Index (GI), and the Odds of Diminished Ovarian Reserve: A Case–Control Study
summary:
# Summary

This case-control study demonstrates that higher dietary glycemic index and glycemic load are significantly associated with diminished ovarian reserve (DOR), as evidenced by reduced antral follicle counts and anti-Müllerian hormone levels in women consuming high-glycemic diets. The underlying biological mechanisms involve high-glycemic diets triggering insulin resistance, systemic inflammation, and oxidative stress, which collectively accelerate ovarian reserve decline and compromise ovarian function. Additionally, elevated dietary glycemic indices promote excess adiposity and hyperinsulinemia, leading to endocrine disturbances including hyperandrogenism and elevated insulin-like growth factor I, which further impair ovulation. Conversely, adoption of lower-glycemic-index foods, particularly whole grains and carbohydrates from dairy sources, improves markers of ovarian reserve and reproductive outcomes. These findings suggest that modulating dietary carbohydrate quality represents a noninvasive lifestyle intervention to preserve ovarian function and fertility, with additional benefits for cardiometabolic health, as diminished ovarian reserve is associated with increased cardiovascular disease risk.

title: Explainable machine learning for neoplasms diagnosis via electrocardiograms: an externally validated study
summary: This study demonstrates that machine learning models can identify neoplasms from electrocardiogram (ECG) patterns with clinically meaningful accuracy, leveraging specific cardiac electrical abnormalities associated with different cancer types. Neoplasms induce cardiovascular changes through multiple mechanisms, including direct cardiac invasion, paraneoplastic effects, and treatment-related cardiotoxicity from chemotherapy, radiotherapy, and targeted therapies, all of which produce detectable ECG alterations such as arrhythmias, conduction delays, and repolarization abnormalities. Certain malignancies, particularly lung and breast cancers, trigger hypercoagulability leading to thromboembolism, while immune checkpoint inhibitors can cause myocarditis and immune-mediated myositis. The analysis identified neoplasm-specific ECG signatures: lower lung cancer showed the highest respiratory predictability (AUROC 0.855), while endometriosis demonstrated exceptional gynecological predictability (AUROC 0.879), with QT-interval and RR-interval changes being particularly discriminative. Notably, age emerged as the most influential feature across respiratory, urological, and digestive neoplasms in older patients, whereas younger age predicted gynecological conditions, with male sex contributing more positively to overall neoplasm prediction. These findings suggest ECG-based machine learning could serve as a non-invasive screening tool for oncologic disease risk stratification.

title: Low anti-müllerian hormone levels increased early pregnancy loss rate in patients undergoing frozen-thawed euploid single blastocyst transfer: a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study demonstrates that low anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) levels independently predict early pregnancy loss in women under 35 years undergoing euploid blastocyst transfer, with AMH ≤1.8 ng/mL conferring significantly elevated risk. AMH, produced by granulosa cells and a member of the transforming growth factor-β superfamily, serves as a stable biomarker of ovarian reserve and oocyte competence beyond chromosomal integrity. The study reveals that AMH deficiency leads to compromised folliculogenesis through reduced FSH sensitivity modulation, inadequate luteinization, and insufficient progesterone synthesis—all factors contributing to pregnancy loss. Mechanistically, low AMH associates with diminished oocyte quality, leading to meiotic errors and chromosomal anomalies that drive embryonic aneuploidy and miscarriage. Notably, the protective effect of adequate AMH on pregnancy outcomes was most pronounced in younger patients with primary infertility and higher antral follicle counts, suggesting that AMH's role in preventing early pregnancy loss extends beyond simple ovarian reserve assessment to encompass fundamental oocyte developmental competence.

title: Association between atherogenic index of plasma and hypertension combined with diabetes mellitus in United States adults: an analysis of the NHANES surveys from 2011 to 2016
journal: Journal of Health, Population, and Nutrition
summary:
# Summary

The atherogenic index of plasma (AIP) emerges as a significant predictor of the coexistence of hypertension and diabetes mellitus in United States adults, with elevated AIP strongly associated with a five-fold increased odds of this combined condition. AIP reflects the balance between elevated triglycerides and reduced high-density lipoprotein cholesterol, both of which contribute to endothelial dysfunction, atherosclerotic plaque formation, and vascular damage. The study identifies insulin resistance and inflammatory mediators as key mechanistic links, whereby high AIP disrupts insulin signaling pathways and promotes metabolic dysfunction, creating a self-amplifying cycle of vascular and metabolic deterioration. Notably, individuals with both hypertension and diabetes demonstrate elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL-cholesterol, and significantly increased AIP values compared to those with either condition alone, placing them at heightened risk for kidney insufficiency and cardiovascular events. Age ≥45 years, Non-Hispanic Black ethnicity, elevated glycohemoglobin, increased waist circumference, and metabolic syndrome consistently strengthened the association between AIP and combined hypertension-diabetes. The findings suggest AIP serves as a valuable clinical biomarker for identifying high-risk populations and may guide preventive interventions targeting the lipid and metabolic abnormalities underlying cardiovascular and renal complications.

title: Fibrosis status, extrahepatic multimorbidity and all-cause mortality in 53,093 women and 74,377 men with metabolic dysfunction associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) in UK biobank
summary: This large-scale study of over 127,000 UK Biobank participants demonstrates that fibrosis status is the strongest prognostic indicator of mortality in metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD), with advanced fibrosis increasing mortality risk 2.5-fold compared to early-stage disease. The non-invasive FIB4 score, a marker of liver fibrosis, was associated with a 94% increase in all-cause mortality and a 41% increase in multimorbidity prevalence, with approximately 30% of high FIB4 individuals developing multiple comorbidities. MASLD patients exhibit extensive extrahepatic disease burden, including cardiovascular conditions (ischemic heart disease, heart failure, arrhythmias), solid and hematological cancers, chronic kidney disease, and mental health disorders, all more prevalent in those with elevated FIB4 scores. Notably, cardiovascular diseases, cancers, and kidney disease collectively accounted for a 10% reduction in the fibrosis-mortality association when adjusted for multimorbidity, identifying these conditions as key mediators of mortality risk. The associations between fibrosis markers and adverse outcomes were consistently stronger in males than females, and more pronounced in older individuals and those with existing multimorbidity, suggesting sex and age-stratified clinical management approaches may be warranted in MASLD populations.

title: The Lancet Commission on rethinking coronary artery disease: moving from ischaemia to atheroma
summary:
# Summary

This Lancet Commission article reframes coronary artery disease (CAD) as fundamentally rooted in atherosclerotic plaque pathology rather than ischemia alone. The pathogenesis involves a complex interplay of endothelial dysfunction, lipid accumulation, inflammation, and vascular smooth muscle cell proliferation, with traditional risk factors—smoking, hypertension, high cholesterol, poor diet, obesity, and diabetes—contributing to these mechanisms. Apolipoprotein B retention drives atherosclerosis, while macrophages engulf oxidized lipoproteins to form foam cells; oxidized and glycated lipoproteins subsequently stimulate cytokine production and leukocyte recruitment that destabilize plaques. Plaques prone to rupture are characterized by thin fibrous caps, large lipid cores, and abundant inflammatory cells, and their rupture or erosion triggers thrombus formation and myocardial infarction. The article also identifies emerging non-traditional risk factors—including hypertensive disorders of pregnancy, air pollution, stress, sleep disruption, and microbiome composition—that contribute to atherogenesis, highlighting the need for comprehensive prevention strategies beyond classical risk factor modification.

title: Molecular Variants in SIRT1 Gene Among Saudi Women Diagnosed with Gestational Diabetes Mellitus: A Case-Control Study
summary:
# Summary

This case-control study in Saudi women identifies molecular variants in the SIRT1 gene as significant contributors to gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) risk through dysregulation of glucose and lipid metabolism. Two SIRT1 single nucleotide polymorphisms—rs4746720 and rs10823112—showed strong associations with GDM and impaired glucose homeostasis, including elevated fasting blood glucose, post-prandial blood glucose, and reduced HDL cholesterol levels. SIRT1 functions as a critical insulin sensor that regulates glucose tolerance, insulin secretion in pancreatic β-cells, and body weight through histone deacetylase activity in skeletal muscle and adipose tissue; deficiency of this protein increases diabetes risk. The study found that GDM women exhibited obesity, elevated blood pressure, and family histories of diabetes more frequently than controls, and identified a mechanistic pathway wherein SIRT1 variants promote visceral obesity, which subsequently leads to insulin resistance and β-cell dysfunction. Clinical management recommendations include lifestyle interventions, metformin, and dietary supplements such as probiotics and inositol to improve glycemic control and reduce GDM progression to type 2 diabetes mellitus.

title: Does Metabolic Status Associate With IVF Outcomes in Women Within Similar Body Mass Index Category: Evidence From a Large Cohort Study
summary: This large cohort study demonstrates that metabolic health status significantly impacts in vitro fertilization (IVF) outcomes independent of body mass index category. Among normal-weight women, those with metabolic dysfunction exhibited substantially lower biochemical and clinical pregnancy rates compared to metabolically healthy peers, despite similar BMI, with elevated blood pressure, testosterone, and increased prevalence of polycystic ovary syndrome and diabetes driving these adverse outcomes. Metabolically unhealthy obese women showed markedly higher miscarriage rates than their metabolically healthy obese counterparts, while the overweight category showed no significant differences in pregnancy outcomes by metabolic status. Metabolically healthy normal-weight women produced more mature oocytes and embryos, and demonstrated higher estrogen levels, suggesting superior reproductive potential. These findings underscore that metabolic parameters—including triglycerides, HDL cholesterol, blood pressure, glucose metabolism, and liver fat index—represent critical determinants of fertility independent of conventional BMI classification, indicating that metabolic phenotyping should inform reproductive risk assessment and treatment optimization in assisted reproductive technology.

title: Development and validation of a nomogram model for predicting one-year unplanned readmission in patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
summary: This study developed and validated a nomogram model to predict one-year unplanned readmission risk in chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) patients. Multivariate analysis identified twelve key predictive variables, including disease duration exceeding ten years, acute exacerbations in the past year, concurrent respiratory failure, elevated white blood cell counts, and comorbidities such as diabetes and coronary heart disease, alongside laboratory markers including lymphocyte counts, platelets, NT-Pro-BNP, lipid profiles, and serum creatinine. The constructed nomogram quantifies individual readmission risk by assigning point values to each predictor, enabling clinicians to stratify patients based on their likelihood of unplanned hospital readmission. Frequent acute exacerbations were shown to accelerate disease progression and substantially increase readmission and mortality risk, highlighting the importance of exacerbation prevention in COPD management. The identification of these modifiable and non-modifiable risk factors provides a practical clinical tool for early intervention and personalized management strategies to reduce the substantial disease burden and healthcare costs associated with COPD readmissions.

title: Association between the C-reactive protein-triglyceride glucose index and new-onset coronary heart disease among metabolically heterogeneous individuals
summary:
# Summary

The C-reactive protein-triglyceride glucose index (CTI) demonstrates a strong association with new-onset coronary heart disease in metabolically heterogeneous individuals, integrating inflammatory and metabolic markers including C-reactive protein, triglycerides, and fasting plasma glucose. Insulin resistance serves as a central mechanism driving coronary pathology through multiple pathways: it activates inflammatory responses, induces oxidative stress, impairs vascular endothelial function, and promotes both platelet adhesion and atherosclerotic progression. The study identifies immune-inflammatory processes as critical mediators, with the C-reactive protein-albumin-lymphocyte index partially mediating the relationship between CTI and coronary heart disease, suggesting that combination use of these indices enhances diagnostic discrimination. Individuals with high CTI values show progressively elevated blood pressure, obesity markers, glycemic parameters, and lipid profiles across increasing CTI quartiles, alongside elevated prevalence of diabetes and hypertension. These findings support CTI as a useful biomarker for coronary heart disease risk stratification in individuals with varying metabolic phenotypes, with inflammation and metabolic dysfunction acting as interconnected drivers of cardiovascular disease.

title: Proportion and Pregnancy Outcomes of Rescued Frozen-Thawed Cycles with Low Serum Progesterone Levels: A Cross-Sectional Study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study examined how low serum progesterone levels affect frozen embryo transfer (FET) outcomes and whether progesterone rescue can mitigate adverse effects. Low serum progesterone concentration on the day of FET was strongly associated with decreased pregnancy and live birth rates alongside increased miscarriage risk, with levels below 10 ng/mL showing particularly poor outcomes even after rescue intervention. Patient body weight and the interval between the last progesterone dose and blood sampling emerged as independent predictors of insufficient progesterone levels, likely due to increased adipose tissue sequestration of this lipophilic hormone in heavier patients. While general progesterone supplementation improves outcomes, intramuscular and vaginal rescue strategies failed to fully restore pregnancy rates to levels observed in cycles with adequate baseline progesterone, whereas subcutaneous and oral supplementation approaches showed more promise. These findings underscore the importance of monitoring serum progesterone levels in artificial FET cycles and suggest that patient-specific factors such as body weight should inform individualized supplementation strategies to optimize endometrial preparation and pregnancy success.

title: What all physicians should know about women’s health: a Delphi study
summary: This Delphi study identifies critical gaps in physicians' knowledge of women's health that contribute to substandard clinical care for female patients. The research reveals that sex and gender differences fundamentally influence disease risk factors, pathophysiology, clinical presentation, diagnostic evaluation, and treatment efficacy across women's health conditions. Beyond biological factors such as sex, age, and genetics, the study emphasizes that cultural and social determinants—including socioeconomic status, lifestyle, and geography—substantially shape health outcomes. Notably, societal norms often fail to recognize the pathophysiology underlying conditions specific to the female life cycle, particularly menstruation and menopause, creating diagnostic and management challenges. The analysis highlights that physician understanding must integrate multiple domains: the societal position of women, patient perceptions of disease and treatment, and pharmacological considerations unique to female physiology. These findings underscore that comprehensive women's health education should address both biological mechanisms and the broader social context affecting women's health experiences and outcomes.

title: Global, Regional, and National Prevalence and Disability-Adjusted Life-Years for Female Infertility: Results from a Global Burden of Disease Study, 1990–2019
summary:
# Summary

This global burden of disease study (1990–2019) reveals that female infertility is a multifactorial condition with profound biological and psychosocial consequences. Biological causes include ovulation dysfunction, tubal blockage, endometriosis, and polycystic ovarian syndrome, with fertility declining sharply in women over age 35 due to reduced ovarian reserve function. Infertility is strongly associated with mental health disorders including depression, anxiety, obsessive-compulsive disorder, and sleep disturbances, which paradoxically increase pregnancy difficulty and psychological stress in affected women. Risk factors spanning lifestyle (smoking, unsafe abortion, sexually transmitted infections), environmental exposure, and socioeconomic deprivation significantly elevate infertility prevalence, particularly in developing countries where reproductive tract tuberculosis, environmental pollution, and unsafe abortion practices compound the burden. Geographic and economic disparities are pronounced, with elevated age-standardized rates in middle-to-high-middle sociodemographic index regions (East Asia and Eastern Europe) and substantially lower rates in high-income countries, suggesting that delayed childbearing, higher educational attainment, and improved healthcare access in developed nations paradoxically correlate with infertility detection and management.

title: Changes in serum NO, ET-1, and VEGF after cannulated screw fixation in patients with femoral neck fractures and their relationship with femoral head necrosis
summary:
# Summary

This study examined serum biomarkers in patients undergoing cannulated screw fixation for femoral neck fractures to identify predictors of femoral head necrosis (FHN). Cannulated screw fixation can damage intraosseous blood vessels and trigger endothelial apoptosis, leading to necrosis through a mechanism involving disrupted vascular function. Patients who developed FHN exhibited a persistent pathologic serum biomarker profile: significantly reduced nitric oxide (NO) and vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), which normally promote angiogenesis and blood vessel formation, coupled with elevated endothelin-1 (ET-1), a potent vasoconstrictor that induces venous constriction and ischemia. Postoperative serum levels of NO, ET-1, and VEGF measured on days 3 and 5 post-surgery provided strong predictive value for FHN development, with protective thresholds identified (NO >35.98 nmol/mL on day 3 and >33.62 nmol/mL on day 5; VEGF >66.45 pg/mL on day 5). Beyond biomarkers, delayed surgery >48 hours post-fracture, severe fracture classifications (Garden III–IV, Pauwels type III), and smoking were independently associated with increased FHN risk, with odds ratios ranging from 1.65 to 2.17.

title: The impact of IGRA positivity in untreated inactive pulmonary tuberculosis on IVF-ET outcomes in infertile women: an ambispective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

Tuberculosis, particularly untreated inactive pulmonary TB, significantly impairs fertility outcomes in women undergoing IVF-ET through multiple mechanisms including tubal obstruction, endometrial involvement, and decreased ovarian function. While IGRA positivity indicating latent TB infection was not significantly associated with clinical pregnancy or live birth rates, it was strongly associated with increased cumulative miscarriage rates (21.5% vs. 15.0%), suggesting that latent TB infection compromises pregnancy maintenance rather than fertilization or implantation. The immunological basis for this adverse effect involves chronic low-grade systemic inflammation, dysregulation of Th1/Th2/Th17 cytokine networks, and disruption of the endometrial immune microenvironment, which are further exacerbated during pregnancy's immune-suppressive state and IVF hormonal stimulation, creating conditions favorable for TB reactivation. Clinical cases documented in this cohort demonstrated that active TB reactivation during pregnancy can cause miscarriage, stillbirth, and low birth weight outcomes. Preventive anti-TB treatment emerges as a critical intervention, with evidence showing it reduces reactivation risk and improves implantation and clinical pregnancy rates while reducing miscarriage rates in women with latent TB infection seeking fertility treatment. These findings underscore the importance of IGRA screening and preventive treatment in infertile populations with high TB prevalence.

title: Correlation between dual-time-point 18F-FDG PET/CT imaging and pathological grade in hepatocellular carcinoma
journal: Quantitative Imaging in Medicine and Surgery
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that dual-time-point 18F-FDG PET/CT imaging provides enhanced diagnostic yield for hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) by correlating metabolic parameters with tumor pathological grade. The key finding is that SUVratio2 and ΔSUVratio measured on delayed imaging show significant positive correlations with histological grades, with these metrics increasing progressively from well-differentiated (grade I) to poorly differentiated (grade III) tumors. Delayed imaging significantly elevates SUVmax and SUVratio values compared to early imaging, reflecting the prolonged retention of 18F-FDG in more aggressive HCC cells due to enhanced glycolytic activity and hypoxia-induced glucose transporter expression. These semi-quantitative parameters offer a non-invasive tool to assess malignancy levels, providing a competitive alternative to dual-tracer approaches and enabling improved tumor characterization, particularly for low-grade HCC that might otherwise be missed on standard imaging protocols.

title: The correlation between stiffness and viscoelasticity of the myometrium in adenomyosis: a prospective study
journal: Quantitative Imaging in Medicine and Surgery
summary: This prospective study demonstrates that myometrial stiffness and viscoelasticity are significantly altered in adenomyosis and can be reliably detected using shear wave elastography (SWE) and shear wave dispersion (SWD), respectively. Adenomyosis involves extensive fibrosis and increased microvascularization that fundamentally modify myometrial tissue properties, resulting in elevated stiffness (Young's modulus) across all myometrial layers—inner, middle, and outer—while water content simultaneously increases throughout the adenomyotic uterus. Notably, stiffness and water content show strong negative correlations with each other, while viscoelasticity and microvascular flow positively correlate with water content, reflecting the inflammatory pathophysiology underlying adenomyosis. The inner myometrium (junctional zone) demonstrates the most significant stiffness changes and emerges as the most sensitive diagnostic parameter, with Young's modulus cutoff values yielding 73% sensitivity for adenomyosis diagnosis. SWE achieved superior diagnostic performance compared to both SWD and conventional MRI, establishing it as a robust non-invasive imaging tool for distinguishing adenomyotic from normal myometrium. These biomechanical changes directly contribute to the abnormal uterine contractions, pelvic pain, and impaired fertility characteristic of adenomyosis.

title: Ovarian damage following surgery for endometriomas, 20 years later: did awareness improve the situation?
summary:
# Summary

This 20-year follow-up study evaluates whether surgical techniques for treating endometriomas have improved outcomes for ovarian reserve preservation. Laparoscopic stripping of endometriomas significantly damages ovarian function, reducing follicle count (median 3 versus 7 in contralateral ovaries), anti-müllerian hormone levels, and responsiveness to ovarian stimulation during assisted reproductive technology, with damage worse following bilateral surgery. The mechanisms of ovarian injury include direct removal of ovarian tissue containing primordial follicles and collateral damage from electrosurgical hemostasis, inflammation, and vascular injury. Despite awareness of these complications and proposed alternative techniques over the past two decades—including reduced use of electrical coagulation, adoption of non-thermal hemostasis, CO₂ laser vaporization, and combined stripping-vaporization approaches—the study found that absence of follicular growth persisted in 18% of operated ovaries and cumulative live birth rates remained reduced at 33%. While cyst stripping remains superior for pain relief, natural fertility, and disease recurrence prevention, the persistent ovarian damage suggests that current surgical practice has not substantially mitigated the adverse effects on reproductive outcomes despite technical refinements.

title: New approaches in the analysis of spent embryo culture media in the IVF process
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies microRNA and metabolic biomarkers in spent embryo culture media (SECM) to predict embryo implantation success in IVF. The researchers found that unsuccessfully implanted blastocysts exhibited elevated expression of hsa-miR-16-5p (230% increase) and hsa-miR-92a-3p (24.5% increase) compared to successfully implanted embryos, suggesting these microRNAs serve as predictive biomarkers for implantation failure. Additionally, fluorescence metabolomic analysis revealed that unsuccessfully implanted embryos displayed elevated metabolic activity in specific zones, with notable levels of tryptophan, tyrosine, and phenylalanine detected in the culture medium. By combining non-invasive microRNA analysis and fluorescence spectrophotometry of SECM, the authors propose a dual-approach screening method that could improve embryo selection without compromising embryo viability, offering a practical advancement for optimizing IVF outcomes in infertility treatment.

title: Understanding the impact of lifestyle and demographic factors on DNA fragmentation index (DFI) among infertile men
summary:
# Summary

This study examined lifestyle and demographic factors influencing sperm DNA fragmentation index (DFI) in infertile men, identifying age and occupational exposure as major determinants of sperm quality. Advanced age, particularly above 45 years, and alcohol consumption were independently associated with significantly elevated DFI, while men in professional, technical, or managerial occupations showed substantially lower DFI than those in manual or service-related work. The mechanisms underlying these associations include increased oxidative stress from aging and occupational exposures (environmental toxins, heat, work stress), impaired chromatin maturation, and prolonged reactive oxygen species exposure during extended ejaculatory abstinence. Elevated sperm DNA fragmentation directly compromises reproductive outcomes by reducing fertilization capacity, impairing embryonic development, and increasing miscarriage and recurrent pregnancy loss risk. The findings emphasize that sperm DNA integrity represents a critical marker of male fertility, with approximately 80% of fragmentation attributed to oxidative stress, and suggest that lifestyle modifications and occupational risk reduction may improve semen quality in infertile men.

title: Zinc as a protective factor against COPD: Insights from observational and Mendelian randomization analyses
summary:
# Summary

This observational and Mendelian randomization study establishes zinc as a protective factor against chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and COPD-related infections. Zinc functions through multiple biological mechanisms including immune cell proliferation and maturation, cytokine release, antioxidant defense, DNA synthesis, and maintenance of epithelial barriers in respiratory tissues. Serum zinc concentrations were significantly lower in COPD patients compared to controls, and higher zinc exposure showed dose-dependent protective effects, with particularly pronounced benefits in smokers and individuals aged 65 years or younger. Genetic analyses using SNPs associated with elevated zinc levels confirmed a causal protective role against COPD-related infections independent of other trace elements. These findings underscore zinc's importance as an immune regulator and anti-inflammatory agent and support zinc supplementation as a potential preventive strategy in COPD management, especially for vulnerable populations.

title: Association between metabolic score for visceral fat and psoriasis: findings from NHANES
summary: This study demonstrates a significant association between the metabolic score for visceral fat (METS-VF) and psoriasis risk using NHANES data, with psoriasis patients exhibiting substantially elevated METS-VF levels compared to controls. The underlying mechanism involves visceral adipose tissue's heightened metabolic activity and production of pro-inflammatory mediators including TNF-α, leptin, and IL-6, which exacerbate systemic inflammation and psoriasis pathogenesis. Excess visceral fat triggers a cascade of immune dysregulation through alterations in gene expression affecting regulatory T cells and macrophages, while simultaneously promoting gut dysbiosis that increases intestinal permeability and allows pro-inflammatory substances to enter circulation. The interconnected relationship between obesity, visceral fat accumulation, and metabolic dysfunction—manifested through elevated diabetes, hypertension, and nonalcoholic fatty liver disease risk—establishes visceral adiposity as a significant contributor to both psoriasis initiation and cardiometabolic disease risk. These findings suggest that METS-VF serves as a reliable biomarker for identifying individuals at elevated risk for psoriasis and associated metabolic complications, highlighting the importance of visceral fat reduction as a potential therapeutic target in psoriasis management.

title: Mechanistic exploration of obesity-related indicators and motor cognitive risk syndrome: a mediated effect based on C-reactive protein triglyceride glucose index
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that multiple obesity-related indicators—waist circumference (WC), waist-to-height ratio (WHtR), and body roundness index (BRI)—significantly increase the risk of motor cognitive risk syndrome (MCR), a preclinical stage of dementia characterized by cognitive decline and gait impairment. The C-reactive protein triglyceride glucose index (CTI), a composite biomarker integrating inflammation and lipid metabolism, mediates approximately 21-26% of the association between these obesity measures and MCR, suggesting that metabolic dysfunction and chronic inflammation represent key pathological pathways. Mechanistically, visceral fat accumulation triggers chronic inflammation and insulin resistance through proinflammatory cytokine release, ultimately leading to cerebrovascular damage and neuroinflammatory reactions that impair cognitive function. The study reveals sex-specific differences, with WHtR showing substantially greater impact in males (odds ratio = 530.60) compared to females, likely reflecting men's greater propensity for visceral fat accumulation and associated insulin secretion dysfunction. These findings support incorporating WHtR and CTI measurements into community health assessments for early detection of cognitive impairment in aging populations.

title: Methylation status of PAX1 and SF-1: implications for diagnosis and prognosis in endometrial cancer
summary: This study examined DNA methylation status of PAX1 and SF-1 genes as biomarkers for endometrial carcinoma, finding that both genes show significantly elevated methylation in cancer patients compared to controls. SF-1 methylation demonstrated superior diagnostic value with an area under the curve of 0.735, making it particularly useful for distinguishing endometrial carcinoma cases. PAX1 methylation exhibited broader clinical utility, showing strong associations with critical prognostic factors including tumor differentiation grade, FIGO staging, and myometrial invasion depth. Furthermore, PAX1 methylation positivity independently predicted poor prognosis, with significant correlations to overall survival and cumulative recurrence incidence. These findings establish PAX1 methylation as a valuable prognostic biomarker for refining risk stratification and treatment planning in endometrial cancer patients, while SF-1 methylation primarily contributes to diagnostic accuracy.

title: Association between cardiometabolic index and stroke risk: insights from the CHARLS cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This CHARLS cohort study demonstrates that cardiometabolic index (CMI), a novel metabolic marker calculated from triglycerides, HDL cholesterol, and waist-to-height ratio, is a strong independent predictor of stroke risk, with a 21% increased risk per unit increase and a critical threshold at CMI 1.6. The relationship between elevated CMI and stroke exhibits a dose-response pattern across quintiles, with the highest quintile showing approximately 13.1% stroke incidence compared to 5.6% in the lowest quintile. Mechanistically, elevated CMI promotes stroke through visceral fat accumulation triggering chronic systemic inflammation via NADPH oxidase, NF-κB, and immune receptor pathways, which subsequently induces endothelial dysfunction and blood-brain barrier disruption. Additionally, elevated CMI drives insulin resistance and lipid metabolism abnormalities that promote atherosclerosis and hypertension, amplifying neurovascular complications. The association between CMI and stroke risk was particularly pronounced in individuals with BMI less than 24 and those without diabetes or heart disease history, suggesting CMI may be valuable for early risk stratification in metabolically vulnerable populations.

title: Polygenic scores for dizygotic twinning: insights into the genetic architecture of female fertility
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: This study develops a polygenic score (PGS) for dizygotic twinning (DZT) to characterize the genetic architecture of female fertility. DZT, which results from hyper-ovulation and poly-ovulation, represents one end of a genetic fertility spectrum, with anovulatory infertility and PCOS at the opposite end; genetic correlations revealed strong negative associations between DZT and anovulatory infertility (rg = −0.698) as well as associations with PCOS and endometriosis. The identified causal genes involved in female fertility include FSHB, GNRH1, and FSHR, all participating in endocrine processes regulating ovulation. The DZT PGS, though explaining only 1.6% of liability variance with low polygenicity (0.20% of SNPs with effects), effectively distinguishes between naturally conceived pregnancies and those requiring medically assisted reproduction, with higher PGS deciles associated with increased DZT risk and lower deciles with increased need for fertility treatments. This research suggests that the DZT PGS has clinical utility for evaluating female fertility status and could be incorporated into clinical practice to identify women across the fertility spectrum, from those at risk of infertility to those predisposed to natural multiple ovulation.

title: Ultrasound characteristics of endometrial receptivity in patients with intrauterine adhesion following endometrial polyp surgery and their impact on pregnancy
summary:
# Summary

Intrauterine adhesion (IUA), a fibrotic complication arising from endometrial trauma during polyp surgery, fundamentally compromises endometrial receptivity and causes infertility, menstrual irregularities, and recurrent pregnancy loss. The condition disrupts endometrial architecture and inhibits angiogenesis, leading to reduced endometrial thickness, volume, and vascular perfusion characterized by decreased vascularization and flow indices alongside elevated pulsatility and resistance indices. Three-dimensional transvaginal ultrasound reliably identifies these markers of impaired receptivity, with endometrial thickness, volume, pulsatility index, vascularization index, and flow index emerging as significant predictors of pregnancy outcome. The severity of adhesion directly correlates with endometrial damage, as moderate and severe IUA demonstrate greater reductions in endometrial parameters and perfusion compared to mild adhesion. Adequate vascular supply is essential for successful embryo implantation and placental development, and the diminished blood perfusion associated with IUA underlies the decline in reproductive potential observed in affected patients. These findings underscore the critical role of measuring endometrial receptivity parameters in predicting pregnancy success following hysteroscopic adhesiolysis treatment.

title: Cardiovascular contributions to dementia: Examining sex differences and female‐specific factors
summary: This review examines how cardiovascular dysfunction contributes to dementia risk with particular attention to sex differences and female-specific factors. Hypertension emerges as a major risk factor, especially during midlife, promoting gray matter atrophy and white matter hyperintensities in women, while paradoxically, declining systolic blood pressure in late life also associates with cognitive decline in women. Metabolic factors including elevated LDL cholesterol and Type 2 diabetes show sex-specific effects on cognitive decline and brain structure, with women experiencing higher dementia risk from diabetes than men. Midlife obesity substantially increases late-life dementia risk, though this association reverses in older age, and smoking accelerates menopause onset while independently damaging brain white matter and gray matter. Physical activity provides greater cognitive protection in women than men, while pregnancy-related hypertensive disorders predict both dementia and structural brain changes including reduced brain volumes and white matter hyperintensities. The protective cardiovascular and neuroprotective effects of estradiol—through promotion of vascular elasticity, endothelial function, and synaptogenesis—decline at menopause, when LDL cholesterol and apolipoprotein B levels increase significantly, establishing menopause as a critical window for dementia risk that warrants targeted intervention.

title: Reproductive History and Infertility Risk: Insights from the 2017–2020 National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey
summary:
# Summary

This National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey study reveals critical associations between reproductive timing and infertility risk in women. Both delayed age at first birth and age at last birth significantly increase infertility risk, with age at first birth showing a J-shaped relationship (optimal around 20.6 years) and age at last birth displaying a U-shaped pattern with a turning point at approximately 25.4 years. Conversely, increased number of live births demonstrates a strong protective effect against infertility, with women having two or more deliveries showing substantially reduced infertility prevalence. Delayed childbearing, particularly first births after the mid-twenties or last births after age 40, contribute to infertility through declining physiological function and reduced ovarian reserve. Demographic factors including higher education level, non-Hispanic White race, and married/cohabiting status were more prevalent in the infertility group, while lifestyle factors such as smoking, alcohol consumption, and body mass index showed no significant association with infertility status.

title: It Never Stopped
summary: I cannot provide a summary of this article's key findings because no relationships were extracted from the text. Without structured data identifying the biological mechanisms, key entities, or directional effects studied, I cannot characterize the article's scientific content. To generate an accurate summary, I would need relationship triples that specify what was investigated, what effects were measured, and what conclusions were reached regarding endometriosis or related biomedical topics.

title: Fibroblast Activation Protein (FAP) as a Serum Biomarker for Fibrotic Ovarian Aging: A Clinical Validation Study Based on Translational Transcriptomic Targets
summary:
# Summary

Fibrotic remodeling of the ovarian stroma emerges as a central hallmark of biological ovarian aging, driven by fibroblast activation and dysregulated extracellular matrix (ECM) remodeling. Fibroblast activation protein (FAP), a serine protease selectively expressed by activated fibroblasts, cleaves fibrillar and basement-membrane collagens and is upregulated by profibrotic cytokines including TGF-β, IL-1β, and TNF-α, thereby disrupting critical follicle-stroma crosstalk and impairing vascularization essential for oocyte competence. In this clinical validation study, circulating FAP levels showed a strong inverse correlation with anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH)—a marker of ovarian reserve—and were independent of chronological age and central hormonal regulation (FSH/LH), suggesting FAP reflects ongoing fibrotic processes rather than accumulated time. Notably, while collectin-11 (COLEC11) correlated with age, only FAP differentiated decreased ovarian reserve status, with AMH emerging as the sole independent predictor of serum FAP in multivariate analysis. The authors propose circulating FAP as a novel, complementary biomarker to AMH that specifically captures stromal health status and fibrotic burden, offering potential clinical utility in assessing ovarian aging beyond follicle quantity alone. This work supports the therapeutic potential of antifibrotic interventions to preserve the ovarian niche and restore reproductive function in conditions characterized by ovarian stromal fibrosis.

title: The Sweet Side of IVF: Biological Role and Diagnostic Potential of Galectin-9 in Female Infertility
summary:
# Summary

Galectin-9 is highly expressed across multiple ovarian cell types including oocytes, cumulus granulosa cells, and theca interna cells, and elevated intrafollicular levels are associated with poor ovarian reserve, low ovarian response, and unfavorable IVF outcomes. Follicular fluid galectin-9 levels positively correlate with peak estradiol and FSH dosage, and notably, women with successful fertilization exhibit significantly higher follicular galectin-9 than those with unsuccessful fertilization, yet paradoxically the non-pregnant cohort shows elevated levels, suggesting a complex relationship between galectin-9 and reproductive success. Galectin-9 demonstrates superior diagnostic potential for predicting oocyte fertilization competence compared to anti-müllerian hormone, though its predictive value for overall IVF outcome and pregnancy success is lower than other follicular fluid biomarkers such as IL-8 and leptin. Beyond its role in regulating cell proliferation and apoptosis during oogenesis, galectin-9 is expressed in the endometrium where its levels vary with the menstrual cycle and implantation window, suggesting involvement in maternal-fetal immune tolerance and placental development. Controlled ovarian stimulation triggers intraovarian galectin-9 expression, and alterations in follicular fluid galectin-9 composition may predict ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome and reflect broader ovarian dysfunction in infertility patients.

title: Maternal BMI and Diet Quality Modulate Pregnancy Oxidative and Inflammatory Homeostasis
summary:
# Summary

Maternal pregestational BMI and diet quality significantly modulate oxidative and inflammatory homeostasis during pregnancy, with overweight and obese women demonstrating elevated levels of inflammatory markers (CRP, hepcidin) and oxidative stress biomarkers including DNA/RNA damage and altered catalase activity. Excess maternal adipose tissue drives a self-perpetuating cycle of low-grade chronic inflammation and oxidative stress, ultimately contributing to placental dysfunction and impaired fetal development, as evidenced by reduced neonatal-to-placental weight ratios and decreased head circumference in affected pregnancies. Conversely, prudent-style dietary patterns rich in plant-based foods, fiber, polyphenols, and healthy lipids—such as the Mediterranean diet—mitigate inflammatory and oxidative stress responses while promoting favorable pregnancy outcomes including extended gestational age and improved neonatal measurements. Western-style dietary patterns high in processed foods, saturated fats, and refined sugars exacerbate oxidative-inflammatory dysfunction and are associated with reduced maternal ferritin and compromised fetal development. Maternal nutritional status appears critical for proper fetal programming, with adequate macro- and micronutrient intake and multivitamin supplementation enhancing total antioxidant capacity. These findings emphasize that both pregestational BMI optimization and dietary quality improvement represent modifiable factors essential for reducing inflammation, oxidative stress, and adverse perinatal outcomes.

title: Single-base polymorphism of the ACE2 and TNF-alpha genes in patients with the cardiac and indeterminate forms of Chagas disease
journal: The Brazilian Journal of Infectious Diseases
summary: This study investigated genetic polymorphisms in the ACE2 and TNF-alpha genes as potential risk factors for different clinical presentations of Chagas disease, a parasitic infection caused by *Trypanosoma cruzi* endemic to Latin America. The ACE2 rs2074192 polymorphism involves genes regulating blood pressure, with the G allele more prevalent in patients with the indeterminate form of the disease, while the TNF-alpha rs1800629 polymorphism affects inflammatory response through increased TNF-alpha production associated with the A allele. Chronic Chagas disease progression is linked to inflammatory and immunological mechanisms and frequently presents with comorbidities including hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, and diabetes, which themselves involve inflammatory pathways and cardiovascular risk. However, the study found no statistically significant associations between these polymorphisms and the cardiac versus indeterminate forms of Chagas disease, despite clinical observations that hypertension and hypercholesterolemia were more prevalent in the cardiac form. These findings suggest that while ACE2 and TNF-alpha genes are biologically relevant to inflammation and cardiovascular regulation in Chagas disease, the specific polymorphisms studied do not serve as reliable genetic markers for distinguishing disease phenotypes in this population.

title: Proteomic prediction of disease largely reflects environmental risk exposure
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that plasma proteomic signatures can predict 23 age-related diseases, but the majority of this predictive power derives from environmental risk exposure rather than causal biological mechanisms. Among 2,122 proteins associated with 22 disease outcomes, Mendelian randomization analysis revealed that only 225 protein-disease pairs (8%) showed evidence of causal relationships, while the remaining associations primarily reflected exposure to environmental factors such as smoking. Smoking exemplifies this pattern: over 90% of proteins associated with lung cancer and COPD correlate with smoking status, and a smoking-derived proteomic score (SmokingPS) substantially improves disease prediction beyond traditional clinical biomarkers like C-reactive protein. The study identifies several genuinely causal protein-disease relationships, including PCSK9 with ischemic heart disease, APOE with Alzheimer's disease, and SORT1 with type 2 diabetes, but emphasizes that the plasma proteome functions primarily as an environmental sensor rather than a window into disease pathogenesis. These findings suggest that proteomic biomarkers' clinical utility for disease prediction may depend less on targeting specific protein pathways and more on their capacity to quantify cumulative environmental risk exposure.

title: Positive Association Between the Cardiometabolic Index and the Risk of Male Biochemical Androgen Deficiency in Adults
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates a strong positive association between elevated cardiometabolic index (CMI)—a composite measure combining waist-to-height ratio, triglycerides, and high-density lipoprotein cholesterol—and the risk of male biochemical androgen deficiency (MBAD) in adult men. After adjusting for age and body mass index, CMI remained significantly associated with MBAD, with the odds ratio increasing linearly across CMI quartiles. The mechanism underlying this association involves multiple pathways: excess adipose tissue in metabolically dysfunctional states produces pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α, IL-6) that suppress the hypothalamic-pituitary-testicular axis, while elevated lipids trigger oxidative stress that impairs male reproductive function. Additionally, increased aromatase activity in adipose tissue converts testosterone to estradiol, which exerts negative feedback on gonadotropin-releasing hormone and reduces luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone production. These findings highlight how cardiometabolic dysfunction mechanistically drives testosterone deficiency and underscore the interconnection between metabolic health and male reproductive endocrine function.

title: Metabolic syndrome in reproductive age: A cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study demonstrates that metabolic syndrome in reproductive-age individuals is characterized by multiple interconnected metabolic and hormonal dysfunctions that compromise fertility. Obesity triggers metabolic syndrome through excessive fat accumulation, which activates inflammatory mechanisms and causes metabolic disorders including hyperglycemia, insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, and elevated uric acid levels. These metabolic abnormalities generate oxidative stress and advanced glycation end products, ultimately contributing to cardiovascular disease risk. In women, metabolic syndrome associates with reproductive disorders including endometriosis and polycystic ovarian syndrome, alongside hormonal imbalances with reduced estradiol and elevated testosterone, while men experience hypogonadism, erectile dysfunction, and reduced sperm motility with decreased testosterone levels. The study's case group exhibited significantly elevated BMI, abdominal obesity, blood glucose, HbA1c, insulin, triglycerides, and uric acid alongside reduced HDL cholesterol and sex hormones compared to controls, establishing metabolic syndrome as a major threat to reproductive health across both sexes.

title: The Impact of Age at First Sexual Intercourse on Female Reproductive Disorders: A Mendelian Randomized Study
summary: Using Mendelian randomization, this study demonstrates that earlier age at first sexual intercourse causally increases the risk of female reproductive disorders, particularly ovarian dysfunction, polycystic ovarian syndrome, and spontaneous abortion, while delaying sexual debut reduces these risks substantially. Body mass index emerges as a critical mediating factor, accounting for approximately 35-38% of the association between early sexual activity and ovarian pathology, suggesting that early sexual behavior promotes long-term weight gain and obesity that subsequently impairs reproductive function. The protective effect of later sexual debut on ovarian dysfunction and PCOS is substantially attenuated when adjusting for body mass index or depression, indicating these are key mechanistic pathways through which developmental timing affects reproductive health. Lifestyle factors including smoking, alcohol consumption, sleep duration, and dietary habits are associated with reproductive disorders, with smoking, alcohol, and coffee intake specifically linked to spontaneous abortion risk. Weight management interventions represent a practical therapeutic avenue to partially counteract the adverse reproductive effects of early sexual activity, highlighting the importance of metabolic regulation in female reproductive health. The findings underscore how developmental experiences during puberty, particularly disruption of normal body composition changes through early sexual activity, create long-term metabolic and endocrine dysfunction affecting multiple aspects of female reproductive capacity.

title: Analysis of factors influencing ovarian reserve in patients with endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is closely associated with infertility affecting 20–50% of patients, and this condition significantly impairs ovarian reserve through multiple mechanisms. Anti-müllerian hormone (AMH), produced by granulosa cells, is reduced in endometriosis patients; however, antral follicle count (AFC) emerges as a more sensitive marker for detecting localized ovarian damage since it reflects ovary-specific reserve while AMH reflects global ovarian reserve. Endometriotic cyst size shows strong negative correlations with AFC on both sides, and larger cysts cause measurable ovarian damage even before surgical intervention; additionally, the presence of adenomyosis, deep nodules, and rectouterine pouch sliding signs further compromise AFC and AMH levels. Age, postoperative recurrence, BMI, and previous surgical treatment all negatively correlate with both AFC and AMH, indicating multiple pathways through which endometriosis degrades ovarian reserve. These findings demonstrate that AFC outperforms AMH in detecting localized ovarian damage in endometriosis, making it a more suitable clinical marker for assessing the impact of disease severity and cyst characteristics on fertility potential.

title: Evaluating the effect of simple follicular cysts on clinical pregnancy outcomes in in-vitro fertilization cycles
summary:
# Summary

This study examined whether simple follicular cysts detected at baseline ultrasound affect clinical pregnancy outcomes in in vitro fertilization (IVF) cycles. The researchers found no statistically significant difference in clinical pregnancy rates between patients with and without cysts, though cysts ≥21 mm showed a trend toward reduced pregnancy rates. Higher total antral follicle count and lower luteal hormone (LH) levels on the ovulation trigger day were independently associated with improved clinical pregnancy outcomes, while luteal estrogen supplementation significantly increased pregnancy rates. Cyst diameter and serum estradiol (E2) levels on the trigger day both predicted cycle cancellation, with larger cysts and higher E2 values associated with increased cancellation risk. The findings suggest that simple follicular cysts do not substantially impair IVF success, though cyst size should be considered when planning treatment initiation, and ovarian reserve markers remain critical predictors of pregnancy outcomes.

title: Association between the dietary inflammatory index and infertility: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates a strong association between high dietary inflammatory index (DII) scores and infertility in both men and women, with each unit increase in DII conferring a 10% higher infertility risk and pro-inflammatory diets increasing infertility odds by 61–77%. Pro-inflammatory diets promote infertility through multiple interconnected mechanisms: they elevate circulating inflammatory markers (interleukins 1, 2, and 6, TNF-α, and C-reactive protein), induce chronic inflammation that impairs endometrial decidualization and promotes endometriosis, trigger insulin resistance and obesity, and dysregulate gut microbiota leading to endotoxin-mediated inflammatory responses and oxidative stress. Conversely, anti-inflammatory diets with high omega-3 fatty acids, vitamins E and C, and micronutrients like zinc and selenium improve fertility outcomes in both sexes by reducing inflammation, enhancing sperm quality parameters, and alleviating menstrual disturbances and dysmenorrhea. Dietary modification targeting inflammation reduction emerges as a promising, evidence-supported intervention strategy for managing infertility across multiple etiologies including endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome.

title: Global Spatiotemporal Trends and Modifiable Risk Factors for Female Infertility: An Age-Period-Cohort Using Global Burden of Disease Study 2021 and Mendelian Randomization Analysis
summary: Using Mendelian randomization analysis on Global Burden of Disease data, this study identified multiple modifiable risk factors causally associated with female infertility. Poor general health, elevated waist-to-hip ratio, and neuroticism emerged as significant causal risk factors, with waist-to-hip ratio effects partially mediated through metabolic markers including HbA1c and triglycerides. Conversely, higher educational attainment, greater body fat percentage, and daytime napping demonstrated protective effects against infertility. Spatiotemporal analysis revealed substantial global variation, with East Asia and the Central African Republic showing the highest infertility rates while Australasia and Australia reported the lowest, with socioeconomic development index inversely correlated with infertility burden. Infertility risk peaked in women aged 35–39 years globally across all development regions. These findings highlight the intersection of metabolic dysfunction, psychological factors, sleep patterns, and socioeconomic circumstances as modifiable determinants of female infertility, suggesting that comprehensive lifestyle and healthcare interventions targeting these factors could improve reproductive outcomes.

title: Alzheimer disease is (sometimes) highly heritable: Drivers of variation in heritability estimates for binary traits, a systematic review
summary: This systematic review demonstrates that late-onset Alzheimer's disease (LOAD) is substantially influenced by genetic factors, yet heritability estimates vary dramatically across studies, ranging from 3.1% to 79%. The wide variation in these estimates reflects methodological differences, including study design (twin vs. SNP-based approaches), population characteristics, participant age at assessment, and disease prevalence, with twin studies generally yielding higher heritability values than genetic marker-based analyses. Key factors influencing heritability estimates include the age of participants and onset of disease, with younger samples or incident cases producing lower estimates, while longitudinal designs with longer follow-up periods tend to generate higher values. The study emphasizes that heritability is not a fixed biological property but a dynamic parameter shaped by environmental effects and population-specific factors; additionally, LOAD disproportionately affects females and genetic components may manifest differently between sexes. These findings highlight the critical importance of accounting for methodological variables, population demographics, and sex-specific effects when interpreting heritability estimates for LOAD and other complex binary traits.

title: Cumulative Live Birth Rate in Patients With Subtle Distal Fallopian Tube Abnormalities: A Retrospective Cohort Study
summary: Subtle distal fallopian tube abnormalities (SDFTA) impair ovum pickup and transport, potentially reducing endometrial receptivity and causing infertility. In this retrospective cohort study, patients with SDFTA undergoing in vitro fertilization achieved favorable cumulative live birth rates, with independent predictors including younger age at oocyte retrieval, greater number of oocytes retrieved, and increased IVF cycles. Notably, the SDFTA group exhibited a significantly lower early miscarriage rate compared to controls (8.3% versus 16.1%), though they experienced elevated ectopic pregnancy rates (5.8% versus 1.3%), particularly with anatomical variants including tubal diverticula, fimbrial agglutination, tubal accessory ostium, and fimbrial phimosis. A high comorbidity rate between SDFTA and early-stage endometriosis was identified, with the inflammatory microenvironment of endometriosis potentially contributing to early tubal dysfunction. The SDFTA group demonstrated higher anti-müllerian hormone levels and longer duration of infertility prior to IVF treatment. These findings suggest that IVF may effectively circumvent tubal dysfunction in SDFTA patients while enabling favorable reproductive outcomes despite anatomical tubal abnormalities.

title: The Relationship Between OCT and VEP Parameters with Disability and Disease Duration in Relapsing–Remitting Multiple Sclerosis
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that optical coherence tomography (OCT) and visual evoked potentials (VEP) are complementary diagnostic tools for assessing neurodegeneration in relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis, particularly following optic neuritis. OCT measures structural integrity through retinal nerve fiber layer (pRNFL) and ganglion cell-inner plexiform layer (GCIPL) thickness, while VEP assesses functional integrity through p100 wave latency and amplitude. Eyes with prior optic neuritis showed significantly reduced pRNFL and GCIPL thickness compared to unaffected eyes, and these structural parameters correlated strongly with disease severity (EDSS scores) and disease duration, indicating progressive axonal and neuronal loss. Extended p100 wave latency associated with demyelination showed negative correlations with all OCT parameters and positive correlations with disability, while p100 amplitude reflected longer-term neurodegeneration and potential remyelination recovery. The strong correlations between OCT thickness measurements and both functional VEP parameters and clinical disability metrics suggest these tools can jointly predict long-term disease progression and treatment failure in MS patients.

title: Age-Based Oocyte Yield in Elective Oocyte Cryopreservation: A Retrospective Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study demonstrates a profound inverse relationship between advancing maternal age and oocyte yield during elective egg freezing for fertility preservation. Analysis of 1,047 women undergoing oocyte cryopreservation with standardized GnRH antagonist and gonadotropin protocols revealed that at the 50th percentile, women aged 30, 35, and 40 retrieved 20, 14, and 9 total oocytes respectively, with corresponding mature oocyte counts of 15, 11, and 6.
