This age-related decline reflects both diminished ovarian reserve and increased chromosomal aneuploidy, factors that progressively reduce oocyte quality and live birth potential. The study provides critical evidence that achieving an ~80% live birth probability requires substantially more cycles at older ages—approximately 8 cycles at age 40 compared to fewer than 2 cycles at age 36—underscoring the biological urgency of elective cryopreservation for women concerned about age-related fertility decline. These findings emphasize the necessity for comprehensive, age-specific counseling to help women develop realistic expectations about oocyte yield and the true reproductive costs of delayed childbearing when considering fertility preservation strategies.

title: Systemic immune-inflammation index and lymphocyte-to-monocyte ratio predict recurrence after radical surgery for stage I/II endometrial cancer
summary: This study demonstrates that systemic inflammatory markers derived from peripheral blood cell counts can effectively predict recurrence risk in early-stage endometrial cancer patients following radical surgery. Both the systemic immune-inflammation index (SII) and lymphocyte-to-monocyte ratio (LMR) show significant independent associations with cancer recurrence, with elevated SII and reduced LMR predominating in patients who experienced recurrence. Multivariate analysis identified LMR as the strongest independent predictor of recurrence, with an odds ratio of 1.795. The combined predictive model incorporating both SII and LMR achieved an area under the curve of 0.876, demonstrating excellent discriminatory power for identifying high-risk recurrence patients. These findings suggest that simple, cost-effective immune-inflammatory markers may serve as valuable biomarkers for post-operative risk stratification in early-stage endometrial cancer, potentially enabling more personalized surveillance and treatment strategies for patients at elevated recurrence risk.

title: Prognostic value of hemoglobin-to-red cell distribution width ratio in patients with thoracoabdominal aortic aneurysm: a MIMIC-IV database-based retrospective study
summary: This retrospective study using the MIMIC-IV database demonstrates that the hemoglobin-to-red blood cell distribution width ratio (HRR) is a significant prognostic biomarker for in-hospital mortality in thoracoabdominal aortic aneurysm (TAAA) patients, with lower HRR values associated with substantially elevated mortality risk. The mechanistic basis for HRR's clinical utility involves chronic inflammation driving oxidative stress, which reduces red blood cell count and triggers release of immature erythrocytes, thereby increasing red blood cell distribution width. Patients with HRR values below 0.85 exhibited significantly higher mortality compared to those above 1.02, with HRR demonstrating a linear negative correlation with in-hospital and post-ICU mortality at multiple timepoints (7-day, 30-day, and 365-day). TAAA pathogenesis involves inflammatory cell infiltration, extracellular matrix degradation, and vascular smooth muscle dysfunction in the arterial wall, processes mediated by macrophages and neutrophils. The high HRR group displayed favorable laboratory profiles including elevated albumin, calcium, and platelet counts with lower inflammatory markers like white blood cell count, suggesting HRR captures the inflammatory-metabolic status relevant to surgical outcomes. These findings establish HRR as a superior independent prognostic indicator compared to hemoglobin or red blood cell distribution width alone for risk stratification in TAAA patients.

title: Editorial: Endocrinology, lipids, and disease: unraveling the links
summary: This editorial synthesizes emerging evidence linking lipid metabolism and endocrine dysfunction to systemic diseases. Specific lipids—cholesterol, diacylglycerol, and phosphoinositides—function as critical regulators of steroidogenesis and catecholamine exocytosis, establishing lipids as key signaling molecules beyond their substrate roles. In endometriosis, integrative bioinformatics identifies hub genes including PTGS2 and ACSL4 at the intersection of fatty acid metabolism and inflammation, directly connecting lipid dysregulation to disease pathogenesis. Multiple metabolic indices, particularly the triglyceride-glucose index, demonstrate strong associations with adverse outcomes including cardiac death and all-cause mortality. Therapeutic interventions spanning pharmacological agents (GLP-1 analogues, vitamin D3, lactoferrin), behavioral approaches (acupuncture), and nutrient optimization show promise in ameliorating glycemic control, lipid profiles, insulin resistance, and inflammation. These findings underscore the central role of lipid metabolism in endocrine-metabolic disease networks and highlight multiple intervention points for clinical management.

title: Prevalence, Risk Factors, and Management Strategies of Hyperlipidemia Among Adolescents of a Tertiary Care Rural Indian Setting: A Cross-Sectional Study
summary: This cross-sectional study examined hyperlipidemia prevalence and risk factors among adolescents in rural India, finding significant gender-based differences in lipid profiles that evolved with age. Hyperlipidemia, characterized by elevated circulating lipids including cholesterol and triglycerides, is a major modifiable risk factor for premature atherosclerosis and cardiovascular disease. The study identified multiple contributing factors including sedentary lifestyles, unhealthy dietary patterns (fast food, sweets, sugar-sweetened beverages), obesity, stress, genetic predisposition, and comorbidities such as diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and metabolic syndrome. Notable gender differences emerged, with males showing higher triglyceride and LDL levels while females demonstrated higher HDL levels and more frequently reported family history of hyperlipidemia. Management strategies proved effective, with regular physical activity and dietary modifications—particularly Mediterranean-style diets—significantly improving lipid profiles by reducing triglycerides and LDL while enhancing HDL and overall vascular health. Key barriers to management implementation included lack of time, insufficient health knowledge, and financial constraints, particularly among female adolescents, highlighting the need for targeted interventions in this vulnerable population.

title: Willingness to pay for assisted reproductive technologies among individuals with infertility in China
summary: This Chinese study examined willingness to pay (WTP) for assisted reproductive technologies (ART) among infertile individuals, identifying critical barriers and determinants of treatment access. Infertility imposed substantial burdens, including negative impacts on physical and mental health alongside catastrophic out-of-pocket expenditures that blocked access to fertility care. Patient demographics significantly influenced WTP: older age reduced willingness across all ART modalities, while higher education increased WTP specifically for preimplantation genetic testing (PGT). The type of infertility diagnosis affected treatment decisions, with male-factor or unexplained infertility associated with lower WTP for IVF and PGT compared to female-factor cases, partly because these patients pursued fewer diagnostic services. Clinical pregnancy rates varied substantially by technique—artificial insemination (AI) at 20%, IVF at 50%, and PGT at 60%—and correlated with WTP levels. Notably, physician cost communication emerged as a powerful intervention, positively associated with increased WTP for both AI and PGT by improving price transparency, enhancing health literacy, and strengthening patient-physician trust.

title: Eyelid heat pain sensitivity in healthy participants
summary:
# Summary

This study used quantitative sensory testing to characterize heat pain sensitivity across different facial body sites in healthy participants, finding that the eyelid exhibits significantly lower heat pain thresholds compared to the forehead and forearm despite all sites being innervated by branches of the trigeminal nerve. The eyelid's reduced thermal tolerance appears attributable to its thin skin structure, which allows thermal stimuli to more readily reach underlying nociceptors, combined with spatial summation effects from high nociceptor density in this region. Notably, while the eyelid showed the lowest pain threshold, paradoxically the forearm generated higher pain intensity ratings, suggesting that threshold and subjective pain experience are dissociable measures influenced by different factors including receptor density and nerve fiber composition. The findings establish the eyelid as a clinically relevant site for investigating trigeminal function and ocular pain mechanisms through quantitative sensory testing, with potential applications for understanding neuropathic mechanisms underlying chronic ocular pain conditions such as dry eye disease. Age was significantly associated with pain intensity ratings but not with heat pain thresholds, whereas sex and study site did not significantly influence thermal sensitivity measures.

title: Determining the association of C-reactive-protein–triglyceride–glucose index and diabetes using machine learning and LASSO regression: A cross-sectional analysis of NHANES 2001 to 2010 results
summary: This cross-sectional analysis of NHANES data identified the C-reactive protein–triglyceride–glucose index (CTI) as a novel composite biomarker strongly associated with type 2 diabetes risk through dual mechanisms: inflammation and insulin resistance. The CTI integrates three key pathophysiologic pathways—C-reactive protein elevation (marking systemic inflammation), elevated triglyceride–glucose levels (reflecting insulin resistance), and their combined contribution to metabolic dysfunction. Machine learning and LASSO regression analysis revealed that CTI shows a significant linear correlation with diabetes prevalence, with levels progressively higher across CTI quartiles, and notably, this relationship demonstrated gender-specific interactions, suggesting sex-dependent metabolic responses. Beyond diabetes, the CTI was associated with multiple cardiometabolic and inflammatory conditions including endometriosis, coronary heart disease, and stroke, positioning it as a potentially valuable multi-system risk predictor. The study underscores how inflammation and insulin resistance synergistically drive diabetes pathogenesis, with classical risk factors including hypertension, BMI, age, and race also contributing significantly to disease risk. These findings suggest CTI may serve as a clinically useful screening tool for identifying high-risk individuals, though further prospective studies are needed to establish its predictive utility in clinical practice.

title: Adverse effects of maternal and paternal body mass index on assisted reproductive techniques outcomes: A time-lapse study
summary:
# Summary

This time-lapse study demonstrates that elevated parental body mass index (BMI) significantly impairs assisted reproductive technology outcomes through distinct maternal and paternal mechanisms. In overweight women, obesity reduces oocyte quantity and quality, decreases follicular fluid antioxidant capacity while increasing lipid concentrations and inflammatory markers, and delays embryonic development as measured by prolonged cleavage times (t5 and t8 durations). In overweight men, obesity elevates oxidative stress markers (reactive oxygen species and malondialdehyde) while reducing total antioxidant capacity, resulting in higher DNA fragmentation, altered sperm chromatin integrity, reduced sperm concentration and motility, and diminished fertility rates. Couples with combined elevated pre-pregnancy BMI exhibited significantly lower clinical pregnancy and fertilization rates, higher miscarriage rates, and slower pre-implantation embryo development, with morphokinetic delays correlating with adverse reproductive outcomes. These findings suggest that parental obesity creates a systemic pro-inflammatory and oxidatively stressed environment that compromises gamete quality, early embryonic development, and implantation potential, highlighting the critical importance of preconception weight management for reproductive success.

title: Uric acid and uric acid/creatinine ratio are associated with GDM in women undergoing IVF/ICSI
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that elevated serum uric acid (SUA) and particularly the SUA/serum creatinine ratio are independently associated with gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) in women undergoing IVF/ICSI, with the ratio showing a stronger association than SUA alone. The mechanistic basis involves uric acid's capacity to reduce nitric oxide availability, inhibit adiponectin production, and promote insulin resistance—all pathways critical for glucose homeostasis and insulin sensitivity. Pre-pregnancy metabolic factors including obesity, higher BMI, elevated blood glucose, and lipid dysregulation correlated with increased SUA levels, suggesting that elevated pre-conceptional uric acid reflects an underlying high-risk metabolic profile that predisposes to GDM. The study further identifies that women with lower baseline blood glucose and systolic blood pressure particularly benefit from lower SUA/creatinine ratios, supporting a stratified risk approach. Since SUA levels normally decline during early pregnancy due to increased glomerular filtration, elevated first-trimester levels may indicate maladaptation to pregnancy and warrant clinical attention. These findings suggest that measuring pre-pregnancy uric acid and the SUA/creatinine ratio could serve as valuable biomarkers for GDM risk stratification in assisted reproduction populations.

title: Association between uric acid to high-density lipoprotein cholesterol ratio (UHR) and female infertility: insights from a cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study demonstrates that the uric acid to high-density lipoprotein cholesterol ratio (UHR) is significantly associated with female infertility, serving as a novel composite biomarker that reflects both oxidative stress and dysregulated lipid metabolism. Elevated UHR levels contribute to infertility through multiple pathways: oxidative stress and inflammatory responses directly impair fertility, while dysregulated lipid metabolism diminishes endometrial receptivity and embryo quality. Infertile women exhibited higher uric acid concentrations, lower HDL-C levels, and consequently elevated UHR compared to fertile controls, with the strongest UHR-infertility associations observed in younger women (18–30 years), obese women, those without diabetes, and those with regular menstrual cycles. Since HDL-C exhibits anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and endothelial-protective functions while elevated uric acid correlates with metabolic disorders including insulin resistance, diabetes, and conditions like polycystic ovary syndrome and endometriosis, the UHR effectively captures the metabolic dysfunction underlying female infertility. These findings suggest that UHR could serve as a practical clinical tool for assessing metabolic and inflammatory burden in infertile women, potentially guiding interventions targeting lipid metabolism and oxidative stress reduction.

title: Morphokinetic behavior in embryos leading to biochemical
summary:
# Summary

This study analyzed morphokinetic patterns of embryos using time-lapse imaging to distinguish between biochemical pregnancy (early pregnancy loss detected by β-hCG), clinical pregnancy, and failed implantation outcomes in assisted reproductive technology cycles. Embryos with faster developmental kinetics—particularly rapid cleavage from the 2- to 8-cell stage, faster pronuclear fading (tPNf), and faster second cleavage (t2)—demonstrated superior blastocyst development, higher implantation rates, and increased pregnancy success. Embryos resulting in negative pregnancy outcomes exhibited significantly slower morphokinetic development across multiple parameters and lower KIDScore rankings, while notably, embryos resulting in biochemical pregnancy displayed morphokinetic patterns similar to those achieving clinical pregnancy, suggesting that biochemical loss arises from endometrial-level factors rather than embryo quality alone. The findings indicate that biochemical pregnancy results from disrupted embryo-uterus communication and altered endometrial receptivity, potentially influenced by poor endometrial receptivity, supraphysiological hormone levels, and impaired decidualized stromal cell function. This research demonstrates the value of morphokinetic analysis in identifying embryos with limited implantation potential and raises important questions about the maternal and endometrial contributions to early pregnancy loss in assisted reproduction.

title: LINE-1 266/97 and ALU 260/111 Copy Number Ratios in Circulating Cell-Free DNA in Plasma as Potential Biomarkers for the Detection of Prostate Cancer: A Pilot Case-Control Study
summary: This pilot study investigates circulating cell-free DNA (ccfDNA) integrity as a biomarker for prostate cancer detection using digital droplet PCR analysis. The researchers measured copy number ratios of two retrotransposable elements, ALU 260/111 and LINE-1 266/97, along with absolute concentrations of EEF1A2 and ESR1 targets in plasma from prostate cancer patients and controls with benign prostatic hyperplasia. They found that ALU 260/111 and LINE-1 266/97 ratios were significantly lower in prostate cancer patients, indicating greater ccfDNA fragmentation, while conversely, EEF1A2 and ESR1 copy numbers were significantly higher, reflecting increased total ccfDNA concentration. The decreased copy number ratios showed negative correlations with EEF1A2 and ESR1 levels, suggesting that increased apoptotic cell death in cancer elevates both ccfDNA fragmentation and overall concentration. ALU 260/111 demonstrated a positive correlation with PSA levels and the study proposes these retrotransposon-based ratios as novel, reproducible biomarkers for prostate cancer detection that may complement or improve upon current PSA screening approaches.

title: Effect of Chinese Herbal Medicine on Pregnancy Outcomes in IVF Patients with Low Ovarian Reserve: A Predictive Model
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluates the effectiveness of Chinese herbal medicine (CHM) on pregnancy outcomes in IVF patients with low ovarian reserve, using anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH), follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), inhibin B, and antral follicle count as markers of ovarian reserve. The results demonstrate that both CHM-only treatment and mixed treatment (CHM combined with conventional therapy) significantly increased live birth rates compared to control, with the mixed treatment group showing an 18.77-fold increase in odds and the CHM-only group showing an 8.98-fold increase. Younger maternal age (≤37 years) was independently associated with improved reproductive outcomes, with significantly higher odds of clinical pregnancy and live birth. The study developed a predictive nomogram integrating treatment type, AMH level, and age to estimate individual live birth probability, with lower AMH levels and older age associated with reduced predicted success rates. These findings suggest that CHM supplementation may enhance reproductive capacity in patients with diminished ovarian reserve, particularly when combined with conventional ART protocols, and that age remains a critical determinant of pregnancy success regardless of treatment modality.

title: Prospective Observational Case Series in Infertile Women with Overweight or Obesity Treated with a Very-Low Calorie Ketogenic Diet (VLCKD) Prior to an In Vitro Fertilization (IVF) Treatment
summary:
# Summary

This prospective observational study evaluated a very-low calorie ketogenic diet (VLCKD) as a pre-treatment intervention for overweight and obese infertile women scheduled for assisted reproductive technology (ART). The study demonstrated that elevated BMI causes metabolic and endocrine imbalances—particularly insulin resistance—that impair oocyte quality, embryo development, and fertility outcomes, while excess adiposity increases inflammation and oxidative stress. Following the VLCKD intervention, 75% of participants achieved ≥10% weight loss while preserving lean mass, accompanied by significant improvements in glucose metabolism, lipid profiles, liver function, and insulin sensitivity markers (fasting glucose, insulin, and HOMA-IR index). Clinically, the diet was associated with a 58% pregnancy success rate overall, with 27% achieving natural pregnancy and 55% achieving pregnancy through ART after initiating VLCKD, with notably all women who conceived during the final phase having achieved >20% weight loss. These findings support the VLCKD as a feasible, well-tolerated, and highly effective weight loss strategy that improves metabolic health and reproductive outcomes in obese infertile women.

title: Effects of AZFc (b2/b4, b1/b3, b2/b3, and gr/gr) deletions and primary duplications on the outcomes of the first intracytoplasmic sperm injection treatment cycle: A single‐center retrospective cohort study
summary: This retrospective study examined how different AZFc (azoospermia factor c) deletions and duplications on the Y chromosome affect intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI) outcomes in infertile men. The b2/b4 deletion was particularly detrimental, significantly reducing both the overall fertilization rate and two-pronuclear fertilization rate compared to controls, and was associated with the lowest testicular volume and seminal concentration among all groups studied. In contrast, partial AZFc deletions (b1/b3, b2/b3, and gr/gr) showed no significant effect on ICSI fertilization rates. Primary AZFc duplications proved most clinically problematic, reducing embryo quality, clinical pregnancy rates per transferred embryo, and live birth outcomes, while also correlating with low birth weight and variable semen phenotypes ranging from azoospermia to normozoospermia. The underlying mechanism involves the susceptibility of AZFc's palindromic and repetitive structures to nonallelic homologous recombination, which generates destabilizing copy number variations and impairs spermatogenesis. These findings indicate that Y chromosome AZFc structural variants have distinct impacts on male fertility and assisted reproductive outcomes, with primary duplications and the b2/b4 deletion representing the most significant risk factors for ICSI failure.

title: A WHO 2021‐based comprehensive scheme outlining sperm parameters’ associations with IVF outcomes in PGT‐A cycles
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive analysis of preimplantation genetic testing for aneuploidy (PGT-A) cycles reveals that sperm motility is the primary semen parameter influencing embryonic outcomes in assisted reproduction. Sperm motility—both before and after processing—associates with fertilization rates, blastocyst formation, and euploidy rates, with measurements below the fifth percentile significantly reducing euploidy blastocyst rates by 2.5%. Combined defects in concentration, morphology, and motility show cumulative negative effects, reducing euploidy blastocyst rates by 4.0% and live birth odds by 27%, while both obstructive and non-obstructive azoospermia substantially impair fertilization and blastulation outcomes. Advanced paternal age (>44 years) specifically reduces day-5 blastocyst formation and blastocyst quality grading, potentially through increased de novo mutations and epigenetic changes, though it does not affect euploidy rates per biopsied blastocyst. Maternal age emerges as the predominant confounding variable affecting euploidy rates and live birth probability, whereas paternal body mass index shows no significant associations with any embryologic outcome.

title: Value of endometrial peristalsis analysis technique in detecting and analyzing endometrial peristalsis features
summary: Endometrial peristalsis (EmP) is a key indicator of endometrial receptivity that significantly influences embryo implantation and pregnancy success rates, with its amplitude, frequency, and direction dynamically regulated by ovarian hormonal fluctuations across the menstrual cycle and involved in critical reproductive processes including sperm transport, embryo implantation, and pregnancy maintenance. The newly developed Endometrial Peristalsis Analysis (EmPA) technique provides noninvasive, quantitative detection of EmP, though respiratory and intestinal movements can interfere with its accuracy. Multiple clinical and anatomical factors modulate EmP characteristics: secretory phase demonstrates higher peristalsis intensity than proliferate phase, anteversion uteri show greater intensity but shorter transfer distances, and multiple induced abortions result in decreased peristalsis intensity and frequency due to endometrial injury. Abnormal EmP patterns, particularly those resulting from endometrial trauma or associated with gynecological pathologies such as adenomyosis, uterine leiomyomas, and chronic endometritis, correlate with reduced pregnancy rates and infertility. Uterine dimensions and endometrial thickness exhibit linear correlations with EmP frequency and intensity, while postmenopausal women and those with grade C endometrial patterns show reduced or absent peristalsis. The EmPA technique's ability to quantitatively measure subtle peristalsis patterns not visible on conventional analysis makes it valuable for assessing reproductive potential and tailoring fertility interventions based on individual EmP profiles.

title: Mendelian Randomization Analysis of the Relationship Between Immune-Related Diseases and Alzheimer’s Disease
summary: Using Mendelian randomization analysis, this study investigated potential causal relationships between immune-related diseases and Alzheimer's disease (AD) risk. While the research found that asthma showed a weak protective association with AD (slightly lower risk), most immune-related conditions examined—including type 1 diabetes, systemic lupus erythematosus, myasthenia gravis, endometriosis, and idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura—demonstrated no significant genetic causal associations with AD. The study confirmed that AD pathogenesis involves multifaceted interactions between genetic predisposition, aging, and immune-inflammatory responses, with neuropathological hallmarks including neurofibrillary tangles, β-amyloid accumulation, brain atrophy, and glial damage. These findings suggest that despite the broader literature suggesting links between immune dysfunction and neurodegeneration, most common autoimmune and immune-related conditions do not have strong genetic causal relationships with AD risk at the population level. The results highlight the complexity of AD etiology and suggest that immune-related disease associations with AD may be less causal than previously hypothesized.

title: Spontaneous pregnancy following intra-ovarian platelet-rich plasma administration in poor ovarian responders according to the POSEIDON criteria: A cross-sectional study
summary: This cross-sectional study demonstrates that intra-ovarian platelet-rich plasma (IO-PRP) administration improves pregnancy outcomes in poor ovarian responders stratified by POSEIDON criteria. IO-PRP treatment enhances ovarian reserve, improves hormonal profiles, and reduces symptoms of premature ovarian insufficiency through growth factors released from platelet secretory granules, which are known to facilitate tissue regeneration. Spontaneous pregnancy following IO-PRP administration was associated with multiple patient factors including maternal age, anti-Müllerian hormone levels, body mass index, follicle and oocyte counts, and partner age. Poor ovarian response, a major cause of female infertility defined by advanced age, abnormal reserve tests, and reduced oocyte retrieval, traditionally requires gonadotropin-based ovarian stimulation. The findings support IO-PRP as a potential co-treatment to overcome poor ovarian response and promote ovarian rejuvenation, though the authors note the spontaneous pregnancy rate remained relatively low, suggesting further investigation is warranted.

title: Association Between Emphysema and Breast Cancer: Data from National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (1998–2016)
summary:
# Summary

This epidemiological study analyzed data from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (1998–2016) to investigate the relationship between emphysema and breast cancer risk. Among 4,937 participants (152 with breast cancer, 4,785 controls), emphysema was significantly more prevalent in breast cancer patients (3.9% versus controls), and multivariate analysis demonstrated that emphysema increases breast cancer risk with an odds ratio of 2.6. The association may be mechanistically explained by dysregulated immune microenvironments and activation of growth factor signaling pathways (particularly EGFR) that are common to both conditions. Additional risk factors for breast cancer identified in the study included age, race, marital status, hypertension, smoking history, and reduced number of pregnancies. The findings suggest that respiratory diseases, particularly emphysema, should be considered as potential risk factors for breast cancer development, with implications for clinical screening and monitoring of affected patients.

title: The association between frailty and recurrent pregnancy loss in reproductive-aged women: a cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study reveals that frailty severity demonstrates a dose-dependent association with recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL) in reproductive-aged women, with pre-frail and frail women exhibiting 30% and 77% elevated RPL risk respectively compared to robust women. Frailty functions as an independent risk factor for RPL through multiple interconnected mechanisms: chronic low-grade inflammation elevates pro-inflammatory cytokines that disrupt embryonic implantation and placental development, while accumulated oxidative stress impairs reactive oxygen species scavenging and induces gamete DNA fragmentation and embryonic developmental arrest. The study identifies frailty as reflecting depleted physiological reserves and reduced adaptability to stress, with concurrent dysregulation of metabolic-endocrine networks including sarcopenia, impaired glucose tolerance, and hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian/adrenal axis dysfunction further compromising endometrial receptivity and luteal phase function. Frail women demonstrated elevated rates of comorbidities including hypertension, diabetes, high BMI, smoking, and poverty, with disparities in prevalence among non-Hispanic Black women. Clinically, the authors recommend early intervention and chronic disease management for pre-frail women and intensified perinatal monitoring for frail women to assess placental function and prevent adverse pregnancy outcomes.

title: Decoding the potential association of liver steatosis with female infertility: an analysis from NHANES 2013–2018
summary:
# Summary

This analysis of U.S. population data from 2013-2018 reveals a significant association between liver steatosis and female infertility, mediated through metabolic dysfunction. Liver steatosis, characterized by excessive hepatic fat accumulation, is strongly linked to metabolic disorders including obesity, hyperglycemia, and dyslipidemia, all of which independently contribute to infertility. Infertile women demonstrate elevated biomarkers of hepatic steatosis (FLI, HSI, and ZJU index scores) and higher prevalence of metabolic risk factors including increased BMI, waist circumference, diabetes, and hypertension compared to fertile women. The study identifies ovulatory disorders and tubal diseases as primary causes of infertility while noting that metabolic dysfunction particularly contributes to premature ovarian failure and polycystic ovary syndrome. Beyond reproductive consequences, female infertility itself is associated with increased risk of cardiovascular disease, certain cancers, depression, and anxiety, highlighting the broader health implications of this condition and the importance of addressing underlying metabolic dysfunction.

title: Correlation of A Body Shape Index and Body Roundness Index as Novel Anthropometric Indices With Semen Analysis Parameters and IVF Outcomes
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how novel anthropometric indices that distinguish visceral from subcutaneous fat distribution correlate with male fertility and assisted reproductive technology (ART) outcomes. The Body Shape Index (ABSI) emerged as particularly predictive, showing strong negative correlations with multiple IVF parameters including embryo yield, blastocyst development rate, and embryo utilization rate. The mechanisms linking obesity to reproductive dysfunction involve visceral adipose tissue accumulation, which triggers oxidative stress, sperm DNA damage, and hormonal dysregulation characterized by decreased testosterone and sex hormone-binding globulin alongside elevated estrogen. Beyond conventional semen parameters, male obesity appears to impair embryo quality through profound molecular mechanisms involving oxidative damage and epigenetic alterations, suggesting that fat distribution patterns specifically influence fertilization outcomes and treatment success. These findings highlight that novel indices reflecting abdominal obesity and central fat distribution may provide superior predictive value for ART outcomes compared to traditional body mass index measurements alone.

title: Lymphocyte-to-monocyte ratio is associated with all‑cause and cardiovascular mortality among individuals with diabetes mellitus in the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey 2003–2018 cohort
summary: This study demonstrates that the lymphocyte-to-monocyte ratio (LMR) serves as a significant independent predictor of mortality outcomes in individuals with diabetes mellitus. Using data from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (2003–2018), participants with high LMR (>2.62) showed substantially reduced risk of both all-cause mortality (HR 0.36) and cardiovascular mortality (HR 0.35) compared to those with low LMR (≤2.62). The protective effect of elevated LMR reflects its role as an inflammatory marker, where higher lymphocyte counts relative to monocytes indicate a more favorable immune balance. Additionally, patients in the high LMR group exhibited lower incidence of comorbidities including congestive heart failure and stroke, further supporting the clinical utility of LMR as a prognostic indicator. These findings suggest that LMR may serve as a simple, clinically accessible biomarker for stratifying cardiovascular and mortality risk in diabetic populations, potentially improving patient risk assessment and management strategies.

title: Anti-Müllerian hormone and inhibin B dynamics in polycystic ovary syndrome: correlation with controlled ovarian hyperstimulation outcomes and pregnancy success
summary: In polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) patients undergoing controlled ovarian hyperstimulation (COH), anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) and inhibin B (INHB) show distinct dynamic patterns that correlate with fertility outcomes. Elevated AMH in PCOS is associated with increased pre-antral and antral follicles yet paradoxically inhibits folliculogenesis, contributing to the characteristic anovulation of the syndrome. During COH, AMH levels decline progressively while INHB levels rise and peak at human chorionic gonadotropin trigger, with both hormones strongly correlating with oocyte retrieval, fertilization rates, and embryo quality. Notably, PCOS patients demonstrate significantly lower top-quality embryo rates and substantially reduced cumulative pregnancy and live birth rates compared to controls, despite elevated baseline hormonal levels. Early INHB measurement at day 5 of gonadotropin stimulation predicts pregnancy outcomes, and AMH levels at oocyte pickup emerge as a negative predictor of top-quality embryo formation, suggesting that lower post-stimulation AMH may reflect optimal follicular development. These findings establish AMH and INHB dynamics as valuable biomarkers for predicting COH response and pregnancy success in assisted reproductive technology for PCOS patients.

title: Metabolic syndrome increases the risk of suicide attempt: evidence from a population-based cohort and genomic analysis
summary:
# Summary

This population-based cohort study demonstrates that metabolic syndrome (MetS) significantly increases suicide attempt risk with a hazard ratio of 1.250, with a dose-response relationship observed between the number of MetS traits and severe suicide events requiring hospitalization or death. MetS impairs cognitive performance, particularly in executive function and decision-making domains, which partially mediates the association between MetS and suicide attempts. Multiple biological pathways link MetS to suicidal behavior, including systemic inflammation and oxidative stress that directly promote suicidal behaviors, as well as vascular dysfunction that compromises cerebral blood flow and disrupts the blood-brain barrier, leading to white matter damage and cognitive decline. MetS also triggers brain structural alterations in the cingulate cortex and hippocampus, and elevates inflammatory cytokines such as interleukin-6, which correlate with brain volume loss and cognitive decline. Genetic analysis confirms shared genetic correlations between MetS, cognitive performance, and suicide attempts, suggesting that the elevated suicide risk in MetS may involve both direct metabolic-inflammatory mechanisms and indirect cognitive impairment pathways that compromise decision-making and impulse control.

title: Clinical characteristics and influencing factors of adverse outcomes following clinical pregnancy after laparoscopic tubal anastomosis
summary: This study examined adverse pregnancy outcomes following laparoscopic tubal anastomosis (LTA), identifying advanced maternal age and elevated body mass index (BMI) as independent risk factors for pregnancy loss. Advanced age drives multiple deleterious mechanisms including embryonic aneuploidy, decline in ovarian reserve and oocyte quality, morphological changes in the fallopian tubes, and age-related physiological alterations such as increased inflammatory responses and blood coagulation disruptions. Obesity contributes through metabolic disturbances including insulin resistance, hormonal imbalances, and lipid metabolism abnormalities that impair ovarian function and embryo development, with visceral adipose tissue specifically linked to reproductive hormone imbalances, ovulatory dysfunction, and implantation failure. Post-surgical complications including fibrosis and scar formation at the anastomosis site compromise tubal function through luminal narrowing and impaired peristalsis, while reduced ciliary activity and microenvironmental disruption lead to ectopic pregnancy and miscarriage. Despite these complications, laparoscopic tubal anastomosis achieved a 72.3% live birth rate with high tubal patency, though the combined predictive value of age and BMI (AUC 0.768) suggests these factors should guide patient counseling. Lower triiodothyronine (T3) levels were significantly associated with adverse outcomes, highlighting thyroid function as a potential biomarker for pregnancy prognosis.

title: Construct a clinical prediction model of cumulative live birth rate for IVF/ICSI in female patients of different ages
summary: This study developed a clinical prediction model for cumulative live birth rates in IVF/ICSI across different female age groups. Female age emerges as a critical determinant, with women over 35 experiencing significant declines in ovarian reserve function, oocyte quality, and increased chromosomal abnormalities. The number of retrieved oocytes is the strongest predictor of cumulative live birth rate, particularly in women over 40, though this relationship differs by age group: women under 35 achieving near-maximum live birth probability with 15-20 retrieved eggs, women aged 35-40 requiring 20-25 eggs for similar outcomes, and women over 40 needing 14+ eggs for 50% probability. The study identified distinct predictive markers for each age stratum—metaphase II oocytes and high-score blastocysts for women under 35, follicle number and MII oocytes for women 35-40, and retrieved oocyte quantity alone for women over 40. However, retrieval of more than 20 oocytes paradoxically decreases live birth rates, correlating with increased immature oocytes and aneuploidy, while also escalating ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome risk, suggesting an optimal retrieval range of 10-20 oocytes exists across age groups.

title: How to enhance prediction of clinical outcomes in poor responders: integrating high-specific assays for anti-mullerian hormone with antral follicle count
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated how high-specificity anti-mullerian hormone (AMH) assays, when combined with antral follicle count (AFC), improve prediction of oocyte yield in poor ovarian responders undergoing fertility treatment. AMH, produced by granulosa cells, acts as a gatekeeper for primordial follicle recruitment by binding to AMH receptor-2 and initiating intracellular signaling cascades. The AL-196 assay (PCOCheck ELISA), which uses linear-epitope antibodies to detect multiple AMH isoforms including proAMH and processed forms, demonstrated superior correlation with oocyte retrieval outcomes compared to conformational epitope antibodies or automated assays. The combination of AFC plus AL-196 provided the strongest predictive accuracy for cumulus-oocyte complex and metaphase II oocyte numbers, substantially outperforming AMH measurements alone. These findings demonstrate that assay-specific epitope recognition of AMH isoforms critically influences clinical prediction accuracy, with linear-epitope antibodies offering enhanced performance for counseling poor responders on expected treatment outcomes.

title: Accelerated biological aging and homeostatic dysregulation in diabetic kidney disease: Evidence from NHANES and Mendelian randomization
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that accelerated biological aging and homeostatic dysregulation are substantially associated with diabetic kidney disease (DKD) risk, with multiple aging metrics showing marked elevations in DKD patients compared to controls. Using NHANES data, biological age acceleration (BioAgeAccel) conferred a 3.25-fold increased odds of DKD, while phenotypic age acceleration (PhenoAgeAccel) and homeostatic dysregulation showed similarly elevated associations, with Mendelian randomization confirming a causal effect of PhenoAgeAccel on DKD. Urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio, a marker of kidney dysfunction, mediated 89-93% of the associations between all aging metrics and DKD, suggesting that accelerated aging drives renal decline through impaired glomerular filtration and albuminuria. The analysis revealed non-linear, threshold-based relationships, with critical acceleration thresholds identified at BioAgeAccel > 2.13 years and homeostatic dysregulation > 3.16 units. These findings advocate for integrating biological aging assessment into DKD risk stratification frameworks to better identify high-risk individuals beyond conventional chronological age and metabolic risk factors.

title: Ovarian reserve and stimulation response before chemotherapy treatment in breast cancer patients undergoing fertility preservation: a historical cohort study
summary:
# Summary

Breast cancer patients undergoing fertility preservation demonstrate substantially compromised ovarian reserve and diminished ovarian stimulation response compared to controls, characterized by significantly lower anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) levels, reduced antral follicle counts, and fewer mature oocytes despite requiring higher gonadotropin doses. Breast cancer itself appears to directly impair ovarian function through effects on follicular growth and oocyte quality, independent of chemotherapy, though chemotherapy further exacerbates gonadotoxicity and reduces ovarian reserves through direct effects on primordial and growing follicles. Hormonally dependent breast cancers show worse ovarian stimulation outcomes than hormone-independent malignancies, and adjuvant aromatase inhibitor therapy with letrozole further compromises oocyte maturation by reducing estradiol levels and promoting immature oocyte accumulation. AMH emerged as the primary predictor of metaphase II oocyte numbers in breast cancer patients, making it a critical biomarker for assessing reproductive capacity in this population. These findings demonstrate that cancer-related mechanisms—including stress-induced hypothalamic dysfunction, malnutrition, and catabolic states—compromise fertility potential prior to and independent of chemotherapy treatment.

title: Diagnostic value of leucine-rich alpha-2-glycoprotein 1 and calprotectin in acute appendicitis: a short review
summary:
# Summary

This review evaluates leucine-rich alpha-2-glycoprotein 1 (LRG1) and calprotectin as biomarkers for acute appendicitis diagnosis. Both proteins are acute-phase inflammatory markers produced at sites of inflammation by neutrophils, hepatocytes, and endothelial cells, and they participate in immune responses through chemotaxis and cell activation. Serum LRG1 demonstrates superior specificity (>80% AUC) compared to traditional markers like C-reactive protein and white blood cell count, while calprotectin offers enhanced sensitivity, particularly in plasma and fecal samples. The authors note that combining these biomarkers with clinical scoring systems may improve diagnostic accuracy, though salivary and urinary LRG1 show inconsistent performance. Notably, the review identifies endometriosis as a confounding pelvic condition contributing to false-positive appendicitis diagnoses in women of reproductive age, highlighting the clinical importance of accurate biomarker-based differentiation.

title: Correlation between serum AMH, AFC, AND histologically quantified primordial follicles: a prospective study in premenopausal women
summary:
# Summary

This prospective study establishes serum anti-mullerian hormone (AMH) and antral follicle count (AFC) as reliable biomarkers of ovarian reserve by demonstrating strong positive correlations with histologically quantified primordial follicles (ρ = 0.75 and 0.85, respectively). AMH, secreted by granulosa cells of preantral and small antral follicles, functions to inhibit premature recruitment of resting follicles into the growth phase, thereby preserving the primordial follicle pool. Both AMH and AFC show strong inverse correlations with follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) and moderate inverse correlations with estradiol, reflecting their role in regulating follicular sensitivity to gonadotropins. Age-associated decline in ovarian reserve was evident, with advancing age negatively correlating with both AMH and primordial follicle counts. Multivariable regression analysis confirmed that both AMH and AFC independently predict primordial follicle count after adjusting for age, BMI, and smoking history, supporting their clinical application in fertility assessment and assisted reproductive technology planning.

title: Long‐Term Adherence to Modifiable Factors and Incident Cardiovascular Disease Among Women With a History of Gestational Diabetes: A Prospective Cohort Study
journal: Journal of the American Heart Association: Cardiovascular and Cerebrovascular Disease
summary: Women with a history of gestational diabetes face substantially elevated risk for cardiovascular disease, partly mediated through progression to type 2 diabetes and hypertensive disorders of pregnancy. Key modifiable risk factors including obesity, smoking, and physical inactivity independently elevate CVD risk, with current smoking conferring approximately 2.7-fold increased hazard compared to never smokers. Long-term adherence to optimal modifiable factors—smoking avoidance, weight control, regular physical activity, healthy diet quality, and moderate alcohol consumption—demonstrates a powerful protective effect, with women maintaining all five optimal factors experiencing zero incident CVD events in this cohort. The study found a dose-dependent relationship where each additional optimal factor adopted reduced CVD risk, while conversely, decreasing the number of optimal factors approximately doubled CVD hazard. Beyond behavioral factors, family history of premature CVD and personal history of hypertensive disorders of pregnancy independently conferred approximately twofold greater absolute risk. These findings underscore that for women with prior gestational diabetes, sustained commitment to comprehensive lifestyle modifications represents a critical strategy for primary CVD prevention.

title: Improving Access to Primordial and Primary Stroke Prevention: Global Considerations
journal: Journal of the American Heart Association: Cardiovascular and Cerebrovascular Disease
summary: This article identifies multiple modifiable and non-modifiable risk factors for stroke across global populations, with particular emphasis on sex-specific vulnerabilities in women. Hypertension emerges as the strongest risk factor for both hemorrhagic and ischemic stroke across all regions, while additional modifiable factors including diabetes, obesity, dyslipidemia, smoking, physical inactivity, and poor diet substantially elevate stroke risk. Women face distinct sex-specific stroke risks including hypertension in pregnancy, hormonal contraception use, and conditions affecting ovarian function such as premature ovarian failure and early menopause, alongside the general endometriosis-stroke association. Environmental and psychosocial factors such as household air pollution, ambient air pollution, and psychosocial stress contribute to stroke burden, particularly in low-income countries. The SIREN study demonstrates that nearly 98% of adjusted stroke risk is attributable to eleven potentially modifiable factors, suggesting that primordial and primary prevention through lifestyle and metabolic management could substantially reduce global stroke incidence. Post-stroke complications including dementia development within five years highlight the broader neurological consequences requiring comprehensive prevention strategies.

title: Abdominal pressure pain thresholds correlate with postoperative pain intensity
summary: This study demonstrates that preoperative abdominal pressure pain thresholds, measured by algometry, are significantly correlated with postoperative pain intensity in bariatric surgery patients. Lower preoperative mechanical pain thresholds—indicating heightened mechanical sensitization—were associated with higher postoperative pain scores, with visual analog scale (VAS) scores showing a significant inverse correlation with algometric measurements (Spearman r = −0.35, p < 0.0001). The underlying mechanism involves inflammatory mediators activating nociceptors and increasing neuronal excitability, leading to hyperalgesia and reduced pain thresholds in both locally injured tissues and remote body areas. These findings suggest that algometry provides a valuable clinical tool for identifying patients at risk for severe postoperative pain and potentially predicting analgesic requirements following abdominal surgery. The results have implications for endometriosis and other chronic pain conditions where central sensitization and altered pain processing contribute to disease-related suffering.

title: Knowledge, Attitudes, and Practices of Elderly Diabetic Patients and Their Families Regarding Comorbid Non-Alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease: A Structural Equation Modeling Analysis
summary: This study examined the knowledge, attitudes, and practices (KAP) of elderly diabetic patients and their families regarding comorbid non-alcoholic fatty liver disease using structural equation modeling. Type 2 diabetes mellitus is strongly associated with NAFLD development through multiple pathways: dysregulated lipid metabolism leads to hepatic fat accumulation, insulin resistance contributes significantly to NAFLD progression, and diabetes substantially increases the risk of NAFLD advancing to non-alcoholic steatohepatitis. Using the KAP model, the researchers found that knowledge, attitude, and practice are interconnected, with knowledge directly influencing both attitudes and health behaviors, while attitudes most strongly predict actual practice (β = 0.64). Education level emerged as a critical determinant, directly shaping knowledge and indirectly affecting both attitudes and practices through these intermediate pathways. Multiple demographic and clinical factors—including age, BMI, NAFLD status, medication use, and comorbidities—significantly influence all three KAP components, suggesting that effective health interventions for elderly diabetic patients with NAFLD must be tailored to individual characteristics and disease status.

title: Association between high-density lipoprotein cholesterol-related inflammatory markers and female infertility: a cross-sectional analysis from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES 2013–2018)
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional analysis of NHANES data (2013–2018) demonstrates that HDL-cholesterol-related inflammatory markers are significantly associated with female infertility in reproductive-aged women. The study found that women with infertility had elevated levels of lymphocyte-to-HDL ratio (LHR), monocyte-to-HDL ratio (MHR), and neutrophil-to-HDL ratio (NHR), alongside higher circulating lymphocytes and monocytes but lower HDL-cholesterol compared to fertile women. These inflammatory markers reflect an underlying pathophysiology where chronic inflammation and metabolic dysregulation disturb the maternal microenvironment, impair endocrine homeostasis, and disrupt normal reproductive function. Multiple lifestyle and clinical factors including advanced age, obesity, diabetes, smoking, and alcohol consumption were also associated with infertility risk. Age-stratified analyses revealed that the PHR and MHR associations with infertility were particularly pronounced in women aged 35 years or younger, suggesting inflammation-related reproductive dysfunction may be especially relevant in younger reproductive-aged populations.

title: Adherence to a healthy lifestyle in association with female infertility risk: the mediating role of uric acid
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that adherence to a healthy lifestyle substantially reduces female infertility risk, with women adopting five to six healthy lifestyle factors exhibiting a 70% lower infertility risk compared to those with fewer factors. The protective effect operates partially through reducing serum uric acid levels, which mediate approximately 9.6% of the lifestyle-infertility association. Elevated serum uric acid—a biomarker of systemic oxidative stress and inflammation—independently increases infertility risk by 31% per standard deviation increment and is notably elevated in women with endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, and hyperandrogenaemia. Among specific lifestyle interventions, maintaining optimal waist circumference and avoiding tobacco smoking emerged as the most impactful modifiable factors for infertility prevention. The beneficial effects of healthy lifestyle adherence were particularly pronounced in younger women under 30 years and those with no prior pregnancies, suggesting that early intervention may offer maximal protection against infertility and its associated complications including metabolic disorders and cardiovascular disease.

title: Gaps in Vitamin D Intake and Status in Moroccan Women
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates widespread vitamin D deficiency among Moroccan premenopausal women, with median dietary intake of 2.89 µg/day—far below the WHO/FAO recommendation of 5 µg/day—and 66% exhibiting serum 25(OH)D concentrations below 20 ng/mL. Vitamin D plays critical roles in calcium-phosphate homeostasis and immune regulation, while deficiency is associated with multiple reproductive complications including endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, and spontaneous miscarriage. Fish and seafood products constitute the primary dietary source (48%), followed by dairy (24%) and meat products (9%), yet higher dietary vitamin D intake showed only modest associations with serum 25(OH)D status. Sun exposure emerged as the strongest predictor of serum vitamin D concentrations, while higher body mass index and lower socioeconomic status were independent contributors to deficiency, explaining why vitamin D deficiency persisted even across higher intake quartiles. These findings highlight that dietary strategies alone are insufficient to address vitamin D deficiency in this population without concurrently addressing modifiable factors such as sun exposure and body weight.

title: Does the Type of Cancer Influence Anti-Müllerian Hormone Levels in Women of Reproductive Age? A Cross-Sectional Study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study examined how different cancer types affect anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels, a key marker of ovarian reserve produced by granulosa cells in growing follicles. Cancer treatments, particularly chemotherapy and radiotherapy, cause substantial ovarian damage through depletion of growing and primordial follicles, leading to reduced AMH levels, amenorrhea, and premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) in approximately 68% of cancer survivors. The study found that lymphoma patients showed significantly lower AMH levels both before and after treatment compared to healthy controls, suggesting the disease itself impairs ovarian reserve independently of chemotherapy, while breast cancer patients demonstrated variable AMH responses. Additionally, carriers of BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutations exhibited lower AMH levels and earlier menopause onset due to impaired DNA repair pathways essential for preserving follicular pools. Inflammatory cytokines (SIL-2R, IL-6, IL-8) showed strong negative correlations with AMH levels in lymphoma patients, indicating an inflammatory mechanism contributing to ovarian reserve depletion. Pre-treatment AMH levels, age, and chemotherapy regimen emerged as critical predictors of post-treatment ovarian recovery and POI risk, with low post-treatment AMH strongly associated with increased POI likelihood, emphasizing the importance of AMH monitoring in cancer survivors of reproductive age.

title: A Comparative Analysis of Gremlin-1 (GREM1), Hyaluronic Acid Synthetase-2 (HAS2), and Prostaglandin-Endoperoxide Synthase-2 (PTGS2) Expression in Cumulus Cells Among Women with Diminished Ovarian Reserve Following Rescue In Vitro Maturation (r-IVM)
summary:
# Summary

This study examined gene expression patterns in cumulus cells from women with diminished ovarian reserve (DOR) versus normal ovarian reserve (NOR) undergoing rescue in vitro maturation (r-IVM) to improve fertility outcomes. The researchers analyzed three key genes—GREM1, PTGS2, and HAS2—that are regulated by the TGF-beta signaling pathway and associated with oocyte quality. Women in the DOR cohort showed significantly upregulation of all three genes following r-IVM, whereas the NOR cohort demonstrated downregulation of PTGS2 and GREM1, suggesting distinct molecular responses to in vitro maturation between groups. Despite lower baseline oocyte numbers and maturation rates in vivo, DOR women achieved improved embryo quality and higher pregnancy rates with r-IVM, indicating that this technique effectively rescues otherwise unusable immature oocytes. The findings suggest that r-IVM can provide meaningful fertility benefits for DOR patients by optimizing the cumulus-oocyte complex microenvironment through hormonally regulated gene expression patterns, with potential implications for women with endometriosis-associated infertility and other causes of compromised ovarian reserve.

title: The mediating role of BMI in the association between composite dietary antioxidant index and infertility in women: a nationwide population-based study
summary:
# Summary

This nationwide population-based study demonstrates that higher dietary antioxidant intake, measured by the Composite Dietary Antioxidant Index (CDAI), is associated with reduced female infertility risk, with each unit increase in CDAI decreasing infertility likelihood by 11%. Body mass index (BMI) plays a critical mediating role in this relationship, partially explaining how antioxidant-rich diets protect fertility. Infertile women exhibited significantly lower intakes of key antioxidants including vitamins A, D, and E, as well as α-carotene, zinc, and selenium. Obesity independently increases infertility prevalence by 6% per BMI unit through multiple mechanisms, including interference with the hypothalamus-pituitary-ovarian axis, impaired oocyte quality, and reduced endometrial receptivity. The mediating effect of BMI was particularly pronounced in women aged 20–40 years (approximately 10%), suggesting that weight management may be essential for optimizing the fertility benefits of antioxidant-rich dietary patterns in reproductive-age women.

title: Social isolation and cardiometabolic burden synergistically predict physical dysfunction in aging Chinese adults: Evidence of risk thresholds and the mediating role of frailty
summary: This study demonstrates that social isolation and cardiometabolic burden synergistically increase physical dysfunction risk in aging Chinese adults through interconnected pathophysiological pathways. Social isolation independently elevates dysfunction risk by 38% and operates through activation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, promoting insulin resistance and muscle breakdown, while advanced cardiovascular-kidney-metabolic syndrome (stage 4) increases dysfunction risk nearly 5-fold. Vascular stiffness, measured by estimated pulse wave velocity, and declining renal function (eGFR) emerge as critical risk factors, with frailty mediating 58% of the vascular stiffness effect on dysfunction, establishing it as a central mechanistic pathway. The pathological cascade originates from adipose tissue dysfunction, which triggers chronic inflammation, oxidative stress, and endothelial damage, ultimately compromising metabolic homeostasis and driving multiple organ dysfunction. These findings identify specific risk thresholds—including a pulse wave velocity inflection point at 7.178 m/s and eGFR threshold at 70.958 mL/min/1.73m²—where dysfunction risk accelerates substantially, with physical dysfunction subsequently predicting disability, reduced quality of life, and all-cause mortality.

title: Divergent Survival Outcomes With Adjuvant Chemotherapy in Stage IA Ovarian Clear Cell Carcinoma: Insights From the SEER Database
summary: This SEER database study reveals striking age-dependent divergence in adjuvant chemotherapy efficacy for stage IA ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC), a malignancy strongly associated with endometriosis. While patients aged ≤50 years experienced significantly worse cancer-specific and overall survival with chemotherapy despite the tumor's known chemotherapy resistance, those aged >70 years derived substantial survival benefits from the same treatment. Beyond age, several factors independently influenced prognosis: extended lymph node dissection (>10 nodes) and married status improved survival, whereas elevated CA-125 levels, higher tumor grade, and right-sided tumor location predicted worse outcomes. The paradoxical age-chemotherapy interaction suggests that tumor biology, immune competency, or cancer stem cell presence differs substantially between younger and older patients, indicating that treatment decisions in early-stage OCCC should incorporate age-stratified prognostic considerations rather than applying uniform chemotherapy protocols.

title: Clinical, Metabolic, and Sonographic Predictors of Endometrial Carcinoma Among Women Presenting With Postmenopausal Bleeding: A Prospective Observational Study From a Tertiary Care Center in South India
summary:
# Summary

This prospective study identifies critical clinical and metabolic predictors of endometrial carcinoma in postmenopausal women with abnormal bleeding. Obesity, metabolic syndrome, diabetes, and hypertension emerge as strong risk factors, mechanistically linked through increased peripheral estrogen production via aromatase activity in adipose tissue and metabolic dysregulation including hyperinsulinemia and chronic inflammation. Reproductive factors including early menarche, late menopause, and nulliparity prolonged lifetime estrogen exposure and significantly elevated carcinoma risk. Transvaginal sonography demonstrating endometrial thickness greater than 5 mm, combined with histopathological findings of atypical hyperplasia and endometrioid adenocarcinoma, provided reliable diagnostic markers. The study emphasizes that lifestyle modifications including weight reduction and management of metabolic comorbidities may substantially mitigate endometrial cancer risk, highlighting the importance of early recognition and intervention in high-risk populations.

title: Age Stratified Analyses of TyG Index eGDR and Additive Effects on T2DM Outcomes in Prevalence and Mortality
summary:
# Summary

This study examines the triglyceride-glucose (TyG) index and estimated glucose disposal rate (eGDR) as biomarkers for type 2 diabetes mellitus risk and mortality outcomes across different age groups. Both markers reflect insulin resistance, with the TyG index incorporating fasting triglyceride and glucose levels to assess hepatic and peripheral insulin sensitivity, while eGDR provides a comprehensive assessment integrating blood pressure, BMI, and HbA1c. The findings reveal that high TyG index and low eGDR demonstrate a strong combined association with elevated type 2 diabetes risk in a dose-dependent manner, with a J-shaped relationship for TyG and an inverse L-shaped relationship for eGDR with all-cause mortality. Notably, younger patients with type 2 diabetes exhibit more severe metabolic dysfunction, characterized by higher TyG levels, lower eGDR values, elevated BMI, triglycerides, LDL-cholesterol, and fasting blood glucose, despite lower HDL-cholesterol levels. Age-stratified analyses demonstrate that the risk threshold for TyG is age-dependent, occurring at lower values (8.53) in younger individuals compared to older adults, suggesting that younger diabetic patients experience heightened insulin resistance-driven metabolic complications. These findings underscore the utility of combined TyG and eGDR assessment as powerful predictive markers for type 2 diabetes outcomes and mortality risk across diverse age populations.

title: Association of infertility treatments and incidence of stroke among women: a systematic review
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review examined the association between infertility treatments and stroke risk in women, finding conflicting evidence that prevents conclusive determination of causality. Infertility treatments—including assisted reproductive technology (ART), in vitro fertilization (IVF), and medications such as gonadotropins, human chorionic gonadotropin, and clomiphene citrate—may increase stroke risk through multiple mechanisms, particularly by inducing hypercoagulability that leads to both arterial and venous thrombosis. Underlying conditions associated with infertility, such as polycystic ovary syndrome, endometriosis, and premature ovarian insufficiency, independently contribute to stroke risk, while complications like ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome and preeclampsia further elevate cardiovascular danger. While most studies reported hazard ratios ranging from 0.82 to 1.66, one investigation (Udell et al. 2013) found significantly higher stroke risk, whereas other studies reported marginally elevated but statistically insignificant associations, and notably, one large cohort study suggested infertility medications might paradoxically reduce ischemic stroke risk. The review emphasizes that distinguishing between stroke risk attributable to background infertility conditions, the direct effects of infertility medications and procedures, and confounding factors such as obesity, smoking, and hypertension remains methodologically challenging and requires further investigation.

title: Self-reported infertility and longitudinal measures of cardiovascular risk factors: The CARDIA study
summary:
# Summary

The CARDIA longitudinal study demonstrates that cardiovascular risk factors are significantly associated with self-reported infertility in women, with cigarette smoking emerging as the strongest predictor (odds ratio 1.85). Beyond smoking, elevated body mass index, glucose, and triglycerides, alongside reduced high-density lipoprotein cholesterol, are independently associated with infertility. The mechanisms linking these risk factors to reduced fertility include smoking-induced toxic effects on ovarian granulosa cells and impaired ovarian reserve, as well as disrupted HDL metabolism affecting oocyte quality and preimplantation embryo development. Among women seeking medical care for infertility, irregular menstrual cycles, uterine abnormalities, and endometriosis were the most commonly reported etiologies. These findings suggest that abnormal cardiovascular risk factor profiles during reproductive years, particularly smoking and lipid dysregulation, represent modifiable risk factors contributing to infertility and highlight the interconnected nature of reproductive and cardiovascular health.

title: Diet and female fertility: a population-based study re-evaluating the need for prescriptive dietary patterns
summary:
# Summary

This population-based study examined how dietary patterns influence female fertility by analyzing the inflammatory potential of diets and their association with self-reported fertility problems. The research identified inflammation as a key mechanistic pathway linking diet to infertility, with higher dietary inflammatory index (E-DII) scores strongly associated with increased odds of fertility problems, while higher dietary quality scores and Mediterranean-style dietary patterns were protective. Diets characterized by pro-inflammatory components—including processed meats, ultra-processed foods, refined carbohydrates, and saturated fats—were associated with worse fertility outcomes, whereas anti-inflammatory diets rich in fruits, vegetables, whole grains, legumes, nuts, and healthy fats improved fertility outcomes. Individuals in the highest E-DII quartile consumed significantly more total fat, saturated fat, sugars, red and processed meats, and refined grains, while those in the lowest quartile had higher intakes of fiber, whole grains, vegetables, fruits, and micronutrients like magnesium and zinc. The findings suggest that adherence to healthy eating guidelines with low inflammatory potential—rather than prescriptive dietary patterns—may effectively improve female fertility outcomes and help address the substantial emotional and quality-of-life impacts of infertility.

title: Impact of Cyclin-dependent Kinase Inhibitor 1A Genotypes on Prostate Cancer Susceptibility Prediction
summary:
# Summary

The CDKN1A gene encodes the p21 protein, a critical regulator of cell cycle control and DNA damage response whose genetic variants influence prostate cancer susceptibility in age- and smoking-dependent manner. Specifically, individuals under age 55 carrying the rs1801270 AC and AA genotypes showed significantly increased risk of early-onset prostate cancer, while the rs1059234 CT and TT genotypes conferred protective effects against prostate cancer in non-smokers, likely through alterations in mRNA stability. Although neither CDKN1A polymorphism serves as a general predictive marker for overall prostate cancer susceptibility, both variants demonstrate utility for risk stratification within specific demographic subgroups. The study highlights how environmental factors like smoking status and demographic characteristics such as age interact with genetic polymorphisms to modulate disease risk, underscoring the importance of personalized, context-dependent approaches to cancer susceptibility prediction.

title: Influence of discordant tubal blockage on clinical pregnancy rates: a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study examines how discordant tubal blockage (DTB)—where one fallopian tube remains patent while the other shows occlusion—affects fertility outcomes in women undergoing assisted reproductive procedures. DTB was strongly associated with reduced clinical pregnancy rates (21.1% versus 47.4% in controls with bilateral patency), independent of successful tubal cannulation, suggesting that achieving patency alone does not guarantee restored fertility. The study identifies multiple mechanisms underlying poor outcomes in DTB patients, including myometrial contractions and pelvic pathology such as endometriosis and adhesions that can create false positive occlusions, as well as persistent ciliary damage and intrinsic tubal dysfunction that cannulation cannot repair. Additionally, younger age and bilateral tubal patency emerged as independent positive predictors of pregnancy success in multivariate analysis. The findings emphasize that tubal patency must be distinguished from tubal function, and that women with prior tubal occlusion or evidence of pelvic pathology warrant careful counseling regarding reduced fecundity despite anatomic restoration.

title: Unexplained infertility’s determinants, correlation with bacterial vaginosis and anti-sperm antibodies among Congolese women: a case‒control study
summary: This case-control study of Congolese women identified multiple determinants of unexplained infertility spanning sociodemographic, behavioral, and clinical domains. Bacterial vaginosis and anti-sperm antibodies emerged as the strongest clinical correlates, with positive anti-sperm antibodies showing the highest odds ratio (14.9) for unexplained infertility, followed closely by bacterial vaginosis (8.91). Behavioral risk factors included sex with casual partners, unsafe abortion, premature menarche, vaginal use of indigenous products, and alcohol consumption, while sociodemographic factors such as higher education level, resourcefulness, obesity, and polygamous union status significantly increased infertility risk. The study reveals that unexplained infertility in this population is multifactorial, with immunological disturbances (anti-sperm antibodies) and reproductive tract infections (bacterial vaginosis) playing central roles alongside lifestyle and social determinants. These findings underscore the importance of comprehensive screening and intervention addressing both biological mechanisms and socioeconomic factors in managing infertility among African women.

title: The effect of environmental stressors on anti-mullerian hormone levels in Lebanese women: a retrospective study
summary: This retrospective study demonstrates that environmental stressors significantly reduce anti-mullerian hormone (AMH) levels in Lebanese women, particularly those with pre-existing reproductive disorders. AMH, produced by ovarian granulosa cells, serves as the primary marker of ovarian reserve and reproductive capacity. The study found that women exposed to major stressful events showed substantially lower AMH levels post-stress compared to pre-stress measurements, with the decline being especially pronounced in patients with PCOS (nearly 50% reduction) and primary infertility. Mechanistically, stress-induced glucocorticoids inhibit hypothalamic gonadotropin-releasing hormone secretion, disrupting the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis and suppressing follicular maturation, while chronic psychological stress directly downregulates AMH protein expression in ovarian tissue. Women with PCOS and primary infertility demonstrated heightened vulnerability to stress-related AMH decline due to their baseline increased anxiety, depression, and psychological distress, whereas those with secondary infertility showed no significant stress-related AMH changes. These findings establish psychological stress as a modifiable factor affecting ovarian reserve and reproductive potential in women with existing fertility disorders.

title: Educational Attainment as the Primary Socioeconomic Determinant of Heart Failure
summary: This study identifies educational attainment as the primary socioeconomic determinant protecting against heart failure risk, demonstrating a robust inverse association where each additional 4.2 years of education reduces heart failure risk by approximately 32%. The protective effect of education operates through multiple interconnected pathways involving modifiable risk factors, particularly myocardial infarction (which mediates 61.4% of the education-heart failure association), body mass index, and body fat percentage. Higher educational attainment is associated with favorable modifications across numerous cardiovascular and metabolic risk factors including smoking cessation, improved sleep duration, reduced triglyceride levels, lower blood pressure, and decreased prevalence of metabolic disorders, hypercholesterolemia, and coronary artery disease. Notably, while intelligence, cognitive function, household income, and socioeconomic deprivation all independently show associations with heart failure risk, these relationships become statistically insignificant after adjusting for educational attainment, establishing education as the dominant socioeconomic factor in heart failure pathogenesis. These findings underscore that the cardioprotective effects of higher education are primarily mediated through behavioral and physiological improvements rather than through other measures of socioeconomic status, suggesting that educational interventions and health literacy may represent key modifiable targets for reducing population-level heart failure burden.

title: Impact of Latent Tuberculosis Infection on Ovarian Reserve and In Vitro Fertilization or Intracytoplasmic Sperm Injection Outcomes: A Retrospective Cohort Study with Propensity Score Matching
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study demonstrates that latent tuberculosis infection (LTBI) significantly compromises ovarian reserve through immune-mediated mechanisms. Women with LTBI showed substantially lower anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) and antral follicle count (AFC) levels compared to controls, with multivariate analysis confirming LTBI as an independent risk factor for these reductions. The pathophysiology involves localized ovarian fibrosis and vascular compromise caused by LTBI-associated immune activation, potentially accelerating pathological ovarian aging and reducing oocyte quantity and quality. Notably, while LTBI impairs ovarian reserve markers and is associated with higher rates of pelvic/tubal and uterine factors in infertile women, it does not significantly reduce live birth rates or cumulative live birth rates following fresh or frozen embryo transfer when women achieve appropriate endometrial conditions.
