The discrepancy between diminished ovarian reserve and preserved IVF/ICSI outcomes likely reflects the low prevalence of active genital tuberculosis (0.9% in this cohort) and the ability of standardized anti-TB treatment to improve clinical outcomes, suggesting that LTBI's primary impact on fertility operates through ovarian damage rather than compromising embryo implantation potential.

title: Plasma Semaphorin 3A as a Potential Biomarker for Cognitive Impairment in Cerebral Small Vessel Disease
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies plasma Semaphorin 3A (Sema3A) as a potential biomarker for cognitive impairment in cerebral small vessel disease (CSVD), particularly in acute ischemic stroke patients. Sema3A, a secreted protein with established roles in axon guidance, neuronal development, and inflammatory regulation, was significantly elevated during the acute phase of recent small subcortical infarction (RSSI) compared to healthy controls, and levels correlated strongly with cognitive impairment severity across multiple cognitive domains. The elevated acute-phase Sema3A levels associated with higher CSVD burden and cognitive impairment appear to operate through white matter hyperintensities and lacunar infarcts, mechanisms supported by Sema3A's known capacity to promote neuronal apoptosis, amplify inflammation, and impair blood-brain barrier integrity. Notably, downregulation of Sema3A expression reduced blood-brain barrier disruption, brain edema, and neurological deficits in experimental models, suggesting potential therapeutic targeting. These findings establish Sema3A as both a mechanistic link between vascular brain injury and cognitive decline and a promising diagnostic marker for stratifying CSVD patients at risk for cognitive complications.

title: Editorial: Vascular dysfunction and endocrine disorders
summary: This editorial examines the interconnected relationships between vascular dysfunction and endocrine disorders, with particular emphasis on metabolic and reproductive health complications. Metabolic syndrome emerges as a central pathology, associated with a 2.56-fold increased risk of subclinical hypothyroidism and exacerbated by vitamin D deficiency, which impairs endothelial function and increases oxidative stress. Several reproductive conditions—including gestational diabetes mellitus and preeclampsia—demonstrate direct vascular consequences, with preeclampsia showing downregulation of key vascular genes (ELMO1, YWHAE, IL6ST) that impair both vasoconstriction and vasodilation. Prenatal glucocorticoid exposure represents a critical developmental programming mechanism, leading to long-term offspring hypertension and vascular dysfunction through disruption of placental protective mechanisms. Type 2 diabetes drives multiple vascular complications through chronic hyperglycemia-induced thromboinflammation and reduced cerebral blood flow, with elevated HbA1c independently predicting hypercoagulability and inflammation. The editorial highlights emerging therapeutic opportunities, notably platelet-rich plasma treatment for improving clinical pregnancy rates and endometrial thickness, while vitamin D supplementation offers potential mitigation of metabolic syndrome-related vascular damage.

title: Association between serum uric acid to high-density lipoprotein cholesterol ratio and female infertility in women aged 18 to 45 years: A study based on NHANES 2013 to 2018
summary:
# Summary

This NHANES-based study demonstrates that the uric acid to HDL-cholesterol ratio (UHR) is a significant marker of infertility risk in reproductive-aged women, with elevated UHR associated with substantially increased odds of infertility across all quartiles compared to the lowest quartile. The elevated UHR reflects multiple interconnected pathological mechanisms—systemic inflammation, oxidative stress, and insulin resistance—that contribute to female infertility through chronic immune dysregulation and endocrine disruption. Infertile women were characterized by higher age, body mass index, serum uric acid levels, and log-transformed UHR values, with associations particularly strong in younger women. The study highlights how uric acid promotes oxidative stress and inflammation while low HDL-cholesterol impairs anti-inflammatory and antioxidant capacity, together compromising immune homeostasis maintained by macrophages, natural killer cells, T cells, and dendritic cells. These inflammatory pathways implicate multiple infertility-related conditions including polycystic ovary syndrome, endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, implantation failure, and recurrent miscarriage through elevated cytokines such as interleukin-6, TNF-α, and transforming growth factor-beta 1. The findings suggest that UHR may serve as a practical clinical biomarker for identifying reproductive-aged women at elevated cardiometabolic and infertility risk.

title: Impact of male factors on morphokinetic parameters: a prospective analysis using time-lapse monitored embryos
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary:
# Summary

This prospective study using time-lapse embryo monitoring demonstrates that paternal factors significantly influence early embryonic development and fertility outcomes. Advanced male age and elevated male BMI were independently associated with delayed embryo morphokinetics across multiple developmental milestones, including pronuclei appearance, cleavage timing (t2, t3, t4, t6), and blastomere formation, even after adjusting for female factors such as age, BMI, and anti-Müllerian hormone. Sperm concentration also showed direct associations with specific morphokinetic parameters, while sperm DNA fragmentation exhibited weaker correlations with overall embryo development. Beyond these direct observations, the study contextualizes findings showing that delayed blastocyst development correlates with increased cleavage anomalies, reduced implantation and pregnancy rates, and diminished live birth rates, while male obesity negatively impacts sperm quality and reproductive outcomes. These results suggest that paternal age and BMI warrant consideration as independent modifiable risk factors in assisted reproductive technology, with potential implications for counseling couples undergoing fertility treatment and understanding how male metabolic and age-related factors contribute to embryonic competence.

title: Apparent Diffusion Coefficient and Native T1 Mapping Histogram Analyses Reveal Tumor Proliferation and Microenvironment in Neuroblastoma Xenografts
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that advanced MRI techniques—apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) and native T1 mapping—can noninvasively characterize neuroblastoma tumor microenvironment and proliferation status. MYCN amplification drives tumor cell proliferation and aggressiveness, with MYCN-amplified cell lines (IMR-32 and SK-N-BE(2)) exhibiting higher Ki-67 expression and microvessel density compared to non-amplified lines. ADC values inversely correlate with tumor cellularity and Ki-67 expression across multiple histogram parameters, reflecting tumor proliferation rates. Native T1 mapping histogram features positively correlate with collagen volume fraction and extracellular matrix content, capturing fibrotic remodeling independent of cellularity. The extracellular matrix composition critically influences tumor behavior, with dense ECM organization associated with poor prognosis and loose structures with favorable outcomes. Together, combined ADC and T1 mapping analysis provides complementary, noninvasive biomarkers for assessing tumor aggressiveness, extracellular remodeling, and risk stratification in neuroblastoma xenografts.

title: Fertility preservation methods in children with the risk of female hypogonadism – possibilities and limitations
summary:
# Summary

Multiple conditions including cancer treatment, autoimmune diseases, infections, and genetic disorders can lead to female hypogonadism and infertility in children. Diagnosis of hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis failure relies on hormonal assessments including anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH), follicle-stimulating hormone, and luteinizing hormone, with AMH serving as a particularly sensitive biomarker for predicting premature ovarian insufficiency across different age groups. Ovarian tissue cryopreservation is currently the only established fertility preservation method available for prepubertal girls, with superior outcomes achieved through vitrification techniques and orthotopic graft placement. Transplantation of cryopreserved ovarian tissue successfully restores ovarian function in 85–100% of cases with pregnancy rates of 30–67%, and over 200 documented births have been achieved using this technique. The American Society for Reproductive Medicine formally recognized ovarian tissue cryopreservation as a non-experimental approach in 2019, establishing it as the standard of care for fertility preservation in children at risk of hypogonadism.

title: Analysis of the diagnostic value of peripheral blood immune inflammatory indicators of female bladder pain syndrome
summary:
# Summary

Female bladder pain syndrome is fundamentally associated with immune-related inflammation, characterized by distinct alterations in peripheral blood immune inflammatory indicators. Patients with FBPS demonstrate significantly elevated neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR), platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio (PLR), and systemic immune-inflammation index (SII), alongside reduced platelet and lymphocyte counts, with elevated NLR identified as an independent risk factor for disease development. The underlying mechanism involves chronic bladder inflammation that promotes neutrophil activation and vascular dilation, leading to extravasation of inflammatory factors and subsequent elevation of systemic inflammatory markers through increased oxidative stress and reactive oxygen species production. These peripheral blood inflammatory indicators, particularly NLR, SII, and PLR, demonstrate diagnostic value as auxiliary standards for FBPS identification and correlate with disease severity parameters such as reduced bladder capacity. The condition frequently overlaps with and is comorbid with multiple systemic diseases including endometriosis, urinary tract infections, and cardiovascular disorders, suggesting a broader inflammatory systemic component. Emerging therapeutic approaches including platelet-rich plasma intravesical injection aim to restore platelet-mediated tissue regeneration and modulate the inflammatory microenvironment in affected patients.

title: Association between healthy beverage index and odds of diminished ovarian reserve in a case-control study
summary:
# Summary

This case-control study examined the relationship between the Healthy Beverage Index (HBI) and diminished ovarian reserve (DOR), finding that women in the highest tertile of HBI scores exhibited an 8% reduction in odds of DOR. Women with DOR demonstrated significantly higher fat mass, waist circumference, and waist-to-hip ratio, alongside markedly lower anti-Müllerian hormone levels and antral follicle counts compared to controls, establishing these as key markers of ovarian reserve and female fertility. The study identifies multiple pathways through which poor beverage choices impair ovarian function, including sugar-sweetened beverages increasing insulin resistance, oxidative stress, and chronic inflammation, which collectively promote androgen synthesis and impaired oocyte development. Conversely, healthy beverages such as water and unsweetened coffee and tea increase satiety and reduce sugary drink consumption, thereby mitigating obesity-related reproductive dysfunction. Non-modifiable factors including age, genetic predisposition, and mutations in genes like BRCA1/2 and FMR1 also contribute to DOR, while modifiable lifestyle exposures including smoking, poor diet, and stress compound these risks. The findings suggest that optimizing beverage intake through adherence to the HBI represents a dietary intervention strategy to preserve ovarian reserve and reproductive outcomes.

title: Machine learning-based preliminary screening tool for clinical pregnancy prediction: towards management of IVF/ICSI stages
summary: This study developed machine learning models to predict clinical pregnancy outcomes in IVF/ICSI cycles by analyzing pre-treatment and treatment-phase parameters. Female age, antral follicle count, and body mass index emerged as the strongest pre-treatment predictors, while serum progesterone levels on HCG day, gonadotropin dosage, and endometrial thickness dominated the treatment phase model. The analysis revealed nonlinear threshold effects: pregnancy success increased when AFC exceeded 13 and endometrial thickness surpassed 12 millimeters, but declined when gonadotropin dosage exceeded 204 IU/day or when HCG-day estradiol and progesterone levels were excessively elevated. The XGBoost machine learning model demonstrated moderate discriminative ability (AUROC 0.617) for pregnancy prediction, with diminished performance in patients with diminished ovarian reserve, highlighting the complex interplay between ovarian reserve markers, hormonal profiles, and embryonic developmental potential in determining IVF success.

title: Electron microscopy visualization of cell-free mitochondrial DNA–containing extracellular vesicles in human plasma, serum, and saliva
summary:
# Summary

This electron microscopy study provides direct visualization of cell-free mitochondria contained within extracellular vesicles in human plasma, serum, and saliva, characterizing distinct particle types and their distribution across biofluids. The research identifies intact, whole mitochondria circulating in healthy individuals at concentrations between 200,000 and 3.7 million per milliliter of plasma, with multiple cellular sources including platelets and adipocytes contributing to the circulating mitochondrial pool. Crucially, the study demonstrates that cell-free mitochondrial DNA (cf-mtDNA) exhibits markedly different biological effects depending on its structural presentation: when encased within intact mitochondria, it appears protective and anti-inflammatory, but when fragmented and membrane-free, it associates with inflammatory conditions through TLR9 activation. The findings reveal dynamic regulation of extracellular mitochondria levels through physiological stimuli such as platelet activation and acute mental stress, supporting their roles in bioenergetic transfer, intercellular signaling, and cellular repair processes. These observations establish circulating extracellular mitochondria as promising biomarkers for various pathological states including depression, diabetes, cancer, and autoimmune diseases, while suggesting their therapeutic potential when introduced as intact structures.

title: Correlation between AMH levels and embryonic aneuploidy rate in PGT-A patients: a retrospective study
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective study of preimplantation genetic testing for aneuploidy (PGT-A) patients demonstrates that anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels are significantly associated with embryonic aneuploidy rates independent of maternal age. Lower AMH levels, reflecting reduced ovarian reserve and decreased oocyte quality, are associated with substantially higher risk of embryonic aneuploidy, with a 40% increase in positive aneuploidy risk for each unit decrease in AMH below 2.54 ng/mL. Patients stratified into higher AMH groups exhibited superior embryological outcomes across multiple parameters, including higher rates of normal fertilization, blastocyst formation, and euploid embryos, alongside markedly lower aneuploid embryo rates. The study identifies advanced maternal age, recurrent spontaneous abortion, and recurrent implantation failure as additional contributors to aneuploidy, though AMH remains an independent risk factor after adjusting for female age and other confounders. These findings suggest that AMH-based ovarian reserve assessment provides clinically relevant information for predicting embryonic chromosomal abnormalities and optimizing reproductive outcomes in assisted reproduction.

title: Determination of the optimal follicle size at the time of trigger in patients with diminished ovarian reserve undergoing ICSI cycle
summary:
# Summary

Diminished ovarian reserve (DOR) is characterized by reduced oocyte number and quality, leading to poor response to ovarian stimulation in assisted reproductive technology cycles. DOR patients exhibit accelerated follicular development, decreased antral follicle numbers, elevated early follicular phase FSH, and lower anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) levels, which reduce inhibition of FSH-induced aromatase activity and promote premature follicular recruitment. The study identifies an optimal follicle size of 15–17 mm at the time of human chorionic gonadotropin trigger, which maximizes cumulus-oocyte complex retrieval rates, metaphase II oocyte maturation, fertilization, and top-quality embryo formation in DOR patients. Triggering at this size avoids both the immature oocytes resulting from early trigger and the post-mature oocytes and premature ovulation associated with delayed triggering when follicles exceed 18 mm. Follicles larger than 18 mm show significantly lower fertilization and embryo quality rates due to follicular luteinization and elevated progesterone, which negatively impact pregnancy outcomes. This research demonstrates that precise timing of final oocyte maturation at the 15–17 mm follicle size range optimizes reproductive outcomes in the challenging DOR population.

title: The Role of Immature Granulocyte Percentage and Other Inflammatory Hematological Markers in Predicting In Vitro Fertilization Success
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies immature granulocyte (IG) percentage as a novel predictor of in vitro fertilization success, demonstrating that lower IG levels significantly correlate with improved clinical pregnancy and live birth outcomes. Elevated IG percentages reflect premature bone marrow release of granulocytes in response to systemic inflammation, which mirrors the immune-inflammatory state of the endometrium and impairs endometrial receptivity through dysregulation of local cytokine networks, impaired decidualization, and altered vascularization. This inflammatory imbalance interferes with trophoblast invasion and embryo implantation by disrupting the delicate immune tolerance required during pregnancy, where regulatory T cells and uterine natural killer cells normally maintain immunological balance. The study found that reduced systemic inflammatory burden, indicated by lower IG percentages and favorable endometrial thickness, creates an optimal microenvironment for successful oocyte maturation, embryo development, and implantation. These hematological inflammatory indices provide accessible biomarkers from routine complete blood counts that can help identify patients at risk of poor reproductive outcomes and guide personalized therapeutic interventions to modulate systemic inflammation.

title: Autoimmune Thyroid Disease and Female Fertility: Does Anti-TPO Accelerate Ovarian Aging?
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates whether anti-thyroid peroxidase (anti-TPO) antibodies accelerate ovarian aging in euthyroid women seeking fertility evaluation. Anti-TPO positivity, present in 17.6% of the cohort, was significantly associated with markers of diminished ovarian reserve: reduced anti-müllerian hormone (AMH), reduced antral follicle count (AFC), and elevated follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH). Path modeling confirmed that anti-TPO exerts direct, independent effects on ovarian reserve markers regardless of thyroid-stimulating hormone or body mass index levels. Notably, anti-TPO effects were strongest in younger (<35 years) and non-obese women, with age and body composition moderating the relationship; effects diminished substantially in women over 40 years old and were weaker in obese women. Anti-TPO ≥9 IU/mL emerged as a significant predictor of diminished ovarian reserve, with logistic regression confirming strong odds ratios for severe reserve depletion (AMH <1 ng/mL and AFC <5). The findings support anti-TPO as a potential biomarker for reproductive risk assessment and suggest that thyroid autoimmunity may impair folliculogenesis through immune-mediated mechanisms, particularly in younger reproductive years.

title: AMH Concentration and Gene Expression Level in Each Follicle and Their Relationship With Fertilization and Blastocyst Formation in Intracytoplasmic Sperm Injection
summary:
# Summary

This study examined anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) expression and concentration within individual follicles during intracytoplasmic sperm injection and their relationship to fertilization and embryo development. AMH, expressed by granulosa and cumulus cells, functions as an inhibitor of early follicular development and decreases progressively as follicles mature and approach ovulation. Notably, follicular fluid AMH concentrations were significantly lower in follicles containing fertilized oocytes compared to unfertilized oocytes, and lower intrafollicular AMH correlated with more advanced follicle development and higher estradiol levels, suggesting enhanced oocyte maturation. However, neither granulosa cell AMH mRNA expression nor follicular fluid AMH concentration reliably predicted blastocyst formation or clinical pregnancy success, indicating that AMH reflects follicle quantity rather than oocyte quality. The study found that follicular fluid AMH measurements were superior to granulosa cell AMH mRNA expression for evaluating intrafollicular AMH status, particularly under controlled ovarian stimulation, though both measurements showed greater variability than serum AMH levels.

title: Association between high-density lipoprotein-related inflammation index and female infertility in US reproductive-aged women: insights from NHANES 2013–2020
summary: This study demonstrates that impaired high-density lipoprotein (HDL) metabolism and elevated inflammatory markers are significantly associated with female infertility in reproductive-aged US women. Infertile women exhibited lower HDL-cholesterol levels and higher monocyte-to-HDL ratios compared to fertile women, indicating a pro-inflammatory state. HDL plays critical protective roles in fertility by interacting with immune cells (monocytes, neutrophils, lymphocytes, and platelets) to suppress inflammation through mechanisms including reduced monocyte adhesion, suppressed neutrophil extracellular trap formation, and regulated lymphocyte proliferation. The study identifies dysfunctional inflammation as a common pathological mechanism in endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, and premature ovarian insufficiency, all of which impair critical reproductive processes including ovulation, embryo implantation, and placentation. The monocyte-to-HDL ratio emerges as a potential composite biomarker for assessing the inflammatory burden underlying female infertility, and the findings support low-dose aspirin as a therapeutic intervention to reduce chronic inflammation and improve conception rates. These results underscore the interconnection between lipid metabolism, innate immunity, and reproductive health in infertile women.

title: Combined magnetic resonance imaging with serum CA125 for dysmenorrhea in adenomyosis
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that combined magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) with serum CA125 measurement effectively predicts dysmenorrhea severity in adenomyosis patients. Adenomyosis, characterized by ectopic endometrial invasion into the myometrium, causes dysmenorrhea through multiple interconnected mechanisms including inflammation, neurogenesis, angiogenesis, and myometrial contractile abnormalities mediated by inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-18) and prostaglandin synthesis. CA125, produced by the peritoneal epithelium and elevated in an inflammatory microenvironment, serves as a biomarker strongly associated with disease severity and dysmenorrhea intensity, with levels above 44.8 U/ml indicating increased dysmenorrhea risk in patients stratified by MRI-based severity classification. The diffuse adenomyosis subtype and larger lesion volumes showed significantly higher CA125 levels and symptom recurrence rates following focused ultrasound ablation surgery. These findings establish CA125 as a complementary clinical tool to MRI imaging for predicting dysmenorrhea outcomes and adenomyosis severity, offering clinicians an objective combined diagnostic and prognostic approach for this common uterine disorder.

title: Association between NHHR and endometriosis and the mediating role of the CALLY index: a cross-sectional study based on NHANES
summary: This cross-sectional study demonstrates a significant positive association between the non-HDL-C to HDL-C ratio (NHHR) and endometriosis risk, with each unit increase in NHHR linked to a 14.3% greater odds of endometriosis. The CALLY index—a composite inflammatory-nutritional marker derived from C-reactive protein, albumin, and lymphocyte counts—partially mediates this relationship, accounting for approximately 14% of the effect. The findings suggest that lipid metabolism dysfunction contributes to endometriosis pathogenesis, as NHHR simultaneously reflects both atherogenic and anti-atherogenic lipid profiles while serving as a marker of systemic inflammation. Higher NHHR quartiles correlated with elevated inflammatory markers (C-reactive protein and white blood cell count) and reduced albumin levels, establishing a mechanistic link between lipid dysregulation and immune-inflammatory abnormalities in endometriosis. The association appears particularly pronounced in younger individuals, suggesting age-dependent metabolic vulnerability. These results support dietary interventions targeting lipid management and anti-inflammatory nutrition as potential therapeutic strategies for reducing endometriosis prevalence.

title: The Effectiveness of the Multimodal Language Model, Google Gemini 2.5 Pro, in Solving the Specialization Exam in Gynecology and Obstetrics
summary: Google Gemini 2.5 Pro demonstrated exceptional performance on the Polish specialization exam in obstetrics and gynecology, achieving a score of 96.63% with 115 correct answers, substantially exceeding the passing threshold and performing comparably to or better than physicians taking the exam for the first time. The model showed superior performance on theoretical questions (97.19% accuracy) compared to clinical case questions (91.67%), indicating differential strengths across question types within gynecological and obstetric knowledge domains. However, a critical limitation emerged: Gemini 2.5 Pro exhibited poor metacognitive awareness, displaying similar confidence levels regardless of question complexity or correctness, and its confidence scores did not correlate with actual answer accuracy, raising concerns about the model's ability to reliably self-assess its own performance. These findings align with broader trends showing that newer AI model versions consistently achieve higher pass rates on medical specialty exams, though Gemini generally performs slightly lower than GPT-4 on comparative assessments. The study underscores AI's substantial potential to support specialized medical education and training while highlighting the necessity for careful validation of model confidence calibration in clinical decision-support contexts.

title: Automated ITSS extraction combined with R2* values obtained from enhanced T2*-weighted angiography in magnetic resonance imaging: a promising approach for differentiate cervical adenocarcinoma from squamous carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that automated intratumoral susceptibility signal (ITSS) extraction combined with R2* values from enhanced T2*-weighted angiography MRI effectively differentiates cervical adenocarcinoma (CA) from squamous carcinoma (CSC). Cervical adenocarcinoma exhibits significantly higher ITSS intensity (ITSSs), ITSS volume (ITSSv), and R2* values, while showing lower alpha-fetoprotein and prognostic nutritional index levels compared to squamous carcinoma. The elevated imaging biomarkers in CA reflect its more aggressive biological behavior, including enhanced vascular endothelial growth factor-A expression leading to extensive neovascularization with microhemorrhages and iron-rich deoxyhemoglobin accumulation. Both ITSSv and R2* emerged as independent risk predictors for CA diagnosis, and their combined assessment improved diagnostic accuracy. These findings suggest that ITSS-based MRI metrics provide non-invasive quantitative tools for cancer subtype differentiation and may help identify patients at higher risk for lymph node metastasis, distant metastases, and poor treatment response.

title: Immunological risk factors for recurrent implantation failure using a deep learning model: a multicenter retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This multicenter retrospective cohort study identified multiple immunological risk factors for recurrent implantation failure (RIF) using deep learning analysis. The research revealed that RIF is strongly associated with immune dysregulation, particularly an elevated Th1/Th2 ratio and multiple autoantibodies including anti-TPO, antinuclear antibodies, anti-dsDNA, and anti-phospholipid antibodies, which were identified as significant independent risk factors in multivariate analysis. Age, body mass index, and alterations in regulatory T cells, natural killer cells, and peripheral T-cell subsets also contribute substantially to RIF risk. The study demonstrated that immunomodulatory interventions such as tacrolimus and intravenous immunoglobulin increase live birth rates in patients with immunological conditions, while vitamin D supplementation enhances natural killer cell function and the Th1/Th2 ratio. These findings establish a comprehensive immunological profile for RIF and suggest that immune-targeted therapeutic strategies may improve outcomes in in vitro fertilization embryo transfer procedures, particularly when combined with lifestyle modifications addressing body mass index and stress.

title: Paternal ischemic heart disease and chance of successful pregnancy outcomes
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated whether paternal ischemic heart disease affects assisted reproductive technology (ART) outcomes and pregnancy success. Contrary to initial clinical concerns, paternal ischemic heart disease prior to oocyte retrieval was not significantly associated with reduced biochemical pregnancy, clinical pregnancy, or live birth rates. The findings contextualize paternal cardiovascular disease within a broader framework of reproductive risk factors, noting that ischemic heart disease involves atherosclerosis and chronic inflammation, conditions potentially affecting sperm quality and male fertility. The study identified multiple established maternal risk factors for pregnancy loss, including advanced maternal age, anatomical abnormalities, autoimmune disorders, and chromosomal abnormalities, alongside paternal factors such as varicocele, metabolic syndrome, and cardiovascular comorbidities that have been associated with adverse pregnancy outcomes. Notably, the research highlighted bidirectional health consequences: while pregnancy loss correlates with increased maternal cardiovascular risks including myocardial infarction and stroke, childlessness associates with cardiovascular mortality in men. The study also examined medications used to treat ischemic heart disease, finding that while aspirin may impair sperm motility, statins showed no significant negative impact on semen quality.

title: Inflammatory storm and metabolic disorders: unraveling heterogeneity in mortality risk for comorbid diabetes mellitus and heart failure via the C-reactive protein-triglyceride-glucose index
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that the combined C-reactive protein-triglyceride-glucose index (CTI) is a superior predictor of 30-day mortality in acute decompensated heart failure patients, particularly those with comorbid diabetes mellitus. Insulin resistance and chronic inflammation drive a pathological cascade in these patients, with insulin resistance directly causing myocardial fibrosis, diastolic dysfunction, and acute decompensation, while chronic inflammation amplifies insulin resistance through autocrine mechanisms in inflammatory cells. Patients with elevated CTI exhibited characteristic metabolic dysregulation including elevated fasting plasma glucose, triglycerides, and inflammatory markers (elevated white blood cell and platelet counts), alongside reduced high-density lipoprotein cholesterol, creating a distinctive inflammatory and metabolic phenotype. The CTI index emerged as the most important prognostic feature for mortality risk in this population, surpassing individual inflammatory or metabolic markers and conventional cardiac biomarkers. Patients with comorbid diabetes and acute heart failure showed particularly elevated CRP and triglyceride-glucose index levels, establishing metabolic disorder and systemic inflammation as interconnected drivers of mortality risk in this high-risk population.

title: Metabolomics Analysis-Based Machine Learning for Endometrial Cancer Diagnosis: Integration of Biomarker Discovery and Explainable Artificial Intelligence
journal: Journal of Clinical Practice and Research
summary:
# Summary

This study employed metabolomics combined with machine learning and explainable artificial intelligence to identify biomarkers for endometrial cancer (EC) diagnosis. Obesity drives 40–50% of EC cases through unopposed estrogen levels, which enhance lipid synthesis and alter glucose-fatty acid metabolism, triggering metabolic reprogramming characterized by elevated branched-chain amino acid consumption and dysregulated lipid synthesis. The research identified 25 statistically significant metabolites distinguishing EC patients, including key molecules involved in lipid dysregulation (m/z=219.125 supporting membrane biogenesis), altered steroid hormone pathways (m/z=672.6961), and oxidative stress (m/z=203.1564), alongside amino acid derivatives like tryptophan and kynurenine that facilitate cancer immune evasion. The LightGBM machine learning model achieved superior diagnostic performance with 0.888 accuracy and 0.955 ROC AUC, substantially outperforming conventional biomarkers like CA-125 (AUC 0.610–0.684). Through SHAP analysis, the study revealed which metabolites most influenced model predictions, demonstrating that integrating metabolomics with explainable AI provides clinically actionable insight into EC pathophysiology and diagnostic potential.

title: Prevalence of physically active females at risk for the female athlete triad in Spain
journal: Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition
summary:
# Summary

This study examined the prevalence of the female athlete triad (FAT)—a syndrome linking low energy availability, menstrual dysfunction, and compromised bone health—in Spanish physically active women. Low energy availability emerged as the central pathological driver, triggering a cascade of hormonal dysregulation including suppressed insulin and leptin alongside elevated cortisol and growth hormone, ultimately causing menstrual dysfunction, osteoporosis, endothelial dysfunction, and infertility. Athletes participating in leanness-emphasized sports and those with eating disorder symptoms demonstrated significantly elevated triad risk, with clinical eating disorder risk conferring a 3.08-fold increased likelihood of triad diagnosis based on the LEAF-Q screening questionnaire. The study revealed that menstrual abnormalities were substantially more prevalent in at-risk participants and that increased training hours and lower body weight further stratified risk, while notably, hormonal contraceptives were commonly used to manage triad-related manifestations including amenorrhea, dysmenorrhea, and endometriosis in this cohort. These findings underscore the interconnected nature of energy balance, reproductive dysfunction, and metabolic health in female athletes, highlighting the importance of comprehensive screening and intervention in sports medicine.

title: Reducing Storage-Related Bias in Reproductive Vitamin D Research: Towards a Stable and Reliable Biomarker of Ovarian Vitamin D Status
summary: Vitamin D exerts critical roles in reproductive health through activation of vitamin D receptors expressed in the ovary, endometrium, and placenta, where it regulates menstrual cycling, luteal function, and progesterone production via steroidogenesis. Vitamin D deficiency is widely prevalent and linked to multiple reproductive complications including infertility, endometriosis, polycystic ovarian syndrome, and pregnancy-related complications such as preeclampsia and gestational diabetes, while adequate vitamin D levels are associated with improved ovarian stimulation response and enhanced fertility outcomes. A major finding of this study is that the vitamin D metabolite 24,25(OH)2D3 remains stable during long-term storage at −80°C, whereas the commonly measured 25(OH)D3 decreases significantly by 32-38% after seven months, introducing substantial measurement bias in reproductive vitamin D research. Serum 24,25(OH)2D3 correlates more strongly with follicular fluid concentrations than serum 25(OH)D3 (R² = 0.77 versus R² = 0.60), making it a more reliable and minimally invasive biomarker for assessing ovarian vitamin D status. These findings highlight critical pre-analytical factors affecting vitamin D measurement and demonstrate that 24,25(OH)2D3, produced via CYP24A1-mediated metabolism, offers superior stability and predictive value for reproductive vitamin D assessment compared to conventional 25(OH)D3 measurement.

title: Dual Impact of Estradiol Fluctuations in the Final Three Days on IVF/ICSI Outcomes: A Retrospective Cohort Analysis
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study reveals that estradiol dynamics during the final three days of controlled ovarian stimulation (COS) exert a dual impact on IVF/ICSI outcomes. A sharp rise in serum estradiol levels during this critical period predicts higher oocyte retrieval rates and serves as a sensitive biomarker of follicular maturation; however, excessively high late-phase estradiol concentrations paradoxically reduce embryo quality and transferable embryo rates, creating a clinically important trade-off between oocyte quantity and embryo competence. Elevated progesterone levels on the trigger day emerge as an independent risk factor associated with reduced clinical pregnancy rates and pregnancy loss. Key protective factors identified include higher antral follicle counts (AFC), mid-cycle estradiol increases, greater stage-2 estradiol elevation, lower initial gonadotropin doses, and shorter stimulation durations, whereas higher BMI, initial doses exceeding 300 IU, and prolonged stimulation duration independently predicted poorer embryo outcomes. These findings suggest that personalized COS protocols incorporating estradiol trajectory monitoring and optimization of starting doses and stimulation duration may improve IVF success by balancing oocyte yield with embryo developmental potential.

title: Association between anti-Mullerian hormone and antithyroid antibodies detectability and positivity in women assisted at a fertility clinic
summary:
# Summary

This study examined the relationship between anti-Mullerian hormone (AMH), a marker of ovarian reserve, and thyroid autoimmunity in women attending a fertility clinic where endometriosis was the primary diagnosis in approximately 70% of cases. While previous research has suggested associations between thyroid dysfunction and reduced ovarian reserve, the authors found no statistically significant correlation between detectable or positive antithyroid antibodies (TPOAb and TgAb) and AMH levels or antral follicle counts in their cohort. Age emerged as the dominant factor affecting ovarian reserve, with significantly lower AMH and follicle counts in women over 35 years compared to younger women, whereas thyroid-stimulating hormone levels showed no correlation with ovarian reserve markers. The findings suggest that while Hashimoto's thyroiditis and overt hypothyroidism compromise fertility through disrupted folliculogenesis and ovulation, antithyroid antibody detectability alone may not directly predict reduced ovarian reserve independent of age and thyroid dysfunction. These results contribute to clarifying the complex interplay between thyroid autoimmunity and fertility in endometriosis patients, indicating that thyroid hormone status rather than antibody presence may be the critical factor in ovarian function.

title: A sex-adjusted 7-biomarker clinical aging clock for translational preventative medicine
summary:
# Summary

This study developed a sex-adjusted seven-biomarker clinical aging clock integrating chronological age, creatinine, hemoglobin A1c, HDL cholesterol, triglycerides, alanine aminotransferase, and albumin to predict biological age and identify premature aging patterns. The biomarker panel revealed that common metabolic and chronic diseases display distinct aging signatures: type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease, and chronic ischemic heart disease showed significant biological age acceleration, while endometriosis, migraine, and thalassemia surprisingly demonstrated younger predicted ages despite chronological advancement. Key metabolic mechanisms underlying accelerated aging include elevated glucose causing advanced glycation end products that damage tissues, elevated triglycerides and low HDL promoting atherosclerosis and metabolic dysfunction, and both elevated and low ALT levels indicating hepatic metabolic dysregulation associated with frailty. These findings suggest that biological aging clocks can serve as sensitive biomarkers for identifying individuals at risk for age-related diseases and premature mortality, enabling targeted preventative medical interventions based on individual metabolic aging trajectories rather than chronological age alone.

title: Constructing a nomogram for ICSI-ET assisted reproductive outcomes in infertile men based on lifestyle and sperm quality: a multicenter retrospective survey
summary: This multicenter retrospective study developed a predictive nomogram for clinical pregnancy outcomes in men undergoing intracytoplasmic sperm injection and embryo transfer (ICSI-ET), identifying both sperm quality parameters and lifestyle factors as critical determinants of success. Multiple lifestyle variables—including age, body mass index, smoking status, alcohol consumption, sleep duration, exercise frequency, and stress levels—alongside sperm parameters such as progressive motility rate and DNA fragmentation index (DFI), were strongly associated with pregnancy outcomes. The research revealed that poor lifestyle habits increase oxidative stress, which damages sperm quality and compromises assisted reproductive technology success; notably, smoking directly reduces testosterone and sperm concentration while increasing estradiol through nicotine-induced testicular atrophy. Elevated sperm DNA fragmentation correlates with oxidative damage markers and epigenetic modifications, further impairing reproductive potential, while progressive sperm motility emerged as a key predictor of pregnancy success. The findings underscore that sperm genetic material integrity and activity can partially compensate for morphological defects, and that age-related declines in sperm quality may involve accumulated genetic variations. Overall, the nomogram provides a clinically useful tool demonstrating that optimizing lifestyle factors alongside assessing objective sperm parameters can improve prediction and potentially enhance fertility outcomes in men with infertility.

title: Evaluación endócrino metabólica de la infertilidad femenina en salud pública primeros
journal: Revista de la Facultad de Ciencias Médicas
summary:
# Endocrine-Metabolic Evaluation of Female Infertility

This study identified multiple endocrine and metabolic dysfunctions as critical contributors to female infertility in a primary healthcare population. Thyroid disorders were the most prevalent endocrine abnormality, found in 70% of infertile women and mechanistically inhibiting folliculogenesis, fertilization, and implantation; subclinical hypothyroidism was particularly common and responsive to levothyroxine treatment to restore fertility. Hyperprolactinemia (29% of patients) and polycystic ovary syndrome (21%) emerged as major causes of anovulation, while metabolic factors including obesity (36%), overweight (31%), and metabolic syndrome (13%) significantly increased infertility risk, with obese women facing three-fold elevated risk. Male factor infertility accounted for 26% of cases, primarily through teratozoospermia, while tubal obstruction (42%) and endometriosis (4%) contributed to female factor pathology. Lifestyle factors including smoking, physical inactivity, and advanced reproductive age compounded these endocrine-metabolic disturbances by further compromising ovarian function and oocyte quality. The findings underscore that achieving a euthyroid state and metabolic optimization are essential interventions before assisted reproduction, with standard biochemical screening including TSH, FSH, estradiol, and prolactin levels recommended for all infertile women.

title: Increased Vitamin D Receptor Immunoreactivity in Endometrial Polyps: An Exploratory Single-Center Pilot Study
summary:
# Summary

This pilot study demonstrates that vitamin D receptor (VDR) expression is significantly elevated in endometrial polyps compared to normal endometrium, with a 2.5-fold increase in VDR positivity rates. The authors propose that this upregulation represents a compensatory mechanism responding to estrogen-driven polyp pathogenesis, since endometrial polyps develop in unopposed estrogen environments and involve chronic inflammation. Vitamin D and its receptor modulate critical cellular processes including antiproliferation, apoptosis, and anti-inflammatory pathways through binding of calcitriol and heterodimerization with retinoid X receptor, which collectively regulate cell cycle progression, angiogenesis, and immune responses. The findings align with VDR overexpression patterns observed in other benign proliferative lesions like colorectal adenomas and suggest vitamin D pathway modulation as a novel therapeutic approach, though notably serum vitamin D levels were not directly associated with polyp formation in prior studies. These results support further investigation of selective vitamin D receptor modulators and supplementation strategies for managing benign endometrial proliferative conditions, particularly given evidence that vitamin D supplementation improves metabolic parameters in related conditions like endometrial hyperplasia and uterine leiomyomas.

title: Ultrasound Radiomics and Peripheral Blood Indices Model for Predicting Pregnancy and Recurrence After TCRA in Intrauterine Adhesion
summary:
# Summary

Intrauterine adhesions (IUAs) represent a significant cause of female infertility, arising from iatrogenic trauma during procedures such as hysteroscopy and curettage surgery. While transcervical resection of adhesion (TCRA) is the standard treatment, postoperative pregnancy rates remain suboptimal and recurrence is common, particularly within 4-6 months after surgery, with outcomes strongly dependent on adhesion severity. This study developed an integrated IUA-PR prediction model combining ultrasound radiomics with peripheral blood inflammatory indices—including platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio, neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio, and systemic inflammatory markers—to forecast postoperative pregnancy and recurrence risk. The model identified distinct inflammatory signatures distinguishing pregnancy from non-pregnancy groups, with elevated white blood cell counts and systemic inflammation markers correlating with poor pregnancy outcomes, while paradoxically certain inflammatory indices (NLR, PLR, SIRI, SII) were elevated in pregnancy achievers, suggesting a complex relationship between inflammatory response and endometrial regeneration. By incorporating postoperative menstrual status, patient demographics, and imaging characteristics, this multimodal predictive approach provides clinically actionable guidance for fertility counseling and may help optimize timing of assisted reproductive interventions following adhesion treatment.

title: The risk of infertility and dietary inflammatory index, a case–control study in Iran
summary: This Iranian case-control study demonstrates that a high dietary inflammatory index (DII) significantly increases infertility risk, with individuals consuming pro-inflammatory diets showing 2.08-fold greater odds of infertility compared to those with lower DII scores. The mechanism underlying this association involves chronic low-grade inflammation impairing reproductive function through multiple pathways, including reduced folliculogenesis, compromised oocyte quality, and disrupted hormonal balance, while inflammatory factors directly diminish ovarian reserve. A diet rich in unhealthy fats and refined carbohydrates elevates inflammatory markers and is correlated with obesity and metabolic disorders, whereas anti-inflammatory nutrients—including omega-3 fatty acids (eicosapentaenoic and docosahexaenoic acids), antioxidants (vitamins A, C, E, zinc, selenium), magnesium, and fiber—are more abundant in healthy individuals and protect ovarian function. Notably, elevated DII consumption of fast foods and saturated fats negatively correlates with anti-Müllerian hormone concentrations and antral follicle counts, suggesting dietary inflammation directly compromises ovarian reserve. The findings support adopting a pro-fertility diet emphasizing whole grains, soy, seafood, full-fat dairy, and vegetable proteins, with adherence to such dietary patterns linked to improved live birth rates in assisted reproduction.

title: Assessment of the causal effect of lifestyle factors on postoperative infections based on Mendelian randomization
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization study identified key modifiable lifestyle factors that causally influence postoperative infection risk following surgery. Elevated body mass index (BMI) and obesity emerged as the strongest causal risk factors for postoperative infections, including both primary and secondary diagnoses, likely mediated through impaired immune responses, difficult surgical access, prolonged operative times, and poor wound microcirculation. Conversely, higher educational attainment and longer sleep duration demonstrated protective effects against postoperative infections. The mechanisms linking obesity to increased infection risk include anesthesia complications, greater surgical bleeding, and increased wound tension, while education's protective role may reflect broader health literacy and preventive behaviors. These findings highlight BMI and educational attainment as modifiable intervention targets for reducing postoperative infection rates and improving surgical outcomes. Traditional risk factors including prolonged surgery duration, high blood loss, poor nutritional status, and compromised immune function were also confirmed as contributors to postoperative complications.

title: The clinical value of PLR, NLR, and MLR in predicting mortality risk in hospitalized patients
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that three hematologic inflammatory markers—platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio (PLR), neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR), and monocyte-to-lymphocyte ratio (MLR)—are significantly associated with mortality risk in hospitalized patients and serve as valuable prognostic indicators. Inflammation acts as a central driver of disease progression and mortality; excessive inflammation triggers organ dysfunction and death through mechanisms involving neutrophil overactivation, platelet-mediated inflammatory responses, and monocyte dysregulation. Elevated levels of all three markers correlate with heightened inflammation, impaired immune function, and poor prognosis, with the mortality group exhibiting substantially higher median values (PLR 166.04, NLR 5.69, MLR 0.49) compared to non-mortality groups. The study identified optimal diagnostic cutoff values with high sensitivity and specificity for mortality prediction: NLR ≥3.79 (AUC 0.866) and MLR ≥0.31 (AUC 0.833), with notably elevated values observed approximately 48 hours prior to death. These inflammatory markers reflect an intensified inflammatory state coupled with suppressed immune function, making them practical clinical tools for identifying high-risk hospitalized patients and predicting fatal outcomes.

title: Causal Relationships Between Pregnancy, Menstrual History, and Endometrial Cancer With Mediating Effects of Metabolism‐Related Traits
summary:
# Summary

This study uses Mendelian randomization to establish causal relationships between reproductive timing, metabolic factors, and endometrial cancer risk. Later age at menarche and earlier age at menopause both causally reduce endometrial cancer risk, with each additional year at menarche decreasing risk by approximately 32% and each year delayed menopause decreasing risk by 24%. Sex hormone–binding globulin (SHBG), a liver-synthesized protein that binds circulating estrogen and testosterone, independently protects against endometrial cancer, with each standard deviation increase in SHBG reducing risk by 41%. The metabolic factors mediating menarche's effect on endometrial cancer include obesity and body composition measures (BMI, waist circumference, waist-to-hip ratio, and body fat percentage), all of which negatively correlate with protective SHBG levels. Prolonged progesterone exposure during pregnancy provides protection against endometrial hyperplasia and cancer, while estrogen drives endometrial proliferation and is a fundamental causative factor in endometrial cancer development. These findings suggest that maintaining healthy body weight and adiposity throughout life, particularly avoiding childhood and adult obesity, represents an effective prevention strategy for endometrial cancer, especially in women with premature menarche.

title: Association of Dietary Inflammation Index with Infertility Risk in US Women: A Cross-Sectional Analysis from NHANES (2013-2020)
summary: This cross-sectional study of US women from NHANES (2013-2020) demonstrates that a pro-inflammatory diet, as measured by a high Dietary Inflammation Index (DII) score, is significantly associated with elevated infertility risk. Women with infertility exhibited substantially higher DII scores and lower fruit consumption compared to fertile women, while also displaying increased rates of obesity, smoking, diabetes, hypertension, and pelvic inflammatory disease alongside reduced physical activity. The mechanism underlying this association involves pro-inflammatory dietary patterns elevating systemic inflammatory biomarkers—including IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, and CRP—which subsequently impair follicular development, oocyte quality, and endometrial health. Notably, the DII proved a more effective predictor of infertility risk than the Healthy Eating Index, suggesting that dietary inflammatory potential specifically captures infertility-relevant mechanisms. Conversely, anti-inflammatory dietary patterns rich in whole grains, vegetables, and fruits may improve the reproductive endocrine environment and reduce infertility risk. These findings underscore the importance of dietary modifications in managing infertility and suggest that reducing dietary inflammatory burden represents a modifiable intervention target for women seeking to optimize reproductive health.

title: Factors associated with the efficacy of mature oocyte production after dual-trigger controlled ovarian stimulation using a GnRH antagonist protocol
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary: This study identifies key clinical and biochemical factors predicting efficient mature oocyte production (EMOP ≥70%) during controlled ovarian stimulation using a GnRH antagonist protocol with dual trigger (hCG + GnRHa). Multivariate analysis revealed that day-2 FSH levels, stimulation duration, and total oocyte count are the strongest predictors of mature oocyte yield, with receiver operating characteristic analysis demonstrating that stimulation duration combined with total oocyte count provides the highest predictive power for identifying poor responders (EMOP <70%). Notably, basal FSH levels >6.095 mIU/mL and stimulation duration <9.5 days are independently associated with diminished mature oocyte production, while extending stimulation beyond 9.5 days significantly improves outcomes in poor responders. The study reveals that AMH levels correlate with LH decline during stimulation, suggesting that higher AMH promotes greater LH suppression by GnRH antagonists, which in turn enhances mature oocyte retrieval through improved hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis sensitivity. These findings establish practical biomarkers for identifying patients at risk of poor oocyte maturation and support individualizing stimulation duration to optimize IVF success, particularly for women with endometriosis or other conditions affecting ovarian response.

title: Nonlinear association of body roundness index with female infertility and the mediating effect of NHHR: A cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study demonstrates a nonlinear positive association between body roundness index (BRI) and female infertility, with the non-HDL to HDL cholesterol ratio (NHHR) serving as a partial mediator of this relationship. Women in the infertility group had significantly elevated BRI (6.35 vs. 5.26) and NHHR (2.54 vs. 2.30) compared to fertile controls, with each unit increase in BRI conferring an 8% increase in infertility risk after covariate adjustment. The underlying mechanisms link abdominal obesity to infertility through multiple pathways: obesity induces hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis dysfunction and lipid metabolism abnormalities that impair steroid hormone synthesis, oocyte development, and ovulation, while abdominal obesity specifically promotes insulin resistance and androgen excess that further compromise follicular development. The correlation between BRI and NHHR likely reflects shared lipid metabolism dysregulation in obesity, positioning NHHR as a novel biomarker for obesity-related infertility risk. Weight loss interventions that restore lipid profiles and hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal function represent potential therapeutic approaches for obese women with infertility.

title: The association between sedentary behavior and infertility: a population-based cross-sectional observational study and Mendelian randomization analysis
summary: This population-based cross-sectional study with Mendelian randomization analysis examined the relationship between sedentary behavior and infertility, finding that sedentary behavior and daily sitting time show observational associations with infertility, though specific sedentary activities (leisure screen time, commuting, and work-related sitting) demonstrated no statistically significant causal relationships in the genetic analyses. Women with infertility demonstrated higher prevalence of multiple risk factors including increased age, elevated body mass index, smoking, sedentary behaviors, hypertension, and pelvic inflammatory disease compared to fertile controls. While daily sitting time initially showed a positive association with infertility in unadjusted analyses, this relationship became non-significant after comprehensive adjustment for confounding variables, suggesting that the observed associations may be mediated through metabolic and inflammatory pathways rather than direct causal mechanisms. The study indicates that infertility's rising prevalence is linked to unhealthy lifestyle behaviors, environmental exposures, and psychological stress, with sedentary behavior emerging as a potential but complex determinant of reproductive health. These findings highlight the multifactorial nature of infertility and suggest that interventions targeting overall lifestyle modification rather than sedentary behavior in isolation may be warranted for reproductive health optimization.

title: Examination of Appendiceal Neoplasms—A Retrospective, Single-Centre, Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study examined 71 appendiceal neoplasms identified among 3,640 appendectomy specimens collected over a nine-year period, finding that appendiceal tumors are rare but show increasing incidence, particularly in younger patients. The study identified distinct age-related patterns: benign neoplasms and adenocarcinomas were more common in older patients, while neuroendocrine tumors (NETs), the most prevalent histological subtype, predominantly affected younger individuals around age 40. Approximately half of appendiceal neoplasm cases were associated with concurrent colorectal carcinoma, with significant correlations between histological subtype and clinical parameters including vascular invasion, complete resection rates, and patient age. Tumor behavior showed strong associations with TNM stage, vascular invasion, and the development and localization of associated colorectal neoplasms, with signet-ring cell adenocarcinomas demonstrating the poorest prognosis. Risk factors for appendiceal neoplasms included tobacco use, advanced age, female sex, and certain gastrointestinal conditions such as pernicious anemia and atrophic gastritis, though obesity and increased appendectomy frequency may also contribute to rising incidence. Most appendiceal neoplasms presented as asymptomatic incidental findings during histopathological examination following appendectomy, though they could occasionally mimic acute appendicitis or other conditions including endometriosis.

title: Sperm DNA Fragmentation in Normozoospermic Men Is Associated with Blastocyst Formation and Quality in Conventional In Vitro Fertilization
summary: This study demonstrates that sperm DNA fragmentation index (DFI) significantly impairs embryo development in conventional in vitro fertilization, even among men with normal semen parameters. Elevated sperm DFI was negatively correlated with blastocyst formation rate, developmental stage, and quality grade, with regression analysis identifying sperm DFI as the only variable inversely associated with all blastocyst outcomes. Interestingly, serum HDL cholesterol levels emerged as the sole male metabolic factor associated with sperm DFI, suggesting a connection between lipid metabolism and sperm DNA integrity. The study mechanistically linked oxidative stress to sperm DNA fragmentation, with advanced glycation end products accumulating in testicular tissue and generating oxidative stress that compromises sperm quality. Additionally, male age was negatively associated with blastocyst quality grade, and higher sperm DFI (>30%) correlated with lower rates of high-quality embryo development and implantation success. These findings emphasize that sperm DNA fragmentation affects fertility outcomes independently of conventional semen parameters and highlight the importance of assessing male metabolic and oxidative factors in couples undergoing assisted reproduction.

title: Global Burden of Pelvic Organ Prolapse, 1990–2021: An Ecological Study and Mendelian Randomization Analysis
summary:
# Summary

This ecological and Mendelian randomization study identifies multiple genetic and epidemiological factors contributing to pelvic organ prolapse (POP) risk. Aging, parity, and obesity represent the three strongest risk factors for POP, which involves structural failure of pelvic ligaments, fascia, and muscles supporting the uterus, bladder, and rectum. Metabolic dysfunction plays a significant role: genetic predisposition to elevated body mass index, waist circumference, hip circumference, fasting insulin levels, diastolic blood pressure, and type 2 diabetes mellitus all increase POP risk, while protective associations exist for higher high-density lipoprotein cholesterol and total cholesterol. Notably, socioeconomic factors including higher educational attainment, college completion, and cognitive performance demonstrate protective effects against POP development. The clinical consequences of POP include vaginal wall protrusion, uterine prolapse, and dysfunction of urinary and intestinal systems, with additional risks from operative vaginal delivery and age-related degeneration of pelvic connective tissues.

title: The association of insulin resistance and obesity with uterine fibroids in non-diabetic populations: a cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study demonstrates that insulin resistance (IR) and obesity are independently associated with uterine fibroid (UF) development in non-diabetic populations, with HOMA-IR and BMI showing significant positive correlations with fibroid presence. The mechanism underlying this association involves multiple pathways: insulin resistance amplifies estrogen- and progesterone-driven proliferative signaling in myometrial tissue, while IR-associated hyperinsulinemia potentiates insulin-like growth factor-I (IGF-I) activity, a key mitogen in fibroid development. Additionally, insulin resistance promotes chronic low-grade inflammation that increases cytokine and growth factor production, and enhances vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) activity to support angiogenesis within fibroids, while excess adipose tissue contributes to elevated estrogen levels that further promote fibroid growth. The strength of association between metabolic markers and UFs varied by age group, with BMI showing stronger associations in younger women (20–38 years) and HOMA-IR showing stronger associations in older women (39–54 years), suggesting age-dependent metabolic contributions to fibroid pathogenesis. These findings indicate that metabolic dysfunction, particularly insulin resistance, is a significant factor in uterine fibroid development and may represent a therapeutic target in fibroid prevention.

title: Multi-ancestry investigation of the genomics of erectile dysfunction
summary: This multi-ancestry genome-wide association study identified multiple genetic variants and genes associated with erectile dysfunction (ED), with the strongest associations at rs78677597 regulating SIM1, alongside variants in PHF21B, NTNG1, and CTNNB1. The study reveals that penile erection fundamentally depends on nitric oxide-mediated smooth muscle relaxation and cyclic GMP signaling, pathways targeted by PDE5 inhibitors, the main ED treatment. Beyond genetics, the research confirms that metabolic conditions (obesity, type 2 diabetes), cardiovascular disease, smoking, and psychological factors (depression, anxiety, PTSD) substantially increase ED risk, while physical activity is protective. Notably, the variant regulating SIM1, an obesity-associated gene, showed pleiotropic effects on multiple cardiometabolic traits, suggesting shared genetic architecture between ED and metabolic dysfunction. Bidirectional causal analyses demonstrated that opioid and cannabis use disorders causally increase ED risk, while ED itself increases risk for type 2 diabetes and obesity. These findings highlight both vascular-neuronal mechanisms and the interconnection between ED and cardiometabolic health across diverse populations.

title: Predictive value of hematological parameters for IVF success: a retrospective analysis
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective analysis examined whether complete blood count (CBC)-derived inflammatory indices predict in vitro fertilization (IVF) success, with implications for understanding endometriosis and metabolic dysfunction in infertility. Contrary to expectations, common inflammatory markers including neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio, platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio, and systemic immune-inflammation index showed no significant association with clinical pregnancy outcomes in the overall cohort. Instead, embryologic variables—particularly the number of two-pronuclei embryos and total embryos produced—emerged as independent predictors of pregnancy success, with higher counts indicating superior oocyte competence and fertilization quality. The study highlights that while pro-inflammatory cytokines impair folliculogenesis, granulosa cell function, and oocyte competence through increased oxidative stress and disrupted endometrial receptivity, peripheral blood inflammatory indices may not adequately capture these localized reproductive tract effects. Notably, excessive oocyte yield showed an inverse relationship with pregnancy, suggesting that overly intense ovarian stimulation may impair embryo-endometrium synchronization. The authors emphasize that population heterogeneity—particularly the inclusion of conditions like endometriosis, PCOS, and metabolic syndrome that feature chronic low-grade inflammation—likely explains discrepancies in prior studies reporting positive associations between CBC parameters and IVF outcomes.

title: Predicting pathological complete response to breast cancer neoadjuvant therapy using multi-combination machine learning models based on vision transformer features
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that Vision Transformer (VIT)-based deep learning features significantly outperform traditional convolutional neural networks (CNN) algorithms for predicting pathological complete response (pCR) to neoadjuvant therapy in breast cancer patients. The VIT model's superior attention mechanism, which selectively weights important image regions, enables more accurate feature extraction from breast imaging data compared to ResNet50 and VGG16 architectures. When combined with the Stepglm forward algorithm, VIT-derived features achieved the highest predictive performance with area under the curve (AUC) values of 0.872 and 0.839 in training and validation cohorts, respectively. The extracted VIT features correlated meaningfully with established radiomics features including gray-level co-occurrence matrix (GLCM) and gray-level size zone matrix (GLSZM) texture descriptors, suggesting they capture clinically relevant tumor characteristics. Accurate prediction of pCR enables clinicians to identify patients who can pursue breast-conserving surgical strategies or potentially avoid surgery altogether. These findings establish VIT-based machine learning as a valuable tool for personalizing neoadjuvant therapy planning and improving clinical outcomes in breast cancer treatment.

title: Association of female reproductive traits with altered aging trajectories: Insights from genetic and observational analyses
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how female reproductive traits influence aging trajectories using genetic and observational analyses. Key findings reveal that later age at first birth, later menarche, higher estradiol levels, and elevated sex hormone-binding globulin are associated with increased longevity and reduced epigenetic age acceleration, while pre-eclampsia shows the opposite effect. Early reproductive events, particularly early first sexual intercourse, are associated with accelerated biological aging as measured by DNA methylation clocks. Cardiometabolic diseases—particularly heart failure, coronary heart disease, and stroke—emerge as major mediators linking reproductive timing to longevity, along with neurodegenerative and pulmonary diseases. These findings demonstrate that reproductive health is intimately connected to systemic aging processes through multiple disease pathways, with cardiometabolic complications playing a central mechanistic role in determining long-term health outcomes.

title: When to Choose In Vitro Fertilization (IVF) When Postponing Conceiving: The Age‐Related Risk of IVF Failure at an Advanced Age
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies age 35.5 years as a critical inflection point for in vitro fertilization (IVF) outcomes, with live birth rates remaining stable until age 35 and then declining sharply thereafter. Women aged 35–37 and 37–40 experience rapid increases in IVF failure risk, with live birth rates declining by 27% and 20% per year respectively in these age groups, while outcomes become increasingly poor after age 40. The mechanisms underlying age-related IVF failure include increased production of aneuploid blastocysts and weakened oocyte viability, both driven by advanced maternal age, leading to higher rates of implantation failure, miscarriage, and chromosomal abnormalities. Age, baseline follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), and duration of infertility all significantly predict live birth rates, with age showing the strongest negative association with fertility outcomes. The authors recommend age 35 as the safe time point for IVF decision-making, suggesting that delaying conception beyond this threshold substantially increases the risk of treatment failure and poor reproductive outcomes.

title: Efficacy and Safety of Folisurge, a Biosimilar Recombinant Follicle-Stimulating Hormone, Versus Gonal-F in Assisted Reproductive Technology Practice in India: A Real-World Study
summary: This real-world study evaluated Folisurge, a biosimilar recombinant follicle-stimulating hormone (r-FSH), against the reference product Gonal-f for controlled ovarian stimulation in assisted reproductive technology. Folisurge demonstrated superior ovarian response outcomes compared to Gonal-f, including significantly higher antral follicle counts, total oocytes retrieved, MII oocytes, MII oocyte rate, follicular output rate, and embryo formation. These improvements were attributed to Folisurge's biochemical characteristics, including a higher degree of O-acetylation that enhances biological activity and consistency. Despite superior oocyte retrieval parameters, both treatments achieved comparable clinical pregnancy rates, with serum β-hCG positivity rates of 75.5% for Folisurge and 76.2% for Gonal-f. The study demonstrates that r-FSH dosing requires individualization based on multiple factors including age, body mass index, serum markers (AMH and FSH), and patient conditions such as polycystic ovary syndrome and severe endometriosis. Overall, Folisurge represents an efficacious and safe biosimilar alternative for infertility treatment in assisted reproductive technology practice.

title: Diaphragmatic downward excursion as a novel metric for assessing Valsalva maneuver efficacy in patent foramen ovale detection by contrast transthoracic echocardiography
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies diaphragmatic downward excursion on the right atrial aspect (DDE-RRA) as a novel objective metric for assessing the efficacy of the Valsalva maneuver during contrast transthoracic echocardiography for patent foramen ovale detection. Patent foramen ovale is associated with serious cardiovascular and neurological conditions including cryptogenic stroke, transient ischemic attack, migraine, and hypoxemia. The Valsalva maneuver increases right atrial pressure and detects right-to-left shunts, substantially improving PFO detection rates compared to imaging at rest. DDE-RRA measurements demonstrated superior diagnostic performance compared to traditional hemodynamic parameters such as mitral and tricuspid valve velocities, with an optimal cutoff of 5 mm achieving 77.8% sensitivity and excellent reproducibility. DDE-RRA correlated well with insufflation manometry and was significantly lower in the adequate Valsalva maneuver group, providing a simple, objective, and non-invasive method for confirming adequate Valsalva performance during echocardiographic evaluation.

title: The value of 4-dimensional hysterosalpingo-contrast sonography in predicting the possibility of spontaneous conception in infertile women
summary:
# Summary

Four-dimensional hysterosalpingo-contrast sonography (4D-HyCoSy) is a valuable diagnostic tool for predicting spontaneous conception in infertile women by comprehensively evaluating tubal patency, uterine morphology, and endometrial characteristics. Within one year after 4D-HyCoSy examination, 46.8% of infertile women achieved spontaneous pregnancy, with the highest conception rates occurring in the first three months post-procedure. Bilateral tubal patency emerged as a strong predictor of natural pregnancy, with type I patency demonstrating the highest pregnancy rate (55.3%), followed by type II (36.3%), while bilateral tubal obstruction resulted in only 16.3% pregnancy rate. Independent predictors of successful spontaneous conception included younger age, shorter infertility duration, lower parity, bilateral tubal patency, and absence of uterine cavity lesions or endometriosis. The study suggests that 4D-HyCoSy may improve spontaneous pregnancy rates through enhanced endometrial blood distribution and receptivity, while helping clinicians determine which patients require assisted reproductive technology intervention versus those likely to conceive naturally.

title: Sperm H3K9me3 levels are associated with embryo developmental dynamics and biochemical pregnancy in IVF patients with normozoospermia
summary:
# Summary

Sperm H3K9me3, a histone modification crucial for constitutive heterochromatin formation and genome stability, varies significantly among normozoospermic men and demonstrates a non-linear association with embryo development and IVF outcomes. The paternal H3K9me3 mark is transmitted to the embryo where it interacts with oocyte-derived heterochromatin protein 1 to regulate cell-fate decisions and genomic integrity during early cleavage divisions. Men in the third quartile of the H3K9me3/H3 ratio showed optimal embryo development, characterized by the highest proportion of zygotes with proper nucleolar precursor body clustering, faster developmental progression, and significantly higher biochemical pregnancy rates compared to those in the lowest quartile. Conversely, men in the lowest and highest quartiles exhibited delayed embryo development at multiple stages and increased proportions of unclustered nucleolar precursor bodies, features associated with increased aneuploidy risk. These findings reveal that paternal epigenetic variation in sperm H3K9me3 levels—undetectable by standard semen analysis—influences embryo quality and reproductive outcomes, suggesting that this histone modification represents an important biomarker for predicting IVF success in male factor infertility of unexplained origin.

title: A new score for predicting incidental appendiceal neoplasms in patients aged ≥40 years with acute appendicitis: a multicenter retrospective cohort study
journal: Turkish Journal of Trauma & Emergency Surgery
summary: This multicenter study developed a five-parameter scoring system to identify patients at risk for incidental appendiceal neoplasms during acute appendicitis evaluation. The key predictive factors identified through multivariate logistic regression were patient age, neutrophil count, appendix diameter on computed tomography, and absence of wall contrast enhancement, with female gender showing borderline significance.
