Notably, advanced age over 40 years emerged as the strongest predictor of neoplasia, and the combination of age over 40 with appendix diameter greater than 10 mm increased malignancy risk more than threefold. Patients achieving the maximum score of 5 points demonstrated a 33.3% incidence of neoplasia, with an additional risk threshold identified at age 50. The most prevalent histologic types of incidental appendiceal neoplasms were neuroendocrine tumors and low-grade mucinous neoplasms, and their detection is clinically important because missed tumors can compromise prognosis through inadequate resection and incomplete staging. This predictive scoring system enables preoperative stratification of patients who may benefit from more aggressive surgical approaches, such as right hemicolectomy, rather than simple appendectomy for advanced-stage appendiceal tumors.

title: Male Stress Is Associated with Ovarian and Endometrial Responses in ICSI Cycles: Is Seminal Plasma the Linchpin?
summary: This study reveals that male stress impairs assisted reproductive outcomes by altering seminal plasma composition, which subsequently affects female ovarian and endometrial responses during intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI) cycles. Male stress activates the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis and sympathetic nervous system, increasing circulating cortisol and seminal noradrenaline levels that compromise both semen quality (reducing sperm count, motility, and increasing DNA damage) and seminal plasma's pregnancy-favorable biological effects. Partners with elevated stress biomarkers—particularly the NEI phenotype-2 profile characterized by high cortisol and noradrenaline—had female partners with reduced oocyte collection and maturation, thinner endometrial thickness, and significantly lower pregnancy rates. Seminal noradrenaline showed strong negative correlations with the number of collected and mature oocytes and weak correlations with fertilized oocytes, suggesting seminal plasma directly influences female reproductive outcomes through transcriptional pathways that regulate endometrial proliferation and angiogenesis. The proinflammatory cytokine interleukin-18 in seminal plasma, elevated with male stress and age, further compounds reproductive dysfunction. These findings indicate that seminal plasma serves as a critical mechanistic link through which paternal stress negatively impacts female reproductive capacity in fertility treatments.

title: Insights from a Pseudo-Polygyny Cohort unmasking high sperm concentration and low forward motility as potential risk factors for recurrent failure of sperm donation IVF (RFDI), a subset of unexplained male infertility (UMI)
summary:
# Summary

This study analyzed sperm donors from a pseudo-polygyny cohort to identify risk factors for recurrent failure of sperm donation IVF (RFDI), a subset of unexplained male infertility. The key finding reveals a paradoxical relationship: donors with RFDI exhibited both higher sperm concentration and significantly lower forward motility rates compared to successful donors, with forward motility rate showing superior correlation to IVF outcomes than progressive motility rate. Beyond semen parameters, RFDI donors experienced substantially elevated miscarriage rates (10.8% vs. 3.9% in non-RFDI donors), suggesting that impaired forward motility may mechanistically compromise embryonic development beyond fertilization. Demographic and lifestyle factors including age, body mass index, and education level were identified as modulators of sperm concentration and motility. The study concludes that forward motility rate represents a more meaningful predictor of sperm donation IVF success than conventional semen analyses, and that the counterintuitive negative impact of high sperm concentration on fertility outcomes warrants further mechanistic investigation in this RFDI population.

title: Linking the plasma proteome to genetics in individuals from continental Africa provides insights into type 2 diabetes pathogenesis
summary: This study linked plasma proteome variation to genetic factors in African populations to identify novel pathways in type 2 diabetes pathogenesis. Key findings revealed that elevated leptin (LEP) and chemokine CXCL5, along with reduced resistin and interleukin-18, characterize the diabetic plasma proteome, while angiogenesis-related protein EDIL3 shows altered expression in insulin-resistant individuals. Genetic analyses identified multiple proteins with causal roles in diabetes development, including ACE, CA13, MLN, SERPINA5, and WFIKKN1, which promote disease risk, contrasted by protective proteins including COMT, GHR, and ICAM5 that reduce susceptibility. Several genetic variants were functionally characterized: the COMT rs4680 variant associates with lower HbA1c and diabetes protection, the rs901886 variant (ICAM5) links immune regulation and inflammation to disease, and rs532436 acts as a pleiotropic variant affecting multiple proteins involved in metabolic and inflammatory pathways. Additionally, lipid dysregulation, mediated through proteins like APOF and LPL, emerged as a critical mechanism driving insulin resistance and diabetes development. These findings establish new genetic and proteomic signatures of type 2 diabetes in African populations and identify potential therapeutic targets through pathways involving inflammation, angiogenesis, and lipid metabolism.

title: Clustered cardiometabolic risk in pregnancy and dysmenorrhea in offspring: Results from a prospective birth cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This prospective birth cohort study demonstrates that maternal pre-pregnancy obesity increases dysmenorrhea risk in offspring through multiple interconnected pathways. Higher maternal pre-pregnancy BMI directly associates with increased dysmenorrhea risk (OR: 1.20), with effects partially mediated by offspring BMI and earlier menarcheal age in daughters. The mechanistic pathway shows that maternal obesity leads to higher BMI in early puberty, which in turn predicts earlier menarche and subsequent dysmenorrhea risk. At the molecular level, maternal cardiometabolic dysfunction, including gestational hyperglycemia and obesity, triggers inflammatory and metabolic disturbances—including activation of TLR4/NF-κB signaling, elevated insulin resistance, and systemic inflammation markers (IL-6, IL-8)—that disrupt endocrine homeostasis and reproductive maturation in offspring. Since dysmenorrhea is strongly associated with later endometriosis and infertility development, these findings suggest that maternal metabolic dysfunction during pregnancy establishes an intergenerational cycle of reproductive dysfunction and metabolic disease in daughters. This work highlights the critical importance of maternal metabolic health in determining offspring reproductive health and menstrual wellbeing.

title: Development of a risk model for low oocyte retrieval in first-cycle IVF patients with diminished ovarian reserve: a retrospective single-center study
summary: This retrospective study developed a predictive risk model for low oocyte retrieval (LOR) in first-cycle IVF patients with diminished ovarian reserve by identifying eight independent predictors: age, basal FSH, antral follicle count (AFC), anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH), LH and progesterone levels on hCG day, number of large follicles on hCG day, and trigger-to-retrieval time (TR-OPU). Notably, extending TR-OPU beyond 35 hours significantly reduced LOR risk, particularly in younger patients, suggesting optimal follicular maturation timing is critical. The study revealed that endometriosis contributes to LOR through follicular fibrosis and increased viscosity of follicular fluid, highlighting a pathophysiological mechanism linking this condition to poor oocyte retrieval. Patients achieving high oocyte retrieval demonstrated significantly higher cumulative pregnancy and live birth rates compared to the LOR group, underscoring the clinical importance of adequate oocyte numbers. While traditional parameters like BMI and gonadotropin dosing were not predictive, established ovarian reserve markers (AMH and AFC) remained strongly associated with retrieval outcomes, validating their clinical utility.

title: The prognostic significance of primary tumor laterality in malignant ovarian teratomas: a 10-year experience at a single institution
summary:
# Summary

Primary tumor laterality emerges as the sole independent prognostic factor in malignant ovarian teratomas (MOT), with right-sided tumors demonstrating substantially superior outcomes compared to left-sided and bilateral cases. Right-sided MOT patients achieved 85% five-year overall survival and experienced a 99% decreased recurrence risk, while bilateral tumors showed only 25% five-year survival with aggressive features including poor differentiation and FIGO Stage III presentation. The anatomical basis for this disparity involves distinct venous drainage pathways: the right ovarian vein drains directly into the inferior vena cava, promoting systemic dissemination, while the left drains into the left renal vein with anatomical proximity to the sigmoid colon, facilitating local tumor implantation and spread. Histopathological subtype, tumor grade, age, size, and treatment methods were not independent prognostic factors, highlighting that tumor location supersedes traditional prognostic markers. Right-sided tumors presented more frequently as smaller, asymptomatic lesions in older patients, whereas left-sided tumors were larger and occurred in younger patients amenable to fertility-sparing surgery, and bilateral cases predominated in malignant transformation of mature cystic teratomas.

title: Ultrasound radiomics and deep learning for predicting antral follicle count and anti-Müllerian hormone
summary:
# Summary

This study developed and validated an artificial intelligence framework combining radiomics and deep learning (Rad-DL model) to predict antral follicle count (AFC) and anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels from routine 2D transvaginal ultrasound images. Accurate assessment of ovarian reserve through AFC and AMH is essential for optimizing controlled ovarian stimulation protocols and identifying patients at risk of poor or excessive ovarian response. The Rad-DL model achieved superior predictive performance compared to individual radiomics or deep learning approaches alone, with R² values of 0.743 for 3D AFC prediction in internal testing and 0.881 AUC for stratifying ovarian response risk, demonstrating 69.1% sensitivity and 84.6% specificity in identifying high-risk patients. Three-dimensional ultrasound measurements (AFC3D) significantly exceeded two-dimensional counts and showed strong correlations with AMH levels, while both metrics inversely correlated with patient age. The fusion model effectively addressed limitations of manual follicle counting and AMH testing by standardizing ovarian reserve evaluation, though deep learning performance declined in external testing with lower-quality ultrasound images, suggesting radiomics provides greater robustness for clinical implementation.

title: Neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio in females as a predictor of embryo acquisition outcomes after IVF/ICSI treatment: a retrospective study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective study demonstrates that the neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR) serves as a significant predictor of embryo acquisition success in IVF/ICSI treatment, with a non-linear relationship to outcomes. While chronic inflammation impairs female fertility through multiple mechanisms—including disruption of endocrine homeostasis and the ovarian microenvironment by interleukins IL-1 and IL-6—moderate inflammation appears beneficial for IVF outcomes. Notably, women with NLR values in the second quartile (1.53-1.95) demonstrated the most favorable treatment outcomes, particularly when combined with primary infertility status, female-factor infertility etiology, and no history of miscarriage, whereas higher NLR quartiles predicted failure to obtain embryos. The study confirms that immune balance is critical for successful reproduction, as excessive neutrophil expansion and lymphocyte suppression disrupt folliculogenesis and oocyte quality, while IVF-induced oxidative stress exacerbates these inflammatory processes. These findings suggest NLR could serve as an accessible peripheral biomarker to stratify IVF patients and identify those at risk for poor embryo acquisition outcomes.

title: The effect of latent Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection on ovarian reserve and pregnancy outcomes among infertile women in China
summary: Latent Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection (LTBI) significantly impairs ovarian reserve and reproductive outcomes in infertile women undergoing assisted reproductive technology. Women with LTBI showed substantially lower anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels—a key marker of ovarian reserve secreted by granulosa cells—compared to non-LTBI controls, with LTBI status identified as an independent negative predictor of AMH levels. The infection was associated with reduced clinical and biochemical pregnancy rates and a notably elevated ectopic pregnancy rate, despite similar antral follicle counts between groups. The mechanisms underlying these reproductive impairments appear to extend beyond structural damage from active genital tuberculosis, suggesting that latent infection may impair granulosa cell function through yet-undefined pathways. These findings highlight LTBI as an underappreciated infectious factor affecting female fertility that warrants screening in infertile populations, particularly in tuberculosis-endemic regions.

title: Association of serum anti-Müllerian hormone levels with early miscarriage: a retrospective cohort and mendelian randomization study
summary: This retrospective cohort and Mendelian randomization study demonstrates that serum anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels show a significant non-linear association with early miscarriage risk in women undergoing in vitro fertilization, with those having AMH levels below 1.1 ng/mL exhibiting substantially elevated miscarriage rates (17.7% versus 10.9% in reference group; adjusted odds ratio = 1.26). Low AMH serves as an established biomarker of diminished ovarian reserve and was associated with decreased oocyte quality, reduced oocyte retrieval numbers, and lower blastocyst transfer rates, all contributing to increased pregnancy loss. Notably, Mendelian randomization analysis failed to establish a causal relationship between AMH and spontaneous or recurrent miscarriage, suggesting the observed associations may be confounded by underlying factors affecting oogenesis and embryo competence. The study also reveals important demographic and clinical correlations, including higher endometriosis prevalence in the low-AMH group and progressive increases in frozen cycle utilization and intracytoplasmic sperm injection use with higher AMH strata. These findings underscore that while low pre-pregnancy AMH levels correlate with miscarriage risk in IVF populations, particularly in non-PCOS patients with AMH less than 1 ng/mL, the relationship appears mediated through oocyte quality rather than direct causality.

title: The Significance of Serum CA-125 Level on the Live Birth Rates of In Vitro Fertilisation in Women with Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis significantly compromises fertility by causing pelvic anatomical distortion and reducing oocyte yield, with deeply infiltrating disease particularly lowering cumulative live birth rates. Serum CA-125, a glycoprotein expressed by coelomic epithelium including the endometrium, fallopian tube, ovary, and peritoneum, is elevated in advanced endometriosis and reflects local inflammatory reactions from peritoneal implant disintegration during menstruation. While serum CA-125 levels overall were not associated with live birth rates in IVF cycles, women with CA-125 ≥35 IU/L on trigger day demonstrated significantly higher pregnancy rates in fresh embryo transfer cycles and showed larger endometriomas, suggesting this threshold may identify a subset with more extensive disease. The GnRH agonist, antagonist, and PPOS protocols all reduced serum CA-125 levels, with PPOS achieving the most substantial reductions on trigger day, while progestin's stabilizing effect on endometriotic tissue likely contributes to improved outcomes. Notably, endometriosis surgery reduced anti-Müllerian hormone levels and required higher gonadotropin doses despite lower baseline CA-125, indicating that surgical intervention paradoxically compromises ovarian reserve while potentially improving IVF outcomes through disease control.

title: Are We Still Mediterranean? Dietary Quality and Adherence in Sicilian Women Undergoing ART: A Prospective Observational Cohort Study
summary: This prospective observational cohort study examined dietary patterns in Sicilian women undergoing assisted reproductive technology (ART), demonstrating that the Mediterranean diet supports reproductive health through multiple physiological mechanisms. Dietary patterns, particularly those rich in monounsaturated fatty acids, polyphenols, carotenoids, folate, and omega-3 fatty acids, influence critical reproductive processes by modulating systemic inflammation, oxidative stress, insulin sensitivity, mitochondrial function, and lipid metabolism, while also shaping the gut and vaginal microbiota to enhance immune tolerance. Mediterranean diet adherence correlated with lower body mass index; however, the study found no significant associations between Mediterranean diet adherence score (MEDAS) and oocyte retrieval, oocyte maturity, clinical pregnancy rates, or anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels. Beyond direct ART outcomes, dietary patterns may exert broader reproductive benefits through epigenetic modulation and optimization of endometrial receptivity and ovarian physiology, suggesting that metabolic and immune pathways may be more relevant than isolated gamete parameters. The authors emphasize that nutritional counseling, particularly Mediterranean diet-based interventions, should be integrated systematically into preconception care and fertility pathways as a foundational component rather than an optional adjunct to enhance long-term reproductive health.

title: Follicular Klotho in the Ovarian Microenvironment: Exploring Its Role in IVF Outcome Prediction
summary:
# Summary

Klotho (KL), a multifunctional protein involved in reproductive physiology, emerges as a significant predictor of in vitro fertilization outcomes and ovarian reserve status. This study demonstrates that follicular fluid KL levels are substantially reduced in women with diminished ovarian reserve (DOR) compared to normal ovarian reserve (NOR) groups, with each one-unit increase in KL associated with a 3.7% increase in implantation probability and 3.2% increase in clinical pregnancy probability. Women in the DOR group exhibited the characteristic hormonal profile of reduced anti-Müllerian hormone alongside elevated follicle-stimulating hormone, luteinizing hormone, and estradiol levels, with correspondingly lower implantation and clinical pregnancy rates. Mechanistically, KL suppresses PI3K/Akt activation to enhance FOXO3 function and promote MnSOD expression, thereby limiting oxidative stress and cellular senescence that drive ovarian aging. Additionally, KL influences Wnt/β-catenin signaling and the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis, with KL deficiency disrupting these pathways and accelerating ovarian aging through oxidative imbalance. These findings establish follicular fluid KL as a potential noninvasive biomarker for predicting IVF success and suggest that KL levels reflect the biochemical microenvironment critical for oocyte development and fertility outcomes.

title: Global burden of ovarian cancer attributable to high BMI, 1990–2021: spatiotemporal trends, risk factors, frontier analysis, and projections to 2036 based on GBD 2021 study
summary:
# Summary

Elevated body mass index (BMI) and obesity represent significant independent risk factors for ovarian cancer development and progression, with the global disease burden attributable to high BMI rising substantially from 1990 to 2021. Obesity promotes ovarian carcinogenesis through multiple interconnected mechanisms, including increased aromatization of androgens to estradiol in adipose tissue, chronic low-grade inflammation (marked by elevated IL-6, TNF-α, and CRP), insulin resistance with elevated IGF-1 levels, and dysregulation of adipokine secretion (leptin and adiponectin). Obesity-driven hyperadiposity accelerates tumor progression and promotes chemotherapy resistance through augmented mitogenic and growth-factor signaling, while individuals in the highest BMI categories exhibit elevated cancer-specific mortality. The burden is particularly concentrated in women aged 40–79 years and shows stark geographic disparities, with low- and middle-sociodemographic index regions experiencing sustained increases exceeding 3% annually, whereas high-income regions implementing integrated BMI surveillance, metabolic risk management, and targeted screening programs have achieved declining burdens. Evidence-based interventions addressing obesity and metabolic dysfunction are essential for reducing the escalating global ovarian cancer burden.

title: Analysis of factors influencing the efficacy of ovarian vein embolization for pelvic venous insufficiency and development of a short-term efficacy prediction model with internal validation
journal: Journal of Vascular Surgery: Venous and Lymphatic Disorders
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies key predictive factors for short-term success of ovarian vein embolization (OVE) in treating pelvic venous insufficiency (PVI). Three independent variables emerged as significant predictors: shorter duration of lower abdominal discomfort, larger left ovarian vein diameter, and absence of internal iliac vein reflux. Patients achieving symptom remission at three months post-OVE demonstrated these favorable vascular and clinical characteristics compared to non-responders. The researchers developed a predictive model incorporating these factors that achieved an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.807, with a decrease in pelvic venous clinical severity score of ≥4.5 points serving as the optimal threshold for distinguishing remission. Notably, classical anatomical variants such as nutcracker phenomenon, iliac vein compression, and ovarian vein reflux patterns did not significantly influence treatment outcomes, suggesting that symptom duration and parametrial vein dimensions are more clinically relevant for predicting OVE efficacy. This internally validated prediction model may help clinicians identify which PVI patients will respond best to endovascular intervention.

title: Sperm chromatin condensation defects and IVF outcomes: a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study of 647 IVF cycles demonstrates that sperm chromatin condensation defects (SCCD) result from abnormal substitution of histones and protamines, leading to incomplete chromatin compaction that impairs sperm function and fertilization outcomes. Elevated SCCD levels (≥30%) were significantly associated with abnormal conventional semen parameters, reduced two-pronuclei cleavage rates, and lower fertilization rates through mechanisms involving impaired paternal genome decondensation, epigenetic reprogramming, and oxidative stress. After adjusting for confounders, increasing SCCD remained independently associated with reduced clinical pregnancy and live birth rates, with a critical threshold identified at 10.6% SCCD—below which each 1% increase decreased live birth probability by 14%, while above this threshold the association plateaued. The detrimental effects of SCCD on IVF outcomes were most pronounced in younger women (<35 years) and men (<40 years) with normal BMI, female factor infertility, and those receiving lower gonadotropin doses, suggesting that SCCD assessment should be incorporated into pre-IVF evaluation protocols.

title: Impact of endometriotic cystectomy on ovarian reserve function and ovulation induction outcomes in women with endometriosis undergoing assisted reproductive technology
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, a chronic estrogen-dependent disorder affecting reproductive-aged women, is strongly associated with infertility, with prevalence rising to 20–50% in infertile populations. While ovarian endometriomas (the most common manifestation, accounting for 17–44% of cases) themselves are associated with fewer oocytes and lower anti-Müllerian hormone levels, particularly when bilateral or large, laparoscopic cystectomy for their removal causes significant iatrogenic damage to ovarian reserve, reducing AMH levels by approximately 38% postoperatively and increasing the risk of premature ovarian insufficiency twofold. This study found that women with a history of endometriotic cystectomy (Group C) demonstrated markedly diminished ovarian reserve markers—including lower AMH, antral follicle count, and higher incidence of diminished ovarian reserve (26.23% versus ~13% in non-surgical groups)—and consequently required higher gonadotropin doses during assisted reproductive technology while retrieving significantly fewer mature oocytes. The mechanism underlying surgical damage involves direct injury to the ovarian cortex and primordial follicles during cyst removal, compounded by surgery-induced local inflammation leading to ovarian fibrosis and impaired blood supply. Although surgical cystectomy provides significant pain relief, current evidence does not support routine preoperative surgery before IVF/ICSI, with recommendations emphasizing individualized decision-making, fertility-preserving surgical techniques, and specialized surgical expertise to minimize iatrogenic ovarian damage in reproductive-aged women with endometriosis.

title: Illness Perceptions in Psychiatric Disorders: Assessing Differences and Associations With Symptom Severity
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how illness perceptions—patients' cognitive and emotional beliefs about their condition—relate to symptom severity across multiple psychiatric disorders. The research found that illness perceptions significantly predicted symptom severity in anorexia nervosa (AN) and generalized anxiety disorder (GAD), though associations differed markedly between conditions. In GAD, higher perceived personal control and lower concern about illness were associated with reduced symptom severity, while in AN, the relationship was paradoxical: greater perceived personal control over the eating disorder correlated with increased eating disorder symptoms, possibly because this perception reinforces rigid control behaviors around food intake. Treatment control showed more uniformly beneficial effects, with stronger beliefs in treatment efficacy predicting lower severity and better treatment adherence across conditions. Demographic factors also mattered, with older age and shorter illness duration predicting lower severity in anxiety and depressive disorders, while female gender was associated with greater depressive symptom severity. The findings suggest that cognitive-behavioral interventions addressing maladaptive illness perceptions—particularly the paradoxical nature of control beliefs in eating disorders—could enhance clinical outcomes across psychiatric populations.

title: Machine learning prediction of live birth after IVF using the morphological uterus sonographic assessment group features of adenomyosis
summary: Adenomyosis, characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue within the myometrium, is present in approximately 20-30% of women undergoing assisted reproductive technology and significantly impairs fertility outcomes through multiple mechanisms. Women with direct adenomyosis features require higher FSH doses, retrieve fewer mature oocytes, achieve less frequent blastocyst-stage embryo transfers, and experience substantially lower live birth rates (24.5%) compared to women without adenomyosis (approximately 45%). A machine learning model using eXtreme Gradient Boosting identified serum anti-Müllerian hormone (s-AMH) and a regular junctional zone on transvaginal ultrasound as the strongest predictors of live birth, with embryo developmental stage and transfer type also critically important. Adenomyosis impairs fertility through disruption of the junctional zone architecture, leading to abnormal inflammatory responses, local hyperestrogenism, progesterone resistance, dysfunctional uterine peristalsis, and defective spiral artery remodeling. Notably, the location of adenomyosis within the myometrium affects pregnancy outcomes, with junctional zone involvement increasing miscarriage risk threefold while outer myometrial involvement paradoxically improves pregnancy chances. Despite their pathognomonic diagnostic value, direct morphological ultrasound assessment group features of adenomyosis demonstrated low predictive ability for live birth, suggesting that ovarian reserve parameters and embryo quality are more influential determinants of IVF success than adenomyosis morphology alone.

title: Associations of Inflammatory Markers and Coronary Heart Disease in Different Gender Groups in Cohort NHANES 2003–2018
summary: This study examined inflammatory markers as predictors of coronary heart disease (CHD) across gender groups using NHANES data from 2003–2018. The research found that multiple inflammatory ratios—lymphocyte-to-monocyte ratio (LMR), platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio (PLR), systemic immune-inflammation index (SII), and neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR)—show significant associations with CHD risk, but with important gender-dependent patterns. In males, LMR, PLR, and SII were negatively correlated with CHD, while in females, LMR and NLR emerged as more accurate predictive markers. CHD patients demonstrated elevated white blood cells, neutrophils, lymphocytes, and monocytes alongside lower platelet counts and reduced LMR levels. The underlying mechanism involves inflammatory cascade activation, where neutrophils, monocytes, and lymphocytes contribute to vascular injury and atherosclerotic plaque formation, with monocytes and macrophages producing inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α and IL-6) that promote plaque development. These findings suggest that inflammatory cell ratios may serve as accessible, gender-specific biomarkers for CHD risk stratification and could help guide personalized cardiovascular prevention strategies.

title: Clinical Predictors of Successful Pregnancy After In Vitro Fertilization (IVF): A Comprehensive Systematic Review of Evidence
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review identifies female age as the most powerful predictor of IVF success, with a non-linear decline particularly beyond 40 years driven by reduced oocyte quantity and quality, increased aneuploidy, and altered endometrial receptivity. Ovarian reserve markers—AMH for predicting oocyte yield and antral follicle count for embryo availability—combined with embryo quality parameters (number of high-quality embryos and Day-3 embryo morphology), strongly predict clinical pregnancy and cumulative live birth rates. Male factor parameters including total progressive motile sperm count and sperm DNA fragmentation index independently influence IVF success by affecting fertilization failure and blastocyst development. Comorbid conditions including endometriosis, adenomyosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, obesity, and elevated BMI further reduce IVF success probability, with concurrent adenomyosis particularly damaging cumulative live birth rates in endometriosis patients. Machine learning algorithms—particularly XGBoost, support vector machines, and random forest models—achieve superior predictive accuracy for clinical pregnancy and live birth outcomes compared to traditional logistic regression approaches. These findings support a multifactorial assessment incorporating female age, ovarian reserve, sperm quality, embryo morphology, and metabolic/reproductive comorbidities to optimize IVF outcome prediction.

title: The value of the pronucleus size of human single pronucleus zygotes as a predictor of fertility outcomes in conventional in vitro fertilization: a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study demonstrates that pronucleus (PN) diameter is a valuable morphological predictor of embryonic development in single pronucleus (1PN) zygotes derived from conventional in vitro fertilization. Larger PN diameters (≥31-32.5 μm) were significantly associated with higher cleavage rates, blastocyst formation rates, and improved blastocyst quality, with PN diameter showing positive correlations with day-3 cell numbers and good-quality blastocyst development. Although 1PN embryos demonstrate inherently lower developmental potential compared to normal zygotes, those with larger PN diameters can be reliably selected for continued culture, and blastocyst-stage culture effectively screens for chromosomal abnormalities by enriching for diploid embryos. Notably, while PN diameter predicts early embryonic development milestones, it shows no significant correlation with clinical pregnancy, ongoing pregnancy, or live birth outcomes once embryos reach the blastocyst stage, indicating that morphological selection at the pronucleus stage optimizes developmental potential but does not independently determine reproductive success.

title: Investigating the association between systemic immune-inflammation index and female fertility: a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study demonstrates that elevated systemic inflammatory markers—including the Systemic Immune-Inflammation Index (SII), Neutrophil-to-Lymphocyte Ratio (NLR), Platelet-to-Lymphocyte Ratio (PLR), and Product of Platelet and Neutrophil counts (PPN)—are significantly associated with reduced female fertility outcomes. Elevated levels of these markers were consistently linked to diminished ovarian reserve indicators, including lower anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) levels, reduced antral follicle counts (AFC), and fewer oocytes retrieved during in vitro fertilization. The inflammatory markers showed dose-dependent effects, with women in the highest quartiles experiencing meaningful reductions across multiple fertility parameters compared to the lowest quartiles. Notably, women undergoing antagonist protocols demonstrated greater susceptibility to the adverse effects of elevated inflammatory markers on ovarian function. These findings suggest that systemic inflammatory markers may serve as practical predictors of ovarian response and treatment outcomes in fertility care, highlighting the clinical importance of addressing chronic inflammation in women seeking reproductive medicine.

title: Clinical Pregnancy Rate in Intrauterine Insemination and Associated Prognostic Factors
summary:
# Clinical Pregnancy Rate in Intrauterine Insemination and Associated Prognostic Factors

This study identifies endometrial thickness and follicular development as critical determinants of IUI success, with endometrial thickness ≥8 mm significantly predicting higher clinical pregnancy rates. Advanced maternal age impairs fertility through reduced egg quality and decreased ovarian reserve, while sperm quality parameters including motility (≥72.5%) and morphology serve as strong predictors of pregnancy outcomes. IUI is particularly indicated for unexplained infertility and mild to minimal endometriosis, with patients exhibiting ovulatory dysfunction benefiting most, whereas those with tubal factor disease or stage III-IV endometriosis show lower treatment efficacy. Notably, many patient factors including BMI, physical activity, addiction status, and post-wash sperm count demonstrated no significant association with clinical pregnancy rates, suggesting that endometrial and follicular parameters are more reliable prognostic markers than demographic or lifestyle variables. The study emphasizes that optimal timing (IUI within 6 months of hysterosalpingography) and maintenance of appropriate endometrial thickness are essential for maximizing conception rates, while anti-Müllerian hormone levels showed only a trend toward significance in predicting outcomes.

title: Non-high-density lipoprotein cholesterol to high-density lipoprotein cholesterol ratio in relation to erectile dysfunction: a cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study demonstrates that the non-high-density lipoprotein cholesterol to high-density lipoprotein cholesterol ratio (NHHR) is inversely associated with erectile dysfunction (ED), with each unit increase in NHHR reducing ED prevalence by approximately 9%. Erectile dysfunction is mediated through multiple pathophysiological mechanisms including endothelial dysfunction, smooth muscle dysfunction, and oxidative stress, with significant contributions from metabolic disorders such as insulin resistance, metabolic syndrome, and dyslipidemia. The study identifies NHHR as a superior metabolic marker compared to conventional lipid parameters for predicting cardiovascular and metabolic disease risk associated with ED, while establishing mechanistic links between lipid metabolism disorders and testosterone deficiency. ED patients demonstrated significantly lower NHHR values and elevated rates of age, smoking, diabetes, and hypertension compared to healthy controls. The findings suggest that chronic inflammation-induced impairment of HDL function and elevated proinflammatory cytokines may contribute to altered lipid-disease relationships, supporting the therapeutic potential of lipid-modifying interventions in ED management.

title: Causal relationships between psoriasis and coronary artery disease: A two-sample Mendelian randomization study
summary: This Mendelian randomization study establishes a strong causal relationship between psoriasis and coronary artery disease (CAD) and coronary heart disease (CHD), with psoriasis conferring an approximately 11-fold increased risk, while reverse analysis found no reciprocal causality. The underlying mechanism involves shared inflammatory pathways in which psoriasis-associated cytokines—interleukin-17, tumor necrosis factor-α, and interferon-gamma—promote vascular inflammation, thrombosis, and atherosclerotic lesion formation through activation of type 1 T helper cells. Additionally, psoriasis-associated metabolic comorbidities including obesity and dyslipidemia contribute to atherosclerosis progression through adipose tissue-derived inflammatory mediators and increased low-density lipoprotein-mediated oxidative stress. While the study demonstrates strong associations between psoriasis and myocardial infarction in prior literature, the direct causal association with MI and angina pectoris was not statistically significant in the Mendelian randomization analysis. These findings suggest psoriasis represents an independent cardiovascular risk factor, potentially warranting enhanced cardiac monitoring and preventive interventions in psoriatic patients.

title: Discriminative role of inflammatory indices in clinical response to ganglion impar blockade: A retrospective cohort study
summary: Ganglion impar blockade effectively treats chronic coccydynia by reducing somatic and neuropathic pain through interruption of sympathetic signaling. Inflammatory indices, particularly platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio (PLR) and systemic immune-inflammation index (SII), emerge as discriminative biomarkers for predicting treatment response, with responders exhibiting significantly higher baseline values of both indices. Coccygeal morphology type III and V are associated with poor treatment response, likely due to their predisposition to recurrent microtrauma and permanent subluxation. The underlying pathophysiology involves biomechanical trauma triggering chronic irritation of coccygeal nerve roots, which perpetuates central sensitization through prolonged inflammatory signaling that induces maladaptive plasticity in pain pathways. Inflammatory mediators sensitize both peripheral nociceptors and central neurons, sustaining ongoing nociceptive signaling and contributing to chronic pain development. Female patients demonstrate superior response rates to ganglion impar blockade compared to males, possibly reflecting anatomical and biomechanical differences in the female pelvis.

title: Inflammation at the crossroads of reproduction: SIRI as a prognostic signature of female infertility in hybrid regression–machine learning models
summary: Chronic systemic inflammation emerges as a critical factor in female infertility, with the Systemic Inflammatory Response Index (SIRI)—a composite biomarker derived from neutrophil, monocyte, and lymphocyte counts—identified as a powerful prognostic signature. Women with infertility demonstrated significantly elevated SIRI levels and higher counts of circulating immune cells, with those in the highest SIRI quartile showing more than double the odds of infertility compared to fertile controls. Beyond inflammation, infertile women exhibited metabolic dysfunction characterized by elevated body mass index, waist and hip circumference, fasting glucose levels, and diabetes prevalence, alongside reduced HDL cholesterol, suggesting an interconnected pathophysiology linking metabolic dysregulation to reproductive failure. Multiple conditions associated with infertility—including endometriosis, polycystic ovarian syndrome, and pelvic inflammatory disease—share inflammation as a common mechanistic pathway, indicating that systemic inflammatory status may serve as a unifying biomarker across diverse etiologies of female infertility. Machine learning models validated SIRI as one of the most influential predictors of infertility status, supporting its potential utility as a clinical tool for risk stratification and patient management in reproductive medicine.

title: Association of Peripheral Inflammatory Markers with Conception Outcomes Among Women with Endometriosis-Associated Infertility
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that endometriosis-associated infertility is driven by chronic systemic inflammation mediated by activated neutrophils and macrophages, which impair ovarian follicle development, endometrial receptivity, and embryo implantation capacity. Peripheral inflammatory indices—particularly the systemic inflammation index (SII), neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR), and derived neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (dNLR)—are significantly elevated in women with endometriosis and inversely correlate with spontaneous pregnancy achievement, with lower values of these markers associated with substantially higher odds of conception. Women who achieved spontaneous pregnancy exhibited markedly reduced inflammatory indices and elevated lymphocyte levels compared to those who did not conceive, suggesting that preoperative inflammatory status effectively predicts postoperative reproductive outcomes. The abnormal immunoinflammatory responses characteristic of endometriosis interfere with implantation, endometrial remodeling, and extracellular matrix dynamics through processes involving epithelial-mesenchymal transition and protein alterations including fibronectin and collagen IV. These findings establish accessible peripheral inflammatory markers—particularly SII, NLR, and dNLR, which demonstrated excellent discriminative ability with area under the curve values exceeding 0.70—as valuable non-invasive biomarkers for identifying endometriosis patients at risk for persistent infertility despite surgical intervention.

title: How well do women understand their menstrual cycles? Insights from 383,085 UK based women
summary:
# Summary

This large-scale study of 383,085 UK women reveals significant gaps in menstrual cycle awareness, with important implications for reproductive health and healthcare engagement. Menstrual cycle awareness functions as a critical component of overall health and reproductive wellbeing, yet the study found that 22.2% of participants could not report their cycle length, with awareness varying substantially by age, contraceptive use, and reproductive status. Younger women under 25 years and those using hormonal contraception showed the lowest cycle awareness, while women who had been pregnant, were actively trying to conceive, or had diagnosed reproductive conditions demonstrated notably higher awareness levels. Limited menstrual health knowledge directly contributes to delayed healthcare engagement and difficulty distinguishing normal variation from pathological symptoms—a particular concern for conditions like polycystic ovary syndrome where diagnostic delays are common. The findings indicate that structured menstrual health education could improve recognition of cycle changes and facilitate earlier communication with healthcare providers, potentially reducing diagnostic delays for endometriosis and other reproductive disorders. These results underscore menstrual cycle awareness as an essential public health priority with implications for gender equity and timely identification of reproductive health concerns.

title: Ovarian reserve integrated damage (ORI) model for predicting ovarian function recovery after laparoscopic ovarian cystectomy for endometriosis: a randomized controlled trial
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis-associated ovarian cysts compromise ovarian reserve when surgically treated, with the hemostatic technique employed during laparoscopic ovarian cystectomy significantly influencing postoperative ovarian function recovery. Bipolar coagulation, while effective for hemostasis, causes thermal damage to ovarian follicles and stromal tissue, resulting in substantial declines in anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH), antral follicle count (AFC), and peak systolic velocity (PSV) compared to alternative approaches. Oxidised cellulose polymer preserves ovarian reserve more effectively by promoting hemostasis through platelet activation and fibrin formation rather than thermal injury, demonstrating superior preservation with AMH decline of only 5.97% versus 19.06% with bipolar coagulation. The study developed the ORI-PeriOp predictive model incorporating intraoperative variables, which outperformed preoperative assessments in predicting poor ovarian function recovery at six months with an area under the curve of 0.936. This model enables individualized risk stratification to facilitate tailored surgical planning and timing of fertility interventions in women with endometriosis undergoing ovarian cystectomy.

title: AI-driven radiogenomics in gynecologic oncology: from radiological digital biopsy to a new paradigm in precision therapy
summary: This article presents AI-driven radiogenomics as a precision medicine approach for gynecologic oncology, bridging molecular genetics and imaging phenotypes to overcome current diagnostic limitations. The study maps how specific genomic defects—including homologous recombination deficiency (HRD), mismatch repair deficiency (dMMR), and HPV integration—drive cascading molecular consequences that manifest as detectable imaging signatures. For instance, HRD leads to accumulation of genomic scars and structural variants, while dMMR generates high microsatellite instability (MSI-H) that triggers frameshift mutations, high neoantigen loads, and dense CD8+ T-cell infiltration—making these tumors responsive to checkpoint inhibitors. The intratumor heterogeneity generated by genomic instability contributes substantially to therapeutic resistance, relapse, and metastasis, yet traditional single-point biopsies fail to capture this spatial complexity. The article proposes that radiomic features—texture heterogeneity, shape irregularity, and peritumoral immune signatures—can serve as non-invasive digital biomarkers reflecting underlying genomic and immune states, enabling treatment selection (PARPi for HRD, ICIs for dMMR/MSI-H) while avoiding sampling bias inherent in conventional pathology.

title: Evaluation of stiffness values of normal uterine myometrium by 3T magnetic resonance elastography
summary: This feasibility study evaluated uterine myometrial stiffness in normal women using 3T magnetic resonance elastography (MRE), a phase-contrast MRI technique that enables precise assessment of soft tissue mechanical properties. The primary findings revealed that age and postmenopausal status are strong predictors of increased uterine myometrial stiffness, with a correlation coefficient of 0.814 between age and stiffness values and significantly higher stiffness in postmenopausal compared to reproductive-age women. The mechanism underlying these age-related changes involves hormonal deprivation in postmenopausal women, which leads to increased fibrosis, reduced tissue elasticity, decreased vascularity, and alterations in the extracellular matrix composition. Notably, reproductive factors including parity and gravida showed no significant associations with myometrial stiffness, nor did BMI, uterine dimensions, or oral contraceptive use. The study provides important baseline reference values for normal uterine stiffness and establishes MRE as a viable clinical tool for detecting pathological alterations in tissue properties associated with conditions such as adenomyosis, fibroids, endometriosis, and uterine malignancies, which characteristically demonstrate elevated stiffness values compared to normal myometrium.

title: Metabolic dysregulation in follicular fluid is associated with adverse reproductive outcomes in women with decreased ovarian reserve
summary: Women with decreased ovarian reserve (DOR) exhibit profound metabolic dysregulation in follicular fluid that directly impairs reproductive outcomes. DOR is characterized by diminished follicular pools and compromised oocyte quality, leading to poor ovarian response and reduced oocyte yield even during assisted reproductive treatment. Metabolomic analysis identified 17 key differential metabolites enriched in steroid hormone biosynthesis, pentose phosphate, and purine metabolism pathways, with specific metabolites like acetamidopropanal, D-ribose, and oxoadipic acid significantly elevated while steroid hormones including 5α-pregnane-3,20-dione and estrone glucuronide are reduced in DOR patients. These metabolic imbalances partially mediate the adverse effects of DOR on both oocyte yield and embryo quality, serving as biomarkers of the compromised follicular microenvironment. DOR patients demonstrate significantly lower clinical pregnancy rates, live birth rates, and high-quality embryo formation alongside characteristic hormonal abnormalities including reduced luteinizing hormone, progesterone, and testosterone but elevated estradiol. These findings establish follicular fluid metabolomic profiling as a non-invasive diagnostic tool for assessing oocyte developmental competence and predicting assisted reproductive technology outcomes in women with diminished ovarian reserve.

title: Circulating miRNA Signatures Associated with Atherosclerosis and Cardiometabolic Comorbidities in People with HIV
summary:
# Summary

HIV infection drives chronic inflammation and persistent immune activation that contribute to premature age-related cardiometabolic comorbidities in people living with HIV despite antiretroviral therapy. Circulating microRNAs (miRNAs) have emerged as biomarkers of cardiometabolic dysfunction, with specific miRNA signatures associated with atherosclerosis and metabolic disease; miR-638, miR-27b-5p, and miR-3613-5p are consistently downregulated in atheroma plaque and metabolic conditions like diabetes and obesity. Additional miRNAs including miR-140-5p and miR-27b-5p show reduced expression in smokers and individuals with low HDL cholesterol, while miR-140-5p is elevated in those with high comorbidity burden. These circulating miRNAs regulate critical pathways including lipid metabolism, inflammation, glucose homeostasis, and endothelial function, with intracellular miRNAs in peripheral blood mononuclear cells playing direct roles in inflammatory and vascular processes. The dysregulation of multiple miRNA species in people with HIV reflects their involvement in both traditional cardiovascular risk factors (smoking, dyslipidemia, diabetes, obesity) and HIV-specific immune dysregulation, suggesting miRNA profiling may identify individuals at greatest risk for atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease.

title: The association between cardiometabolic index and hypertension-diabetes comorbidity among adults aged 45 and older: evidence from two national databases
summary: This study demonstrates that cardiometabolic index (CMI), calculated from waist-to-height ratio and triglycerides-to-high-density lipoprotein cholesterol ratio, is strongly associated with hypertension-diabetes comorbidity in adults aged 45 and older across two independent national databases (CHARLS and NHANES). Individuals in the highest CMI tertile showed significantly elevated prevalence of comorbid hypertension and diabetes compared to the lowest tertile, with each unit increase in log-transformed CMI associated with substantially higher odds of the comorbidity condition. Sex and smoking status were identified as effect modifiers of this relationship, suggesting that CMI associations with hypertension-diabetes comorbidity vary by these demographic factors. Notably, the study also identified a threshold effect in this association, indicating a potential inflection point beyond which CMI risk escalates. The findings position CMI as a useful biomarker for identifying adults at risk for comorbid cardiometabolic disease and suggest broader applications of CMI in research on metabolic disorders including endometriosis. These results support the utility of CMI as a simple, non-invasive metric for population-level surveillance of hypertension-diabetes comorbidity risk.

title: The Italian version of the Female Genital Self-Image Scale: psychometric properties and associations with sexual function and psychological health
summary: This study validated the Italian version of the Female Genital Self-Image Scale (FGSIS-It) and demonstrated its strong psychometric properties for assessing women's perceptions of their genitals. The research found significant positive correlations between female genital self-image and multiple health outcomes, including sexual function, self-esteem, body appreciation, and overall psychological wellbeing. Women with a positive genital self-image reported greater sexual satisfaction, enhanced sexual attractiveness perception, and were more likely to engage in positive health behaviors including seeking gynecological care. Conversely, poor genital self-image was associated with delayed or avoided medical visits and increased risk of sexual dysfunction, particularly in women under 45 years old. The validated FGSIS-It instrument reliably measures genital self-image as an important component of overall body image and sexual self-concept in Italian-speaking populations. These findings underscore how genital self-perception significantly influences sexual function and psychological health, highlighting the clinical importance of addressing body image concerns in women's healthcare.

title: Microbial Consortia-Immunoglobulin G Glycosylation Combination Scores Identify Severe Hepatic Steatosis and Significant Fibrosis in Patients with Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease
summary: This study demonstrates that combination models integrating fecal microbiota composition with serum immunoglobulin G (IgG) glycosylation features provide superior diagnostic accuracy for metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) compared to conventional clinical scores. Specific bacterial taxa were associated with disease severity: Megamonas was linked to severe hepatic steatosis, while Christensenellaceae R-7, UCG-005, CAG-56, and Lachnospira were associated with significant hepatic fibrosis; conversely, Eubacterium eligens and Clostridia UCG-014 were enriched in patients without severe steatosis. IgG glycosylation patterns, particularly ratios such as IgG2-N4H5F1/IgG1-N4H5F1 and bisected-IgG2/bisected-IgG1, predicted severe steatosis, while IgG2-N4H3F1/IgG1-N4H3F1 and IgG galactosylation indices identified significant fibrosis. The microbial-glycosylation combination scores achieved areas under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.776 for severe steatosis and 0.810 for significant fibrosis, substantially outperforming established noninvasive scores like HSI and FAST. These findings suggest that intestinal dysbiosis modulates systemic immune responses through altered IgG glycosylation, representing a potential therapeutic target through microbiota modification or glycosylation-based interventions in MASLD patients.

title: Fertility preservation in women with endometriosis: How, when, and for whom?
journal: The Journal of International Medical Research
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis causes infertility through multiple mechanisms including follicular loss, reduced ovarian reserve, pelvic adhesions, impaired endometrial receptivity, and chronic pelvic inflammation. Ovarian endometriomas are particularly damaging, associated with lower anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels, and surgical treatment further compromises ovarian reserve through cystectomy or ablation techniques. For women with endometriosis seeking fertility preservation, oocyte cryopreservation is the most widely used approach, with dienogest pretreatment showing promise through increased melatonin levels in follicular fluid that may improve oocyte quality. Patient age at oocyte retrieval is the strongest predictor of live birth success, with women under 35 years demonstrating significantly better outcomes, and fertility preservation is increasingly recommended by specialists—particularly for bilateral or recurrent ovarian endometriomas. Despite growing clinical adoption, fertility preservation counseling remains inconsistently offered across centers, and insurance coverage is limited to select European countries, highlighting disparities in access to these reproductive preservation options.

title: Determining factors associated with the number of oocytes with appropriate morphology in infertile women: A cross-sectional study
summary: This cross-sectional study identified multiple factors that determine the number of morphologically normal oocytes (MNOs) in infertile women, with significant implications for assisted reproductive technology success. Advanced maternal age emerged as a primary determinant, showing a nonlinear inverse association with both MNO quantity and quality, with effects becoming particularly pronounced after age 35 due to accumulated oxidative stress, reduced mitochondrial function, and increased aneuploidy. Metabolic and endocrine factors substantially impacted oocyte competence, including elevated fasting blood sugar, increased luteinizing hormone/follicle-stimulating hormone ratio, and elevated thyroid-stimulating hormone, all inversely associated with MNO counts. Structural ovarian factors such as prior ovarian surgery and ovarian abnormalities significantly reduced MNO production, while polycystic ovary syndrome negatively affected oocyte quality through hyperinsulinemia-induced disruption of folliculogenesis and oocyte maturation. Clinical conditions including endometriosis, smoking, obesity, and alcohol consumption further compromised oocyte quality through oxidative stress, hormonal disruption, and inflammatory changes. The findings underscore that MNO count is a critical predictor of fertility outcomes and ART success, with multiple modifiable and non-modifiable risk factors requiring comprehensive assessment in infertile women.

title: Association Between Body Fat Percentage and Endometriosis Severity Based on American Association of Gynecologic Laparoscopists 2021 Endometriosis Classification
summary: This study reveals a paradoxical inverse relationship between body composition and endometriosis severity, whereby women with severe endometriosis (AAGL stages III–IV) exhibit significantly lower body fat percentage, body mass index, waist-hip ratio, and mid-upper arm circumference compared to those with mild-moderate disease. The mechanisms underlying this association involve complex metabolic and inflammatory pathways: shorter menstrual cycles—driven by both high body fat percentage and low BMI through distinct hormonal mechanisms—increase retrograde menstruation risk and endometriosis susceptibility, while low body adiposity may exacerbate neurogenic hyperalgesia. Conversely, obesity disrupts menstrual regularity through aromatase-mediated estrogen synthesis in adipose tissue and elevated adipokine-driven inflammation (TNF-α, IL-6, C-reactive protein), paradoxically reducing menstrual regurgitation exposure. The study demonstrates that endometriosis links to multiple inflammatory and oxidative stress-associated conditions including asthma, autoimmune diseases, and cardiovascular disease, substantially reducing quality of life. These findings suggest that distinct adiposity patterns mechanistically influence endometriosis development and severity through opposing pathways involving menstrual dysfunction, hormonal imbalance, and systemic inflammation, with treatment options including hormonal medications and surgery for symptomatic relief.

title: Investigating Unilateral Ovarian Endometrioma's Effect on Ova and Embryo Quality in 409 Infertile Endometriotic Patients
summary:
# Summary

This study of 409 endometriotic patients investigated how unilateral ovarian endometriomas affect oocyte and embryo quality during assisted reproductive technology. Endometriosis causes infertility through multiple mechanisms including pelvic anatomical disruptions, endocrine and immunological dysfunction, and elevated inflammatory markers and oxidative stress, with ovarian endometriomas specifically triggering local inflammation and iron-mediated oxidative damage that compromises oocyte maturation. Notably, healthy ovaries yielded significantly more mature MII oocytes, total embryos, and high-grade embryos compared to endometrioma-affected ovaries, though endometrioma size itself was not significantly associated with these reproductive outcomes. Patients with higher anti-müllerian hormone levels (>3 ng/dL) and those classified as good responders to ovarian stimulation demonstrated superior oocyte retrieval and embryo production, while poor responders had the worst outcomes. The findings suggest that assisted reproductive technology is a suitable treatment for endometriosis-related infertility, though embryo aspiration rather than cystectomy may better preserve ovarian function during endometrioma management.

title: Prevalence and Determinants of Low Serum Vitamin D Among Women Attending Infertility Clinics in Japan: A Real‐World Multicenter Cross‐Sectional Study
summary:
# Summary

This multicenter cross-sectional study examined vitamin D status among infertile Japanese women and its associations with reproductive outcomes. Vitamin D receptors and metabolizing enzymes are expressed in reproductive tissues, where vitamin D influences multiple fertility-critical processes including folliculogenesis, steroidogenesis, endometrial receptivity, and immune tolerance. The study found that vitamin D insufficiency was highly prevalent (94% in supplement-free women), with higher body mass index and winter season associated with increased deficiency risk, while supplementation effectively raised serum 25(OH)D levels. Serum 25(OH)D concentrations showed robust associations with improved fertility outcomes, including better clinical pregnancy rates, live birth rates, and assisted reproductive technology success, and vitamin D supplementation improved immune balance by reducing abnormally elevated Th1/Th2 cell ratios in both peripheral blood and endometrial tissue. Lower vitamin D deficiency was inversely associated with tubal factor, diminished ovarian reserve, and male factor infertility, suggesting protective effects across multiple infertility etiologies. These findings support vitamin D supplementation achieving serum 25(OH)D >30 ng/mL as a biologically plausible intervention to optimize immune environments and reproductive outcomes in infertile women.

title: Association between CD56, CD38 and chronic endometritis and analysis of pregnancy prognosis: a single-center retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated CD56 and CD38 as immunohistochemical markers for diagnosing chronic endometritis (CE) and predicting pregnancy outcomes in infertile women. CD56, a natural killer cell marker, and CD38, a plasma cell marker, were both significantly elevated in endometrial samples from CE patients compared to controls, with positive correlations to CD138, the gold-standard CE marker. Elevated CD56+ uterine natural killer cells disrupt normal endometrial function by promoting excessive vascular remodeling and trophoblast invasion, while high CD38-expressing interstitial plasma cells create a pro-inflammatory microenvironment that impairs endometrial receptivity. Chronic endometritis reduced endometrial receptivity and was associated with adverse pregnancy outcomes including infertility, recurrent spontaneous abortion, and repeated implantation failure. When CD38 levels exceeded 9.5 cells per high-power field, the 12-week pregnancy continuation rate was significantly diminished, suggesting CD38 is superior to CD56 for evaluating pregnancy prognosis in CE patients.

title: Construction and validation of risk prediction model for uterine fibroids: a retrospective cohort study based on MIMIC database
summary: This retrospective cohort study from the MIMIC database developed and validated a risk prediction model for uterine fibroids by identifying demographic, biochemical, and hematologic factors associated with disease development. Uterine fibroids, benign neoplasms composed of smooth muscle cells, fibroblasts, and extracellular matrix, are primarily regulated by estrogen and progesterone and show significantly higher prevalence in Black women compared to other racial groups. Key risk factors identified include hypertension, increased body weight and height, elevated red blood cell and platelet counts, and elevated albumin and total cholesterol levels, while decreased hemoglobin, white blood cell counts, and the TyG metabolic index were paradoxically associated with increased fibroid risk. The model demonstrated that machine learning and artificial intelligence techniques effectively improve diagnostic accuracy and prediction speed for uterine fibroids. This comprehensive analysis of clinical and biochemical variables provides a foundation for developing improved risk stratification tools that could enhance early detection and clinical management of this common gynecological condition across diverse populations.

title: Shorter Anogenital Distance in Women with Adenomyosis Diagnosed by MUSA 2022 Criteria: A Prospective Case–Control Study
summary: This prospective case-control study demonstrates that women with adenomyosis have significantly shorter anogenital distance measured from the anus to the fourchette (AGD-af), averaging 23.78 mm compared to 27.88 mm in controls, with AGD-af remaining an independent predictor of adenomyosis after adjusting for age and BMI. Adenomyosis, characterized by ectopic infiltration of endometrial glands and stromal cells into the uterine muscular wall, manifests clinically through dysmenorrhea, heavy menstrual bleeding, and pelvic pain, while sharing with endometriosis a pathophysiology driven by estrogen excess and progesterone resistance. The shorter AGD-af in adenomyosis patients suggests exposure to a relatively hypoestrogenic or hypoandrogenic intrauterine hormonal environment during the critical 8-14 week gestational window, when AGD is determined by the balance between fetal androgen and estrogen levels. This finding parallels the previously documented association of reduced AGD with endometriosis and contrasts with polycystic ovary syndrome, where hyperandrogenism is associated with longer AGD, supporting the hypothesis that prenatal hormonal milieu influences adenomyosis pathogenesis. The stability of AGD-af across postnatal life, independent of age, BMI, vaginal delivery, or episiotomy, positions it as a validated biomarker of in utero androgen exposure and a potentially useful non-invasive clinical marker for adenomyosis assessment.

title: Erectile and Clitoral Dysfunction as Harbingers of Cardiovascular Disease: A Perspective
summary:
# Summary

Sexual dysfunction in both men and women serves as an early clinical indicator of underlying cardiovascular disease, with vasculogenic erectile dysfunction preceding coronary artery disease by approximately 2-5 years and female sexual dysfunction occurring 1.51 times more frequently in patients with established cardiovascular disease. The shared pathophysiological mechanisms linking sexual dysfunction and cardiovascular disease center on endothelial dysfunction, where reduced nitric oxide bioavailability—impaired by inflammation and oxidative stress—fails to adequately promote vasodilation in genital tissues. The vulnerability of genital vasculature, particularly the small-diameter clitoral arteries (0.7-1.1 mm), makes these tissues especially susceptible to atherosclerotic changes. Autonomic imbalance characterized by increased sympathetic tone and decreased parasympathetic activity contributes to sexual dysfunction, exacerbated in women by menopause-related estrogen deficiency that drives both autonomic dysregulation and vulvovaginal tissue changes. Multiple cardiovascular risk factors including dyslipidemia, diabetes, obesity, smoking, and metabolic syndrome directly contribute to sexual dysfunction through systemic inflammation. While lifestyle modifications and phosphodiesterase type 5 inhibitors improve erectile function and reduce cardiovascular risk, clinicians should recognize female sexual dysfunction accompanied by systemic inflammation as a potential cardiovascular risk marker warranting comprehensive cardiovascular evaluation.

title: Real world utilization of coronary artery calcium scoring in a large academic health system
summary: Coronary artery calcium (CAC) scoring demonstrates strong predictive value for future cardiovascular events in a dose-dependent manner, making it a clinically useful tool for risk stratification. Higher CAC scores are significantly associated with increased initiation of preventive pharmacologic therapies and lifestyle modifications, suggesting that quantified cardiovascular risk effectively motivates clinical intervention. The study highlights that female-specific reproductive and metabolic conditions, including endometriosis, polycystic ovarian syndrome, and pregnancy-related complications such as gestational hypertension and preeclampsia, represent important cardiovascular risk factors that warrant consideration in preventive cardiology. This real-world utilization data from a large academic health system demonstrates that CAC scoring effectively translates risk quantification into behavioral and therapeutic changes, supporting its integration into preventive cardiovascular care strategies, particularly for populations with metabolic or reproductive risk factors.

title: The Role of the C‐Reactive Protein–Triglyceride Glucose Index in Predicting New‐Onset Chronic Diseases: Evidence From a Longitudinal Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

The C-reactive protein–triglyceride glucose index (CTI) is a composite biomarker integrating systemic inflammation with insulin resistance and lipid metabolism disturbances. This longitudinal cohort study demonstrates that elevated CTI levels significantly predict new-onset chronic diseases, including diabetes (OR=1.932), dyslipidemia (OR=1.645), stroke (OR=1.676), hypertension (OR=1.411), liver disease, and osteoarthritis, with nonlinear associations observed for some conditions. The underlying mechanisms involve chronic low-grade inflammation damaging vascular endothelial function, oxidative stress promoting vasoconstriction and atherosclerosis, and insulin resistance impairing glucose and lipid metabolism while reducing insulin sensitivity in adipocytes and muscle. These pathogenic pathways are further amplified by lifestyle factors such as smoking, alcohol consumption, and elevated body mass index, which correlate with higher CTI quartiles. The CTI therefore represents a potentially valuable integrated predictor of cardiometabolic and inflammatory disease risk, capturing the interconnected nature of metabolic dysfunction better than individual biomarkers alone.

title: Inverse association between cardiometabolic index and endometriosis in women of reproductive age: A cross-sectional, population-based study
journal: The Journal of International Medical Research
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional population-based study reveals a significant inverse association between cardiometabolic index (CMI) and endometriosis risk in reproductive-age women, with each one-unit increase in CMI corresponding to a 42% reduction in endometriosis odds in fully adjusted models. The cardiometabolic index, calculated from the triglyceride-to-HDL cholesterol ratio and waist-to-height ratio, demonstrates superiority to conventional markers like BMI in capturing metabolic dysfunction. Women with endometriosis exhibited elevated CMI values, central obesity, and atherogenic dyslipidemia despite paradoxically higher metabolic risk profiles, suggesting that endometriosis represents a systemic metabolic disorder linked to chronic inflammation. Age significantly mediates the CMI-endometriosis relationship, with women aged 30–34 years showing the highest endometriosis risk, while oral contraceptive use moderates this association. The findings support chronic inflammation as a shared mechanistic pathway underlying both endometriosis and cardiometabolic abnormalities, positioning CMI as a refined metabolic biomarker for assessing endometriosis-related systemic dysfunction beyond reproductive pathology.

title: Association of peripheral natural killer cell percentage with age, body mass index, serum anti-Müllerian hormone, various autoantibodies, and thrombophilia-related markers in women undergoing in vitro fertilization
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated the association between peripheral natural killer (NK) cell percentage and multiple biomarkers in women undergoing in vitro fertilization, revealing important connections between immune function and reproductive outcomes. Peripheral NK cells, which predominantly exhibit a CD56 dim phenotype, were found to be negatively associated with both body mass index and serum anti-Müllerian hormone levels, suggesting that elevated pNK percentages may impair ovarian reserve. Among autoantibodies examined, only anti-β2-glycoprotein 1 IgG antibody showed a significant association with altered pNK percentages, and NK cells are hypothesized to damage ovarian function through granzyme B secretion, which induces apoptosis in granulosa cells. Although pNK cell percentage has been previously linked to adverse reproductive outcomes including recurrent pregnancy loss and repeated implantation failure—with cutoff values ranging from 12% to 18% considered abnormal—this particular cohort did not demonstrate significant associations between elevated pNK levels and IVF failure, suggesting population-specific variation in pNK pathophysiology. The findings support the clinical use of intravenous immunoglobulin G for women with elevated pNK percentages and reproductive failure, though the complex interplay between systemic NK cell profiles, ovarian function, and immune tolerance requires further investigation.

title: Association Between Metabolic Score for Insulin Resistance (METS-IR) and Risk of Infertility: Evidence from NHANES 2013–2020 and Mendelian Randomization Study
summary: This study establishes a significant association between the Metabolic Score for Insulin Resistance (METS-IR) and infertility risk through analysis of NHANES data and Mendelian randomization approaches. Insulin resistance impairs reproductive function across multiple pathways, including disruption of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis, impaired follicular development and endometrial receptivity in women, and reduced sperm quality in men. The metabolic dysfunction underlying insulin resistance triggers chronic low-grade inflammation, with elevated interleukin-6 and TNF-α driving ovarian dysfunction, impaired spermatogenesis, and reduced embryo implantation, while triglycerides demonstrate a causal relationship with infertility. Mendelian randomization analysis confirms that elevated triglycerides and body mass index causally contribute to infertility risk, with the METS-IR-infertility association particularly pronounced in younger women and mediated primarily by BMI. The study identifies interleukin-18 and CXCL11 as protective factors against infertility, suggesting potential therapeutic targets. These findings demonstrate that metabolic and inflammatory pathways converge to impair reproductive function, establishing METS-IR as a clinically relevant biomarker for identifying infertility risk in diverse populations.

title: Inflammation meets insulin resistance: the role of the CRP-triglyceride-glucose index in association with frailty among middle-aged and older adults in China
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that the C-reactive protein-triglyceride-glucose index (CTI) is an independent predictor of frailty in middle-aged and older Chinese adults, with each unit increase in CTI associated with 15% higher odds of frailty and a dose-response relationship evident across tertiles. The findings support the central role of chronic low-grade inflammation and insulin resistance in frailty pathogenesis, with CTI serving as an integrated biomarker that captures the synergistic burden of these two metabolic disturbances. Excess adiposity drives both the inflammatory and metabolic dysregulation reflected in the CTI, suggesting that metabolic risk aggregation rather than body weight alone determines frailty susceptibility. A CTI threshold of 7.95 was identified as a turning point above which the index showed significant association with frailty burden, providing a practical tool for risk stratification. The study connects frailty to multiple adverse health outcomes including cardiovascular disease, stroke, falls, and mortality, while highlighting shared upstream determinants such as socioeconomic disadvantage, smoking, physical inactivity, and cardiometabolic risk factors including obesity, hypertension, and diabetes. These findings underscore that targeting metabolic dysregulation and chronic inflammation through CTI assessment may offer a more effective approach to mitigating frailty progression than addressing isolated risk factors.

title: Cumulative inflammatory burden of metal mixtures is associated with central obesity, cardiovascular disease, and mortality: findings from NHANES
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that cumulative exposure to multiple heavy metals, quantified through a Metal Mixture Inflammatory Index (MMII), significantly increases cardiovascular disease risk and mortality in a U.S. population-based cohort.
