Heavy metals including cadmium, lead, mercury, antimony, and uranium trigger a systemic inflammatory cascade—marked by elevated C-reactive protein, neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio, and other inflammatory biomarkers—that damages vascular endothelium and promotes oxidative stress and insulin resistance. Each one-unit increase in MMII was associated with nearly 6.5 times higher odds of cardiovascular disease and substantially elevated all-cause mortality risk (HR = 2.39), though notably not cardiovascular-specific mortality. Central obesity, measured by weight-adjusted waist index, partially mediates this relationship, explaining approximately 11% of the metal mixture-cardiovascular disease association. These findings highlight that the cumulative inflammatory burden of environmental metal co-exposures represents a critical cardiometabolic health risk factor beyond individual metal effects alone.

title: Development of a novel meta-polygenic risk score for risk assessment and clinical manifestation prediction in systemic lupus erythematosus
summary:
# Summary

This study developed a novel meta-polygenic risk score (metaPRS) for systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) by integrating genetic information from five traits: SLE development, smoking initiation, serum selenium levels, endometriosis, and Graves' disease. The metaPRS demonstrated robust predictive performance with an odds ratio of 2.12 and showed significantly higher distribution in SLE patients compared to controls, with individuals in high-risk groups exhibiting nearly five-fold increased SLE risk. The metaPRS was strongly associated with multiple SLE-specific autoantibodies including anti-dsDNA, anti-Smith, anti-ribonucleoprotein, and anti-SSA antibodies, and performed particularly well in predicting childhood-onset SLE. Notably, genetic variants driving SLE susceptibility and clinical manifestations were distinct, with SLE-specific genetic variants exclusively driving clinical manifestations while both disease-specific and risk factor variants contributed to overall SLE susceptibility. Compared to conventional single-trait polygenic risk scores, the metaPRS achieved superior diagnostic discrimination and improved SLE disease prediction by 4.43%, offering enhanced risk stratification and early diagnosis potential in East Asian populations.

title: Late-onset menopause and attenuated aortic stiffness
summary: Late-onset menopause (age ≥55 years) is associated with attenuated aortic stiffness and significantly lower cardiovascular disease risk compared to normal-onset menopause, with late-onset postmenopausal women exhibiting approximately 15% lower pulse wave velocity. The reduction in aortic stiffness is primarily driven by lower structural-dependent stiffness (~85% of the effect), with the remaining contribution from reduced load-dependent stiffness and a favorable circulating milieu. Serum from late-onset postmenopausal women produces 35% lower aortic elastic modulus than serum from normal-onset women, a difference attributable to reduced mitochondria-derived reactive oxygen species (mitoROS)-dependent stiffening mechanisms. Triglyceride-related lipid metabolites, particularly TG(16:0), drive these serum effects on aortic stiffness through mitoROS-mediated pathways, with late-onset women having significantly lower circulating levels of these proatherogenic lipids and lower BMI and triglycerides overall. The preserved vascular endothelial function in late-onset postmenopausal women, combined with their attenuated aortic stiffness, mechanistically explains their substantially lower blood pressure and reduced cardiovascular mortality risk, establishing age at menopause as an important female-specific modulator of cardiovascular disease risk independent of traditional risk factors.

title: Association between body mass index and anti-Müllerian hormone in women with ovarian endometrioma and dermoid cyst
summary: This study examined the relationship between body mass index (BMI) and anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH), a marker of ovarian reserve, in women with ovarian endometrioma compared to those with dermoid cysts. While a statistically significant inverse association was observed between BMI and AMH (approximately 2.3% lower AMH per 1 kg/m² higher BMI), BMI explained only 1% of AMH variance and demonstrated minimal clinical utility as a surrogate for ovarian reserve in this population. Women with endometriomas had lower BMI and reduced AMH levels compared to those with dermoid cysts, though age emerged as the strongest determinant of AMH decline. The findings suggest that BMI's weak association with AMH in endometriosis may be obscured by its failure to capture adiposity distribution, adipose tissue immune activity, and local ovarian factors that more strongly influence ovarian reserve. The authors conclude that local ovarian factors related to the endometriotic lesion itself, rather than systemic metabolic status reflected by BMI, likely play a more dominant role in determining ovarian reserve in women with endometrioma.

title: Multivariable AI-based analysis of immune–lifestyle patterns associated with recurrent pregnancy loss: an exploratory retrospective study
summary: This exploratory study employed artificial intelligence-based analysis to identify integrated immune-lifestyle patterns associated with recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL), revealing that multiple interconnected factors contribute to this condition. Metabolic dysfunction, particularly elevated body mass index, promotes oxidative stress and systemic inflammation, both of which compromise reproductive outcomes, while lifestyle factors including smoking and alcohol use independently elevate RPL risk. The study highlights critical immune mechanisms underlying RPL, specifically demonstrating that proper T-cell balance is essential for pregnancy success—Th1-like cells facilitate early implantation-related inflammation, while Th2 responses subsequently maintain fetal tolerance, and disruptions in the Th1/Th2 ratio, natural killer cell dysfunction, and thyroid autoimmunity collectively increase RPL susceptibility. Using a TabNet deep learning model, researchers identified statistically significant associations between RPL and multiple biomarkers including vitamin D3, thyroid-stimulating hormone, specific immune cell populations (CD3, CD4, CD8, CD19, and NK cells), and autoantibodies against thyroid peroxidase, thyroglobulin, β2-glycoprotein, and double-stranded DNA. This multivariable approach demonstrates that RPL arises from dysregulated immune tolerance mechanisms acting in concert with adverse metabolic and lifestyle factors, suggesting that comprehensive assessment of immune-metabolic profiles may improve clinical management and patient stratification.

title: The predictors of sepsis-related acute kidney injury
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary: Sepsis-related acute kidney injury involves multiple interconnected pathophysiological mechanisms, including hemodynamic instability, microcirculatory dysfunction, and inflammatory dysregulation triggered by dysregulated host response to infection. The study identifies key clinical predictors of adverse outcomes in sepsis-associated acute kidney injury, with serum creatinine serving as the primary marker for defining and staging the condition. Multivariate analysis demonstrates that mechanical ventilation requirement and platelet/lymphocyte ratio emerge as independent predictors of mortality in this patient population. The SOFA score comprehensively evaluates organ dysfunction across multiple systems relevant to sepsis pathology, including respiratory, cardiovascular, renal, hepatic, coagulation, and central nervous system function, providing prognostic insight. Additionally, arterial blood lactate concentration and platelet counts correlate with worse clinical outcomes, though the platelet/lymphocyte ratio shows particularly strong association with mortality risk. These findings establish practical biomarkers and clinical parameters for risk stratification and prognostication in patients experiencing sepsis-related acute kidney injury.

title: The use of serum level of microRNA-485-5P in prediction of renal function in lupus nephritis patients
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary: Serum microRNA-485-5P (miR-485-5P) emerges as a promising biomarker for lupus nephritis, the renal manifestation of systemic lupus erythematosus that can progress to end-stage renal disease. Elevated miR-485-5P levels show strong positive correlations with key indicators of renal dysfunction, including serum creatinine, proteinuria, and reduced estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR), while also correlating with disease activity scores and inflammatory cytokine levels. These associations position miR-485-5P as a potential predictor of renal function decline and progression to end-stage renal disease in lupus nephritis patients. The biomarker's correlation with multiple parameters of kidney injury and systemic inflammation suggests it may capture the pathological processes underlying SLE-induced renal damage. This miRNA-based approach could enhance clinical monitoring and risk stratification in lupus nephritis, complementing conventional renal function markers like creatinine and eGFR for chronic kidney disease staging.

title: Vitamin D in gynecological diseases
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary:
# Vitamin D in Gynecological Diseases: Summary

Vitamin D plays a critical role in reproductive health and immune regulation, with deficiency strongly associated with multiple gynecological disorders including endometriosis, uterine myomas, dysmenorrhea, and cervical pathologies. Serum vitamin D deficiency is significantly correlated with increased risk of high-risk HPV infection, abnormal Pap smear results, and larger uterine fibroid sizes, suggesting that vitamin D supports both local and systemic immune function in the female genital tract. Sufficient vitamin D levels demonstrate protective effects across several conditions, reducing endometriosis risk by 24%, myoma incidence by 32%, and breast cancer incidence by 45%, while vitamin D supplementation effectively reduces dysmenorrhea severity and promotes regression of mild cervical intraepithelial lesions. The protective mechanisms involve vitamin D's involvement in anti-inflammatory pathways and prostaglandin regulation, particularly through suppression of proinflammatory cytokines implicated in conditions like dysmenorrhea and endometriosis. These findings establish vitamin D deficiency as a widespread risk factor for gynecological malignancies and benign uterine pathologies, while supporting vitamin D supplementation as a potential therapeutic strategy for multiple gynecological conditions affecting reproductive function.

title: Associations between daytime napping and parameters of ovarian reserve among women undergoing assisted reproductive technology
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how daytime napping influences ovarian reserve parameters in women undergoing assisted reproductive technology, revealing that sleep patterns significantly affect reproductive outcomes. Moderate daytime napping (≤1 hour) was associated with improved ovarian reserve markers, including higher antral follicle counts, lower basal FSH levels, and reduced testosterone concentrations, with women napping ≤1 hour showing a 43% reduced risk of elevated FSH (≥10 IU/L). The beneficial effects of daytime napping appear mechanistically linked to circadian rhythm regulation, as disruptions in circadian rhythms impair ovarian reserve through endocrine pathways that dysregulate the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis and reduce oocyte quality. Notably, the associations between napping and ovarian reserve were particularly pronounced in women with insufficient nocturnal sleep duration (≤7 hours) or poor sleep quality, suggesting that strategic daytime napping may partially compensate for inadequate nighttime sleep. These findings highlight sleep architecture and circadian timing as modifiable lifestyle factors affecting fertility outcomes, with implications for counseling infertile women, particularly those with sleep disorders or circadian misalignment.

title: Comparison of elastography score and strain ratio values according to the presence of stress urinary incontinence in transperineal elastography
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that stress urinary incontinence (SUI) is associated with significantly reduced elasticity and stiffness of the levator ani muscle, as measured by transperineal elastography. Women with SUI exhibited lower elastography scores and strain ratio values both at rest and during Valsalva maneuvers compared to controls, indicating impaired biomechanical support of the pelvic floor. The levator ani muscle plays a critical role in maintaining urethral closure pressure and continence, and damage or loss of elasticity in this muscle contributes to urethral hypermobility and incontinence. Vaginal delivery was identified as a significant risk factor, with women in the SUI group having higher numbers of deliveries and demonstrating softer levator ani muscle tissue, suggesting childbirth-related trauma leads to chronic mechanical loading and loss of muscle elasticity. The findings indicate that SUI represents both an anatomic disorder and a functional problem related to tissue biomechanics dysfunction. Transperineal elastography emerges as a noninvasive, objective imaging technique capable of detecting differences in pelvic floor muscle stiffness and may facilitate early detection and assessment of SUI severity.

title: A phenome-wide hunt for risk factors of Alzheimer’s disease: from metabolic clues to neuroimaging evidence
summary:
# Summary

This phenome-wide study establishes lipid metabolism, particularly elevated low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C), as a causal risk factor for Alzheimer's disease through multi-level evidence spanning genetic, metabolic, neuroimaging, and cognitive domains. Mendelian randomization analyses confirmed a significant causal association between LDL-C and AD, while observational data from the ADNI cohort demonstrated that higher LDL-C levels correlate with reduced volumes in the hippocampus, entorhinal cortex, and hippocampus-amygdala transition area, alongside poorer cognitive performance. The study further identified that cholesterol-to-total lipid ratios in small LDL particles show negative associations with the entorhial-hippocampal complex, suggesting that lipid composition and particle characteristics influence neurodegeneration. Beyond LDL-C, the research contextualizes AD within a broader metabolic dysfunction framework, showing that metabolic syndrome components (abdominal obesity, hypertension, elevated fasting glucose, hypertriglyceridemia, and reduced HDL-C) are associated with increased AD risk, particularly in individuals carrying multiple metabolic risk factors and lacking the protective APOE-ε4 allele. These findings suggest that targeting lipid metabolism and metabolic syndrome management may represent preventive strategies for cognitive decline and Alzheimer's disease development.

title: Contribution of DNA methylation predicted leptin to all-cause, cancer, and cardiovascular mortality among US adults aged 50 years and older: findings from NHANES 1999–2002
journal: Therapeutic Advances in Endocrinology and Metabolism
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how DNA methylation-predicted leptin levels associate with mortality outcomes in US adults aged 50 and older over a 17-year follow-up period. Leptin, an adipocyte-derived hormone regulating energy balance and metabolic processes, showed a J-shaped association with all-cause mortality, with increased hazard ratios in the lowest and highest quartiles compared to reference groups, suggesting that both extremes of leptin levels confer elevated mortality risk. DNA methylation, an epigenetic modification affecting gene expression, mediated leptin's effects on disease outcomes, though body mass index accounted for only a small portion of this association. While leptin interacts with inflammatory pathways and associates with obesity-related conditions including insulin resistance and chronic inflammation, it showed no statistically significant associations with cancer or cardiovascular mortality specifically after full adjustment. The findings underscore leptin's broader role in chronic disease beyond traditional metabolic dysfunction and highlight the importance of epigenetic mechanisms in predicting mortality risk in aging populations.

title: Clinical characteristics and associated risk factors for diminished ovarian reserve among Chinese women: a matched case-control study
summary:
# Summary

This matched case-control study identified multiple independent risk factors for diminished ovarian reserve (DOR) in Chinese women. Demographic and lifestyle factors including non-Han ethnicity, manual labor occupation, and obesity were significantly associated with DOR, as were clinical features such as light menstrual flow, while multiparity was protective. Importantly, TORCH infections—particularly Toxoplasma gondii with markedly elevated odds ratios—were strongly associated with DOR, with particularly pronounced effects in younger women aged 20–35 years. Women with DOR demonstrated characteristic hormonal dysregulation including elevated FSH, estradiol, and luteinizing hormone alongside reduced anti-Müllerian hormone, antral follicle count, prolactin, and testosterone, reflecting impaired ovarian feedback mechanisms and declining follicle number. These endocrine and sonographic findings correlated with reduced oocyte quantity and quality, ultimately contributing to reproductive dysfunctions including infertility, menstrual irregularities, and recurrent miscarriage.

title: Sagittal Double‐Hump Deformity of the Lumbosacral Spine: An Anatomical Risk Factor for Surgical Landmark Obscuration During Laparoscopic Sacrocolpopexy
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies sagittal double-hump deformity of the lumbosacral spine as an anatomical risk factor that significantly impairs surgical landmark visualization during laparoscopic sacrocolpopexy (LSC), a procedure used to treat pelvic organ prolapse. The double-hump deformity, characterized by anterior protrusions of the L4-L5 and L5-S1 intervertebral disks resulting from L4-L5 hyperlordosis combined with L5-S1 segmental flattening, is associated with reduced L5-S1 angulation, decreased L5-S1 disk height, and associated spinal degenerative changes including disk narrowing and spondylolisthesis. In 50% of double-hump cases, sacral promontory visualization was rated as difficult, and patients with this deformity experienced increased blood loss during surgery. The anatomical distortion causes potential confusion between the L4-L5 disk and the sacral promontory, potentially leading to inadvertent mesh fixation to intervertebral disks with serious consequences including discitis, osteomyelitis, and chronic pelvic pain. The authors recommend careful preoperative imaging and meticulous surgical dissection in patients with recognized double-hump anatomy to mitigate the risk of erroneous fixation and other operative complications.

title: FSH Receptor Asn680Ser Polymorphism Modulates Intrafollicular Nitric Oxide Bioavailability and Ovarian Responsiveness During IVF
summary:
# Summary

The FSHR Asn680Ser polymorphism modulates ovarian responsiveness during in vitro fertilization by affecting intrafollicular nitric oxide bioavailability. Women homozygous for the Ser680 allele demonstrate superior ovarian response compared to Asn allele carriers, producing significantly more total oocytes, mature metaphase II oocytes, and properly fertilized two-pronuclear embryos, despite sometimes requiring higher FSH doses. This enhanced response is mechanistically linked to elevated intrafollicular nitric oxide metabolite concentrations in Ser/Ser individuals, which promotes angiogenesis, vascular tone regulation, steroidogenesis, and oocyte competence through cGMP-dependent pathways. FSH binding to FSHR triggers downstream signaling cascades including PI3K/AKT, ERK1/2, p38 MAPK, and β-arrestin-dependent pathways that collectively govern granulosa cell proliferation, steroidogenic function, and follicular maturation. These findings indicate that the FSHR genetic variation primarily influences local follicular function and oocyte competence rather than systemic circulating hormone levels, establishing a genetic basis for variable IVF outcomes across different FSHR polymorphisms.

title: HbA1c as a Predictor of Complications in Anterior and Posterior Colporrhaphy
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c) as a predictor of complications following anterior and posterior colporrhaphy, a surgical treatment for pelvic organ prolapse. The research demonstrates that patients with HbA1c ≥6.5 have significantly elevated surgical risk, with 3.16-fold higher odds of postoperative complications and increased mortality compared to those with HbA1c ≤6.4. Elevated HbA1c impairs surgical outcomes through multiple mechanisms: hyperglycemia promotes pathogenic bacterial growth and surgical site infections, impairs immune function and wound healing, and diabetes-associated autonomic neuropathy compromises bladder emptying, predisposing patients to urinary tract infections. Additionally, comorbidities more prevalent in the high HbA1c group—including insulin-dependent diabetes, hypertension, and higher ASA classification—contribute to postoperative morbidity through endothelial dysfunction and microvascular disease. The findings establish preoperative glycemic control as a critical modifiable risk factor and suggest that HbA1c optimization before colporrhaphy could reduce perioperative complications in this high-risk surgical population.

title: The association between the red blood cell distribution width-to-albumin ratio and female infertility: A cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study demonstrates that the red blood cell distribution width-to-albumin ratio (RAR) is significantly associated with female infertility, with infertility risk increasing substantially when RAR exceeds 3.32. RAR functions as a composite biomarker reflecting both systemic inflammation and nutritional status, as elevated red blood cell distribution width indicates inflammation and oxidative stress while low albumin levels reflect chronic inflammation and malnutrition. The pathophysiological mechanisms underlying this association involve systemic inflammation and oxidative stress impairing critical reproductive processes, including follicular development, ovulation, and embryo implantation, while oxidative stress directly damages ovarian cells through mitochondrial injury, DNA fragmentation, and apoptosis that reduces oocyte quality. The RAR-infertility association was more pronounced in women aged 35 years or older and those with lower education levels, suggesting that socioeconomic and demographic factors modulate this relationship. These findings position RAR as a potentially valuable clinical biomarker for identifying women at elevated infertility risk and highlight the central role of inflammation and oxidative stress in female reproductive dysfunction.

title: Clinical value of combined serum galectin-9 and urine MCP-1 testing for assessment of systemic lupus erythematosus disease activity and early diagnosis of lupus nephritis
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that combined serum galectin-9 and urinary monocyte chemoattractant protein-1 (MCP-1) testing provides superior diagnostic and prognostic value for systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) disease activity and lupus nephritis (LN) detection. Serum galectin-9 and urinary MCP-1 levels were strongly positively correlated with SLE disease activity scores (SLEDAI) and progressively increased from inactive SLE to active SLE with LN, with MCP-1 levels particularly elevated in proliferative nephritis subtypes. Mechanistically, galectin-9 regulates immune homeostasis by promoting helper T cell apoptosis and regulatory T cell differentiation, while MCP-1 drives renal inflammation by recruiting monocytes and T lymphocytes and elevating expression in renal tubular epithelial cells and mesangial cells. Combined detection of both biomarkers yielded an area under the curve of 0.866 for LN diagnosis, outperforming either marker alone, and elevated levels of both were independently associated with increased LN risk alongside traditional markers like low serum C3 and anti-dsDNA positivity. These findings establish serum galectin-9 and urinary MCP-1 as valuable non-invasive biomarkers for assessing SLE disease severity and predicting nephritis development.

title: Association of sperm DNA fragmentation index with semen quality and ART outcomes: real-world evidence from a retrospective cohort
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective cohort study demonstrates that sperm DNA fragmentation index (DFI) serves as an independent marker of male fertility, with elevated DFI strongly associated with reduced semen quality parameters including sperm concentration, progressive motility, and total motility. High DFI significantly decreased the two-pronuclear (2PN) fertilization rate, suggesting impaired fertilization capacity; however, notably, elevated DFI did not compromise subsequent embryological development, clinical pregnancy rates, or live birth outcomes in assisted reproductive technology (ART) cycles. The study identified older paternal age, smaller testicular volumes, and varicocele as clinical correlates of elevated DFI, with the underlying pathophysiology likely involving DNA strand breaks during spermatogenesis caused by poor lifestyle factors, systemic diseases, and genital tract infections. These findings support incorporating DFI testing into standard semen evaluation protocols to advance male fertility assessment beyond traditional microscopy to the molecular level, particularly for predicting fertilization outcomes in ART.

title: Evaluating the Role of Lipid Accumulation Product Index in Endometriosis: Findings from NHANES and Mendelian Randomization
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that the lipid accumulation product (LAP) index, a composite measure of visceral fat and triglycerides, is significantly elevated in women with endometriosis and causally associated with disease risk. Mendelian randomization analysis confirms that elevated triglyceride levels directly increase endometriosis susceptibility, with metabolic dysfunction more broadly linked to disease development. The proposed mechanistic pathway involves visceral fat accumulation triggering chronic low-grade inflammation through increased pro-inflammatory cytokines (interleukin-6, tumor necrosis factor-alpha) and cellular senescence, which collectively impair estrogen metabolism and facilitate ectopic endometrial cell migration and invasion. Endometriotic lesions themselves exhibit abnormal lipid accumulation with elevated phosphoethanolamine, sphingomyelin, diglycerides, phosphatidylcholines, and triglycerides. The LAP index also serves as a sensitive marker for metabolic comorbidities frequently observed in endometriosis patients, including insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, and metabolic syndrome, suggesting that metabolic dysfunction represents a significant modifiable risk factor in endometriosis pathogenesis.

title: Determining risk factors predicting miscarriage among couples undergoing assisted reproductive treatment: a systematic review
summary: This systematic review identifies multiple modifiable and non-modifiable risk factors for miscarriage among couples undergoing assisted reproductive treatment. Demographic and lifestyle factors significantly influence miscarriage risk, including advanced parental age (particularly over 35-40 years), abnormal body mass index (both high and low), smoking, alcohol consumption, and psychological stress. Clinical factors including hormonal imbalances (abnormal FSH, AMH, and TSH levels), poor ovarian reserve, thin endometrium, genital tract infections, and ovarian structural abnormalities substantially increase miscarriage likelihood, while treatment characteristics such as embryo transfer protocols and grading also play important roles. The review highlights that increasing age is associated with decreased egg quality and mitochondrial dysfunction, and that obesity specifically triggers systemic inflammation and insulin resistance, amplifying miscarriage risk. Conversely, nutritional interventions including vitamins D and E, folic acid, omega-3 fatty acids, and antioxidants improve fertility outcomes, while regular exercise and stress management support better reproductive results. These findings underscore that miscarriage prevention in ART requires comprehensive management addressing both intrinsic biological factors and modifiable lifestyle characteristics.

title: Genetic variants of CYP2C19 and CYP2C18 and their clinical implications in the Saudi population
journal: Journal of Taibah University Medical Sciences
summary:
# Summary

This study characterizes genetic variants in CYP2C19 and CYP2C18, cytochrome P450 enzymes responsible for metabolizing numerous clinically important drugs including clopidogrel, tamoxifen, omeprazole, and progesterone. Key variant alleles CYP2C19∗17 confers ultra-activity while CYP2C19∗2 and CYP2C19∗3 cause loss-of-function, directly influencing pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics across multiple drug classes. In the Saudi population specifically, five high-frequency variants (rs7067866, rs7916649, rs4917623, rs12248560, and rs11188072) were identified, with rs12248560 and rs4917623 notably associated with endometriosis and female infertility, while rs12248560 also correlates with altered endometrial thickness. The research demonstrates that genetic variants affecting CYP2C19 and CYP2C18 enzymatic activity have broader clinical implications extending beyond drug metabolism to reproductive disorders and metabolic conditions, supporting the application of pharmacogenomic profiling for personalized medicine and informed clinical decision-making in populations with distinct genetic architectures.

title: Beyond the Scale: Effects of Maternal Obesity on Embryo Morphokinetics and IVF Outcomes
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how maternal body mass index (BMI) affects embryo development and in vitro fertilization (IVF) outcomes in a large cohort. Higher BMI was significantly associated with anovulatory infertility, increased endometrial thickness, and reduced peak estradiol levels and estradiol-to-oocyte ratios, likely reflecting reduced granulosa cell responsiveness and altered steroidogenesis in obese women. Notably, while embryo morphokinetics showed accelerated cleavage in overweight women during early development, clinical pregnancy and live birth rates were not significantly different across BMI groups, with endometrial thickness—not BMI itself—emerging as an independent predictor of clinical pregnancy. However, obesity had pronounced effects on obstetric outcomes, with cesarean section rates nearly doubling in obese patients (51.9% versus ~25–28% in other groups) and gestational diabetes occurring twice as frequently in overweight and obese women compared to normal weight controls. These findings suggest that while maternal obesity impairs ovulatory function and steroid hormone production through mechanisms including insulin resistance, inflammation, and altered adipokine signaling, the IVF process may partially compensate for oocyte quality differences, though downstream metabolic complications in pregnancy remain substantially elevated.

title: Early Postoperative Albumin and Neutrophil Dynamics for Risk Stratification After Cytoreductive Surgery in Ovarian Cancer: A Retrospective Multicenter Cohort Study
summary: This retrospective multicenter cohort study identifies early postoperative serum albumin as a strong independent predictor of early surgical complications following cytoreductive surgery for ovarian cancer. Serum albumin reflects multiple physiological parameters including nutritional status, protein balance, systemic inflammation, liver function, and metabolic reserve; hypoalbuminemia is consistently linked to poor wound healing, increased infection risk, and prolonged hospitalization. Multivariable analysis demonstrated that lower postoperative albumin levels independently predicted early complications with an odds ratio of 0.26, while higher body mass index and surgical complexity also increased complication risk. Among patients with preserved albumin levels (≥3 g/dL), reduced postoperative neutrophil decline independently predicted complications, suggesting that dynamic neutrophil changes provide additional prognostic information when baseline nutritional status is maintained. The early postoperative period reflects both baseline nutritional status and the acute metabolic response to surgical trauma, with tumor-related inflammation altering white blood cell behavior, cytokine production, and metabolic pathways. Incorporating these low-cost metabolic and inflammatory biomarkers into early postoperative risk stratification protocols may improve identification of high-risk patients and support targeted postoperative interventions.

title: Epidemiology, Etiopathogenesis, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Male Infertility—Current Trends and Future Directions: A Narrative Review
summary: Male infertility is a multifactorial condition involving genetic, endocrine, structural, environmental, and lifestyle factors, with sperm DNA fragmentation emerging as a particularly significant pathological mechanism in idiopathic cases. Key structural and acquired causes include varicocele, which induces testicular hyperthermia and oxidative stress leading to sperm DNA damage and apoptosis, as well as chromosomal abnormalities such as Klinefelter Syndrome and Y-chromosome microdeletions that cause azoospermia. Molecular mechanisms underlying male infertility have been substantially expanded to include epigenetic abnormalities, mitochondrial impairment, aberrant DNA methylation patterns, dysregulated protamine expression, and altered metabolomic profiles, alongside emerging environmental exposures including microplastics, bisphenol A, dioxins, and pesticides that collectively reduce sperm concentration and motility. Female factors contributing to couple infertility include polycystic ovary syndrome and endometriosis-induced tubal pathology, while infertility itself carries significant psychosocial consequences including depression, anxiety disorders, and eating disorders. Beyond biological mechanisms, age, cigarette smoking, substance abuse, obesity, and sedentary lifestyle represent modifiable risk factors affecting fertility outcomes in both sexes.

title: Association between low-density lipoprotein cholesterol and frailty in adults aged ≥70 years: a cross-sectional study from Beijing, China
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study of Chinese adults aged 70 and older reveals an inverse association between low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C) and frailty, with adjusted analyses demonstrating that lower LDL-C levels are significantly associated with increased frailty risk. Beyond lipid metabolism, frailty showed strong correlations with aging, cardiovascular disease, reduced body mass index, polypharmacy, coronary heart disease, and diminished physical activity and functional capacity. The authors propose that chronic inflammation serves as a common mechanistic pathway linking both LDL-C and frailty, wherein pro-inflammatory cytokines drive muscle catabolism and upregulate LDL receptors, while simultaneously promoting endothelial dysfunction through oxidized LDL accumulation. Additional findings indicate that hematologic parameters (red blood cells, hemoglobin, platelets) and inflammatory markers (high-sensitivity C-reactive protein) also differ significantly across frailty categories, suggesting multifactorial biological involvement. The study highlights that nutritional insufficiency may impair LDL-C synthesis in frail individuals, and notes cholesterol's essential roles in host defense, steroid hormone synthesis, and vascular homeostasis—functions potentially compromised in the frail state. Overall, these results underscore frailty as a complex geriatric syndrome with substantial implications for falls, hospitalization, and mortality risk in older populations.

title: Prediction of Recurrence After Endometrial Polypectomy in Women of Childbearing Age Based on Machine Learning Algorithm
summary: Endometrial polyps in women of childbearing age are associated with estrogen-driven endocrine dysregulation and chronic inflammatory processes, with postoperative recurrence remaining a significant clinical challenge. This study identified five independent predictors of recurrence following endometrial polypectomy: older patient age, posterior uterine wall polyp location, history of prior uterine surgery, functional histopathological polyp type, and lower hemoglobin concentration. A machine learning random forest algorithm incorporating these predictors achieved superior performance in both training (AUC = 0.838) and validation cohorts (AUC = 0.760), accurately stratifying recurrence risk. The mechanistic basis for recurrence involves advancing age-associated endocrine dysregulation, uterine surgery-induced chronic inflammation and endocrine disturbances, and the estrogen-dependent nature of functional polyps that synchronize with the endometrial cycle. Protective factors against recurrence include higher hemoglobin levels and non-functional polyps, whereas elevated estrogen secretion in reproductive-age women drives rapid polyp growth and increased recurrence risk. This predictive model provides clinicians with a data-driven approach to identify high-risk patients who may benefit from intensified postoperative surveillance or adjunctive therapeutic interventions to prevent recurrence and preserve fertility.

title: Mejorar la comunicación sobre la equidad en la salud en la investigación observacional (STROBE Equidad): la lista de verificación ampliada y su elaboración*
summary: I cannot provide a summary of this article's key findings because no relationship triples were extracted from it. The article appears to focus on reporting guidelines for observational research studies that address health equity (STROBE Equity checklist), rather than on endometriosis-specific biomedical mechanisms or clinical findings. To generate a meaningful summary within my specialization, I would need structured data on relationships between biological entities, clinical interventions, or disease mechanisms. If you have an endometriosis-related article with extracted relationship triples, please provide that instead.

title: Modifiable and Nonmodifiable Risk Factors for Breast Cancer: A Comprehensive Scoping Review
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review identifies both nonmodifiable and modifiable risk factors contributing to breast cancer development. Nonmodifiable genetic factors—including BRCA1/BRCA2 mutations, polymorphisms in DNA damage repair genes, and polygenic risk scores—confer substantially elevated lifetime breast cancer risk, with effects that vary by molecular subtype and race/ethnicity, particularly among Black women who experience higher incidence of aggressive triple-negative breast cancer. Reproductive and hormonal factors such as early menarche, late menopause, hormone replacement therapy, and nulliparity increase risk through prolonged estrogen exposure. Modifiable lifestyle factors—including smoking, excessive alcohol consumption, obesity, metabolic dysfunction, and Western dietary patterns—promote breast cancer development, while protective factors such as physical activity, Mediterranean diet adherence, fruit and vegetable consumption, and breastfeeding reduce risk. The review emphasizes that preventive strategies focusing on lifestyle modification, genetic screening when indicated, and reproductive optimization through nonhormonal methods represent actionable approaches to reduce breast cancer incidence across populations.

title: Association of widespread pain and peak alpha frequency cannot be isolated to a single network
summary: This study examined the relationship between peak alpha frequency (PAF), a measure derived from electroencephalography, and widespread pain in patients with chronic pelvic pain syndromes. The researchers found that individuals with widespread pain demonstrated significantly lower PAF globally and across multiple brain regions (frontal, sensorimotor, parietal, and occipital) compared to those with localized pain, suggesting that widespread pain is associated with broadly distributed changes in cortical function rather than dysfunction isolated to a single neural network. Notably, PAF measured using channel-based methods showed larger effect sizes than component-based approaches in distinguishing widespread from localized pain, indicating that global brain electrical activity patterns are more informative than isolated network analysis. The sensorimotor cortex emerged as particularly relevant, with both regional PAF reductions and sensorimotor component analysis correlating with pain sensitivity, though the reduction in PAF was observed across all electrodes in widespread pain conditions. These findings suggest that widespread pain reflects diffuse alterations in cortical electrophysiology linked to heightened sensory sensitivity, rather than localized network dysfunction, providing insights into the neurobiological basis of chronic overlapping pain conditions including endometriosis-related pelvic pain.

title: Moderating effect of dysmenorrhea on the association between number of pregnancies and antenatal depression in the third trimester: a cross-sectional study
summary: This cross-sectional study examined how dysmenorrhea moderates the relationship between reproductive history and antenatal depression during the third trimester. The research found strong associations between dysmenorrhea and elevated antenatal depression scores (EPDS), with dysmenorrhea independently linked to worsening depressive symptoms in pregnant women. Notably, the number of pregnancies and deliveries showed a significant positive association with depression scores, but this effect was substantially amplified in women with dysmenorrhea, demonstrating a critical interaction between these factors. Protective factors including higher maternal age, greater educational attainment, and higher partner education levels were inversely associated with depression scores, particularly in the non-dysmenorrhea group. The study also highlights that severe antenatal depression can precipitate complications such as hyperemesis gravidarum, prolonged labor, and neonatal asphyxia. These findings underscore dysmenorrhea as a key clinical moderator that exacerbates perinatal mental health risk, particularly among women with multiple prior pregnancies, suggesting dysmenorrhea screening and management should be integrated into prenatal mental health care.

title: Diagnostic utility of pleural fluid adenosine deaminase in tuberculous pleural effusion in patients with exudative effusion: a retrospective multicenter study (ADATPE)
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective multicenter study evaluates pleural fluid adenosine deaminase (pfADA), a purine catabolic enzyme abundant in lymphoid tissue, as a diagnostic biomarker for tuberculous pleural effusion (TPE), a common complication affecting up to 20% of pulmonary tuberculosis patients. The authors established an optimized local cutoff of pfADA ≥36 U/L that achieved 82.9% sensitivity and 80.6% specificity, with an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.852, outperforming previously published standards. When combined with the LDH/ADA ratio ≤16, this dual criterion yielded superior diagnostic specificity of 90.78% and overall accuracy of 83.70%. Among patients with exudative pleural effusion in the cohort (n=276), younger age, higher pleural fluid protein, elevated pleural ADA, and lower LDH/ADA ratio were significantly associated with TPE diagnosis. The findings demonstrate that pfADA offers practical advantages including low cost and rapid turnaround time, making it a valuable diagnostic tool when interpreted alongside other clinical and laboratory parameters, though thoracoscopic pleural biopsy remains the gold standard for definitive diagnosis.

title: Influence of Borderline and Pathological Sperm Morphology on Pregnancy Outcomes following Intrauterine Insemination in Pregnant Women: A Retrospective Study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective study examined how sperm morphology affects pregnancy outcomes in couples undergoing intrauterine insemination, comparing borderline and pathologically abnormal sperm morphology. Pathological sperm morphology was strongly associated with adverse outcomes, including significantly elevated first-trimester abortion rates (26.7% versus 2.8% in the borderline group) and reduced live birth rates (69.3% versus 91.7%), while each unit increase in the percentage of morphologically normal sperm reduced abortion risk by approximately 40%. The findings indicate that sperm morphology is a critical predictor of IUI success independent of clinical pregnancy rates, as abnormal morphology correlates with recurrent spontaneous abortion, aneuploid embryos, and gestational complications even among women who initially achieve pregnancy. These results support the recommendation that sperm morphology assessment should be routinely considered in evaluating male fertility and predicting pregnancy continuation beyond early stages in assisted reproductive medicine.
